```
> news:S%pMa.29299$8B.3874@twister.nyroc.rr.com...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> ANSI X3.23-1985:  "Except in the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph, represents one
or
> more of the character that has the lowest ordinal position in the program
> collating sequence."
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> In an 8-bit encoding system in which there are 256 characters in the
program
> collating sequence and in which the default collating sequence is in use,
it
> strikes me as unlikely that LOW-VALUES would have a value higher than
@00@,
> the hex representation of the lowest character in that set of 256
> characters.  I don't know how I'd reconcile such a situation with the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

    I am not certain, but I think that some Cobols used to work differently
when dealing with a numeric field, eg moving the lowest LEGAL value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: The actual value of LOW-VALUES

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-07T07:46:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bec13n$20lv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<S%pMa.29299$8B.3874@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <bduqc7$1rmt$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <BSmdnbrWNZS19p6iXTWJkg@comcast.com>`

```

"Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net> wrote in message
news:BSmdnbrWNZS19p6iXTWJkg@comcast.com...

>     I am not certain, but I think that some Cobols used to work
differently
> when dealing with a numeric field, eg moving the lowest LEGAL value.

If they did it would have been an extension in which the implementor defined
(or even failed to define) the consequences.  Also, this definition might
present problems with signed fields; do you mean the lowest legal MAGNITUDE?
And if so, is the sign positive or negative?

LOW-VALUES is defined in standard COBOL as a character-based entity.
Essentially, I *believe* a statement like MOVE LOW-VALUES TO NUMERIC-ITEM is
handled as if LOW-VALUES is supposed to produce the same results as:
    01  NUMERIC-ITEM PIC S9(5)V99.
    01  TEMP-FIELD PIC X(5).
    01  TEMP-FIELD-NUMERIC REDEFINES TEMP-FIELD PIC 9(5).
    ...
     MOVE LOW-VALUES TO TEMP-FIELD.
     MOVE TEMP-FIELD-NUMERIC TO NUMERIC-ITEM.

Note that the results of the first MOVE are defined, but the results of the
second are not.  For a numeric data item, "its content when expressed in
standard data format must be one or more numeric characters  ..."  Only in
implementations and operating environments in which the bit pattern of the
character that LOW-VALUES represents also happens to represent a numeric
character are the results of the second MOVE defined in standard COBOL, and
that's not true for either ASCII or EBCDIC.

The LOWEST-ALGEBRAIC function was introduced in the 2002 standard at least
in part to provide a standard mechanism for determining the lowest legal
value for a given numeric field.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

## Thread: Printing Esc Sequence in PowerCobol — 6 msgs

### Printing Esc Sequence in PowerCobol

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-02T13:27:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3066396.1057152433@dbforums.com>`

```

hi.

Does anybody here knows why when I print
an ESC sequence  (usually PCL escape Sequence)
using Powercobol  doent  work ( Nothing happens)
Dont Print anything.

But if , redirect   to a TXT  file, and

using  TYPE  TXTFILE  >  LPT1  , works   fine

Do you know why ?

Tks

Carlos Lages
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing Esc Sequence in PowerCobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T07:11:08+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdvatb$fnf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3066396.1057152433@dbforums.com>`

```
Carlos lages wrote:

> Does anybody here knows why when I print
> an ESC sequence  (usually PCL escape Sequence)
> using Powercobol  doent  work ( Nothing happens)
> Dont Print anything.

Does it print if you don't send an ESC ?  ie only send text. It may be that 
you are using the wrong name for the print file.

How is the SELECT and FD defined ?
 
> But if , redirect   to a TXT  file, and
> using  TYPE  TXTFILE  >  LPT1  , works   fine

When you 'redirect' to a TXT file is this using DISPLAY in the program and 
'>' on the command line ?  Or are you WRITEing to a file ?

One difference is that TYPE is a DOS program and will not use the Windows 
printer driver in the same way.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Printing Esc Sequence in PowerCobol

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-02T22:15:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3069069.1057184133@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3066396.1057152433@dbforums.com> <bdvatb$fnf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

any others  Reports works fine
all the programs use the same way to print

only this one that has ESc sequence  Doesnt work.

I my  research, I think Power Doesnt Interpret the ESC (alt+27) command.

The worst thing is that I get no Erros, simply nothing happens.

tks

Carlos Lages
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing Esc Sequence in PowerCobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T12:30:08+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdvtkj$nd5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3066396.1057152433@dbforums.com> <bdvatb$fnf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3069069.1057184133@dbforums.com>`

```
Carlos lages wrote:

> I my  research, I think Power Doesnt Interpret the ESC (alt+27) command.
> 
> The worst thing is that I get no Erros, simply nothing happens.

The ESC sequence is probably upsetting the Windows print driver.  You are 
not supposed to send control sequences through the driver, you are supposed 
to control it with the Windows API (or have Cobol do this for you).

When you use TYPE this is a DOS program and Windows just passes whatever 
you send through without doing anything at all, thus the ESC sequences have 
no effect on Windows driver and work on the printer.

Try setting up a 'generic text only' Windows printer and send the sequences 
through that.

Do you get a document created in the print queue ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing Esc Sequence in PowerCobol

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T08:34:36+01:00

```
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Frederico
```

```
news:bdvatb$fnf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...

>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

that

want to
use PCL, you will need to do "raw" printing.

version of windows and the correct options.
```

## Thread: Net Express Dialog error — 3 msgs

### Net Express Dialog error

- **From:** dkrekel <member27880@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-02T14:10:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3066684.1057155050@dbforums.com>`

```

I am getting an error 17 (DS-SCREEN-MANAGER-ERROR) when trying to load a
screen set in 2.0 runtime. The function it is executing is (0) which is
screenset initialize. The return code is (5), P2ERR-CANT-INITIALIZE. I
can take the same GS and run it against 3.0 runtime and it works fine.

Any Ideas?
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express Dialog error

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-07-04T11:11:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3075000.1057317081@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3066684.1057155050@dbforums.com>`

```

programs and GS 2.0 in runtime > 2.0 ok
programs and GS 3.0, 3.1, 4.0 in runtime 2.0 not work
```

#### ↳ Re: Net Express Dialog error

- **From:** dkrekel <member27880@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-16T14:16:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3116952.1058364969@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3066684.1057155050@dbforums.com>`

```

The problem was in 2.0 I had a character based EXE calling a GUI
based GNT. This works in 3.1 but does not in 2.0. We had to change
the she service program over to character based GNT and it worked
fine. We currently have about 40-50 screensets originated in 2.0 and
maintained in 3.1 and they work great. We have even developed 4-5
screen sets in 3.1, recompiled the Cobol source it 2.0 and run the
3.1 screenset against the 2.0 run time with out any problems. However
there are all GUI based EXE's. This may be how we are getting around
the limitation. We will have to operate in this mode until we can
update the system to 4.0.

Thanks Dave K
```

<a id="t-17c24a3946"></a>
## Thread: [ACD-CD-CDI-CLC] Request ?Fergell? website... — 2 this month / 3 total

### [ACD-CD-CDI-CLC] Request ?Fergell? website...

- **From:** Barry Smith <bnsmith001@earthlink.nonet>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T01:00:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.comp.databases,comp.databases,comp.databases.ibm-db2,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns93ACCB7D32A98bnsmith001earthlinkn@207.217.77.25>`

```
About two months ago, I was searching for a mainframe FAQ site
to research some DB2-UDB and IMS DB/DC questions.

Someone replied with what appeared to be an IBM.net or IBM.com
wesite under a user name like FERGELL or something similar.

When I visited the website, I found a a WEALTH of information,
full of language information and error codes and "trivia"
that helped me "bone up" on CICS (which was a little rusty).

Unfortunately, after a move from one town and apartment to
another to another town and apartment (and putting too much 
stuff in storage) I lost the printouts (with the web address 
at the bottom) and the email file that I saved to a CD (in 
storage somewhere)...
```

<a id="t-e28a599461"></a>
## Thread: getting the Asc character in Power — 9 msgs

### getting the Asc character in Power

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T01:58:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com>`

```

hi,
In Microfocus i used to do this to get Asc character
01  linha-x.
      03  l-x  pic  99  comp-x.
if I move  59  to l-x  and display Linha-x  I get  ";"
if I move 171 to l-x  and display Linha-x  I get  "1/2" Simbol.

But in Power Doest work.

If i use Function Char(59)  I get  "?;"         Two Positions
If I use Function Char(171) I get only "??"    Two position
and dont show me "1/2"  Simbol

Does anybody here Knows how to help me in this case

tks

Carlos Lages
```

#### ↳ Re: getting the Asc character in Power

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-02T22:10:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NYmcndePLs09A56iXTWJjg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com>`

```
Carlos lages wrote:
> hi,
> In Microfocus i used to do this to get Asc character
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does anybody here Knows how to help me in this case

01  linha-x        pic x.

MOVE FUNCTION CHAR(59) TO LINHA-X. (now contains ";")
MOVE FUNCTION CHAR(171) TO LINHA-X (now contains God-knows-what since there
is no ASCII character for 171).

There is no single-byte comp field in Fujitsu COBOL (unless you take
extraordinary measures).

Maybe the "?" is Fujitsu's way of telling you it doesn't know what you want?
```

#### ↳ Re: getting the Asc character in Power

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T15:38:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f03b016_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com>`

```
Carlos,

I think what you REALLY want (it isn't what you asked about) is to be able
to move a numeric value to a single byte and have it automatically
translated to the equivalent ASCII value.

You can do that with the following (although it is tedious...):


 01  char-num  pic 999 binary.
 01  char-x   redefines char-num.
     12 filler      pic x.
     12 target    pic x.

 PROCEDURE       DIVISION.
     move 059 to char-num

If you now check "target" it contains ";" which I believe is the desired
result.

I would've thought the same effect could be obtained by  using:

     move function CHAR(59) to target

...but it gives ":" in target instead of ";".

I have no idea why this is and I'm too busy to investigate it at the moment.
It may be a bug or may be correct behaviour.

Either way, you can get the effect you want with the definitions above.

Pete.


"Carlos lages" <member129@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com...
>
> hi,
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: getting the Asc character in Power

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T08:02:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F042970.3060207@Netscape.net>`
- **References:** `<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com> <3f03b016_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> I would've thought the same effect could be obtained by  using:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> It may be a bug or may be correct behaviour.

It's correct.  Function Char(1) will give you the character at position 
0 of the character set.  So, if 59 is ";", Function Char(60) should give 
it to you.

It's weird, I agree, but I was told that was intended behavior.  There's 
a 5-character control sequence that one can use in the Unisys 2200 
environment to signify the start of entry (SOE).  However, the SOE is in 
the character set (character 30).  So, I was using Function Char(30) and 
wondering why it wouldn't work.  I contacted someone at Unisys, and they 
said that you need to add 1 to that number, and when I changed it to 31, 
it worked like a charm...  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: getting the Asc character in Power

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-04T17:23:22+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f050fd2_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com> <3f03b016_7@news.athenanews.com> <3F042970.3060207@Netscape.net>`

```
Thanks for that, Daniel.

It seems weird to me and you'd think they would at least document such a
requirement.

Never mind...at least now we know <G>.

Pete.
```

```
application and figure out for the font you are using which character
value gives you a 1/2 symbol.

Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com>...
> hi,
> In Microfocus i used to do this to get Asc character
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: getting the Asc character in Power

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-02T22:38:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307022138.47b2a111@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com>`

```
Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com>...
> hi,
> In Microfocus i used to do this to get Asc character
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> But in Power Doest work.

Fujitsu makes a COMP-* fields more than one byte depending on compiler
options.  The defult is BINARY(WORD) giving 2 bytes for a PIC 9 COMP.
You could set BINARY(BYTE) or redefine the above to:

    01  Linha-All.
        03  L-x         PIC 9(4) COMP-5.
    01  Linha-REDF REDEFINES Linha-All.
        03  Linha-X     PIC X.
        03  FILLER      PIC X.
 
> If i use Function Char(59)  I get  "?;"         Two Positions
> If I use Function Char(171) I get only "??"    Two position
> and dont show me "1/2"  Simbol

The characters you actually get are dependent on the code page used. 
Perhaps you were running in DOS with a particular code set to get the
1/2 character.

For specific characters it may be better to put it into the source
code literal using say: ALT + 1 + 7 + 1 (where numbers are on numeric
keypad while ALT held down) then you will see what you get (if text
editor is in Windows too).
```

#### ↳ Re: getting the Asc character in Power

- **From:** Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T21:10:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3073338.1057266619@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com>`

```

Guys,
the point is  using 59 I got  ";"
but any other like 171, 138, etc  I got  nothing, at least
in debug.
I did not check if I sent to a Printer If this works.

By the way this Routine Takes a Several Numbers , 44 to be
exactily and sent to a  PCL command "RASTER"  to
convert in Binary "10010101" etc
when this sequence is sent to a printer becames a CODEBAR
Interleave 2 to 5.

The PCL command Need to receive ";" instead  Decimal 059 or  Hex "3B"

there are no Problems with 059 ";" but I can not get
the character from Decimal 171 or Hex "AB"


This routine works since 1990 until now using MF, and several Cobol
Dialect, But in Power i am getting this error.

Tks
Carlos lages
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: getting the Asc character in Power

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T18:51:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307031751.6e6bc5d9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3069757.1057197539@dbforums.com> <3073338.1057266619@dbforums.com>`

```
Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com> wrote 

> the point is  using 59 I got  ";"
> but any other like 171, 138, etc  I got  nothing, at least
> in debug.
> I did not check if I sent to a Printer If this works.

If you are using PIC 99 COMP-? then Fujitsu will not allow you to put
in a value greater than 99 because it will truncate the value - unless
TRUNC option is set correctly, and you also add rather than move.

Best is to use PIC 9(4) COMP-5 (little-endian) and redefine as PIC X
and FILLER PIC X.

But you cannot get 'nothing'.  You must get 'something' in the range
x'00' to x'FF' in the character position.  Note that with 171 on a
little-endian COMP-? the value will be in the first byte and in a
big-endian COMP-? the value will be in the 2nd byte.  COMP-5 and
COMP-X should be little-endian.  COMP is probably big-endian.

> By the way this Routine Takes a Several Numbers , 44 to be
> exactily and sent to a  PCL command "RASTER"  to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The PCL command Need to receive ";" instead  Decimal 059 or  Hex "3B"

They are the same thing, just different representations.
 
> there are no Problems with 059 ";" but I can not get
> the character from Decimal 171 or Hex "AB"

I can't thisnk why x"AB" won't 'work' for you.  I suspect that it
won't display a character that you recognise.
 
> This routine works since 1990 until now using MF, and several Cobol
> Dialect, But in Power i am getting this error.

Strictly speaking it is not an 'error', just a difference, or probably
several differences.  Some of these may need compiler options, such as
BINARY(BYTE) and TRUNC(?).

Mainly it may be a difference between the way that a Windows program
sends data to the Windows printer driver and the way that a DOS
program sending data is treated.
```

<a id="t-d179c20cc1"></a>
## Thread: Runs on MicroFocus Net Express but not on Unix server express — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Runs on MicroFocus Net Express but not on Unix server express

- **From:** paul_pratte@umsl.edu (Paul Pratte)
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T07:29:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ca79d5c.0307030629.60bc3a49@posting.google.com>`

```
A MicroFocus Cobol program runs fine on the PC but gets SQLCODE=0100
on all subsequent queries.

I have a program that performs a main query to database A. Here is
some Pseudo Code that represents the overall flow.

Do While more rows in main query

   Deletes row from table 1,2,3,4 in database B (Key from main query)
   COMMIT
   Insert row in table 1 in database B
   COMMIT
   Opens query 2 in database A SQLCODE = 0
   Fetches from query 2 (always gets SQLCODE = 100)

   Opens query 3 in database A SQLCODE = 0
   Fetches from query 3 (always gets SQLCODE = 100)

   Opens query 4 in database A SQLCODE = 0
   Fetches from query 4 (always gets SQLCODE = 100)

   Fetch row from main query
end 

On the pc the fetches for Queries 1,2, and 3 have rows, SQLCODE=0.
I have tried different MODE=, hold_cursor=yes, WITH HOLD on cursors
from DBase A.

No cursor is from a prepared statment. 
MicroFocus Server Express 2.2 on UNIX and Net Express 3.1 on the PC.
Oracle 9i client setups to 9i database (A) and 8i database (B).


Any help would be appreciated. Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Runs on MicroFocus Net Express but not on Unix server express

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-04T10:21:20+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f04ace8_9@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<4ca79d5c.0307030629.60bc3a49@posting.google.com>`

```
Paul,

Try running it without the commits (until end of run).

In some systems COMMIT resets the cursor, even if you specify WITH HOLD at
the time you declare it.

If it works without the commits, it will confirm that that is the problem.

You might also consider positioned update/delete if you are running from a
cursor.

Finally (a bit obvious, but sometimes overlooked...) ensure that the data is
exactly the same on both systems...

Pete.

"Paul Pratte" <paul_pratte@umsl.edu> wrote in message
news:4ca79d5c.0307030629.60bc3a49@posting.google.com...
> A MicroFocus Cobol program runs fine on the PC but gets SQLCODE=0100
> on all subsequent queries.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any help would be appreciated. Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Runs on MicroFocus Net Express but not on Unix server express

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-05T00:54:50+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f0579a5_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<4ca79d5c.0307030629.60bc3a49@posting.google.com> <3f04ace8_9@news.athenanews.com> <panagvcd8e12gtsiiah9v04sk9mvhlmnv4@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:panagvcd8e12gtsiiah9v04sk9mvhlmnv4@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 4 Jul 2003 10:21:20 +1200 "Peter E.C. Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> :>In some systems COMMIT resets the cursor, even if you specify WITH HOLD
at
> :>the time you declare it.
>
> Then one would not expect +100.
>
> One would get the SQLCODE indicating invalid cursor (-514).

Not necessarily. (Certainly not if you don't check it <G>)

I don't personally use SQLCODE I use SQLSTATE. But I can assure you
Binyamin, I have had the code for "Record not found" when a cursor has been
reset by COMMIT. It happened to me in the last week, using the SQL Server
Engine on Access 2002. I was using keyed delete on a cursor positioned row,
followed by a commit. (I opted for this because positioned delete wouldn't
work due to a foible of SQL Server which requires the cursor declaration to
specify "for browse" instead of "for update"; Fujitsu COBOL rejects this...)
The delete received SQLSTATE 01000 (SQLCODE +100). I removed the commits and
everything ran perfectly.  It was precisely this experience that prompted my
post.

Now that you mention it, it is possible I may have missed checking a code or
received something unexpected on the prior fetch. I'm not sure. You might be
right. It is true that I wouldn't have checked for invalid cursor...

 My post was intended to help him eliminate a possible source of the error.

However, if my own experience is flawed in this area it probably didn't
help...

What's your advice?

>
> Also, the logic flow below indicates the open was after the commit.

Yes, that is fair comment. It is impossible to determine from his post,
however, whether "main query" is cursor based or not. I assumed that it
could be.

>
> :>If it works without the commits, it will confirm that that is the
problem.
> :>
> :>You might also consider positioned update/delete if you are running from
a
> :>cursor.
> :>
> :>Finally (a bit obvious, but sometimes overlooked...) ensure that the
data is
> :>exactly the same on both systems...
> :>

I take it you have no issue with the above <G>?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Runs on MicroFocus Net Express but not on Unix server express

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-04T16:28:59+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t10bgv8cg5c69kdvgc6g02gjb0pu0louof@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4ca79d5c.0307030629.60bc3a49@posting.google.com> <3f04ace8_9@news.athenanews.com> <panagvcd8e12gtsiiah9v04sk9mvhlmnv4@4ax.com> <3f0579a5_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
On Sat, 5 Jul 2003 00:54:50 +1200 "Peter E.C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

:>"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
:>news:panagvcd8e12gtsiiah9v04sk9mvhlmnv4@4ax.com...

:>> On Fri, 4 Jul 2003 10:21:20 +1200 "Peter E.C. Dashwood"
:>> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

:>> :>Try running it without the commits (until end of run).

:>> :>In some systems COMMIT resets the cursor, even if you specify WITH HOLD
:>at
:>> :>the time you declare it.

:>> Then one would not expect +100.

:>> One would get the SQLCODE indicating invalid cursor (-514).

:>Not necessarily. (Certainly not if you don't check it <G>)

:>I don't personally use SQLCODE I use SQLSTATE. But I can assure you
:>Binyamin, I have had the code for "Record not found" when a cursor has been
:>reset by COMMIT. It happened to me in the last week, using the SQL Server
:>Engine on Access 2002. I was using keyed delete on a cursor positioned row,
:>followed by a commit. (I opted for this because positioned delete wouldn't
:>work due to a foible of SQL Server which requires the cursor declaration to
:>specify "for browse" instead of "for update"; Fujitsu COBOL rejects this...)
:>The delete received SQLSTATE 01000 (SQLCODE +100). I removed the commits and
:>everything ran perfectly.  It was precisely this experience that prompted my
:>post.

That appears to be an implementor error (if 01000 always maps to +100).

:>Now that you mention it, it is possible I may have missed checking a code or
:>received something unexpected on the prior fetch. I'm not sure. You might be
:>right. It is true that I wouldn't have checked for invalid cursor...

That's error checking for you. You skip a few and it bites you.

:> My post was intended to help him eliminate a possible source of the error.

:>However, if my own experience is flawed in this area it probably didn't
:>help...

:>What's your advice?

Probably verifying all codes and then tracing.

:>> Also, the logic flow below indicates the open was after the commit.

:>Yes, that is fair comment. It is impossible to determine from his post,
:>however, whether "main query" is cursor based or not. I assumed that it
:>could be.

Perhaps.

The logic might not be anything like what he specified.

:>> :>If it works without the commits, it will confirm that that is the
:>problem.

:>> :>You might also consider positioned update/delete if you are running from
:>a
:>> :>cursor.
>
:>> :>Finally (a bit obvious, but sometimes overlooked...) ensure that the
:>data is
:>> :>exactly the same on both systems...

:>I take it you have no issue with the above <G>?

Quite often it is a good idea to verify the obvious.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Runs on MicroFocus Net Express but not on Unix server express

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** paul_pratte@umsl.edu (Paul Pratte)
- **Date:** 2003-07-05T15:53:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ca79d5c.0307051453.36955c6f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4ca79d5c.0307030629.60bc3a49@posting.google.com> <3f04ace8_9@news.athenanews.com> <panagvcd8e12gtsiiah9v04sk9mvhlmnv4@4ax.com> <3f0579a5_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
All queries are cursor based that were shown in Pseudo code. After the
insert there is a "select into" that gets a main key that a Sequence
created.
There is no positioned update as the queries are from Database A (Read
only) and the inserts are on Database B. The only query for Database B
is the one to initially create a record in table 1. So, the FOR UPDATE
does not apply. Different databases.

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3f0579a5_6@news.athenanews.com>...
```

```
Commented out the commit and rollback in each paragraph. Still same
result.
I agree and have experienced the commit and autoclosing of cursors.
But in this case the cursor is not even opened. And on the pc, same
code going to the same databases (these are the only sources of input
data), works!  I am starting to think it is a MicroFocus bug of some
sort in Server Express on Unix.
I tried the query with the information from the main query and each
sub query does indeed have data.
```

#### ↳ Re: Runs on MicroFocus Net Express but not on Unix server express

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-04T13:34:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<be3rkg$6qr$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<4ca79d5c.0307030629.60bc3a49@posting.google.com>`

```
Paul,

I'm assuming that you're running the same version of the Oracle client on
both Win32 and UNIX (which platform is it, by the way?) ? I'm assuming that
you're using Pro*COBOL here, and that you've precompiled locally on the
target platform.

Following on from Peter's suggestion, have you tried the CLOSE_ON_COMMIT
option to procob? I've attached a snippet from the Oracle docs below.

Simon.
WITH HOLD Clause in DECLARE CURSOR Statements

Any cursor that has been declared with the clause WITH HOLD after the word
CURSOR remains open after a COMMIT or a ROLLBACK. The following example
shows how to use this clause:

EXEC SQL

DECLARE C1 CURSOR WITH HOLD

FOR SELECT ENAME FROM EMP

WHERE EMPNO BETWEEN 7600 AND 7700

END-EXEC.

The cursor must not be declared for UPDATE. The WITH HOLD clause is used in
DB2 to override the default, which is to close all cursors on commit.
Pro*COBOL provides this clause in order to ease migrations of applications
from DB2 to Oracle. When MODE=ANSI, Oracle uses the DB2 default, but all
host variables must be declared in a Declare Section. To avoid having a
Declare Section, use the precompiler option CLOSE_ON_COMMIT described next.
See "DECLARE CURSOR (Embedded SQL Directive)".

CLOSE_ON_COMMIT Precompiler Option

The precompiler option CLOSE_ON_COMMIT is available to override the default
behavior of MODE=ANSI (if you specify MODE=ANSI on the command line, any
cursors not declared with the WITH HOLD clause are closed on commit):

CLOSE_ON_COMMIT = {YES | NO}

The default is NO. This option must be entered only on the command line or
in a configuration file.

Note: Use this option carefully; applications may be slowed if cursors are
opened and closed many times because of the need to re-parse for each OPEN
statement. See "CLOSE_ON_COMMIT".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Using the crystal ball

- **From:** Gisle Forseth <GForseth@acucorp.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T23:07:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7BDF3C5E711686498AC6685001541ECE361647@arthur.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <2h8hfvccktghnte5jhfgqa1he777ib2jj7@4ax.com> <3ef8fb73_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Mr. Dashwood,

I am sorry for being a bit late with this reply, but I've been
travelling.

I just don't understand how you can reason like this, can you please
tell me why you are predicting that one of the most successful COBOL
software company the last decade, currently celebrating its 15 years
anniversary should "disappear" / be absorbed within 7 years?

Wouldn't you consider your foundation a little bit biased, given that
you are a die hard Fujitsu, and before that, what a coincidence, you
happened to use MF?

To bring in a little balance, I could state that Fujitsu and MF would be
gone by 7 years, why not? To me that seems as rational as the opposite.

May I suggest that you desist from making statements that has no root in
reality.

Regards,

Gisle Forseth

> -----Original Message-----
> From: Peter E.C. Dashwood [mailto:dashwood@enternet.co.nz]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using the crystal ball

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-05T00:22:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f05722c_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <2h8hfvccktghnte5jhfgqa1he777ib2jj7@4ax.com> <3ef8fb73_4@news.athenanews.com> <7BDF3C5E711686498AC6685001541ECE361647@arthur.acucorp.com>`

```

"Gisle Forseth" <GForseth@acucorp.com> wrote in message
news:7BDF3C5E711686498AC6685001541ECE361647@arthur.acucorp.com...
> Mr. Dashwood,
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Glad to...

COBOL is in decline. The reasons for this are manifold. The Market place has
moved on and the methods for developing Computer systems have moved on.

In any diminishing Market there will be a rationalisation of the players.

I predict the winners here will be MicroFocus, Fujitsu, and IBM (it may have
escaped your notice that MicroSoft dropped out of direct involvement with
COBOL some years ago. They pay lip service to support for COBOL as part of
.NET but I have yet to find COBOL code samples on any MSDN sites. I still
have to painstakingly "translate" VB and C samples into COBOL...)

Why these 3? Not because of superior products. (They all have a good
product, as I'm sure AcuCorp does too...). It is because they are the only
ones that have the financial resources to stay the course and the will to do
so. IBM will HAVE to because of the legacy from their mainframe sites, the
other two actually WANT to, they have both invested years in building a
market and responding to changes in it. (BTW, Do AcuCorp offer an Object
Oriented version of COBOL?).

My comment was not intended as a criticism of AcuCorp,


> Wouldn't you consider your foundation a little bit biased, given that
> you are a die hard Fujitsu, and before that, what a coincidence, you
> happened to use MF?
>
Nope. Before that I used IBM, ICL, Burroughs, Univac, NCR, Control Data
Corporation and Honeywell. I made it my business right from the start (1965
for me) to get as much different experience as possible, on as many
platforms as possible, with the object of becoming a consultant. To this day
I have no particular bias for any computer company and your "die hard
Fujitsu" comment is way out of order. I am not retained by Fujitsu in any
capacity and I do not receive inducements from them or any other company to
promote or use their products. I say nice things about Fujitsu because I use
it every day and it is a good product. There is no implicit criticism of
other products. (Now that we have cleared my credentials perhaps you would
clear your own...Like, who do you work for?)


> To bring in a little balance, I could state that Fujitsu and MF would be
> gone by 7 years, why not? To me that seems as rational as the opposite.
>

If that is your opinion, indeed, why not? And, if you expressed such an
opinion I would press you on it, exactly as you have done with me. That's
what forums are for...

> May I suggest that you desist from making statements that has no root in
> reality.
>

(Soooo tempting to digress into a discussion about reality...but, I sense
you are seriously wounded by my comments so I'll keep this serious...).

 1. The statements I make about the future (just like ANY statements made by
ANYBODY about the future) are opinions. I like to think they are informed
opinions, but I'm quite happy to debate them and clarify them for anyone who
disagrees. I arrive at these opinions by observing and thinking. They are
usually extrapolations from observable evidence (I claim no psychic
powers...)

2. If I was to comply with  your suggestion, I would be posting only matters
of verifiable fact. There are a number of people here who do that already,
and do it much better than me.

3. If I didn't post opinion I wouldn't receive mail like yours which debates
it and causes me to think about it again. So, I wouldn't grow much, would I?

4. There are many people in this forum who are happy to debate speculation
and matters of opinion, and the indications are that most people prefer me
to post, even if they don't agree with me. I like that. I believe that
"argument" in the Classical sense (not just pointless bickering or a pissing
contest) is good for all concerned. For that reason I defend vehemently the
right of people with whom I have no agreement whatsoever, to post here.

Now that we've cleared that up I come to YOUR post...

First off, could you please cite some reference, statistics, or data, which
shows AcuCorp to be the "most successful COBOL
software company in the last decade"?

If you can prove this, I shall apologise sincerely (I don't say I'm sorry
when I'm not...) for missing AcuCorp from the list of "survivors".

Please be aware I am NOT "anti" AcuCorp, or any other COBOL software vendor.

I just call them like I see them.

Pete.
```

```
"ShadowFox" <me@email.com> wrote:

>P. S. RM-cobol (liant) bought Micro Focus almost 5 years ago
>Enjoy Bob

Bob:

I'm not sure if this is a "tongue-in-cheek" posting or if I am missing
something, but Liant didn't purchase Micro Focus.

Some years ago, Intersolve and Micro Focus merged (or perhaps it was
Micro Focus which purchased Intersolve).......in any case, the new
corporation was dubbed Merant.

A couple years ago, Micro Focus was divested from Merant and purchased
by a group of San Francisco based venture capitalists.

Micro Focus has operated on it's own ever since.

Perhaps Paul can correct me if I am incorrect in my understanding of
history.

Perhaps you are thinking of one of these other acquisiton events which
took place many years ago:

Micro Focus purchased mbp.

The owner of ACE COBOL purchased Ryan McFarland.  Later on, Ryan
McFarland was sold to Liant.




>
>"Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote in message
…[154 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Bob Wolfe
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3127520.1058553416@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <2h8hfvccktghnte5jhfgqa1he777ib2jj7@4ax.com> <3ef8fb73_4@news.athenanews.com> <7BDF3C5E711686498AC6685001541ECE361647@arthur.acucorp.com> <3f05722c_2@news.athenanews.com> <beueqv$1ns$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <hlPRa.78932$sY2.35071@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <6itfhvkp0akfncegnugq5ls8c7lb3r31ce@4ax.com>`

```

Bob and Bob...
   The comment about Liant buying Micro Focus was so incorrect that
   I couldn't even bring myself to respond thinking it had to be a
   joke posting.
   But here's the true set of facts... In 1996, Micro Focus acquired
   Intersolv (and for those history buffs out there - Intersolv was
   actually a prior merger of Indexed Technologies who had a application
   design package called Excelerator, and Sage Software who had PVCS,
   and together they had build a COBOL generator known as APS).
   Intersolv was much larger than Micro Focus, but was a bit of a
   falling star at the time. Shortly after the acquisition, the
   Intersolv people somehow got control of the board of directors and
   forced some key people out and took over effectively the entire
   corporation. They decided to name the newly formed company "MERANT".
   All of the Micro Focus founders and many key people were either
   forced out or left on the own in a fairly short period after his...
    Mernant treated COBOL as a dirty word. Last year at COBOL Expo, Ian
    Archibald, who I beleive is the current Micro Focus Vp of
    development publicly apoligized during a keynote address to the
    entire group of attendees and stated that "COBOL has been a dirty
    word at Merant". And he promised that Micro Focus was going to rise
    back up out of the ashes. And I actually wish them very well and
    God's Speed in doing so.
    Regarding the Liant remark there is another bit of history that
    could possibly attribute to such a comment. Sometime before the
    Merant mess, Micro Focus acquired a German company known as MBP
    cobol. I can't remember for sure, but I think they bought MBP from
    Liant - someone please correct me if I'm wrong. I think this
    happened around 1994 or 1995, but I can't really remember...
Thanks,
-Howard

Originally posted by Bob Wolfe 
> "ShadowFox"  wrote:
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
http://www.flexus.com[/url] 
```

```
Ah yes.... the shareholders did take some time to approve things. There
was even a shareholder suit by Intersolv shareholders as I remember hat
delayed things quite a while. In fact Marcello came into Micro Focus in
April of 1996 and wrecked havoc - that's it... And then set in motion
the Intersolv Acquisition. But one thing I am curious about - your
numbers about revenues. At the time that Micro Focus set it's sites on
Intersolv, It was my understanding that Gross revenues for Micro Focus
were around $140 million and that Micro Focus had about 7-800 employees
and Intersolv had revenues of arounf $300 million and around 1800
employees. This was the basis of my comments about Intersolv being a
much larger company when acquired by Micro Focus.....
Thanks,
-Howard
```

```
"hhinman" wrote:

Another "fact" eh?

(a) I believe you mean Mr. Martin Waters, not Mr. Marcelo Gumucio.
(b) Mr. Waters became CEO of Micro Focus on 17th July 1997 (replacing Mr.
Gumucio).
(c) Mr. Waters & Mr. Gary Greenfield (Intersolv) were the respective CEO's
at the time of the acquisition.

IMO, your summary of (presumably) Mr. Waters tenure is obnoxious.

> numbers about revenues. At the time that Micro Focus set it's sites on
```

```
Gary,

  I'm afraid we both have a few things wrong. I was indeed talking
  about Marcello, and he came into Micro Focus in April of 1996 - and
  that I am 100% certain of.... Marcello was introduced to the Micro
  Focus customer base at the 1996 User's Conference in Orlando a month
  later in May.
   As far as I'm concerned the downward spiral began when Marcello took
   over. I actually reported directly to him for about 30 days. It was a
   less than impressive experience. And Micro Focus lost it's wonderful
   soul the day he came aboard in my humble opinion.
   Mr. Waters I never met, nor do I know and I believe I left before he
       ever came aboard, so I can't offer an opinion. Gulliard I do
       remember and even participated in a couple of calls with him. The
       same with Harold Hughes, who I believe was also the Intel CFO at
       one time. Both were there before Marcello or Mr. Waters. I was
       told that Mr. Waters (who I believe was British if I have the
       right guy) was actually compassionate and responsible, but such
       qualities evidently were not viewed as valuable by the new board
       and he was forced out. I don't know what the truth is in this, as
       I heard it from third parties.
   The mistake in my original posting was that I forgot that there was a
   period after Macello came in before the Intersolv Acquisition was
   actually begun. I had though both happened simultaneously.
   Another mistake I made (at least timewise) was the shareholder suit
   by Intersolv shareholders. It most certaining took place, but later
   than I had thought.  Here's a link about the lawsuit:

  http://portland.bizjournals.com/portland/stories/2003/01/20/da-
  ily62.html

   And regarding your comment about my comments regarding Mr. Waters
   tenure as being obnoxious, please note your observation is based on a
   very incorrect assumption as noted above - it was Marcello's tenure I
   was talking about, and I'll stick to my opinion about such....
Thanks,
-Howard

…[46 more quoted lines elided]…

Gary.
```

```
Gary,
    I guess we all are entitled to our own opinions, so I'll treat yours
    with a bit more dignity that you do mine. You are indeed a good
    corporate boy... And regarding my comments about Marcello. Well, I
    worked directly for him (you did not), and I think anyone with a
    sense of value and loyalty would be greatly offended by some of his
    actions. But I guess it is all water under the bridge right now.
    Micro Focus was a great company before he took power, and I believe
    that most of the poeple who were there know this... The result a few
    years later was something very different, wasn't it...? It was
    reported last year that "Micro Focus is trying to rise from the
    ashes" (or words to that effect..). What ashes are these, Gary? Are
    they Marcello's or Mr. Waters, or Mike Gulliard's or whoever else
    was in the management mix - or could it be that each of these burned
    a separate supporting pillar until the whole thing came crashing
    down, Gary...? Or are you instead going to try to convince everyone
    how little I must know and that nothing ever did happen to Micro
    Focus....?
Thanks,
```

```
"hhinman" wrote:
> Gary,
>     I guess we all are entitled to our own opinions, so I'll treat yours
>     with a bit more dignity that you do mine. You are indeed a good
>     corporate boy...

Classic. You say one thing and then immediately follow it with something
else.

I have no problem at all with you expressing opinions; I can choose to agree
with them or otherwise.

I have attempted to help you with your cluttered thinking, confused
timelines and recall of events; many of which you have used to
(authoritatively) construct & deliver "facts".

>     And regarding my comments about Marcello. Well, I
>     worked directly for him (you did not), and I think anyone with a
>     sense of value and loyalty would be greatly offended by some of his
>     actions. But I guess it is all water under the bridge right now.

Again, your entitled to that opinion. And again, in my circles, your
viewpoint is universally counter to the one I'm familiar with.

>     Micro Focus was a great company before he took power, and I believe
>     that most of the poeple who were there know this... The result a few
>     years later was something very different, wasn't it...?

At the start of Mr. Gumucio's tenure (April 1996), Micro Focus stock price
was $15. When he left (June 1997), the stock price was $30. The stock
continued to climb under Mr. Waters and hit $57 in May 1998 (just before the
Intersolv acquisition). So Howard, I agree with you, that the result "a few
years later" (as they may "reasonably" be attributed to Mr. Gumucio) was
something very different from the "great" company you were familiar with
just a few short years earlier.

Somehow I doubt that's the picture you had in mind. And somehow, sadly, I
doubt any of these facts will cause you to reflect and revise your picture.
Go on, surprise me.

>     It was
>     reported last year that "Micro Focus is trying to rise from the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>     down, Gary...?

These "ashes" to which you refer to Howard, were the end-result of Micro
Focus operating as a much maligned business unit within MERANT. This entity
came into being a few months after the Intersolv acquisition and the
replacement of Mr. Waters by Mr. Greenfield. Mr. Greenfield, for whatever
reasons, felt the COBOL business had a limited life and used the revenue as
a "cash cow" to fund other initiatives. Micro Focus operated this way from
early 1999 until it was sold in August 2001.

>     Or are you instead going to try to convince everyone
>     how little I must know and that nothing ever did happen to Micro
>     Focus....?

I'm not the one making the major contribution in this regard Howard.

Gary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol development environment

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T19:09:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3131762.1058728154@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <2h8hfvccktghnte5jhfgqa1he777ib2jj7@4ax.com> <3ef8fb73_4@news.athenanews.com> <7BDF3C5E711686498AC6685001541ECE361647@arthur.acucorp.com> <3f05722c_2@news.athenanews.com> <beueqv$1ns$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <hlPRa.78932$sY2.35071@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <6itfhvkp0akfncegnugq5ls8c7lb3r31ce@4ax.com> <3127520.1058553416@dbforums.com> <u23Sa.94476$ye4.66771@sccrnsc01> <3129204.1058593096@dbforums.com> <tNlSa.102746$H17.31678@sccrnsc02> <3130595.1058667147@dbforums.com> <D_nSa.102038$N7.13058@sccrnsc03> <3130819.1058680316@dbforums.com> <7iASa.108977$H17.33032@sccrnsc02>`

```

Gary,
    It's rather common business knowledge that new management coming in
    and decimating a company to cut costs will indeed artificially
    inflate the share price for some period of time, and it is my
    opinion that this is exactly what you describe below. Now I do
    agree that in making the Intersolv acquisition, that Micro Focus
    lassoed a large somewhat falling star and the result was being
    dragged along into the abyss, but the bottom line in my humble
    opinion is that while many choose to blame Gary Greenfield for the
    demise of Micro Focus,  I'm afraid, I have to look back a bit
    further and in my opinion feel it was Marcello who set the ultimate
    problems into motion with his lack of knowledge of the COBOL
    business, and the internal alliances he made and by forcing certain
    key individuals out, such as the founder and chairman. By the time
    Mr. Waters took over, I suspect the harm was already too great and
    he faced a monumental task in trying to turn things back around.
    But as far as I'm concerned the harm was already done. And of
    course the board overseeing all of this should have the ultimate
    blame laid upon their shoulders in my opinion. Evidently, (and I
    really don't know the details), they did figure out that Marcello
    was not good for Micor Focus and sent him on his way (at least
    that's what I heard, but again, I wasn't there sitting in those
    discussions). But this is not an uncommon story in Silicon Valley.
    And as I stated previously, I am privy to things you are not
    because of my then position and the fact that I actually worked for
    Marcello for a short time  before I left.
     It was not long after Marcello arrived that there were only a few
     individuals left from the original Micro Focus management team that
     had run the company quite well in my opinion. And the individuals
     left were not the most impressive voices from the bunch either.
     So in any regards, I think we'll agree to disagree. And yes, as I
     stated previously, my time and facts were a bit distorted as I had
     forgetten the exact timeline of the Intersolv acquisition. And I do
     thank you for correcting such in your normally gentle way... :-)
Regards,
-Howard

Originally posted by Gary 
> "hhinman" wrote:
> > Gary,
…[86 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Gary. 
```

```
Howard, can we discuss specifics? To this point, you've largely dismissed
the points I've made and, instead, have chosen to respond with more "story
telling" (IMO, it does nothing for your credibility).

"hhinman" wrote:
>     It's rather common business knowledge that new management coming in
>     and decimating a company to cut costs will indeed artificially
>     inflate the share price for some period of time, and it is my
>     opinion that this is exactly what you describe below.

Why do you persist in using personal "wisdom" & "insight" to establish
"fact"?

Some more facts for you to digest (if that's even possible):

(1) Net revenue & #employees for fiscal year ending Jan 31st 1996 -- $135m &
827
(2) Net revenue & #employees for fiscal year ending Jan 31st 1998 -- $168m &
831

I suspect the clue to share price performance is somewhere in here (and not
in your subjective "analysis").

>     Now I do
>     agree that in making the Intersolv acquisition, that Micro Focus
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     demise of Micro Focus...

Look for what you want Howard. But you'll never really see anything.

This is actually quite bizarre, as Mr. Greenfield himself was
straight-forward about the decisions he took and why he took them.

>     ...I'm afraid, I have to look back a bit
>     further and in my opinion feel it was Marcello who set the ultimate
>     problems into motion with his lack of knowledge of the COBOL
>     business, and the internal alliances he made and by forcing certain
>     key individuals out, such as the founder and chairman.

I would assert that Mr. Gumucio is precisely the reason that Micro Focus is
still here today and (once again) thriving.

I used the word "obnoxious" because your dismissive summary of Mr. Gumucio
was in my view wholly unfair. I also used the "transparant" precisely
because of your loyalty to the founder & chariman. Loyalty is an admirable
trait, but can be misplaced in a business context (an observation, not a
judgment).

>     By the time
>     Mr. Waters took over, I suspect the harm was already too great and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     blame laid upon their shoulders in my opinion.

More "story telling". Or is that a "fact" now?

>     Evidently, (and I
>     really don't know the details), they did figure out that Marcello
>     was not good for Micor Focus and sent him on his way (at least
>     that's what I heard, but again, I wasn't there sitting in those
>     discussions). But this is not an uncommon story in Silicon Valley.

Conventional wisdom has it that Mr. Gumucio wanted to grow Micro Focus
organically. Mr. Waters wanted to grow Micro Focus through acquisition. The
board made their choice. For better or worse.

>     And as I stated previously, I am privy to things you are not
>     because of my then position and the fact that I actually worked for
>     Marcello for a short time  before I left.

Good for you Howard. What a trump card you hold.

>      It was not long after Marcello arrived that there were only a few
>      individuals left from the original Micro Focus management team that
>      had run the company quite well in my opinion.

(1) Net income losses for fiscal year ending Jan 31st 1995 -- $3.5m
(2) Net income losses for fiscal year ending Jan 31st 1996 -- $11.2m
(3) Net income losses for fiscal year ending Jan 31st 1997 -- $14.7m (*)

(*) Q3 & Q4 of this fiscal year, under Mr. Gumucio were profitable (which
gives you a good indication of the size of the problem in Q1 & Q2).

<snip>

Gary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ *MY* memories of Micro Focus & the mid-90's (was: Cobol development environment

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T14:48:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bferm9$tkt$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <2h8hfvccktghnte5jhfgqa1he777ib2jj7@4ax.com> <3ef8fb73_4@news.athenanews.com> <7BDF3C5E711686498AC6685001541ECE361647@arthur.acucorp.com> <3f05722c_2@news.athenanews.com> <beueqv$1ns$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <hlPRa.78932$sY2.35071@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <6itfhvkp0akfncegnugq5ls8c7lb3r31ce@4ax.com> <3127520.1058553416@dbforums.com> <u23Sa.94476$ye4.66771@sccrnsc01> <3129204.1058593096@dbforums.com> <tNlSa.102746$H17.31678@sccrnsc02> <3130595.1058667147@dbforums.com> <D_nSa.102038$N7.13058@sccrnsc03> <3130819.1058680316@dbforums.com> <7iASa.108977$H17.33032@sccrnsc02>`

```
Deleting all of Gary's and Howard's comments below, let me add my own VERY
personal viewpoint.

First, let me say that I went on "long term disability" in June '96 (and was
officially - but "oddly" - still a semi-employee until June '97).  Any
information I have past that time is TOTALLY hear-say.

June of '96 was the 3rd (or possibly the 4th) of the "Micro Focus " LAY-OFFs
(redundancies in "British") of the '90s.  (There had been another set in the
mid-80's - I think - in the pre "IBM COBOL/2" days).  To me, this indicates
SOMETHING was going on to "require" a "right-sizing" of the company
(matching staff to income) well before any acquisition/merger.

I don't know WHICH "Gary" is involved in this thread - but I have a guess.
If it is the one that I think it is, he would be well aware of the "VisOC"
planning and roll-out "issues" that occurred in the early to mid-90's.

Historically (and some of this pre-dates my time with Micro Focus) there was
a time when MF's majority income was from its "ports" to Unix and other
non-PC environments of its COBOL systems.  At the time that IBM and
Microsoft were still TOGETHER in the original OS/2 development, Micro Focus
was *the* provider of the COBOL/2 product. (Interestingly enough a "stripped
down" version of their own development for incredibly ODD reasons.  The
original IBM COBOL/2 product was a '74, not an '85 Standard compiler).
During the late 80's and early 90's, there was a shift to the majority of
MF's revenue (as I recall) coming from "off-loading" (of IBM development and
POSSIBLY production) COBOL (and a little Assembler and even PL/I) systems.
(Some direct sales, some via IBM and/or Microsoft).

As IBM and Microsoft got more and more "hostile", MF eventually produced a
Microsoft "badged" (and tailored) product - and continued to provide an IBM
product - as well as the "purchased from Micro Focus" products.  (CICS, IMS,
and various DB2 solutions were also ongoing at this time).

There were some "mis-steps" during the early 90's in Micro Focus delivered
products.
  - Anyone remember the LOAD time for the dialog-system generated Character
Workbench?
 - Anyone remember the "bug reports" on Workbench V3.0 (as I recall its
number)?

I know that there are some in CLC who have CUSTOMER recollections of VisOC
and what it did or did not (or was reported to or not reported to) be.
However, it is MY impression that it was a semi-trial balloon to get Micro
Focus "better" targeted at the low-end "individual" (or small site) PC
development market.  (It ALSO included a very new "internal" rework of some
of the base technology, but that wasn't clear to the outside world.)

What *I* saw happening in the early 90's (and this may only reflect my role
in the company) was an ATTEMPT to shift the company from getting its major
revenue from
  - IBM mainframe shops using the PC as a DEVELOPMENT platform
        to
 - Developers (enterprise or individual) moving PRODUCTION to the
"Workstation"

For whatever reason(s) (and I have my own personal view - but others
certainly may disagree with this), this shift was a SERIOUS blow to the
revenue stream of pre-merger/acquisition Micro Focus. (Economic issues in
Japan didn't help the company either - as that had been one of the
previously growing revenue sources.)

Therefore, with the slowing revenue stream along with several "poor" (if not
disastrous) product releases, there were frequent (at least annual)
"management RE-organizations".  Even when new CEO's or "whatever" were NOT
coming in, it became harder and harder to see exactly what the Micro Focus
"vision" was - much less how it was DELIVERING on any vision.  All of this
pre-dated the "merger" that may or may not have been an "acquisition" and
may or may not have involved "inflated" stock prices.

  ***

Both Gary and Howard (and possibly other "visitors" to CLC) may remember
(and even KNOW) things that I don't - but I thought that I would, at least,
express my impression of those "days" -  now long gone.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3131958.1058736200@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <2h8hfvccktghnte5jhfgqa1he777ib2jj7@4ax.com> <3ef8fb73_4@news.athenanews.com> <7BDF3C5E711686498AC6685001541ECE361647@arthur.acucorp.com> <3f05722c_2@news.athenanews.com> <beueqv$1ns$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <hlPRa.78932$sY2.35071@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <6itfhvkp0akfncegnugq5ls8c7lb3r31ce@4ax.com> <3127520.1058553416@dbforums.com> <u23Sa.94476$ye4.66771@sccrnsc01> <3129204.1058593096@dbforums.com> <tNlSa.102746$H17.31678@sccrnsc02> <3130595.1058667147@dbforums.com> <D_nSa.102038$N7.13058@sccrnsc03> <3130819.1058680316@dbforums.com> <7iASa.108977$H17.33032@sccrnsc02> <bferm9$tkt$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

Bill,
   Very well stated, I must say. There are a few things left out,
   because as you stated, you were not privy to some things.
   I'll fill in a few of the blanks. COBOL/2 was indeed build for IBM
   for OS/2 and then later licensed to Microsoft. This was effectively a
   $900 product with COBOL workbench at the time  was listed at around
   $4500 as I memeber (plus IMS and CICS optional add-ons at an
   additional $1500). At this time (I believe around 1987...). a small
   company named REALIA had a very fast OS/VS compatible COBOL compiler
   and toolset that was beginning to become a thorn in Micro Focus' side
   and was threatening sales because of it's price differential. It sold
   for around $900. So the strategy adopted at the time was to equip IBM
   and Microsoft (the two largest software "sellers" in the business)
   with a $900 compiler (which actully discounted to under $600 as I
   remember) and let them bury REALIA with this compiler.
But Micro Focus also wanted to protect it high end priced Workbench
product so certain items were not included in the COBOL/2 product. These
were certain critical mainframe emulation features, as I remember.
   And I think it is fair to state that this strategy much have worked,
   as REALIA was acquired later by Pansophic who were then acquired by
   C/A, and while C/A produced a more expensive REALIA Workbench for a
   bit, the emphasis seemed to dissipate in this product line and it is
   pretty much dead now.
   But in keeping some of the "goodies" out of the COBOL/2 compiler

>
> Both Gary and Howard (and possibly other "visitors" to CLC) may
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> "hhinman" wrote:
```

```
As a MF customer at the time, I'll chime in here on one thing...

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<bferm9$tkt$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>...
> 
> I know that there are some in CLC who have CUSTOMER recollections of VisOC
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

At this time I had started working on the Windows version of our
system - we had decided on that instead of OS/2.  We got a bit
confused with VISOC was released - we called our MF rep and said -
look - you have two ways of doing Windows - this way and DS - which
one is it?  They had to get back to us - verbally we got that VISOC
was going forward, but in writing we got that DS was the way to go. 
The company went with DS - I went elsewhere.
```

- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <beueqv$1ns$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <hlPRa.78932$sY2.35071@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <6itfhvkp0akfncegnugq5ls8c7lb3r31ce@4ax.com> <3127520.1058553416@dbforums.com> <u23Sa.94476$ye4.66771@sccrnsc01> <3129204.1058593096@dbforums.com> <tNlSa.102746$H17.31678@sccrnsc02> <3130595.1058667147@dbforums.com> <D_nSa.102038$N7.13058@sccrnsc03> <3130819.1058680316@dbforums.com> <7iASa.108977$H17.33032@sccrnsc02> <bferm9$tkt$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <bfdfc3e8.0307202026.72da5ead@posting.google.com>`

```

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0307202026.72da5ead@posting.google.com...
> At this time I had started working on the Windows version of our
> system - we had decided on that instead of OS/2.  We got a bit
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The company went with DS - I went elsewhere.

Yeah, me too...

I thought DS was fine, but we were desperate for OO at the time and VISOC
was the logical progression towards it.

Up until that time I had been a happy MicroFocus bunny using DS and
workbench and quite satisfied. (I STILL think Animator is one of the most
impressive pieces of software I have seen in my entire career...)

So what did they do? Withdrew support for VISOC overnight, without recourse,
and simply shafted the User base.

Around the same time, Fujitsu offered free copies of their compiler to
remind us we had an option...

Today I use Fujitsu. I can't see any reason to change.

Pete.
```

```
> June of '96 was the 3rd (or possibly the 4th) of the "Micro Focus "
LAY-OFFs

> I don't know WHICH "Gary" is involved in this thread - but I have a guess.

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T06:45:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3132799.1058769936@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <2h8hfvccktghnte5jhfgqa1he777ib2jj7@4ax.com> <3ef8fb73_4@news.athenanews.com> <7BDF3C5E711686498AC6685001541ECE361647@arthur.acucorp.com> <3f05722c_2@news.athenanews.com> <beueqv$1ns$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <hlPRa.78932$sY2.35071@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <6itfhvkp0akfncegnugq5ls8c7lb3r31ce@4ax.com> <3127520.1058553416@dbforums.com> <u23Sa.94476$ye4.66771@sccrnsc01> <3129204.1058593096@dbforums.com> <tNlSa.102746$H17.31678@sccrnsc02> <3130595.1058667147@dbforums.com> <D_nSa.102038$N7.13058@sccrnsc03> <3130819.1058680316@dbforums.com> <7iASa.108977$H17.33032@sccrnsc02> <bferm9$tkt$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <1BKSa.109451$N7.15419@sccrnsc03>`

```

For Both Bill and Gary's edification, Micro Focus had a total of 3 major
layoffs up until and including the 1996 layoff. The first actually came
in 1985 (April in fact). The stock had gone from 10 pounds to 90 pence
in a very short time because of accounting irregularities. If I remember
correctly, it had to do with booking pre-sales (sales not yet completed)
as revenues or something like this. Approximately 10% of the workforce
was laid off. Then next layoff came in 1995 as I remember and then the
one in 1996. I believe there additional smaller layoffs from 1996
onward, but I can't confirm that. I believe a number of people left
between 1996 and present.
Thanks,
-Howard
```

```
hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>Bob and Bob...
>   The comment about Liant buying Micro Focus was so incorrect that
>   I couldn't even bring myself to respond thinking it had to be a
>   joke posting.

Howard:

I didn't suggest that Liant bought Micro Focus.  In fact, if you read
my original reply to the posting:

"Some years ago, Intersolve and Micro Focus merged (or perhaps it was
Micro Focus which purchased Intersolve).......in any case, the new
corporation was dubbed Merant."

You will quickly see that in fact I suggested that it was either a
merger or that it was Micro Focus which purchased Intersolv.

Why would you suggest that I was the one who posted that Liant
purchased Micro Focus?  I never made any such claim at all.....in
fact, I was trying to point out the error to the original poster!

I have noticed that your recent postings have resulted in heated
discussions........I understand why.  I think that it is perfectly
normal behavior for a person to become upset when you attribute a
comment to them which they never made.

>   But here's the true set of facts... In 1996, Micro Focus acquired
>   Intersolv (and for those history buffs out there - Intersolv was
>   actually a prior merger of Indexed Technologies who had a application

It was actually Index Technologies of Cambridge, Massachusetts.

>   design package called Excelerator, and Sage Software who had PVCS,

Excelerator was a Computer Aided Software Engineering (CASE) tool.

And Sage Software (based in Rockville, Maryland) had merged with
Polytron who was the original developer of PVCS (Polytron Version
Control System).

>   and together they had build a COBOL generator known as APS).

Sage Software was already selling the APS COBOL code generator before
the merger......it was not a joint effort.  The integration of the
CASE tool and the application generator may very well have been a
joint effort, but the generator was in existence prior to the merger.

>   Intersolv was much larger than Micro Focus, but was a bit of a
>   falling star at the time. Shortly after the acquisition, the
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>    happened around 1994 or 1995, but I can't really remember...

Howard.....you really didn't read my original reply.....did you....?

Because if you had read my original reply to the poster, you would
have seen this quote as well and not repeated what I had already
posted:

"Perhaps you are thinking of one of these other acquisiton events
which took place many years ago:

Micro Focus purchased mbp."

....and I can indeed correct you on the mbp acquisition as well.  mbp
was indeed a German company, located in Dortmund, but they were never
owned by Liant.  Michael Hicks was the manager of the United States
operation upon acquisition and Chris Glazier was the chief technician
here in the United States.  Chris and I have known each other for
years.

I would appreciate it greatly, if in the future you do not suggest
that I posted a statement, when it was clear that I did not post it.

Thank you.

>Thanks,
>-Howard
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.flexus.com[/url] 


Bob Wolfe
```

```
"SkippyPB" wrote:
> What do you call an amrless, legless man hanging on the wall?

Art.

Groan. Let's list them all now shall we?

http://www.humorhost.com/fjgallery/miscjokes/noarms.shtml
What happened when the man with no arms tried to masturbate?
He was stumped.

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs in a gully?
Rocky

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs in a field of marijuana?
Bud

What do you call an electrician with no arms and no legs?
Sparky

What do you call a plumber with no arms and no legs?
Wet

What do you call a cat with no legs?
Dogfood

What do you call a dog with no legs?
It doesn't matter. He won't come when you call him anyway.

What do you call two guys with no arms or legs hanging over your window?
Curt n' Rod

What was the name of the limbless guy that fell in the fire?
Bernie

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs burried 6 feet under?
Doug

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs burried 3 feet under?
Douglas

What do you call a man with no arms, no legs and no torso?
Dick

What do you call a legless and armless boy on a baseball team?
First base

What was the name of the limbless guy that was boiled by cannibals?
Stu

What was the name of the limbless girl who was stuck on a femce?
Barb

What was the name of the limbless guy that worked at the soda plant?
Tab

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs that sits on top of a
podium?
Mike

What do you call a man with no arms and no legs under your car?
Jack

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs hanging on your wall?
Art

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs in front of your door?
Matt

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs in your mail box?
Bill

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs water-skiing?
Skip

What do you call a girl with no arms and no legs rolling around on the
beach?
Sandy

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs in a hole in the ground?
Phil

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs trying to hold-up a bank?
Rob

What do you call a girl with one leg shorter than the other?
Eileen

What do you call a woman with one leg shorter than the other, married to a
politician?
Tipper

What do you call a girl with no arms and no legs on a dirt road?
Dusty

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs who left a smudge on your
floor?
Mark

What do you call a fly with no wings?
A walk

What do you call a guy with no arms and no legs that just fell out of a
boat?
Bob

What do you call a man with no arms and no legs flying over a fence?
Homer

What do you call a man with no arms and no legs sitting on a grill?
Frank

What do you call a woman with no arms and no legs sitting on a grill?
Patty

What do you call a man with no arms and no legs sitting in a pile of leaves?
Russell
```

```
ROFL!

Some of those are SO cool...

While we're on a light-hearted roll...

Young lad keeps pestering his father for a TV in his bedroom.

Eventually, father relents and kid disappears to his room and isn't seen for
some hours.

Finally emerges and comes downstairs. Kid says quizzically to father: "Dad,
what is love juice?"

Father looks at mother and they decide the time has come that kid should be
informed so they give him the full story...

Kid takes it all in incredulously and sits in stunned silence when the
biology lesson is complete.

Father says: "Well, son, what were you watching?"

Kid says..."Wimbledon..."

Last one...

A woman is berating her husband.

"You're an idiot! You've always BEEN an idiot and you always WILL be an
idiot! If there was a world contest for idiots you'd come second!"

Husband ventures meekly, "Why second?"

"Because you're an idiot!"

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol development environment

- **From:** hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-03T14:59:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3071824.1057244363@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <bda9r3$ere$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

Bill,
    I must reluctantly and respecfully take exception to your comment
    recomending IBM Visual Age and Micro Focus over Fujitsu -
    particularly the comment thatis is "**possible** to use Fujitsu for
    such, indicating it is less than desirable. Please read the
    COMPUTERWORLD story at the following link:

   http://www.computerworld.com/hardwaretopics/hardware/server/story/0,-
   10801,68844,00.html

   We unplugged a 3090 class MVS mainframe and rolled it out to the
   parking lot after porting ALL of their mainframe apps to Windows NT -
   and that was 3 years ago... Please tell me how many Micro Focus or
   AcuCOBOL customers you know for certain have ever pulled a plug on a
   3090 class mainframe.....? I was at Micro Focus for 12 years and
   never knew of a single customer who did so - Micro Focus customers
   developed MVS COBOL applications primarily to send them back to the
   mainframe for production usage - not to decouple them from the
   mainframe for good. There is a serious difference between Mainframe
   **EMULATION** and **WINDOWS PRODUCTION**, and more people need to
   realize such. It really irks me how hung up on silly issues like
   EBCDIC data emulation when looking to migrate their COBOL to the PC
   environment. Sure, it's possible so use EBCDIC data with some
   compilers, but why?! Nothing else on the PC can use EBCDIC! And there
   is overhead associated with the EBCDIC **EMULATION** as well. If you
   are going to use an application in the Windows environment, then make
   the little or no effort of making sure it is ASCII compatible so you
   can use the wealth of other PC tools and software with it for
   goodness sake.  I have taken 1650 MVS COBOL programs through the
   Fujitsu Compiler in the past week and it works like a champ (as
   usual). The vast majority compile without changes. The few that
   require changes are quite simple. Fujitsu does an excellent job with
   MVS COBOL. The customer had tried an appication under Micro focus and
   it ran 12 times slower they claim. I am working on multiple projects
   at this moment porting large scale MVS COBOL shops to Windows with
   the intent of unplugging mainframes. With all due respect, I would
   use no other product for such than the Fujitsu compiler, and yes it
   is true I am a consultant to Fujitsu, but there are few people in the
   industry with my level of experience doing this and knowing you
   personally, I think you should know better.......  (with all due
   respect of course.. :-)  )
-Howard


Originally posted by William M. Klein 
> The following is *not* true for Bull (I don't know for them) but
> for doing
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hans Jakschik 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol development environment

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-04T02:29:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f04e1d0.102996236@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <bda9r3$ere$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3071824.1057244363@dbforums.com>`

```
hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com> wrote:

> Please tell me how many Micro Focus or
>   AcuCOBOL customers you know for certain have ever pulled a plug on a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>   mainframe for good.

During the '90s many organizations, especially US and State Government, ported
their entire mainframe workload to Unix. Micro Focus was the most commonly used
compiler. I've worked on these systems; their mainframe origin shows in the code
style. 

CICS compatibility/emulation is not an important issue. In most cases, CICS was
scrapped and the user interface rewritten in GUI. This gave rise to the phrase
client/server, where client was the new GUI stuff and server was the old
mainframe code. Batch programs required little or no change.

> There is a serious difference between Mainframe
>   **EMULATION** and **WINDOWS PRODUCTION**, and more people need to
>   realize such.... The customer had tried an appication under Micro focus and
>   it ran 12 times slower they claim. 

They were running interpreted .int code rather than native .gnt. When comparing
apples to apples, Micro Focus and Fujitsu run at about the same speed. 

>Originally posted by William M. Klein 

>> It is *possible* to use Fujitsu products for this, but that is
>> *not* their
>> primary target (and they really have NOT kept up with IBM mainframe
>> developments after VS COBOL II).

Neither have mainframe application programmers kept up with developments after
VS COBOL II They're still writing COBOL as it was when their Shop Standard was
written. As a result, compatibility with LE-era extensions is usually not an
issue.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol development environment

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-07T14:53:19-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F09B38F.99948E0@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <bda9r3$ere$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3071824.1057244363@dbforums.com> <3f04e1d0.102996236@news.optonline.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
>> much snipped << 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> issue.

Speaketh for thyself Robert.  Among other things the new date standard
for the last mainframe shop I was at specified FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE and
the first upgrade was to LE so that we could use the latest compiler.  I
will be posting requirements to SHARE (mainframe IBM user group) that
IBM COBOL support the 2002 standard because there are functions I have
wanted for 20+ years.  I will agree that we need to get people to take
advantage of the many advances in COBOL since COBOL VS.  Most of them
actually came with VS COBOL II in the IBM mainframe world.
```

```
I agree with you, BUT I've worked with people who never open a manual.
```
