###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T07:17:03+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bepn3c$r5d$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <bfdfc3e8.0307111934.f6343c2@posting.google.com> <vgv37d5elg897e@corp.supernews.com> <CBSPa.16138$BM.5256172@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:

> OPTIONAL will NOT (that's 'not') prevent an OPEN failure on 'file not
> present.'  It may prevent an immediate ABEND in the IBM batch environment,
> but that does not make the OPEN successful!

Well, one of us completely misunderstands optional files.

Given a file does not exist and is specified as OPTIONAL then an OPEN will 
be _successful_ with a file-status of '05' and all READs will take AT END 
or INVALID KEY and/or report a status of '10' or '23' as appropriate.

If a file is not present is OPTIONAL and  OPENed I-O then it {should} be 
created.  If it is not OPTIONAL then it will fail to OPEN. (MF has 
extension to treat I-O files as OPTIONAL automatically).

What other point would there be to having it OPTIONAL.
```

```
Invariably, when computer people say this, what they mean is:  "This is the
only way _I_ can think of..."
(And sometimes they lie because they can't be bothered doing it by another
way, or it is an otherwise unattractive proposition...)

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens)
- **Date:** 2003-07-11T09:18:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c60a0b82.0307110818.107465f0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <G1WdnZOiWaROi5OiXTWJiQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<G1WdnZOiWaROi5OiXTWJiQ@giganews.com>...

> It is common. It was the only technique to use before file status (about
> 1960 or so) and continues by inertia.

Actually, it looks to me like FILE-STATUS didn't show up in *standard*
COBOL until 1974.  See ANSI X3.23-1974 Page XIV-13 Item 7.  It was
made available in some implementations of '68 COBOL as an
ANSI-74-compatible extension.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
I don't condone nor support any virus spreads... in fact the reason to my
reaction was this specific reason... I had to rebuild my PC for the 4th time
( ! ) in two weeks. Behaviour like this should be prevented at all costs.

Kind rgds

TC
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0307111927.3e5701a@posting.google.com...
> Apologies ahead of time, but this is just too good to pass up...
>
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message
news:<bencku$1rm$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> >
> > Does anyone from the Antipodes know who else beside support@, help@ and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thi.Nguyen @ optus.com.au
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Is CLC being singled out? WAS: Re: IBM's new 24/7 VSAM

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T20:11:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f0fc33a_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bemfsr$29e$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f0f2dfb$0$727$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bencku$1rm$1@panix1.panix.com> <bfdfc3e8.0307111927.3e5701a@posting.google.com> <3f0faa30$0$26534$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au>`

```
Theo,

I have to admit to some concern when I read the Doc's post, describing your
mail attack.

This kind of behaviour can never be condoned, or even tolerated.

However, I give you full marks for taking responsibility for what you did
and trying to make it right.

I also sympathise with the multiple destruction of your system due to virus
attacks.

This raises another point...

It seems to me (and I'd welcome views from others here) that CLC is becoming
subject to "enemy action".

Maybe there are person or persons unknown out there who just have an axe to
grind with COBOL and COBOL people.

We had the "spam lover" post against Bill, (Robert claims it wasn't him,  he
also claims he is NOT the Robert Wagner who is  known for this kind of
anti-social behaviour; I hope that's true...Thankfully, it was apparently a
"one-off"...), we have had a spate of "Microsoft Patch" type rubbish being
sent to various people who post here regularly, so Theo's attack against the
Doc is really unwelcome. (It would be anyway, but you catch my drift...).

Last week I had nine posts of the "MS Patch" nonsense from different
addresses and all to the address I use for this NG. (I have a number of
other e-mail addresses, some of which are "spam deflectors". I recommend
others to do likewise.)

My virus detection catches them every time and the people doing it must
realise it is just stupid and pointless, but they keep sending them...

If  nothing else, being a regular poster here does encourage me to keep my
defences current.

Am I just being paranoid, or are others having similar experience?

Pete.

<snipped Theo's post>
```

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f10973a_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bemfsr$29e$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f0f2dfb$0$727$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bencku$1rm$1@panix1.panix.com> <bfdfc3e8.0307111927.3e5701a@posting.google.com> <3f0faa30$0$26534$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <3f0fc33a_6@news.athenanews.com> <s2UPa.11031$Ag6.987248@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f102e75_5@news.athenanews.com> <mf%Pa.16809$BM.5361345@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Fair comment, Michael.

There are extremely rare occasions where, in a civilized society, legal
action is the best of the alternatives.

I agree with you it is much better if it can be prevented from coming to
that.

I guess I'd rather see a Society that over litigates, than one where
vigilantes implement their own kind of "justice"...

But then, I don't live in America.

Here we all just "get along" <G> (well...most of the time...)

(Hey, the sun shines and there's only 4 million of us...when you have a
dispute you buy a few beers and go to the beach...By sundown you are so laid
back you let it go...<G>)

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bemfsr$29e$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f0f2dfb$0$727$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bencku$1rm$1@panix1.panix.com> <bfdfc3e8.0307111927.3e5701a@posting.google.com> <3f0faa30$0$26534$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <3f0fc33a_6@news.athenanews.com> <s2UPa.11031$Ag6.987248@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f102e75_5@news.athenanews.com> <mf%Pa.16809$BM.5361345@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3f10973a_8@news.athenanews.com> <Y42dnfE4XcrkAY2iXTWJkg@giganews.com> <3f10a154$1_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> LOL!
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

My partner was once called to a bar to determine whether a man sitting with
his ho in a back booth was armed. As he approached, the man sucker-punched
him. The bitch pulled a switch-blade from her purse and cut Charlie across
the back of his left hand. Then the fellow sliced my partner in the belly
with a broken beer bottle and fled out the front door. Charlie did manage to
land one blow to the chin of the 'lady.' Broke her fuckin' jaw in two
places. Ultimately she got 20 years for attempted capital murder.

Four hours after the bar brawl, while Charlie was being stiched back
togethers, other members of this small-town police force found the miscreant
wobbling down a deserted lane. Three days later these officers managed to
carry this now-toothless person 50 miles deep into Louisiana and admonished
him to never, ever, come back to Texas.

Happens more than one would think. Even today, in our enligtened times.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is CLC being singled out? WAS: Re: IBM's new 24/7 VSAM

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T21:56:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qWGdnZIUoPbtV42iXTWJiw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <mf%Pa.16809$BM.5361345@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3f10973a_8@news.athenanews.com> <Y42dnfE4XcrkAY2iXTWJkg@giganews.com> <beqcae$ftf$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>>
>> Been there. Vigilante justice is better. Fast, cheap, effective. And
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> receiving that which you have here called 'better'.

I'm of the Billy Budd school.

But you raise a straw man argument. The only people who should worry about
vigilante justice happening to them are those to whom it mostly should
happen.

Consider the oft-quoted commandment: "Better 50 guilty go free than one
innocent man should suffer." Where'd the "50" come from? Is that the
cut-off? How about 100? Wouldn't the principle be the same?

And if 100, why not 2000? And if 2000, why not empty the nearest prison
under the possibility that there may be an innocent person unjustly
confined?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is CLC being singled out? WAS: Re: IBM's new 24/7 VSAM

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T09:12:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<berlsr$q7d$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <Y42dnfE4XcrkAY2iXTWJkg@giganews.com> <beqcae$ftf$1@panix1.panix.com> <qWGdnZIUoPbtV42iXTWJiw@giganews.com>`

```
In article <qWGdnZIUoPbtV42iXTWJiw@giganews.com>,
JerryMouse <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>I'm of the Billy Budd school.

You're a fan of sodomy, rum and the lash?  Everyone needs a hobby, I
guess... I am an American, of the 'Innocent Until Proven Guilty in a Court
of Law' school.

>
>But you raise a straw man argument.

Wishing that someone 'never be blessed by receiving receiving that which
they have here called 'better'' is expressing a hope, not an argument.

>The only people who should worry about
>vigilante justice happening to them are those to whom it mostly should
>happen.

... and that, I believe, is just about anyone who 'don't look right' to 
someone else.  This is along the lines of 'the only people who should 
worry about criminal justice are criminals'... maybe that is why there's 
so much about them in the Bill of Rights about it, eh?

>
>Consider the oft-quoted commandment: "Better 50 guilty go free than one
>innocent man should suffer." Where'd the "50" come from? Is that the
>cut-off? How about 100? Wouldn't the principle be the same?

This argument appears to have as much in it worthy of examining as:
Consider that 2 + 3 = 5.  Where's the cut-off?  You put two numbers
together and come up with something larger, how about 2 + 3 = 6?  
Wouldn't the principle be the same?

>
>And if 100, why not 2000? And if 2000, why not empty the nearest prison
>under the possibility that there may be an innocent person unjustly
>confined?

There's a difference between a 'commandment' and a 'law', last I looked...  
at least in the United States of America in which I live.  


If you wish to discuss the actual quotation you are mangling I suggest
that you first research it; when you are able to present it in at least
one of it's original forms, with attribution, then we might begin to
discuss both the status that it has as law (hint: none) and the impact
that it might have on a society (hint: compare and contrast 'In all
criminal prosecutions, the accused shall enjoy the right to a speedy and
public trial, by an impartial jury of the state and district wherein the
crime shall have been committed' and 'Kill them all, God will know his
own').

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is CLC being singled out? WAS: Re: IBM's new 24/7 VSAM

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T11:15:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gMmcncqhJso_GIyiU-KYvA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <Y42dnfE4XcrkAY2iXTWJkg@giganews.com> <beqcae$ftf$1@panix1.panix.com> <qWGdnZIUoPbtV42iXTWJiw@giganews.com> <berlsr$q7d$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
> If you wish to discuss the actual quotation you are mangling I suggest
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> them all, God will know his own').


Only pendantic fuddy-duddies would insist on the original quote - and I
suggest you will never find the original author - but be my guest. The
concept, "Better 50/10/500 go free..." is imbedded in the theory of law - at
least that's what I learned in law school.

And there is no conflict at all between the 4th Amendment and vigilantism;
the principle's the same. Identical, in fact.

As a real-world matter, there are few, very few, people in prison who are
actually guilty of the crime for which they were convicted. They are in the
slammer for what could be proved; much of the time what could be proved is
not what the miscreant actually did.

For example, about 9 out of 10 people who are charged with a crime plead
guilty at the first opportunity. Why? Because the crime to which they plead
is not the one they committed, it's a lesser offense.
```

```
>The
>concept, "Better 50/10/500 go free..." is imbedded in the theory of law - at
>least that's what I learned in law school.

What you were taught and what you learned might, possibly, not be the same 
thing; hence the request that you re-visit your sources.  It might be said 
that only smug, self-satisfied rhetoricians would be satisfied by what 
they remember instead of what they were taught.

>
>And there is no conflict at all between the 4th Amendment and vigilantism;
>the principle's the same. Identical, in fact.

That might just possibly be the reason that there are Amendments other 
than the 4th... such as the one I quoted.

>
>As a real-world matter, there are few, very few, people in prison who are
>actually guilty of the crime for which they were convicted. They are in the
>slammer for what could be proved; much of the time what could be proved is
>not what the miscreant actually did.

How curious... an unsubstantiated quotation resting on an unsubstantiable 
premise of 'real-world' proof.  As I stated above, if you wish to discuss 
the actual quotation you are mangling I suggest that you first research 
it.

DD
```

```
On Sat, 12 Jul 2003 21:40:34 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
<michael.mattias@gte.net> enlightened us:

>"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
>news:3f102e75_5@news.athenanews.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

While the U.S. could do with a bit of tort reform, try living in a
country that has no tort laws at all (e.g. Thailand) and see how
people's lives are damaged by commercial entities (and common
citizens) and the effected people have little or no recourse.  The
commercial entities in all but the most egregious cases continues to
make money and produce a  bad product or provide questionable service.

Because there is no litigation and because many countries don't have
our equivalent of a better business bureau, the offending companies
can go on about their business with their sins unnoticed by the
masses.

Yes, the U.S. is too litigious, but it is better for the citizens that
there is a retardant buffer and recourse when wronged in place.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

THE AFTERNOON HAVING A GOOD TIME.

Steve
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bes68r$l2d$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <3f102e75_5@news.athenanews.com> <mf%Pa.16809$BM.5361345@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <v933hvsq7cd6prjl86ijpr8gnqohi0in6b@4ax.com>`

```
In article <v933hvsq7cd6prjl86ijpr8gnqohi0in6b@4ax.com>,
SkippyPB  <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:
>On Sat, 12 Jul 2003 21:40:34 GMT, "Michael Mattias"
><michael.mattias@gte.net> enlightened us:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>make money and produce a  bad product or provide questionable service.

One of my favorites - and this is from my memory, which is, admittedly, 
porous - happened in Japan; one of the Major Electronics Manufacturers, in 
the 1960's, shipped a batch of television sets with bad flyback 
transformers which had a nasty tendency to catch fire.  Somebody tried to 
sue the company after their house burned down - Japanese construction 
being a bit light on fire-retarding characteristics at the time - and this 
was So Shocking - how *dare* a mere individual cause such shame to a Major 
Corporation by pointing out that their product was shoddy! - that the 
court found for the defendant... because the plaintiff could not prove 
that somebody did not enter their house while they were sleeping and set 
the fire in precisely the place where the television set was located.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Microsoft Security Update worm (was: Is CLC being singled out? )

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T21:59:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1061ec.7948794@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bemfsr$29e$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f0f2dfb$0$727$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bencku$1rm$1@panix1.panix.com> <bfdfc3e8.0307111927.3e5701a@posting.google.com> <3f0faa30$0$26534$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <3f0fc33a_6@news.athenanews.com> <s2UPa.11031$Ag6.987248@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
The spammer is someone reading CLC. He could be someone with a bone to pick, but
more likely he is a regular poster who acquired the W32/Gibe.b worm and is now
unwittingly spamming us as he reads. The worm harvests email addresses from a
temp file containing postings you just read and sends them via an internal SMTP
(you need not be running Outlook) to somebody. That somebody originates the
spam. 

Notice all the exe files you got as attachments are exactly the same size:
155,648. Compare any two and you'll find they're exactly the same.

Now see McAfee's description and notice the same size:
http://vil.mcafee.com/dispVirus.asp?virus_k=100088

Who is it? Most people don't read their own postings, therefore the worm host's
email address will NOT be on the spammer's mailing list. I went to a CLC
archive, extracted the "From:" lines and compared that list to the spammer's
mailing list. Frequent (and semi-frequent) posters NOT receiving the spam are: 

jjgavan@shaw.ca
ricksmith@mfi.net
wsimmons5@cox.net
pogo50@talk21.com
mwojcik@newsguy.com
member30421@dbforums.com (hhinman)
jallan@smrtytrek.com
richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com

If your email is listed above AND you are posting from a Win/NT machine AND it
is running Microsoft's Winsock (if you don't know, it is), please go to your
%windir% (usually \windows or \winnt) and look for file DXD3R*.exe. If you see
DXD3Render.exe or DXD3Rndr.exe, you are infected!! Announce it here and stop
READING until your machine is disinfected.

Another clue: winojoe posted here once in January 2003 and he's still on the
spammer's mailing list. That means anyone who started reading after January
cannot be the gatherer. It also means the list is cumulative. Changing your
email address today will not stop spams to the old addess for a long time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microsoft Security Update worm (was: Is CLC being singled out? )

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T19:29:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vh16hkg1ju2g0f@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f1061ec.7948794@news.optonline.com...
[snip]
> Who is it? Most people don't read their own postings, therefore the worm
host's
> email address will NOT be on the spammer's mailing list. I went to a CLC
> archive, extracted the "From:" lines and compared that list to the
spammer's
> mailing list. Frequent (and semi-frequent) posters NOT receiving the spam
are:

I do 'read' my own postings because Outlook Express shows
them as it does all others.

I am regularly sent the Microsoft Security Update; but the ISP
strips the attachments or notifies me that the e-mail was
infected and makes the 'cleaned' e-mail available through
their web site. Hence, I do not know what you mean by "posters
NOT receiving the spam". Clearly, any who did not receive
the spam could not be infected. Perhaps you could clarify.

>
> jjgavan@shaw.ca
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

AND it
> is running Microsoft's Winsock (if you don't know, it is), please go to
your
> %windir% (usually \windows or \winnt) and look for file DXD3R*.exe. If you
see
> DXD3Render.exe or DXD3Rndr.exe, you are infected!! Announce it here and
stop
> READING until your machine is disinfected.

I am still using Win95. Winsock.DLL is present. Neither of the
files shown are present.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microsoft Security Update worm (was: Is CLC being singled out? )

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T08:45:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f10ca63.34696723@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bemfsr$29e$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f0f2dfb$0$727$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bencku$1rm$1@panix1.panix.com> <bfdfc3e8.0307111927.3e5701a@posting.google.com> <3f0faa30$0$26534$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <3f0fc33a_6@news.athenanews.com> <s2UPa.11031$Ag6.987248@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f1061ec.7948794@news.optonline.com> <vh16hkg1ju2g0f@corp.supernews.com>`

```
The spam email list I used was dated 7/10/2003. Your email address wasn't on it.
I see now (below) the list covers a date range of 6/29 through 7/10. You did not
post anything during that period, which is why you were not on the spammer's
list. 

What about the other suspects I listed? Only two posted during that period:
member30421@dbforums.com (hhinman) posted many mesages.
richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com posted one message on 7/09 in which he
complained about delay in postings appearing. There is a good chance this one
did not appear to the spammer by 7/10.

Howard is the prime suspect. The others I listed may disregard; they weren't on
the email list because they didn't post during the period.

Yahoo threw me off by failing to find winojoe within the period. It made me
think the list was cumulative. Digging deeper shows he did post on 6/29, as did
everyone else on the spam list. I now see the list is not cumulative; it covers
a short time span of 1-12 days. 

Correction: in my original, change "DXD3R" to "DX3DR". I hate it when the
dyslexia kicks in. 

The plot thickens ..

Another spam mailing list dated 7/11, one day later, contains CLC posters on
7/10 through 7/11, including Howard Hinman,  followed by about 15 other emails
unrelated to CLC. It would appear the spammer went off and read other
newsgroups. I looked up each to see whether they have a common interest. They do
not. The first two were into DVD, then quality audio, then movies, food, two
interested in organ transplants. Several showed up in net abuse & spam groups
as the abuse receiver. 

Where would such a diverse collection of individuals come from? It's likely the
spammer searched on a keyword that happened to select them. I can't figure out
what it was. 

Clarification on reading your own postings -- reading means Opening in Outlook.
Seeing them listed on a summary, even with preview, doesn't count. 

The spam is originated by the infected machine, not by some third party as I
first thought. But it is not posted through the infected machine's normal SMTP
server else it would have a Posting Host line. It is being posted through a
machine that allows forwarding. 

The worm only sends spam. It doesn't do anything malicious to the infected
machine. However, it also contains a back door. With more development, it could
be made to do anything... like turning the machine into a denial of service
attacker. The software to do that is not in place but it could be installed from
afar.


---- history below ----

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>the spam could not be infected. Perhaps you could clarify.

>> jjgavan@shaw.ca
>> ricksmith@mfi.net
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microsoft Security Update worm (was: Is CLC being singled out? )

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T17:04:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f117023.77134687@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bemfsr$29e$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f0f2dfb$0$727$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bencku$1rm$1@panix1.panix.com> <bfdfc3e8.0307111927.3e5701a@posting.google.com> <3f0faa30$0$26534$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <3f0fc33a_6@news.athenanews.com> <s2UPa.11031$Ag6.987248@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f1061ec.7948794@news.optonline.com>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

A spam sent out today (7/13) contains the following non-CLC email addresses. The
cutoff period appears to be 7/11/03. In addition to reading CLC, our tormenter
read messages by these people. Here is where each posted on that date:

<sales@soundengine.com>,

<strutter_uk@hotmail.com>,
uk.music.makers.dj

<rareoldthings@aol.com>,
many music groups

<lamont_nj31@hotmail.com>,
alt.music.makers.dj

<boybles@yahoo.com>,
alt.music.makers.dj

<xmicks@aol.com>,
alt.music.makers.dj

<pop@goestheemail.com>,
alt.music.makers.dj

<rezrec@sbcglobal.net>,
alt.music.makers.dj

<freaks@free.fr>,
alt.music.makers.dj

<groovy1959@hotmail.com>,
alt.music.makers.dj

<marcwilgeenmail@postbus.net>,
alt.music.makers.dj

<apqx95@dsl.pipex.com>
alt.music.makers.dj

Looks like our boy is a dj. Let's see who else posted to alt.music.makers.dj on
7/11. One of them might be him. One thread in particular caught my attention. 
The subject is "keygen for PCDJ Red" . Messages 3 and 4 were posted by "FUQ
(fuq@nospam.com)". Text of all but one of his messages has been DELETED from
Google, which helpfully provided a pointer to the #1 hacker site:
www.astalavista.box.sk (Slovakia).  In the virus itself, following the registry
entry "Coded by Begbie", it says "in Slovakia". No, hackers aren't in Slovakia,
it's their adopted home base. 

A reply by "D Side (pop@goestheemail.com)" reads "I so love keygens and cracks -
it's just the best way to get a virus into someones machine :-)". The
knowledgable Mr. Fuq responds "thats bullshit. almost all cracks and keygens r
clean." (he's right) [A crack/keygen is a program that fools commercial software
into thinking it's been registered or seen a 'key CD'. ]

Elsewhere, Mr. Fuq posts to alt.games.champ-man, where he knows about British
soccer; uk.politics.drugs, where he knows about cannabis; and alt.music.trance,
where he knows about sound mixing, etc. 

I see three possibilities:

1. Someone is deliberately spamming CLC and alt.music.makers.dj.
   An unlikely combination of vendettas. 

2.  Mr. Fuq has a professional interest in audio and is deliberately spamming
CLC. 
   Possible. His public persona is a pot smoking dj, but he also knows about
hacking and MAYBE cancels his own postings to conceal it. 

3. A regular CLC reader (not necessarily poster) is also interested in music.
He's unknowingly infected and spamming both without being aware of it.
   I think that's the most likely explanation.
```

- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bemfsr$29e$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f0f2dfb$0$727$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <bencku$1rm$1@panix1.panix.com> <bfdfc3e8.0307111927.3e5701a@posting.google.com> <3f0faa30$0$26534$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au> <3f0fc33a_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> Last week I had nine posts of the "MS Patch" nonsense from different
> addresses and all to the address I use for this NG. (I have a number of
> other e-mail addresses, some of which are "spam deflectors". I recommend
> others to do likewise.)

That patch is getting annoying.  My ISP finally set up filtering, and 
I've only seen 1 in the last 2 months.

I think someone is just going out and harvesting e-mail addresses from 
news archives.  I do have to give props to Netscape's web mail spam 
filtering.  Since I changed e-mail addresses on my newsgroup posts a 
couple of weeks ago, I've had exactly 2 pieces of spam come in on that 
address.  Using the mail reader that comes with NS, you can set it up to 
read the web account like it's a regular POP3 account - I'm thinking of 
changing to actually using that address as my primary one.

> If  nothing else, being a regular poster here does encourage me to keep my
> defences current.

DoD hooks us up on that - we get Norton AV free on our personal 
computers, and can get a copy for our home computers as well.  If you 
use their install and have a persistent connection, it keeps everything 
updated, and you don't have to subscribe to get updates.  :)  When I 
get out of the Air Force, I'm going to miss that.

> Am I just being paranoid, or are others having similar experience?

I don't know how much of it is targeted at CLC - I also post on 
comp.sys.unisys, and use my other e-mail address on the MySQL ODBC 
mailing list.  Of course, I'm getting offers now, 2 a day each, for 
pills to make my schlong bigger and a filter so you can order PPV 
digital cable shows and not pay for them.

I'm looking forward to getting this new NS 7.1's junk filter set up - it 
looks like it'll do a pretty good job.  The documentation said to 
"train" it for a couple of weeks (it identifies way too much stuff at 
first), then set up a rule for all "junk" mail.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM's new 24/7 VSAM

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T05:35:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030714013505.03537.00000325@mb-m05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3f0eb6e7$0$30568$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au>`

```
>From: "Theo Charalambous" westleylodge@optusnet.com.au 
>Date: 7/11/03 9:09 AM Eastern Daylight Time

>
>Does anyone in the group, work on any sites that use IBM's 24/7. If you do
>can they verify any of the following
>

We run our mainframe 24/7.

>1. Could batch run against CICS

I assume VSAM files owned by CICS.  Yes you can - I've written a few programs
that do so but it the batch program itself doesn't do the updating - CICS does
via DPI calls (don't quote me on that - DPI - my memory is porous on this :D). 

>2. Does it have a rollback facility

No - rollback is done by CICS because any updates to the files is done by CICS.

>3. Are there any changes required to change legacy COBOL/VSAM applications

>3. Are there any changes required to change legacy COBOL/VSAM applications
>to the new 24/7 AMS
>

I'm not sure what the new 24/7 AMS is so I assume we aren't using it - but I
would think there probably would be.  I know for sure that there would be to do
what I'm doing.
```

## Thread: 1 track = ?? bytes — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### 1 track = ?? bytes

- **From:** "KK" <KK@KK.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-11T23:13:02+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f0ed3fe$1@shknews01>`

```
I just wanna know how many bytes are stored in a single track.
Is there any calculation?
```

#### ↳ Re: 1 track = ?? bytes

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-11T15:34:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bemlde$qnh$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3f0ed3fe$1@shknews01>`

```

On 11-Jul-2003, "KK" <KK@KK.com> wrote:

> I just wanna know how many bytes are stored in a single track.
> Is there any calculation?

Sure, there are lots of calculations.   Of course not all tracks are the same
size.   Even mainframe IBM shops may have different types of disks with
different sizes.   There are some variations in architecture where some disks
have different track sizes on the same disk.   (variable speed).

What I'm saying is, we need to know more about your environment to help you
further.
```

## Thread: EOF Technique — 37 msgs

### Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-11T17:51:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cOCPa.50178$0v4.3392714@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com>`

```

"Jack Straw" <jackstraw@witchita> wrote in message
news:le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com...
| I'm an experienced programmer, trying to get up to speed on Cobol.
| I've read the source to a few programs from various places, and I have
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
| boolean.

You are correct.

|
| Am I missing something, or is it just dumb luck that I have weird code
| examples?
|
| The first technique seems very common.

On the IBM mainframe:
It is, in the past it was not uncommon the let the program ABEND rather than
handle the error in the program by not specifying FILE STATUS.
(If you specified FILE STATUS, and you missed the error, the program would
keep chugging along.)
Things are changing, in some shops I've found that if you are missing a "DD"
statement for an output file, the system allocates a temporary file. (The DD
statement specifies the physical file associated with the Logical file in
the SELECT).

Even if you use the file status, you can still use AT END.

Here's another sample:
PERFORM UNTIL EOF-FILE
       READ SomeFile
            AT END
                 SET EOF-FILE  to TRUE
           NOT AT END
                  PERFORM  0000-PROCESS-REC
       END-READ
END-PERFORM.
```

#### ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T01:52:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f0f665e.31748933@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <cOCPa.50178$0v4.3392714@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:


>On the IBM mainframe:
>It is, in the past it was not uncommon the let the program ABEND rather than
>handle the error in the program by not specifying FILE STATUS.
>(If you specified FILE STATUS, and you missed the error, the program would
>keep chugging along.)

Which is exactly what your example, below,  does. 

>Things are changing, in some shops I've found that if you are missing a "DD"
>statement for an output file, the system allocates a temporary file. (The DD
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-PERFORM.

What if some other exception occurs, like sector not found or sector corrupted?
You said he could use file status and then provided an example which doesn't
check it. Tsk tsk. The code should read something like this.

05  file-status              pic  x(02).
     88   any-exception value '10' thru high-values.
     88   end-of-file value '10' thru '19'.

read SomeFile     
perform until any-exception
    perform process-record
    read SomeFile
end-perform
if not end-of-file
    display 'error ' file-status ' on SomeFile'
    goback returning (whatever)
end-if
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T11:49:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CBSPa.16137$BM.5256246@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <cOCPa.50178$0v4.3392714@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f0f665e.31748933@news.optonline.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f0f665e.31748933@news.optonline.com...
> >
> >Even if you use the file status, you can still use AT END.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> >END-PERFORM.


OR...without setting flags..

PERFORM
     READ SomeFile
            AT END
                 EXIT PERFORM
            NOT AT END
                  PERFORM  0000-PROCESS-REC
       END-READ
END-PERFORM.

MCM
```

```
| >(If you specified FILE STATUS, and you missed the error, the program
would
| >keep chugging along.)
|
| Which is exactly what your example, below,  does.

NO it doesn't.
It will abend if file status isn't specified, and I'll get an error message
with the file status from the system. (on the mainframe). It's a freebie!

|
| >Things are changing, in some shops I've found that if you are missing a
"DD"
| >statement for an output file, the system allocates a temporary file. (The
DD
| >statement specifies the physical file associated with the Logical file in
| >the SELECT).
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
| What if some other exception occurs, like sector not found or sector
corrupted?

On the mainframe, you don't get sector not found?
You should read more carefully.

On the mainframe, the program will abend if you don't specify FILE STATUS.
Remember, it's an input file.
Output files are another story.

| You said he could use file status and then provided an example which
doesn't
| check it. Tsk tsk.

COULD doesn't mean MUST, does it?.

The original post indicated that he had a handle on file status, and he was
asking about AT END. (This guy is NOT a trainee.) I have no doubt that he
could come up with a solution using FILE STATUS without your help.
The original sample had an AT END, and I gave an example with NOT AT END.

When did you start reading minds?
If I did use FILE STATUS, I could have tested it in 0000-PROCESS-REC, and
again, if I didn't the program would ABEND on the mainframe.

|The code should read something like this.

MY example didn't need a priming read, try again.
I know you can do it, if you really try.

What does YOUR code have to do with the AT END condition?

I have an idea, why don't you read the original post, and respond to it.

|
| 05  file-status              pic  x(02).
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
|
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T21:59:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3f0f665e.31748933@news.optonline.com...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>with the file status from the system. (on the mainframe). It's a freebie!

Don't count on it. WMK pointed out it doesn't always work on VSAM. You know what
they say about assuming.

>| >Even if you use the file status, you can still use AT END.
>| >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>You should read more carefully.

I do read carefully. I check for errors before performing 0000-process-rec; you
don't.

>| You said he could use file status and then provided an example which
>|doesn't check it. Tsk tsk.
>
>COULD doesn't mean MUST, does it?.

I don't see 'could' in the above quote; I see "if you use the file status".

>When did you start reading minds?

I imagine mind reading would return LOTS of errors. :)

>MY example didn't need a priming read, try again.
>I know you can do it, if you really try.

No problem. 

 05  file-status              pic  x(02).
      88   end-of-file value '10' thru '19'.
      88   any-exception value '10' thru high-values.

 perform until any-exception
     read SomeFile
     if  not any-exception
         perform process-record
     end-if
 end-perform
 if not end-of-file
     display 'error ' file-status ' on SomeFile'
     goback returning (whatever)
 end-if
 
Do you prefer the duplicated test on any-exception?

>What does YOUR code have to do with the AT END condition?

No change there. It has to do with checking ALL file status exceptions, not just
end of file.
```

```
rather

I know, that's why alomost everybody, including me, uses FILE STATUS for
VSAM.
I should have specified SEQUENTIAL FILES, I didn't.
It was an error.

|
| >| >Even if you use the file status, you can still use AT END.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
| I do read carefully. I check for errors before performing
0000-process-rec; you
| don't.

Why does it matter where you check?

How does a "sector not found" error occur on the mainframe?

| >| You said he could use file status and then provided an example which
| >|doesn't check it. Tsk tsk.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| I don't see 'could' in the above quote; I see "if you use the file
status".

Read YOUR comment again.

|
| >When did you start reading minds?
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
| Do you prefer the duplicated test on any-exception?

Nope, especially since you don't need any-exception.
This is how *I* would code it. (On the mainframe)

  05  file-status               pic XX.
          88  GOOD-READ    VALUE  '00'.
          88   end-of-file           value '10'.

  SET  GOOD-READ  TO  TRUE.
  PERFORM        UNTIL  NOT  GOOD-READ
      READ   SomeFile
      EVALUATE    TRUE
      WHEN  GOOD-READ
                   PERFORM 1000-PROCESS-RECORD
      WHEN  END-OF-FILE
                    CONTINUE
      WHEN OTHER
                   display 'error ' file-status ' on SomeFile'
                   GO TO  9999-ABEND-ROUTINE
      END-EVALUATE
  END-PERFORM.

Seems to be a bit cleaner, and clearer than your example.
Don't you agree?

|
| >What does YOUR code have to do with the AT END condition?
|
| No change there. It has to do with checking ALL file status exceptions,
not just
| end of file.

The original question was about the AT END, what does your example have to
do with that?
Do you understand the question?
```

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

>| >| What if some other exception occurs, like sector not found or sector
>| >corrupted?

>How does a "sector not found" error occur on the mainframe?

The subject was "some other exception"; sector not found was but one example. 

I've seen mainframes get sector not found in the good old days. Especially when
I was writing low level IO drivers and disk optimizers. :)

>This is how *I* would code it. (On the mainframe)
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>Don't you agree?

Yes, except the GO TO. In real life I'd put the read and OTHER handling in a
callable program.  

Technically, good-read should be '00' thru '09'. Reading an indexed file on an
alternate (illiterate, should be alternative) index returns '01' for duplicates,
on some platforms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T13:37:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <cOCPa.50178$0v4.3392714@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f0f665e.31748933@news.optonline.com> <8MSPa.51414$3o3.3428261@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com...
| "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
|
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
| The subject was "some other exception"; sector not found was but one
example.
|
| I've seen mainframes get sector not found in the good old days. Especially
when
| I was writing low level IO drivers and disk optimizers. :)

It would have been more appropriate to use "bad track", or "unrecoverable
I/O error" as mainframe programmers would be more familiar with that
terminology.

|
| >This is how *I* would code it. (On the mainframe)
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
| Yes, except the GO TO. In real life I'd put the read and OTHER handling in
a
| callable program.

Normally, when I code an abend routine, I will include things that pertain
to that program, like record counts, totals, and keys.
It doesn't hurt to give the programmer, who has to fix the problem, all the
information that they could use.

That means doing it at the main program level.
So, I will return from a called module, and I will abend in the main
program.

|
| Technically, good-read should be '00' thru '09'. Reading an indexed file
on an
| alternate (illiterate, should be alternative) index returns '01' for
duplicates,
| on some platforms.

That's why I said on the mainframe.
I could get an '04' which is unacceptable, because it indicates a bad record
length. (So, I wouldn't use '00' thru '09'.)

This was for a sequential file which will not get duplicates, since it has
no key.

If I wanted to use '00' thru '09', I would code it this way.

 05  file-status.
         88   end-of-file           value '10'.
    10                             PIC X.
        88  GOOD-READ    VALUE  '0'.
    10                             PIC X.

Here is a link to the Status Key Values, on the mainframe.

http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3lr10/6.1.8.9.1?SHE
LF=&DT=20020920180651
```

```
the

Otherwise you run the risk of serious database corruption,
if you happen to be running in an IMS environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T00:10:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f11f052.109954702@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <cOCPa.50178$0v4.3392714@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f0f665e.31748933@news.optonline.com> <8MSPa.51414$3o3.3428261@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <LgdQa.52867$0v4.3588569@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com...
>| "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>| >This is how *I* would code it. (On the mainframe)
>| >
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>program.

I agree but I'd do it on three levels: top management, middle management and
file clerk. GO TO is always a symptom of bad structure. Notice how mine
eliminates the GO TO and condition testing inside the middle loop. I too abend
in the main program -- for all errors, not just IO -- while displaying
problem-specific information close to the point where the problem is detected. 
The top guy's message may be too generic. 

This trivial example doesn't show the advantage of three levels. Imagine a
typical program with multiple middle loops and at least as many files. 

main:
 01  diagnostic-info.
       05  status-code.
            10  status-code-1           pic x.
            10  status-code-2..
                 15  status-code-2n  comp pic s99.

 call 'middle' using diagnostic-info
 if  return-code > 1
     (display diagnostic information)
 else
     move zero to return-code
 end-if
 goback returning return-code.

middle: 
 call 'fileaccess' using diagnostic-info, record-area
 perform until return-code not = zero 
     (process record logic)
     call 'fileaccess' using diagnostic-info, record-area
 end-perform 
 goback returning return-code.   

fileaccess:
 if  file-not-open
     open input file-name
     perform check-status-code
     set file-open to true
 end-if
 read file-name into record-area
 perform check-status-code
 add 1 to record-count in diagnostic-info 
 move record-key in record-area to record-key in diagnostic-info
 goback returning 0.

check-status-code.
 if  status-code-1 not = '0'
     if  status-code-1 not = '1'  
         display 'IO error ' status-code ' on filename'
         if  status-code-2 not numeric
             display '(' status-code-2n ')'
         end-if
         move 12 to return-code
     else
         move 1 to return-code
     end-if
     if  file-open
         close file-name
         set file-not-open to true
     end-if
     goback returning return-code
 end-if.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T01:13:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zsnQa.53546$0v4.3644390@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <cOCPa.50178$0v4.3392714@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f0f665e.31748933@news.optonline.com> <8MSPa.51414$3o3.3428261@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <LgdQa.52867$0v4.3588569@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f11f052.109954702@news.optonline.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f11f052.109954702@news.optonline.com...
| "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
|
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
| >| Yes, except the GO TO. In real life I'd put the read and OTHER handling
in
| >a
| >| callable program.
| >
| >Normally, when I code an abend routine, I will include things that
pertain
| >to that program, like record counts, totals, and keys.
| >It doesn't hurt to give the programmer, who has to fix the problem, all
the
| >information that they could use.
| >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| I agree but I'd do it on three levels: top management, middle management
and
| file clerk. GO TO is always a symptom of bad structure.

For *ME*, GO TO is the preferable way to handle the condition of  abandoning
the program.
The other option is to continually test an "abandon" run indicator, which
adds unnecessary complexity to the program, and may unwittingly be ignored..

From the infamous "Go To Statement Considered Harmful", Edsger W. Dijkstra

"The remark about the undesirability of the go to statement is far from new.
I remember having read the explicit recommendation to restrict the use of
the go to statement to alarm exits, but I have not been able to trace it;
presumably, it has been made by C. A. R. Hoare."

|Notice how mine
| eliminates the GO TO and condition testing inside the middle loop. I too
abend
| in the main program -- for all errors, not just IO -- while displaying
| problem-specific information close to the point where the problem is
detected.
| The top guy's message may be too generic.

AND, as we both pointed out, lack critical information.

END OF MY COMMENTS.
|
| This trivial example doesn't show the advantage of three levels. Imagine a
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
|  end-if.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T15:07:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bet70u$ata$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <LgdQa.52867$0v4.3588569@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f11f052.109954702@news.optonline.com> <zsnQa.53546$0v4.3644390@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:

> From the infamous "Go To Statement Considered Harmful", Edsger W. Dijkstra

In fact there is nothing wrong with a GO TO at all.  It is a very clear 
statement that is unambiguous and always does what is required.

Where the _actual_ problem occurs is at the target of a GO TO, a label.

In COBOL, labels may be used for a variety of actions:

     PERFORM label
     PERFORM xxx thru label
     GO TO label
     drop thru

If each label is only used for one thing only, eg PERFORM this-label but 
never PERFORM .. THRU this-label nor GOTO this-label nor drop thru, then 
the process of understanding the program is considerably simplified. 

When reading a program it is done small parts at a time while building up 
how the whole program fits together.  Given a 'PERFORM paragraph-x', for 
example', it is quite clear what will happen.  When inspecting paragraph-x, 
given that only paragraphs will be performed (no THRU or SECTIONs) then it 
is equally clear what path is taken.

However, if there are a variety of paths through a label, such as drop thru 
_and_ PERFORM, then the complexity increases.

If a program has a termination point in the first paragraph (STOP RUN, EXIT 
PROGRAM etc), and has not PERFORM THRU, GO TO, or SECTIONs, then one can be 
fairly confident that each label will only be referenced in one way.

Whenever there is a GO TO in the program then it is necessary to increase 
the amount of checking that is done at each label to ensure which paths are 
actually taken through that point: one must cater for drop thru.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T23:56:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yTpQa.678$ym.190934@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:zsnQa.53546$0v4.3644390@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> | >| Yes, except the GO TO. In real life I'd put the read and OTHER
handling
> in
> | >a
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> For *ME*, GO TO is the preferable way to handle the condition of
abandoning
> the program.
> The other option is to continually test an "abandon" run indicator, which
> adds unnecessary complexity to the program, and may unwittingly be
ignored..
>
> From the infamous "Go To Statement Considered Harmful", Edsger W. Dijkstra
>
> "The remark about the undesirability of the go to statement is far from
new.
> I remember having read the explicit recommendation to restrict the use of
> the go to statement to alarm exits, but I have not been able to trace it;
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> | This trivial example doesn't show the advantage of three levels. Imagine
a
> | typical program with multiple middle loops and at least as many files.
> |
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think you are both missing the boat.  I'd use a simple open, and have a
file obect handle everything but the at end in a declaratives section. That
is exactly how a modern compiler works, and what exception handling is
designed for.  Why re-write it at the code level?

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T07:43:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Technically, good-read should be '00' thru '09'. 

Technically, only the first byte should be used to indicate success, end of 
file, key error, etc. the 2nd byte may contain additional information, '0' 
indicates no more information applicable.

> Reading an indexed file
> on an alternate (illiterate, should be alternative) index returns '01' for
> duplicates, 

'02'.

Note that you should also get an '02' on a WRITE or REWRITE that creates a 
duplicate key on _any_ index (where duplicate keys have been allowed).

> on some platforms.

It should be on all conforming '85 or later platforms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T01:40:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iSnQa.53566$0v4.3646220@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
| Robert Wagner wrote:
|
| > Technically, good-read should be '00' thru '09'.
|
| Technically, only the first byte should be used to indicate success, end
of
| file, key error, etc. the 2nd byte may contain additional information, '0'
| indicates no more information applicable.

This is a bit tricky.
The '04' says you have an incorrect record length, so it might not be
advisable to continue processing.
'02' may be acceptable for alternate index processing. (I just know someone
somewhere has written a program that isn't supposed to create duplicate
keys.)

GOOD-READ could be:
"00"
"00" or "04"
"00" or "02"
"00" or "02" or "04"

|
| > Reading an indexed file
| > on an alternate (illiterate, should be alternative) index returns '01'
for
| > duplicates,
|
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| duplicate key on _any_ index (where duplicate keys have been allowed).

I wonder what you get when you satisfy "02" & "04" in the same read.

"02" - For a READ statement  the key value for the current key of
reference was equal to the value of the  same key in the next record within
the   current key of reference.

"04" - A READ statement was successfully  executed, but the length of the
record   being processed did not conform to the fixed file attributes for
that file.

|
| > on some platforms.
|
| It should be on all conforming '85 or later platforms.

Thanks for the info.
I was assuming that the Status Code values might be vendor/platform
dependent.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T23:23:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307132223.360b90da@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <iSnQa.53566$0v4.3646220@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote 

> This is a bit tricky.
> The '04' says you have an incorrect record length, so it might not be
> advisable to continue processing.

> '02' may be acceptable for alternate index processing. (I just know someone
> somewhere has written a program that isn't supposed to create duplicate
> keys.)

Duplicate keys are specified in the SELECT, if they are not allowed
then you will never get an '02'.

> GOOD-READ could be:
> "00"
> "00" or "04"
> "00" or "02"
> "00" or "02" or "04"

You say 'GOOD-READ' but these also apply to WRITE and '05' and '07'
apply to OPEN.  Others may be added in future standards.

> I wonder what you get when you satisfy "02" & "04" in the same read.

Good point.

> I was assuming that the Status Code values might be vendor/platform
> dependent.

The '9x' codes are vendor and/or platform dependent. The '0x' through
'4x' are defined in the standard.  The standard ones should always be
used by the vendor where applicable.  However, it may be that the
behaviour can be changed using a compiler directive - MF has
ANS85(syntax).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T13:27:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f12a2b0.155623961@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <iSnQa.53566$0v4.3646220@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0307132223.360b90da@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote 
>
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>ANS85(syntax).

In an ideal world, compilers would follow the standard and agree with each
other. This Web page shows what they do in reality. 

http://www.adtools.com/support/tip_filestat.htm

It shows they generally do follow the standard, with the notable exception of
IBM OS/VS. This once most common IBM mainframe compiler _frequently_ breaks with
the standard. Others are ambiguous. What does MF return for read on unopened
file? The page shows both 47 and 9-002. I wouldn't be surprised to find it does
one for flat files and the other for indexed. 

Not discussed on the page are supplemental return codes provided in various ways
such as FCB, call or extra syntax in the select. FWIW, SQL return codes are very
similar. For instance, Informix returns SQL standard values but in addition can
return an extremely useful English language description of the problem .. if you
know enough to ask for it. Few programmers know the feature exists; I've never
seen it used. 

There is a typo on the Web page: 02 is printed as 00.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T12:58:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307141158.4551b4e1@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <iSnQa.53566$0v4.3646220@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0307132223.360b90da@posting.google.com> <3f12a2b0.155623961@news.optonline.com>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> In an ideal world, compilers would follow the standard and agree with each
> other. This Web page shows what they do in reality. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>_frequently_ breaks with the standard. 

The observant amonst us will have noticed that OS/VS has the same
entries as '74' for the standard status values.  This is because OSVS
_does_ follow the 'standard', but that happens to be ANS'74 - it is
not an '85 compliant compiler, you need VS Cobol II for ANS'85.

> Others are ambiguous. What does MF return for read on unopened
> file? The page shows both 47 and 9-002. I wouldn't be surprised to find
> it does one for flat files and the other for indexed. 

MF can return '74 standard codes or '85 standard depending on a
compiler settimg.  ANS85(syntax) give '85 syntax but '74 behaviour. 
This then only returns those status codes defined in '74 (and thus not
'47') supplemented by vendor codes (and thus '9/002').

This was a feature to ease the transition to '85 while programs
extended their status checking code.

> Informix returns SQL standard values but in addition can return an
> extremely useful English language description of the problem .. if you
> know enough to ask for it. Few programmers know the feature exists; I've
> never seen it used. 

From the Fujitsu Language manual:
------------------------------------------------------------------------
SQLMSG 

If an exception event occurs during the execution of an SQL statement,
a message indicating the nature of the event is posted to the
application program. SQLMSG is the area where this message is stored.
The user can print or display the SQLMSG contents by using an output
statement. SQLMSG must always be defined as alphanumeric data item
with level number 01 or 77 in the embedded SQL declarative section.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

With MF and several embedded SQLs it is sqlerrmc (see SQLCA.CPY or
sqlca.h).

You don't need to 'ask for it'.

I use it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T16:07:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f141e78.91124348@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <iSnQa.53566$0v4.3646220@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0307132223.360b90da@posting.google.com> <3f12a2b0.155623961@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307141158.4551b4e1@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>The observant amonst us will have noticed that OS/VS has the same
>entries as '74' for the standard status values.  This is because OSVS
>_does_ follow the 'standard', but that happens to be ANS'74 - it is
>not an '85 compliant compiler, you need VS Cobol II for ANS'85.

Putting it another way, ANS'74 and OSVS are 'compliant' with ANS'85 to the
extent 85 didn't change existing 74 codes, it just added new ones. 

>> Others are ambiguous. What does MF return for read on unopened
>> file? The page shows both 47 and 9-002. I wouldn't be surprised to find
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>'47') supplemented by vendor codes (and thus '9/002').

Good point, but which vendor is MF in 74 mode compatible with? Not IBM. CIS 74?
Maybe, I don't know. It appears to be compatible with 85 MF with the addition of
made up values such as 9-002. That's not useful to someone compiling an old 74
mainframe program. Since 9x codes aren't in the 74 standard, I would have used
IBM as the de-facto standard. 

>From the Fujitsu Language manual:
>------------------------------------------------------------------------
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>You don't need to 'ask for it'.

You (or the COBOL run-time) needs to provide a pointer to SQLMSG. Sounds like
Fujitsu is doing it for you, under the covers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T16:19:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307151519.111ee509@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <iSnQa.53566$0v4.3646220@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0307132223.360b90da@posting.google.com> <3f12a2b0.155623961@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307141158.4551b4e1@posting.google.com> <3f141e78.91124348@news.optonline.com>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> Good point, but which vendor is MF in 74 mode compatible with? Not IBM.
> CIS 74?
> Maybe, I don't know. It appears to be compatible with 85 MF with the
> addition of
> made up values such as 9-002. 

The MF ANS'74 error codes are those that were used in the MF ANS'74
Cobol systems which predated ANS'85.  These were CIS Cobol (1978) and
Level II Cobol (1983ish).

All '9x' codes are 'made up'.

> That's not useful to someone compiling an old 74
> mainframe program. 

It wasn't supposed to be, it _was_ useful to someone moving earlier MF
ANS'74 code from CIS Cobol and Level II Cobol to the ANS'85 MF Cobol/2
(and later to NetExpress).

> Since 9x codes aren't in the 74 standard, I would have used
> IBM as the de-facto standard. 

Why IBM ?  Why not one of the many other Cobol systems, such as RM,
Accu, Burroughs, etc.  In any case IBM 9x codes don't cater for things
that MF Level II needed, such as 'record locked'.

MF CIS Cobol and Level II Cobol were not designed for rehosting IBM
systems (which were primarily batch and CICS) but for developing
interactive multiuser small business systems using X/Open facilities. 
They were competing with RM, Accu, MS and Data General not mainframes.

> >You don't need to 'ask for it'.
> 
> You (or the COBOL run-time) needs to provide a pointer to SQLMSG. Sounds
> like Fujitsu is doing it for you, under the covers.

And MF, and the C based embedded SQL pre-processors that I have used.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T16:51:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf241u$120g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <iSnQa.53566$0v4.3646220@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0307132223.360b90da@posting.google.com> <3f12a2b0.155623961@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307141158.4551b4e1@posting.google.com> <3f141e78.91124348@news.optonline.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f141e78.91124348@news.optonline.com...

> Since 9x codes aren't in the 74 standard,  ...

Not strictly true.  The '74 standard defines 0x (successful completion), 1x
(at end), 2x (invalid key), 3x (permanent error), and 9x
(implementor-defined unsuccessful completion) codes.

ANSI X3.23-1974 page IV-2, 1.3.4.1 Status Key 1:  "9  -- Implementor
Defined.  The input-output statement was unsuccessfully executed as a result
of a condition that is specified by the implementor.  This value is used
only to indicate a condition not indicated by other defined values of status
key 1, or by specified combinations of the values of status key 1 and status
key 2."

The same wording appears also on page V-2 (in the paragraph with the same
number and name for relative I/O) and on page VI-2 (ditto for indexed I/O).
In all three cases paragraph 1.3.4.2 states "When status key 1 contains a
value of '9' indicating an implementor-defined condition, the value of
status key 2 is defined by the implementor."  (rule 3 for sequential and
relative I/O,  rule 4 for indexed I/O).

What I believe happened here is that implementors provided "9x" status codes
in '74-compliant COBOL for certain circumstances.  The '85 standard has a
rather wider set of status codes (and a whole new category -- "logic
error"), and the standard now mandates specific, platform-independent status
codes for some of the circumstances for which '74-compliant programs may
have previously produced "9x" implementor-defined status code values.

Note also that the standard requires that "9x" codes always indicate that
the requested I/O did not complete successfully.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T14:22:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<beuvu9$9n9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <3f107e3f.15201276@news.optonline.com> <zW0Qa.52086$3o3.3482807@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f1115a3.53962809@news.optonline.com> <besd24$veo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <iSnQa.53566$0v4.3646220@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <217e491a.0307132223.360b90da@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0307132223.360b90da@posting.google.com...
> "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote
>
<snip>
>
> > I was assuming that the Status Code values might be vendor/platform
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> ANS85(syntax).

Just to clarify, this was true UNTIL the 2002 COBOL Standard.  The 2002
Standard introduced "0x" *implementor* defined status codes for SUCCESSFUL
implementor defined situations.  Traditionally (and still in the 2002
Standard) 9x status codes were SUPPOSED to indicate UNSUCCESSFUL implementor
situations.  The new 0x ("x" is alphanumeric) codes are supposed to handle
things like the IBM "97" status where a successful OPEN was done - but with
a VSAM verify.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T16:59:25+00:00

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T23:52:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <cOCPa.50178$0v4.3392714@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3f0f665e.31748933@news.optonline.com> <beufcq$i0s$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-DF8FB7.00392715072003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
File Status "97" indicates a (semi-)successful open of a VSAM file - but
with a VERIFY.

For various 9x file status values (with current IBM OS/390-z/OS compilers)
see:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3lr10/6.1.8.9.1
```

<a id="t-56e3834650"></a>
## Thread: Byte manipulation. — 15 msgs

### Byte manipulation.

- **From:** jmoore207@hotmail.com (Jeff)
- **Date:** 2003-07-11T11:47:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d1a5c17a.0307111047.605d1b89@posting.google.com>`

```
I was wondering if anyone had an idea on a quick solution. Here is my
problem I have 2 diff files that both are cust-name and are entered by
users.

cust-name pic x(20).
cust-adr1 pic x(20).
cust-adr2 pic x(20).
cust-adr3 pic x(20).

One has a key Cust-id the other has a prop-id as its key which does
not match the cust-id. I am tring to find duplicates so I can delete
proposals that already have accounts in the Customer file. I read the
Proposal file get the name,adr etc and than read the Customer file to
see if they match. The problewm is the Prop-name field may say Joes
Apts. or Joe's Apt. or Joe's Apts but the customer file has Joe's
Apartments. So I have in some cases 4 proposals that are actually 1
account in the customer file. Does anyone have any ideas on how to
find all the possible duplicates? Below is an example.

Customer file: 
Cust-id 123456  
Cust-name Arbor Apts
Cust adr1 123 Main St.
Cust-Adr2 Atlanta, Ga 30339

Propsal file
Prop-id 09890
Prop-name Arbor Apts.
Prop-Adr1 123 Main St.
Prop-Adr2 Atlanta, Ga 30339

Prop-id 12323
Prop-name Arbor Apts.
Prop-Adr1 123 Main Street
Prop-Adr2 Atlanta, Ga 30339

As you can see there can be differences in 3 diff fields.
In my program I compared Cust-Name and Prop-Name then cust-adr1 and
prop-adr1
if they match I delete the proposal and I have found 1500 so far but I
suspect it is more like 8000. Any Ideas?
```

#### ↳ Re: Byte manipulation.

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-11T19:28:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ben355$2rd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<d1a5c17a.0307111047.605d1b89@posting.google.com>`

```

On 11-Jul-2003, jmoore207@hotmail.com (Jeff) wrote:

> One has a key Cust-id the other has a prop-id as its key which does
> not match the cust-id. I am tring to find duplicates so I can delete
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> find all the possible duplicates? Below is an example.

There are commercial programs you can buy to do this.  I worked in a company
that had trade-secret data cleansing to work on this problem - the solution was
in COBOL, but I'm not allowed to discuss it.   It took several programmers
several months in isolation to write it though.  No solution is perfect.
```

#### ↳ Re: Byte manipulation.

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-11T20:05:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c347e7$b78a0880$fb95f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<d1a5c17a.0307111047.605d1b89@posting.google.com>`

```
You could try some kind of Soundex algorithm. Of course you would have to
decide how to handle cases like "apt." vs "apartment". Convert the fields
to their soundex equivalent and see if they match.

You could sort the characters in the fields and then compare how many
characters in one field occur in the other. By experimenting you could
determine a percentage such that if that percentage of characters match
then you have a possible match.

Neither of these give you an exact solution, they give more of a "fuzzy
match".
```

#### ↳ Re: Byte manipulation.

- **From:** VinceD777@medscape.com (Vincent D'Amato)
- **Date:** 2003-07-11T14:25:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5b082d9d.0307111325.34f68c26@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d1a5c17a.0307111047.605d1b89@posting.google.com>`

```
The only solution would be to enumerate all the acceptable synonyms-
that 'St' is synonymous with 'Street', for instance. You can see this
from considering
the following example:


        (1)    Arbor Apts
               123 Main St.
               Atlanta, Ga. 30339

        (2)    Arbor House
               123 Main St.
               Atlanta, Ga. 30339


These could be the same ("Arbor House Apts"), or they could be
different.  Eg, Arbor House could be a restaurant on the ground floor
of the apartment building.
```

#### ↳ Re: Byte manipulation.

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T02:21:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f0f6b39.32992386@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<d1a5c17a.0307111047.605d1b89@posting.google.com>`

```
Copy both flat files to your desktop (unless you have direct access to them via
SAMBA or such). 

Write duplicate schemas (ascii text files) describing them to the Microsoft ODBC
driver for flat files. 

Using msquery (came with Excel), ACCESS or similar, write SQL to compare them
with something like: WHERE proposal.name not like customer.name(1:5) & '%'. 
Both flat files must be in the same directory, which is what you CONNECT to.
Table names are the schema names. 

There is a learning curve. Once you know how, you can do a comparison like this
in 5-10 minutes. . 

--- history below ----

```

#### ↳ Re: Byte manipulation.

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T20:22:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f0fc5e8_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<d1a5c17a.0307111047.605d1b89@posting.google.com>`

```
Jeff,

You have had some good suggestions.

I have a "fuzzy logic" de-duplicator which I wrote in COBOL years ago, and I
also have a Soundex routine, written at the same time. Both of these could
be used online as addresses were entered, or in batch against existing
files. These were used for Australian addresses but could be easily modified
to suit any Western Country.

However, having thought it over, I have decided not to make them free.
<sorry!>

(The first time this software was run by the client I developed it for, it
paid for itself several times over, just on saved postage. The fact that it
could also produce address labels pre-sorted for the Post Office gave
further savings. It is valuable code.)

 If you are interested in getting the Source code contact me privately, but
I will charge you for it.

If you decide to develop it yourself, I'll gladly help you. (without
charge).

Pete.
```
