##### ↳ ↳ Re: Byte manipulation.

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T11:49:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CBSPa.16136$BM.5256249@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<d1a5c17a.0307111047.605d1b89@posting.google.com> <3f0fc5e8_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f0fc5e8_6@news.athenanews.com...
> I have a "fuzzy logic" de-duplicator which I wrote in COBOL years ago, and
I
> also have a Soundex routine, written at the same time. Both of these could
> be used online as addresses were entered, or in batch against existing
> files. These were used for Australian addresses but could be easily
modified
> to suit any Western Country.
>
> However, having thought it over, I have decided not to make them free.
> <sorry!>


And a good decision, IMO.

Algorithms used by list brokers to eliminate duplicates (and other
'undesireables') are highly proprietary, as the per-name price the broker
may expect is dependent on the quality of the list.

While many in the technical community place direct marketers somewhere
between a rock and the snail under the rock, I'm sure there's nothing any of
us hold in such reduced esteem as receiving multiple emails from the same
former Nigerian official who desparetly needs our help in moving funds
embezzled from the former government and is willing to pay a handsome
commission if only we can supply a bank account number.

If only that former official had used a quality list with duplicates
carefully removed!

FWIW, I wrote an article on 'merge-purge' programming techniques which was
published in the January 2001 issue of "BASICally Speaking" magazine. While
the programming references use the BASIC language, the 'big picture'
strategy is applicable to any language.

The code from that article is public domain and may be downloaded from the
publishers web site at http://www.infoms.com/bsp/list9-04.zip

The text of the article may be obtained by purchasing a reprint of the
article from Information Management Systems ($4.00 US). You can start here
if interested: http://www.infoms.com/backiss.htm
```

```
A lot depends on whether this is a one of or will be done often.

There are commercial packages that do this kind of address list compare.

You could write an address parser that tokenizes the address, converting
strings into standard abbreviations and compare the parse results.
```

```
Jack,
The read at end is the IO and test.
EG
1000-read file.
     Read mastin into work-area at end
         perform 300-end-routines
         perform 400-close-files
         stop run.
Just my own 2 cents.
Bob fixit4you@attbi.com
```

<a id="t-a226205c09"></a>
## Thread: Call by Reference/Content vs. Using by Reference/Value — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Call by Reference/Content vs. Using by Reference/Value

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T11:01:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vh0c7scbi9ka8d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Good morning (afternoon, evening, etc.)...

I'm working on "componentizing" our mammoth date/time copy proc, and 
I've got a question the manual doesn't answer.  In the Procedure 
Division header, if I do something like...

Procedure Division
     Using by Value     LS-Input-Date   LS-Input-Offset-Block
           by Reference LS-Output-Date  LS-Reject-Code
     Entry P315-Offset

...then, in the calling program, I would say...

Call "P315-Offset"
   Using My-Input-Date   My-Input-Offset-Block
         My-Output-Date  My-Reject-Code
on Exception
     Move 7000 to My-Reject-Code
End-Call

...  I would expect that the runtime would pass the parameters the way 
the subprogram is expecting them.  Or is it?  Would I need to change the 
call to say something like...

Call "P315-Offset"
   Using by Content   My-Input-Date   My-Input-Offset-Block
         by Reference My-Output-Date  My-Reject-Code
on Exception
     Move 7000 to My-Reject-Code
End-Call

?  If that's the case, then it begs the question "Well, what happens if 
I pass a parameter by reference that the subroutine is expecting to be 
passed by content/value?"  Just curious...  I've sort of stuck my neck 
out and said I can do this (correctly and quickly), so I'm not wanting 
to be proven wrong.  :)  Thanks...
```

#### ↳ Re: Call by Reference/Content vs. Using by Reference/Value

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T16:48:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030712124846.23548.00000307@mb-m22.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<vh0c7scbi9ka8d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i  writes ...

>Good morning (afternoon, evening, etc.)...
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Entry P315-Offset

When you pass by value, the compiler builds [a copy of] the data in the
argument list; only floating point (comp-1 and comp-1), binary (comp or binary)
or single-byte literal or data item may be passed by value.

When you pass by reference [the default], the address of the data item is
passed in the argument list, and it may point to any type of data (elementary
or group)

When you pass by content, the address of a copy of the data item is passed; it
looks to the subroutine the same as passing by reference, but if the subroutine
changes the data passed to it, that change is not reflected back in the caller.



>
>...then, in the calling program, I would say...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

A subroutine may have "by reference" (the default) or "by value" for all or any
of the parameters it expects. For items passed by value, [a copy of] the data
is passed in the parameter list, for items passed by reference, the address of
the data [or address of a copy of the data - the subroutine cannot tell the
difference] is passed. Only certain types of data may be passed by value in
COBOL. (see above).

The runtime plays no role in this whatsoever. The issues are all decided at
compile time.



> Call "P315-Offset"
>   Using by Content   My-Input-Date   My-Input-Offset-Block
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>to be proven wrong.  :)  Thanks...

So, if you pass by reference and the subroutine expects it to be passed by
value, the subroutine will treat the data it finds there as data, not as the
address that it really is; a likely abend of type S0C4, S0C7, or possibly some
others, will almost certainly eventually occur.

<ad>
These issues, and many more, are covered in our course "Inter-Language
Communication in OS/390". We cover calling styles and conventions in four
languages (COBOL, Assembler, PL/I, and C) as well has a ton of other useful and
interesting stuff.

Course description available at:
http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220descr.htm

Course objectives at:
http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220j.htm

Detailed topical outline at:
http://www.trainersfriend.com/m220o.htm
</ad>

Kind regards,



-Steve Comstock
800-993-9716
303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<vh0c7scbi9ka8d@corp.supernews.com> <20030712124846.23548.00000307@mb-m22.aol.com>`


## Thread: EOF Technique — 1 msgs

### Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** Paul Robinson <postmaster@paul.washington.dc.us>
- **Date:** 2003-07-12T18:26:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F10526A.2256BB9B@paul.washington.dc.us>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com>`

```
Jack Straw wrote:

> I'm an experienced programmer, trying to get up to speed on Cobol.
> I've read the source to a few programs from various places, and I have
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> looking at the file status after the read.

1.  Because the method you describe above was the most common way taught
in most programming books.
2.  Testing file status is system dependent, and not something which can
be readily done in a programming book where the writer wants to be
non-system-dependent.

At least it's somewhat better than the way it used to be done:

1000-read file.
    read TV-GUIDE into ARMCHAIR
        at end go to bathroom
   end-read
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Microsoft 4.5 and Win200 Pro

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T17:20:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<beterj$e49$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<ber89n$3s2$1@newsread.albacom.net> <rFbQa.19$eP6.18805@news20.bellglobal.com> <berq1p$blb$1@newsread.albacom.net> <bfdfc3e8.0307131303.77aa5c11@posting.google.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:

> You don't do your customers any favors using a discontinued development
> tool.

MS Cobol 4.5 is a stable, relatively error free version of '85.  If it 
doesn't have any support issues then why would one need support ?
 
> Time to upgrade.

>> ... for any customers with old computers.

Assuming that the 'old computer' is running, MS-DOS or Win95/98:

You are suggesting that he obtain a recent compiler.  This would probably 
require a more recent version of Windows to run the programs on - XP is the 
only one available, this would require a new computer as it is likely that 
an old computer has inadequate CPU, RAM and HD space.

So, after paying out several thousand dollars, exactly what benefits would 
arise for the customer ?

Perhaps it will spend 99.95% of the time waiting for keystrokes rather than 
the current 97%. 

Businesses are not there to be revenue sources for computer companies. 
Computers are for being tools.  If there is a _business_case_ for upgrading 
then make it.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<ber89n$3s2$1@newsread.albacom.net> <rFbQa.19$eP6.18805@news20.bellglobal.com> <berq1p$blb$1@newsread.albacom.net> <bfdfc3e8.0307131303.77aa5c11@posting.google.com> <beterj$e49$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <bfdfc3e8.0307141406.5b14a52d@posting.google.com>`

```
thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote 

> Since he stated he was running on Windows 2000 Professional, I stand
> by my advice to acquire a modern and supported compiler.

There is a difference between where he is wanting to run the compiler
in order to compile versions of his programs, and the target machines
which are "for any customers with old computers".

> Were he still on DOS with this customer - of course you would be
> correct, but he's not.

We don't know what his customers are running on their old computers,
it may be DOS. It is not necessary to compile on the same machine as
the program is run on, in fact licence agreements for the MS Cobol
compiler will preclude installing it on customer machines.
```

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:beterj$e49$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> require a more recent version of Windows to run the programs on - XP is
the
> only one available, this would require a new computer as it is likely that
> an old computer has inadequate CPU, RAM and HD space.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Perhaps it will spend 99.95% of the time waiting for keystrokes rather
than
> the current 97%.
>
> Businesses are not there to be revenue sources for computer companies.
> Computers are for being tools.  If there is a _business_case_ for
upgrading
> then make it.
>

    The major business case for replacing something that works, i.e. MS-DOS
and
a 16 bit compiler, such as MS Cobol 4.5 or Microfocus ver 3.4 (workbench -
no relation
to net express) is Sales.

    Given a choice between a simple, fast, and reliable program on MS-DOS,
and a slow,
less than reliable program on windows, with a 32 bit compiler, buyers will
choose the
2cd choice almost every time.

    Never mind the fact that many a windows pc might need to be reformatted
and have windows
reinstalled every year or so, while a DOS pc can continue to run until the
government depreciation
runs out.  Or beyond.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Microsoft 4.5 and Win200 Pro

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T17:04:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f115d5e.72328442@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<ber89n$3s2$1@newsread.albacom.net>`

```
"Mauro" <maspa31@hotmail.com> wrote:

>I have any package write in Microsoft Cobol 4.5 and now I have a computer
>with O.S. Win2000 Pro.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sombody can help me?

The problem is with COBDIR. Does it specify a full path, not just "SET
COBDIR=."?

It sounds like you are running under CMD.EXE. This is a 32-bit app, therefore
does not use AUTOEXEC.NT. 

The simplest solution is to create a shortcut using COMMAND.COM, which is a
16-bit app that does use AUTOEXEC.NT. The command, of course, is "COBRUN
yourprog". 

Alternatively, you could use the PIF editor to modify your PIF to specify
Options = \winnt\system32\AUTOEXEC.NT. 

Another alternative, start regedit, go to
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Environment,
add an entry for COBDIR.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Microsoft 4.5 and Win200 Pro

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T07:51:18+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<besdg3$vm8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<ber89n$3s2$1@newsread.albacom.net>`

```
Mauro wrote:

> Sorry for my bad english
> I have any package write in Microsoft Cobol 4.5 and now I have a computer
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sombody can help me?

The _actual_ problem is that MS Cobol 4.5 can compile and run programs for 
MS-DOS, Windows 3.x and OS/2 2.x.  Windows NT and 2000 (but not XP) has an 
OS/2 'personality'.

When you try to run a program the Cobol notices that there are several 
environments available and tries to use the 'best' one - OS/2 in protected 
mode.  This, however requires a different setup and run-time from DOS.

The best way to fix it is to use FORCEDOS command to ensure that only the 
DOS environment is used:

   FORCEDOS COBOL ......
   
As Windows98 does not have the OS/2 'personality' the compiler works in DOS 
without complaint.
```

## Thread: OT?: Book tout, Faster Than The Speed Of Light, Jo���o Magueijo — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### OT?: Book tout, Faster Than The Speed Of Light, Jo���o Magueijo

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-13T22:46:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f11da26.104277492@news.optonline.com>`

```
The book is about two things: 

.. Cutting edge theoretical physics (published 2003). Real science, not
half-baked ideas or new age. 

.. How the creative process works in physics, applicable to other endeavors such
as programming. How bureaucratic red tape, accountability and other aspects of
modern management stifle the activity management is charged with promoting. How
scientists are divided into cults:

"As with every cult, people who do not conform to the party line are ostracized
and persecuted. When a bright young string theorist wrote a paper providing
dangerous ammunition to the loop people, a stringy little bitch commented that
"if he writes another one like that he'll lose his string theory union card." A
mob mentality has developed, and being tagged "string" or "loop" opens or closes
doors accordingly. If you acquire the loop label, don't even think of applying
for a string job.

Great animosity, even visceral hatred, has developed between these factions. The
"final answers" provided by religious fanatics, with their singular panoply of
denominations, come to mind. The world would be so much better without religious
fundamentalism, whether it be of the scientific or nonscientific variety.
Sometimes I think the existence of these people provides the best proof that God
does not exist."

Another passage seemed as applicable to client/server and OO as it is to
science:

"Needless to say, if VSL turns out to be correct, these people will at once
sharply deny all their previous slurs and start working on it. They are
bandwagon passengers, those who play safe and lead an easy life, generously
rewarded by funding agencies and the scientific establishment. John Barrow once
remarked that any new idea goes through three stages in the eyes of the
scientific community. Stage 1: It's a pile of shit and we don't want to hear
about it. Stage 2: It's not wrong bit it certainly has no relevance whatsoever.
Stage 3: It's the greatest discovery ever made and we found it first."
```

## Thread: EOF Technique — 34 msgs

### Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T08:35:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com>`

```
In article <le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com>,
 Jack Straw <jackstraw@witchita> wrote:

> I'm an experienced programmer, trying to get up to speed on Cobol.
> I've read the source to a few programs from various places, and I have
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> The first technique seems very common.

Comment 1:  "Move 'y' to ws-eof-switch" is a very old style.  You can 
now use the cleaner and less error prone conditional known as the 
88-level.  I have no idea why people will not let this die...

Comment 2:  There are somewhere between 20 and 100 file status codes.  
The vast majority of times you really only want to open the file, read 
to eof, close the file.  Why check for things you otherwise do not want 
to handle.  Let the system trap those.  On keyed reads you also get the 
'invalid key' exception handler to make it easier (?IBM extension?).

To further complicate things, many status codes are not relevant to 
certain types of files / open modes / read modes.  You could do lots of 
checking for everything, but 99.9999999% of the time a flat, sequential 
file is going to get a '10' (EOF) and nothing else.  The 0.00000001% 
that you do get something else will likely be in testing when you have 
something set up not quite right.

Comment 3:  Some environments (z/OS & LE) will provide some very nice 
diagnostic messages if you skip the FILE STATUS clause and allow the 
system to handle your errors.
```

```
And, I understand you are referring to Sequential (QSAM) files, not VSAM.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <don.leahy@nospamwhatever.leacom.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T10:46:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<enzQa.379$r34.3117@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <SCyQa.53944$3o3.3642767@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:SCyQa.53944$3o3.3642767@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> Try this, leave out the DD statement for an output file, and run your
> program.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>
>
Check the value of your shop's CBLQDA setting.  If CBLQDA(ON) is in effect,
a missing or misspelled DD statement causes Z/OS to dynamically allocate a
file on your behalf.  At the end of the job step, it deletes the file.
Helpful, eh?  I have seen programmers spend hours, sometimes days, searching
for non-existent logic errors in their code because of this 'feature'.
FWIW, IBM recommends CBLQDA(OFF) for Z/OS systems, because then you get the
system error message you expect when this situation occurs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ CBLQDA, IBM, ANSI, etc (was: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T14:32:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bev0ha$vej$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <SCyQa.53944$3o3.3642767@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <enzQa.379$r34.3117@news20.bellglobal.com> <RUAQa.54068$3o3.3653267@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
A little information on the "dynamic allocation" *requirement* of the '85
ANSI Standard (and what IBM did with QBLQDA).

The '85 ANSI Standard introduced a REQUIREMENT for the COBOL system to
*create* an OUTPUT file - if one is not currently available - in some
(most?) cases.  Almost all vendors implemented this in a "useful" way.  Even
IBM's VM '85 Standard COBOL system did this in a reasonable way.

UNFORTUNATELY, for IBM's MVS, OS/390, and z/OS systems - rather than
creating a CATALOGED dataset (using some option - with a default such as the
first 7-characters of the job name - to determine the high-level qualifier
*and* using SMS to determine space) IBM created a temporary UNCATALOGED
dataset.  In a multi-step JCL job, this is somewhere between useless and
dangerous. With VS COBOL II, there was little one could do about this.

When users started SCREAMING about this (before IBM introduced the CBLQDA
compiler option), IBM decide NOT to fix the fact that such datasets were
"unfindable" and instead introduced a non-Standard, non-portable solution
and allow (even recommend) customers to run with QBLQDA(OFF) to "detect"
missing DDNames when compared to Select/Assign statements.

P.S.  If you want to read "obscure" logic, try determining exactly how IBM
has defined "available file" for VSAM files!!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CBLQDA, IBM, ANSI, etc (was: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <don.leahy@nospamwhatever.leacom.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T16:22:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aiEQa.1187$ym.292621@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <SCyQa.53944$3o3.3642767@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <enzQa.379$r34.3117@news20.bellglobal.com> <RUAQa.54068$3o3.3653267@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bev0ha$vej$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks for the history Bill.

Believe it or not, I was just recently able to convince my local sysprogs to
change it to OFF.  Their first reaction was that there had been no change,
that the system had always behaved that way, and OFF was the default.  Every
Cobol programmer in the shop (well, the 10% who care about such things
anyway) *knew* that this wasn't the case.

The story I heard was that IBM originally delivered CBLQDA(ON) as the
default, with a recommendation to turn it OFF.  My local sysprogs accepted
the recommendation and set it to OFF.  A few years later, IBM changed the
default to 'OFF'.  The local sysprogs knew that in the past they didn't use
the default value, so, by reflex, they switched it to 'ON'.  And for a long
time no one complained.

I was finally approached by a desparate colleague who had been banging his
head against the wall for two days, trying to figure out why, no matter what
he did, his output file remained empty.  He could see the EXCP counts, so he
knew data was being written *somewhere*.  It turned out he had a misspelled
ddname, and Z/OS was writing to output to an uncataloged temporary file.


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bev0ha$vej$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net...
> A little information on the "dynamic allocation" *requirement* of the '85
> ANSI Standard (and what IBM did with QBLQDA).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> (most?) cases.  Almost all vendors implemented this in a "useful" way.
Even
> IBM's VM '85 Standard COBOL system did this in a reasonable way.
>
> UNFORTUNATELY, for IBM's MVS, OS/390, and z/OS systems - rather than
> creating a CATALOGED dataset (using some option - with a default such as
the
> first 7-characters of the job name - to determine the high-level qualifier
> *and* using SMS to determine space) IBM created a temporary UNCATALOGED
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> > | > Try this, leave out the DD statement for an output file, and run
your
> > | > program.
> > | > I got quite a surprise once when the system created a temp file for
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

allocate
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CBLQDA, IBM, ANSI, etc (was: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T00:52:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-B5DDF3.00521615072003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <SCyQa.53944$3o3.3642767@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <enzQa.379$r34.3117@news20.bellglobal.com> <RUAQa.54068$3o3.3653267@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bev0ha$vej$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <aiEQa.1187$ym.292621@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
That is a different take.  I was not happy when it was off.  I prefer 
CBLQDA(ON) so that SYS(PRINT|PUNCH|OUT) are automatic and do not need to 
be explicitly coded.

What I really would love to see is a CBLQIN(ON) option that would allow 
SYSIN to be accepted regardless of its existance.  Thus one could 
specify parms or not and have it function as a SELECT/OPTIONAL file.

In article <aiEQa.1187$ym.292621@news20.bellglobal.com>,
 "Don Leahy" <don.leahy@nospamwhatever.leacom.ca> wrote:

> Thanks for the history Bill.
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
I've never actually been bitten by this, but I see two problems with 
your answer.  

The first, creating an HLQ with the first 7 bytes of the jobname might, 
well, they might not line up with a valid catalog and/or ACF rule to 
allow creation.

The second, can't you use JCL referbacks to reference the uncataloged 
datasets?  I've not been bitten, so I'm not sure of the details, but if 
you have a dd in stepA that builds a nameless dataset, can you not reuse 
that in another step via *.steA.nmlsdsn?

In article <bev0ha$vej$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>,
 "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> A little information on the "dynamic allocation" *requirement* of the '85
> ANSI Standard (and what IBM did with QBLQDA).
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> has defined "available file" for VSAM files!!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CBLQDA, IBM, ANSI, etc (was: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <don.leahy@nospamwhatever.leacom.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T08:54:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SQSQa.429$r34.6996@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <SCyQa.53944$3o3.3642767@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <enzQa.379$r34.3117@news20.bellglobal.com> <RUAQa.54068$3o3.3653267@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bev0ha$vej$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <joe_zitzelberger-3EFC74.00470715072003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-3EFC74.00470715072003@corp.supernews.com...
> I've never actually been bitten by this, but I see two problems with
> your answer.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If it is NEW,PASS you can.  In this case the temporary file has a
disposition of NEW,DELETE,DELETE, so you cannot reference it after the step
is completed.

This bizarre behaviour *is* documented:
z/OS consideration--You should use CBLQDA(OFF) under z/OS, because this
prevents a temporary data set from being created in case there is a
misspelling in your JCL. If you specify CBLQDA(ON) and have a misspelling in
your JCL, Language Environment creates a temporary file, writes to it, and
then z/OS deletes it. You receive a return code of 0, but no output.


From CEEA3110 z/OS V1R2.0 Language Environment Programming Reference
```

#### ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T14:48:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On 14-Jul-2003, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

> Comment 1:  "Move 'y' to ws-eof-switch" is a very old style.  You can
> now use the cleaner and less error prone conditional known as the
> 88-level.  I have no idea why people will not let this die...

Partially because we can't yet SET SW-EOF-SWITCH to FALSE, in many environments.

While I prefer using 88 level here, I am unconvinced that this is cleaner and
less error prone than the older alternative.   It is a style I like though.  
But when I am looking for references for a particular switch, I need to catch
all of its 88 levels as well as the switch itself.   That's more work, and might
be missed by a maintenance programmer.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T16:37:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h%AQa.54074$3o3.3655024@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
|
| On 14-Jul-2003, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| Partially because we can't yet SET SW-EOF-SWITCH to FALSE, in many
environments.

If I need it, I include an 88 to reset the switch.

|
| While I prefer using 88 level here, I am unconvinced that this is cleaner
and
| less error prone than the older alternative.   It is a style I like
though.
| But when I am looking for references for a particular switch, I need to
catch
| all of its 88 levels as well as the switch itself.   That's more work, and
might
| be missed by a maintenance programmer.

You can specify the switch without a name, and only 88 levels.
This forces use of the 88 levels (until someone names it and moves to it).

i.e.
05                            PIC X         VALUE LOW-VALUE.
     88  sw-color-is-red                 VALUE   'R'.
     88  sw-color-is-blue                VALUE   'B'.
     88  sw-color-is-unknown         VALUE LOW-VALUE.
```

```
>      88  sw-color-is-unknown         VALUE LOW-VALUE.

I didn't know this, I'll try it.   But it still means more variables to check.

better.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T16:59:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f12e04e.9661601@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 14-Jul-2003, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Partially because we can't yet SET SW-EOF-SWITCH to FALSE, in many
environments.
>
>While I prefer using 88 level here, I am unconvinced that this is cleaner and
>less error prone than the older alternative.   It is a style I like though.  
>But when I am looking for references for a particular switch, I need to catch
>all of its 88 levels as well as the switch itself.   That's more work, and
might
>be missed by a maintenance programmer.

I don't name switches, removing the temptation to reference them directly.
(Another reason to avoid initialize) There is no reason to reference them both
ways.

If you can't say FALSE, use the exact same name prefixed by not-.

 05     pic x.
     88  end-of-file  value 'e'.
     88  not-end-of-file value low-value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T19:02:59-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AGGQa.1948$eP6.304360@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f12e04e.9661601@news.optonline.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f12e04e.9661601@news.optonline.com...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >While I prefer using 88 level here, I am unconvinced that this is cleaner
and
> >less error prone than the older alternative.   It is a style I like
though.
> >But when I am looking for references for a particular switch, I need to
catch
> >all of its 88 levels as well as the switch itself.   That's more work,
and
> might
> >be missed by a maintenance programmer.
>
> I don't name switches, removing the temptation to reference them directly.
> (Another reason to avoid initialize) There is no reason to reference them
both
> ways.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am currently working on a system written in exactly this manner, and I am
adhering to the style. It does have certain advantages.  However, for
maintenance, it also has a couple of huge disadvantages.

The biggest disadvantage, to my mind, is that you cannot easily see what a
value *is* when single stepping the code. For example, I have code that says

        if end-of-file
            blah
        if not-end-of-file
            blah-blah.

Now, I get to that point, and my debugger tells me that *both* conditions
are false.  I know I have some weird file condition, and the status byte
probably tells me what it is.  The status byte, however, is completely
invisable ... it does not even have a Cobol data name. If I really want to
find out what the status byte is, I have to go back to source and stick a
display in, or at the very least a dataname reference that I can point to in
my debugger.

Looking at my code, I do not even really know if those conditions are using
the status byte.  They may be referencing some value set in the declarative
section.

There are problems with both styles.

Donald
```

```
look up the 88 level name and find the data name that it applies to, and
then set up a watch point for that parameter.  The point though, is that it
is awkward, and necessitates jumping back and forth in the code to see what
is going on.  There is an advantage in being able to reference specific
checkable data areas down at the procedure level.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <FZJQa.3221$104.313196@news20.bellglobal.com> <bf0rfq$506$1@panix1.panix.com> <8ZSQa.2392$eP6.417740@news20.bellglobal.com> <bf10k0$3mf$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bf10k0$3mf$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <8ZSQa.2392$eP6.417740@news20.bellglobal.com>,
> Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
> ><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message
news:bf0rfq$506$1@panix1.panix.com...
> >> In article <FZJQa.3221$104.313196@news20.bellglobal.com>,
> >> Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >Oh, there is always a way.  I can page back to my working storage
section,
> >> >look up the 88 level name and find the data name that it applies to,
and
> >> >then set up a watch point for that parameter.  The point though, is
that it
> >> >is awkward, and necessitates jumping back and forth in the code to see
what
> >> >is going on.  There is an advantage in being able to reference
specific
> >> >checkable data areas down at the procedure level.
> >>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yep.  Given:

    01                 PICTURE X VALUE "A".
        88 YES-ANSWER        VALUE "Y".
        88 NO-ANSWER          VALUE "N".

    and, somewhere in the code
        if yes-answer
            blah
        else
        if no-answer
            blahblah
        else
            display what-the-heck-is-it.
There is nothing to display.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T13:54:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf1f4e$4k7$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <8ZSQa.2392$eP6.417740@news20.bellglobal.com> <bf10k0$3mf$1@panix1.panix.com> <uMWQa.1696$ym.453757@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
In article <uMWQa.1696$ym.453757@news20.bellglobal.com>,
Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bf10k0$3mf$1@panix1.panix.com...
>> In article <8ZSQa.2392$eP6.417740@news20.bellglobal.com>,
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
>There is nothing to display.

Ahhhhh, I see... it is not that 'a DISPLAY cannot be done based on a 
condition-name', it is 'a DISPLAY of an un-named data-area cannot be 
done'.

Thanks for the clarification... my thought was along the lines of

if yes-answer
    perform a-rtn
else
if no-answer
    perform b-rtn

else
    display 'garbage in yes-answer/no-answer flag'.

... which, of course, does not display the contents of the field but tells 
you that something is big-time wrong.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T16:07:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1424be.92730433@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f12e04e.9661601@news.optonline.com> <AGGQa.1948$eP6.304360@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3f12e04e.9661601@news.optonline.com...
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>my debugger.

Can't you hover the mouse pointer over the (implied) filler to see the value?

The only way it can contain values other than the 88s is a group move, NOT
initialize, or default initialization.  I'd look for references to the group
name, especially moving spaces to it. To minimize initialization problems, I
always use low-values for false.  

>Looking at my code, I do not even really know if those conditions are using
>the status byte.  They may be referencing some value set in the declarative
>section.

Huh? Unqualified names must be unique.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T13:50:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NbXQa.2493$eP6.446779@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f12e04e.9661601@news.optonline.com> <AGGQa.1948$eP6.304360@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f1424be.92730433@news.optonline.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message >

> Can't you hover the mouse pointer over the (implied) filler to see the
value?
>
Possibly ... the real problem though is that I cannot see it at my
breakpoint, in the procedure division.  I have to search trhough working
storage, find the 88 level, see what parameter it is based on, and then
chack that value. At the procedure division level, I only have true/false
logic levels available.

> The only way it can contain values other than the 88s is a group move, NOT
> initialize, or default initialization.  I'd look for references to the
group
> name, especially moving spaces to it. To minimize initialization problems,
I
> always use low-values for false.
>

Not really ... it could have 7 or 8 other 88 levels assigned to it.  In
fact, all 256 states could have 88 levels associated them, and it could be
set to anything at all, even without a data name.  You have no way, looking
at the code, to know how exhaustive the list is.

> >Looking at my code, I do not even really know if those conditions are
using
> >the status byte.  They may be referencing some value set in the
declarative
> >section.
>
> Huh? Unqualified names must be unique.
>
yes, that is true, but it is not what I meant. Take the following code:

        open x.
        if not no-file
            continue
        else
            perform create-new-file.

I would presume, reading that, that the condition "no-file" was a condition
name based on a file status 29.  That may be completely misleading, though
... I could have a declaratives section that filters out sharing errors, and
sets a completely different flag for all but a "new" file. I cannot really
state for sure what parameter is being checked without going back to the
working storage section.

Don't get me wrong ... I use 88 levels quite a bit.  The point though, is
that they are not a panacea ... they can hide data at the debugging level
too, and that is not always a good thing.

Donald



>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T13:56:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf1175$oas$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f12e04e.9661601@news.optonline.com>`

```

On 14-Jul-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> If you can't say FALSE, use the exact same name prefixed by not-.
>
>  05     pic x.
>      88  end-of-file  value 'e'.
>      88  not-end-of-file value low-value.

One big advantage - future programmers won't move something into an unnamed
field.

On the other hand:
1.   I can't display the value while debugging.
2.   I wonder how this works with on-line debuggers (which I don't use) -
especially when "X" is in that value above.
3.   There still are two variable names to search for to find when that switch
changes.  (your names do help).


Is there any difference between an unnamed field and a FILLER?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Program "debugging" and "understanding" (was: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T12:07:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf1cd4$cs1$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f12e04e.9661601@news.optonline.com> <bf1175$oas$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <joe_zitzelberger-5B90E8.11573915072003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
For those working on the PC and who are interested in finding out where data
(under a variety of names - including filler) is modified within an
application, you might want to look at the REVOLVE product from Micro Focus.
See:

  http://microfocus.com/products/revolve/datasheet.asp

I certainly wouldn't purchase the product JUST to track "eof switches" - but
it does provide SIGNIFICANT "application understanding" facilities.  This
can be a real help to those who are "taking over" the maintenance of an
application that they didn't "build" originally themselves.

On the IBM mainframe, the "old" Viasoft products provided similar
capabilities. (I can't remember - off the top of my head - who bought
Viasoft, but I think the products are still available from another vendor.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T16:45:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f142b5c.94424897@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3f12e04e.9661601@news.optonline.com> <bf1175$oas$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 14-Jul-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>1.   I can't display the value while debugging.

Give it a name, temporarily. Use names beginning with 'debug-' so you can search
for and remove them when you're finished.

Put indicators into a group and display the group-name. If you use meaningful
values, rather than 'y' and 'n', it's easy to see which indicators are on.

>2.   I wonder how this works with on-line debuggers (which I don't use) -
>especially when "X" is in that value above.

The debuggers I've seen will display any data field, including filler. Group
names are not just blapped like a string, they are formatted with subordinate
names, including fillers, next to values. 

>3.   There still are two variable names to search for to find when that switch
>changes.  (your names do help).

That's the reason for the naming convention. If you don't mind verbosity, you
_could_ give the indicator a name and qualify 88 references with that name.
Perhaps this will answer your concern:

if end-of-file in file-x-indicator 

>Is there any difference between an unnamed field and a FILLER?

No.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EOF Technique

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T00:54:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-6D55D2.00542615072003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<le1sgv85u45941ifp1qh67vpqado4fhm0m@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-12BF17.08354114072003@corp.supernews.com> <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <beufrr$i42$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> On 14-Jul-2003, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> be missed by a maintenance programmer.

You can always set "NOT-EOF-SWITCH to TRUE"...I've seen plenty of people 
who moved 'X' or space or '0' or whatever to WS-EOF-SW and cause unusual 
results...more than enough to feel more comfortable with 88's regardless 
of truth or falseity.
```

```
Nope - it's been in every COBOL I've ever used.  :)
```

```
 CTRL+C and  CTRL+V  to Get  "Class Name"  , now i really forgot how can
 i get ( where can i find) Class name from an Objetc  in Power.

Carlos Lages


 ENVIRONMENT     DIVISION.
 DATA            DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 LINKAGE         SECTION.
 01  POW-PLISTITEM OBJECT REFERENCE [Class Name].
 PROCEDURE       DIVISION USING POW-PLISTITEM.

------------------This what  i am using ---------
 LINKAGE         SECTION.
 01  POW-PLISTITEM OBJECT REFERENCE POW-CLISTITEM.
 PROCEDURE       DIVISION USING POW-PLISTITEM.
```

## Thread: Problem w/MF COBOL and MQDISC — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Problem w/MF COBOL and MQDISC

- **From:** paul.davis@watkins.com (paul davis)
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T11:48:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83569b3d.0307140723.33cd71f@posting.google.com>`

```
We are getting the following error message in our AIX 4.3.3
environment when we run a Micro Focus v 2.2 COBOL program and do a
MQDISC (MQ v 5.3) in the program:

Execution error : file 'WMTEST6'
error code: 114, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
114     Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal 11)

We are compiling the program using Net Express v 3.1.11 and publishing
the executable to AIX.

Could you please cut and paste the following program and compile and
run it in your environment and see if you get the same error message?

Here is the test program I am using:

       PROGRAM-ID.               WMTEST6.
       AUTHOR.                   John Doe.
       INSTALLATION.             ABCDEF Co.
       DATE-WRITTEN.             July 14, 2003.
       DATE-COMPILED.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER.  IBM-AIX.

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

       DATA DIVISION.

      *================================================================*
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      *================================================================*

       01  MQAPI-HCONN                 PIC S9(09).
       01  MQAPI-COMPLETION-CODE       PIC S9(09).
       01  MQAPI-REASON-CODE           PIC S9(09).

      *================================================================*
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *================================================================*

           DISPLAY '*** BEFORE CONNECT'.

           CALL 'MQCONN' USING 'AQD2',
                               MQAPI-HCONN
                               MQAPI-COMPLETION-CODE
                               MQAPI-REASON-CODE.

           DISPLAY '*** AFTER CONNECT'.

           CALL 'MQDISC' USING MQAPI-HCONN,
                               MQAPI-COMPLETION-CODE,
                               MQAPI-REASON-CODE.

           DISPLAY '*** AFTER DISCONNECT'.


Thanks for your help.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem w/MF COBOL and MQDISC

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-14T20:29:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f13117e.22255693@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<83569b3d.0307140723.33cd71f@posting.google.com>`

```
paul.davis@watkins.com (paul davis) wrote:

>We are getting the following error message in our AIX 4.3.3
>environment when we run a Micro Focus v 2.2 COBOL program and do a
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks for your help.

My guess is the connection fails, then it gets a bad pointer when you try to
disconnect a connection that doesn't exist. Try displaying MQAPI-COMPLETION-CODE
after the connect.

Aren't the three variables supposed to be COMP? It will work ok the way you
wrote it, will use the first four bytes of the nine byte variables, but the
display will be meaningless.
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem w/MF COBOL and MQDISC

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T00:36:20-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-BB5DC8.00362015072003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<83569b3d.0307140723.33cd71f@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <83569b3d.0307140723.33cd71f@posting.google.com>,
 paul.davis@watkins.com (paul davis) wrote:

> We are getting the following error message in our AIX 4.3.3
> environment when we run a Micro Focus v 2.2 COBOL program and do a
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for your help.

I'm not an AIX guru, but I think MQ calls are standard across various 
platforms.  What is the 'AQD2' literal parm?

As much as I recall the first parm on a connect call was the HCONN.  
Perhaps you are warming your feet in your literal pool on that first 
call.

I freely admit that I might have the Qmgr out of order, but in that case 
a literal is totally inappropriate as it could be up to 64 bytes.  You 
might want to try:

   01 MQAPI-QMGR         PIC X(64) value 'AQD2'.

Maybe that will fix it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Problem w/MF COBOL and MQDISC

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T09:14:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf0cp6$8ud$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<83569b3d.0307140723.33cd71f@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-BB5DC8.00362015072003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I just checked the MQSeries docs, where they provide a COBOL example -- see
http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/epubs/html/csqzal09/csqzal09tfrm.htm . The
parameter block should be defined thus :

*    W02 - Data fields derived from the PARM field
 01  W02-MQM                     PIC X(48) VALUE SPACES.
*    W03 - MQM API fields
 01  W03-HCONN                   PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 01  W03-COMPCODE                PIC S9(9) BINARY.
 01  W03-REASON                  PIC S9(9) BINARY.

SimonT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem w/MF COBOL and MQDISC

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T16:07:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f141e2e.91049630@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<83569b3d.0307140723.33cd71f@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-BB5DC8.00362015072003@corp.supernews.com> <bf0cp6$8ud$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
"Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote:

>I just checked the MQSeries docs, where they provide a COBOL example -- see
>http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/epubs/html/csqzal09/csqzal09tfrm.htm . The
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 01  W03-REASON                  PIC S9(9) BINARY.

The first parameter to MQCONN is the queue name. If you're getting it from a
PARM, as in the textbook example, then it needs to be a named variable. If
you're hard-coding it, as Paul did, a literal is fine. The COBOL function will
take care of string termination with null; you don't have to pad it with spaces.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Problem w/MF COBOL and MQDISC

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **Message-ID:** `<b73e9a33.0307282036.13d0620d@posting.google.com>`

```
Was this problem ever resolved? We're getting the exact same problem
with an MQDISC call, but none of the suggested solutions are relevant
to us. We've just upgraded from MQ v5.2 to MQ v5.3 and the problem has
only reared its ugly head since the upgrade. All of our COBOL MQ
programs seem to be affected.
```

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>

```
In another thread the subjects of FILE STATUS, and error handling came up.

While I can remember having to recreate datasets because of DASD hardware
failures, especially on S/360, and S/370, it's been quite some time since
I've had to do so.

I can't recall a DASD hardware failure on an S/390 system.

I'm wondering if anyone else, on the mainframe, has had the same experience.
It's something I really didn't notice until the subject came up.
```

```
Ah yes, the 2311. I forgot about that one. I also remember at my 1st
job, an IBM contraption that looked like a JukeBox. It wound up in a
closet gathering dust.

Another was a Rube Golberg device that plucked 2 by 6-inch pieces of
mag tape from slots and wrapped them around a rotating cylinder and
read the data from them. Years later, they seemed to resurrect the
same idea to implement their Mass Storage product.

Regards, Jack.
```

```
Ah, yes, the 2321 Data Cell, Noodle Snatcher.
Bin/Bin/Cylinder/Cylinder/Head/Head.  I used to repair them for IBM in my
former life as an IBM CE. Has anyone heard of the seven "nevers." I can't
remember them, but I know they existed. If you did things in the wrong
order, you could destroy a large part of the machine.
```

<a id="t-b6c6fbaf6e"></a>
## Thread: OT: Science test — 22 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Science test

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T01:41:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f135575.39657131@news.optonline.com>`

```
When you add instant coffee to water boiled in a microwave, it foams.
When you do the same to water heated on top of the stove, there is no foam.
Why?

1.  In order for oxygen in water to change from liquid to gaseous state it must
contact a crystal. Pots are made of metal or ceramic (glass, porcelain), which
are both crystalline. Oxygen bubbles are formed only where water touches the
surface. Water heated by convection is circulating because the bottom is hotter
than the top. In short course, nearly all oxygen molecules will touch the
surface and some will become gaseous. Water heated in a microwave is not
circulating because all parts of the water are heated uniformly. Oxygen in the
center never touches the cup. When you add crystals such as instant coffee, the
oxygen touches a crystal for the first time and some of it becomes gaseous. 

2.  Heat merely indicates molecular movement. Various kinds of movement can be
imparted to water molecules. Convection heat makes he lattice vibrate, like
plucking the springs of an old-fashioned bed frame. Microwaves make the water
molecules rotate like tops at the frequency of microwaves -- 4 million rpm. When
you add coffee crystals to vibrating water, they simply dissolve. The air
trapped in them also dissolves in the water, which is no longer boiling at this
point and has a shortage of air.  When you add coffee crystals to water
molecules spinning like little power tools, the structure of the crystals is
ripped apart in less than a second. Gaseous air trapped in the coffee crystals
is released and floats to the surface causing foam.


3.  Coffee is acidic. There are many acids in coffee, of which the highest
volume is chlorogenic acid (3-caffeylquinic acid) with formula C16H18O9.
http://www.coffeeresearch.org/science/sourmain.htm
As we know, acids are electron recptors. It is not so common knowledge that the
water in a cup is a single molecule. The bond in water, called hydrogen bond, is
unique. It is looser than covalent and ionic bonds, where particles hold hands
tightly. It is more like a soup of oxygen atoms floating in a broth of hydrogen.
Bonds between the two are weak and constantly changing. Microwaves bombard the
water with electrons. Most pass thru but some are stopped by collision with
oxygen or the cup and stay behind. The water now has a surplus of electrons,
which temporarily reside in the outer (only) shell of hydrogen atoms.  When we
add chlorogenic acid, each molecule bonds to two of these unstable hydrogens,
thereby releaving them of the extra electron and making them normal. The
resulting C16H20O9 is unstable. It quickly releases one CO2, thereby converting
chlorogenic acid into carboxylic acid C15H20O7, a harmless substance found in
all foods containing DNA. Carboxylic acid tastes less acidic than chlorogenic
acid because it forms fewer acetate anions in solution. This explains why
microwave coffee tastes flatter. 
Foam is caused by the carbon dioxide released. 

4.  Boyle's Law says that when a gas is heated it becomes bigger. Water has air
bubbles in it. As we heat the water those bubbles become bigger. Some become big
enough to overcome the water's viscosity and float to the surface. Convection
heats both air and water, which explains why you see so many bubbles when water
is boiling. Microwaves do not heat air, only water. Thus the air bubbles are
cooler than the water, at least for awhile until they have a chance to absorb
heat from the surrounding water. Coffee crystals have sites that transfer heat
from the water to the air bubbles in less than a second. The rush of
suddenly-big-enough air bubbles causes the foam.

------------------
The above is original; I didn't copy any of it.

It is a good test because the seemingly obvious answer is incorrect. Looking up
an explanation will increase your chance of choosing the wrong answer. When it's
over I'll give a single very simple experiment that corroborates the wrongness
of three answers and the correctness of one.
```

```
the
> surface. Water heated by convection is circulating because the bottom is
hotter

> circulating because all parts of the water are heated uniformly. Oxygen in
the

coffee, the

gaseous.

can be

like

this

is
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Science test

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-16T15:43:55-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307161443.4c1a7c25@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3f135575.39657131@news.optonline.com> <bf3q32$tk8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
"Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote 

> My understanding of this fits none of these explanations perfectly by (1)
> is the closest.

They are all wrong.  In a microwave the water can become
'superheated'.  The temperature can rise above boiling point because
even heating does not create convection and there are no points where
steam can formate.  The air _dissolved_ in the water is not driven out
by the gas (steam) bubbles as it is when boiled.

When the coffee (or sugar or salt or inactive sand or just abouut
anything) is added the particles provide points where steam bubbles
form and this also drives out the dissolved gasses to create the foam.

Easy to prove - just measure the tempearture.  It rises to over 100
decgrees in the microwave when superheated, and drops back to 100
after 'boiling' removes the excess temperture into the latent heat of
steam.

This has caused some severe burns when the water has 'exploded' -
never heat pure water in a microwave.
```

```
Years ago there was a news story about a new kind of efficient light
source: a quartz capsule of sulfur which was blasted with microwaves. What
will happen if the above salt crystals are replaced with powdered sulfur?
Blasts of intense white light? I haven't tried it yet. [NOW I DID! No
brilliant light. Instead, the plasma forms and the sulfur reacts with air
to create a cloud of acrid gas. Hold nose, turn on the fans, and leave the
kitchen at a run!]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Science test

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T06:10:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f161f90.222496042@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3f135575.39657131@news.optonline.com> <bf3q32$tk8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <217e491a.0307161443.4c1a7c25@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>"Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>by the gas (steam) bubbles as it is when boiled.

Water heated for coffee is not superheated; it is at or just below boiling, so
the comment about superheating is irrelevant. Dissolved air will be gasified
("driven out") if it contacts steam bubbles or a rough surface. Lack of
circulation ("convection") leaves some air and water which had not contacted
either. You are correct: they cause the foam.

Two conditions are needed for phase transition from liquid to gas, technically
called nucleation: heat and a 'rough' surface with  bumps or pits at least a few
microns deep (technically, the criterion is shape rather than depth). Once the
first bubble is formed, rising bubbles are themselves nucleating sites which
enable the formation of more bubbles. 

When you heat water in an old metal pan with scratches, have you noticed bubbles
forming first along the scratches? That's because scratches are rougher than the
rest of the surface. Roughness causes irregular heat distribution.

>When the coffee (or sugar or salt or inactive sand or just about
>anything) is added the particles provide points where steam bubbles
>form and this also drives out the dissolved gasses to create the foam.

The addition of anything _rough_. A smooth glass rod will not do it. A polished
metal spoon does it poorly. I said "crystal" to make it sound New Age. I wasn't
'wrong' because coffee, sugar and salt crystals are rough, as are the inside of
all coffee cups and most glasses. (FWIW, drinking hot coffee from a glass is
called 'Russian style'). 

You can stop coffee and cocoa from foaming by first stirring the water with a
wooden spoon, a pencil, anything with a rough surface. 

>This has caused some severe burns when the water has 'exploded' -
>never heat pure water in a microwave.

The first statement is true. Putting anything into superheated water starts a
nucleation chain reaction. The first bubble becomes a nucleating site for more
bubbles. The whole cup of water will vaporize in less than a second. 

The second statement is overly cautious. In order for this to happen, one
condition is necessary and the other is a practical necessity. The interior of
the glass must be free of rough spots.  If a single bubble forms, it will
trigger others. The practical necessity is lack of dissolved air and other
gasses such as chlorine. If any gas bubbles form, they will trigger steam
bubbles. I said practical necessity because gasses such as air nucleate more
easily than water, thus a surface feature not rough enough gasify water could
gasify air.

To create superheated water, put distilled water in a high quality crystal
glass, boil it once to expel dissolved gasses, stir gently with a wooden spoon
to insure they have gasified, put a very thin coat of oil on top to prevent
evaporation, then set the microwave to 1.5x normal boiling time. If you don't
see bubbles by the end of that time, you have a glass of superheated water. When
you set it off by adding a grain of anything, superheated steam will hit the
ceiling. Needless to say, you don't want your hand or face in the path.

Incidents of accidental explosions are reported a few dozen times per year in
the US. It's rare because nearly all containers have _some_ rough spots. All
inexpensive drinking glasses that I've tried did. You need laboratory-grade
polishing, which _might_ be found in a crystal glass. As a practical matter, you
also need to remove dissolved gasses by boiling the water twice. It's difficult
to create superheated water when you're trying; the chance of it happening by
accident is very low. If you're still jumpy, look for bubbles. Bubbles indicate
it's not superheated. 

The incorrect answers played on common misconceptions. There are no air bubbles
in water; if there were, they'd float to the surface. Microwaves don't make
water molecules rotate like tops. They start to rotate at 2.45G rps, then
quickly bang  into each other. There are no 'extra electrons' left in food. If
foaming were caused by coffee acid or air in coffee crystals, salt and sugar
would not foam.
```

```
The question was 'Why does coffee make foam?' It was not 'Why does a cup of
water explode in your face?'  nor 'Is it dangerous to microwave pure water?'
Kindly respond to the question posed rather than one to which you have a better
answer. 

Foaming usually occurs BELOW the boiling point of water. It isn't necessary nor
desirable to boil the water to make coffee or cocoa. If you do that, it's too
hot to drink right away. Many people have found the best temperature is around
150F. If you heat it to 180F or above, you might be sued for $2.7M. 

My source is 
http://www.lectlaw.com/files/cur78.htm

"coffee served at home is generally 135 to 140 degrees.
...

Other testimony
showed that as the temperature decreases toward 155 degrees, the extent
of the burn relative to that temperature decreases exponentially.  Thus,
if Liebeck's spill had involved coffee at 155 degrees, the liquid would
have cooled and given her time to avoid a serious burn."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Science test

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T00:02:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1889cf.380790196@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3f135575.39657131@news.optonline.com> <3f161f90.222496042@news.optonline.com> <bf668k$j99$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f16beea.263295445@news.optonline.com> <bf73k4$1if$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3f16beea.263295445@news.optonline.com>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>Water heated for coffee is not superheated; it is at or just below boiling,
so
>>>>the comment about superheating is irrelevant.
>>>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Kindly respond to the question posed rather than one to which you have a
better
>>answer. 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>not something which does not exist in the posting whatsoever.

I already did. In my response to Richard (clipped) I described the conditions
necessary to produce superheated water. The container must be completely free of
'rough spots' (microscopic, too small to feel by touch). Nearly all coffee cups
and the vast majority of drinking glasses have such rough spots, therefore are
incapable of exploding. Moreover, dissolved gas prevents superheating because it
provides sites for the formation of steam bubbles. The water would have to be
boiled twice, the first time to eliminate air. 

Notice they don't say how many reports were received. That's because the
probability of accidently superheating water once in a year is lower than the
chance of these hazards:

Pedestrian killed by vehicle         44,000:1
Dying in car crash                      47,000:1
Drowning                                  76,000:1
Plane crash                             250,000:1
Electrocuted                            550,000:1
Drowning in a bathtub               803,000:1
Exploding water                     1,000,000:1

I arrived at 1,000,000:1 by estimating that half the US adult population
microwaves a cup of water at least once a year. I divided by the number of
reported explosions times 10 to conservatively include unreported explosions. 

>To the question 'why does coffee make foam?' one can accurately answer 'it 
>makes foam because the water is not heated sufficiently to make it blow up 
>in your face.'

If the objective is accuracy rather than fear-mongering, one could say zones of
water contain dissolved air heated above the saturation point. When a rough
surface such as coffee contacts those zones, gasified air causes foaming.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Science test

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T15:31:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f196299.48797274@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3f135575.39657131@news.optonline.com> <bf668k$j99$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f16beea.263295445@news.optonline.com> <bf73k4$1if$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f1889cf.380790196@news.optonline.com> <bfa4uo$54c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>support your invented 'conclusion'.

My injury estimate was from a Web page saying 'A dozen cases of facial burns are
reported every year (in the US)'. When I went to look for the primary source I
realized it doesn't exist. There is no central burn registry in the US. The Web
page was referring to newspaper accounts, and someone's guess as to their
number.  A single incident reported in twelve papers would count as twelve
'reports'. On the other hand, incidence my be grossly under-reported. Doctor
visits are not routinely reported in the newspaper. 

I didn't make up the statistic but it was based on bad data. There is no way of
measuring the risk based on injury reports. 

I found this comment about frequency of occurrence:

"So, we know it's possible. But is it common? Not very, says Louis Bloomfield,
professor of physics at the University of Virginia. "Fortunately, serious
microwave superheating accidents are unusual � this is the first injury I've
ever heard about." This contradicts the email's claim that such injuries are a
"fairly common occurrence." A search of the medical literature on microwave
injuries also undermines that claim. There are references to trauma caused by
everything from overheated pizza to exploding eggs, but no mentions I could find
of serious injury due to surges or explosions of boiling water. "

http://urbanlegends.about.com/library/weekly/aa011900b.htm

I myself searched PubMed and NLM for references to superheated water, without
finding any. If "the doctor who was attending to him stated that this a fairly
common occurrence", per the Urban Myth chain letter, there would be references
in the medical literataure. How else would the doctor know?

This article describes a research lab's successful attempt to create an
'explosion'. The result would be better described as a gush or sudden overflow
which did not rise to face level. 

http://www.impi.org/ResourceLiterature/MWarticle4.html

Here is a movie of another, similar experiment. As you can see, foam rises no
more than one inch above the container.

http://csusap.csu.edu.au/~cspald01/superheating.mpeg

A Web search found dozens of quotations of the Urban Myth chain letter beginning
"My 26 year-old son...", but only two actual cases over a five year period. One
was the ABC News report (suspect, they have a history of alarmism), the other
from a fire chief in Gurnee/Naperville, Il. If explosions were truly common, one
would expect to see more than one unbiased report.

As for my numerator, it's easy to substantiate. Statistics show 88% of US
households have a microwave oven and 75-80% use it regularly. 

http://www.eia.doe.gov/emeu/recs/appliances/appliances.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Science test

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net>

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f161f90.222496042@news.optonline.com...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
> >"Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote
> >
> >> My understanding of this fits none of these explanations perfectly by
(1)
> >> is the closest.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Water heated for coffee is not superheated; it is at or just below
boiling, so
> the comment about superheating is irrelevant. Dissolved air will be
gasified
> ("driven out") if it contacts steam bubbles or a rough surface. Lack of
> circulation ("convection") leaves some air and water which had not
contacted
> either. You are correct: they cause the foam.
>
> Two conditions are needed for phase transition from liquid to gas,
technically
> called nucleation: heat and a 'rough' surface with  bumps or pits at least
a few
> microns deep (technically, the criterion is shape rather than depth). Once
the
> first bubble is formed, rising bubbles are themselves nucleating sites
which
> enable the formation of more bubbles.
>
> When you heat water in an old metal pan with scratches, have you noticed
bubbles
> forming first along the scratches? That's because scratches are rougher
than the
> rest of the surface. Roughness causes irregular heat distribution.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The addition of anything _rough_. A smooth glass rod will not do it. A
polished
> metal spoon does it poorly. I said "crystal" to make it sound New Age. I
wasn't
> 'wrong' because coffee, sugar and salt crystals are rough, as are the
inside of
> all coffee cups and most glasses. (FWIW, drinking hot coffee from a glass
is
> called 'Russian style').
>
> You can stop coffee and cocoa from foaming by first stirring the water
with a
> wooden spoon, a pencil, anything with a rough surface.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The first statement is true. Putting anything into superheated water
starts a
> nucleation chain reaction. The first bubble becomes a nucleating site for
more
> bubbles. The whole cup of water will vaporize in less than a second.

    If you consider the heat of vaporization as compared to the amount of
heat that it takes to raise the temperature of water one degree, it would be
impossible
for the water to vaporize.  The water could explode and scatter into tiny
droplets
all over the ceiling, but it would not vaporize instantly.

    The heat of vaporization is 540 times as large as the amount of heat
needed
to raise the water temp 1 degree.  With normal (non lab) equipment, you
would be
lucky to raise the water temp 20 degrees.

    I tried this once (without a thermometer).  I used an empty mayonnaise
jar, under the assumption that it would have few scratches (I use a spatula
to
remove mayo mostly).  I put clean, filtered water into it, and double boiled
it.

    It did foam vigorously when I added some sugar.  Nothing dangerous.

    But I will admit that this might be a problem with a new coffee mug.

    You really ought to keep track of how long it takes to heat a mug of
water.


>
> The second statement is overly cautious. In order for this to happen, one
> condition is necessary and the other is a practical necessity. The
interior of
> the glass must be free of rough spots.  If a single bubble forms, it will
> trigger others. The practical necessity is lack of dissolved air and other
> gasses such as chlorine. If any gas bubbles form, they will trigger steam
> bubbles. I said practical necessity because gasses such as air nucleate
more
> easily than water, thus a surface feature not rough enough gasify water
could
> gasify air.
>
> To create superheated water, put distilled water in a high quality crystal
> glass, boil it once to expel dissolved gasses, stir gently with a wooden
spoon
> to insure they have gasified, put a very thin coat of oil on top to
prevent
> evaporation, then set the microwave to 1.5x normal boiling time. If you
don't
> see bubbles by the end of that time, you have a glass of superheated
water. When
> you set it off by adding a grain of anything, superheated steam will hit
the
> ceiling. Needless to say, you don't want your hand or face in the path.
>
> Incidents of accidental explosions are reported a few dozen times per year
in
> the US. It's rare because nearly all containers have _some_ rough spots.
All
> inexpensive drinking glasses that I've tried did. You need
laboratory-grade
> polishing, which _might_ be found in a crystal glass. As a practical
matter, you
> also need to remove dissolved gasses by boiling the water twice. It's
difficult
> to create superheated water when you're trying; the chance of it happening
by
> accident is very low. If you're still jumpy, look for bubbles. Bubbles
indicate
> it's not superheated.

    I didn't have any crystal.  You could probably flame polish something.
I have no
idea how you would flame polish the inside of a glass container.  You can
buy distalled water
at the auto parts store.  Peak sells it for use in batteries, etc.


>
> The incorrect answers played on common misconceptions. There are no air
bubbles
> in water; if there were, they'd float to the surface. Microwaves don't
make
> water molecules rotate like tops. They start to rotate at 2.45G rps, then
> quickly bang  into each other. There are no 'extra electrons' left in
food. If
> foaming were caused by coffee acid or air in coffee crystals, salt and
sugar
> would not foam.
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Science test

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T20:35:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f24329a.241755327@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3f135575.39657131@news.optonline.com> <bf3q32$tk8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <217e491a.0307161443.4c1a7c25@posting.google.com> <3f161f90.222496042@news.optonline.com> <KrOcnepi1KGkzYGiXTWJiA@comcast.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3f161f90.222496042@news.optonline.com...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>lucky to raise the water temp 20 degrees.

Excellent point. 

The heat of vaporization of water is 40.7 kJ/mol.  A mol of water weighs 18g. In
order to get 90% of the water to vaporize, as described in The Urban Myth email,
you'd have to heat it to 742C. (The container would glow red at 600C.) Heating a
500cc (14 mol) glass of water to 742C would require 783kJ of energy. A 750 watt
microwave oven running at 100% efficiency would take 1,043 seconds or 17 minutes
to accomplish that. With practical inefficiencies, more like 25 minutes.

I don't recall them saying they microwaved the water for 25 minutes nor that the
cup was glowing red.
```

```
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> writes
>This has caused some severe burns when the water has 'exploded' -
>never heat pure water in a microwave.

I have seen examples of microwaved fluids exploding when metal objects 
(teaspoons) were put into the liquid. Also, I knew a man who microwaved 
an egg and, when chopping the top off with a teaspoon, it exploded into 
his face. He wears a beard to hide the scars.
```
