## Thread: Fujitsu's NETCOBOL: Any luck linking with ODBCCP32.LIB — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu's NETCOBOL: Any luck linking with ODBCCP32.LIB

- **From:** pvieira@emporsoft.pt (Paulo Vieira)
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T02:30:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5b8d7c7.0307150130.36e7bd61@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
For a few hours now, I've been trying to create a program in Fujitsu
Netcobol that automaticaly creates a datasource, using the ODBC API.
This is part of a project where the intended purpose is to automate
the instalation, so the user shouldn't be bored with tough questions.
After browsing thru MSDN, I decided that ODBCCP32.DDL was the module
to use, since it has built in all the functionality I need.
The problem:
In spite of having included ODBCCP32.LIB in my project, the call to
"SQLConfigDataSource" (which, by the way, has this function exported
as the "Dependency Walker" from Visual Studio, shows) gives a link
error: unresolveld external symbol SQLCONFIGDATASOURCE
And yes, I've the ALPHAL(WORD) compile directive.
Has any of you got any success doing this?
Is it there any caveats I should be aware of?

Here is a test program:

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.	TSTODBC.
 ENVIRONMENT     DIVISION.
 DATA            DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01		W_DATASOURCE	PIC X(64).
 01		W_SERVER	PIC X(64).
 01		W_NETWORK	PIC X(64).
 01		W_ADDRESS	PIC X(64).
 01		W_DATABASE	PIC X(64).
 01		W_LANGUAGE	PIC X(64).
 01		W_OEMTOANSI	PIC X(64).
 01		W_DESCRIPTION	PIC X(64).
 01		W_NULL		PIC X VALUE X"00".
 01		STR		PIC X(64).
 01		W_OPER		PIC S9(9) COMP-5 VALUE +1.
 01		W_DRIVER.
 	05	W_DRIVER_T	PIC X(10) VALUE "SQL Server".
 	05			PIC X	VALUE X"00".
 01		W_ATTR		PIC X(256).
 01		I		PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
 01		RETURNVALUE	PIC S9(9) COMP-5.

 PROCEDURE       DIVISION.
 	PERFORM GATHER_DATA
 	STRING
 		"DSN=" DELIMITED BY SIZE
 		W_DATASOURCE DELIMITED BY X"00"
 		X"00" DELIMITED BY SIZE
 		"DESCRIPTION=" DELIMITED BY SIZE
 		W_DESCRIPTION DELIMITED BY X"00"
 		X"00" DELIMITED BY SIZE
 		"SERVER=" DELIMITED BY SIZE
 		W_SERVER DELIMITED BY X"00"
 		X"00" DELIMITED BY SIZE
 		"DATABASE=" DELIMITED BY SIZE
 		W_DATABASE DELIMITED BY X"00"
 		X"00" DELIMITED BY SIZE
 		INTO W_ATTR
 	END-STRING
 		
 	CALL "SQLConfigDataSource" USING
 		W_NULL
 		W_OPER
 		W_DRIVER
 		W_ATTR
 		RETURNING RETURNVALUE
 	END-CALL
 	EXIT PROGRAM
 	.	
 GATHER_DATA.
 	MOVE "tst_created" TO STR
 	PERFORM TRUNC 
 	MOVE STR TO W_DATASOURCE

	MOVE "data source autmaticaly created" TO STR
	PERFORM TRUNC
	MOVE STR TO W_DESCRIPTION
		
	MOVE "XION-TEMP" TO STR
	PERFORM TRUNC
	MOVE STR TO W_SERVER

	MOVE "(default)" TO STR
	PERFORM TRUNC
	MOVE STR TO W_NETWORK

	MOVE "192.168.1.1" TO STR
	PERFORM TRUNC
	MOVE STR TO W_ADDRESS

	MOVE "TestDB" TO STR
	PERFORM TRUNC
	MOVE STR TO W_DATABASE

	MOVE X"00" TO STR
	PERFORM TRUNC
	MOVE STR TO W_LANGUAGE

	MOVE "NO" TO STR
	PERFORM TRUNC
	MOVE STR TO W_OEMTOANSI
	.
 TRUNC.
	PERFORM VARYING I FROM 64 BY -1 UNTIL I < 1 OR STR (I:1) NOT = " "
		CONTINUE
	END-PERFORM
	MOVE X"00" TO STR (I + 1 : 1)
	.
 END PROGRAM TSTODBC.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu's NETCOBOL: Any luck linking with ODBCCP32.LIB

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T11:08:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VgRQa.21189$BM.6000394@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<b5b8d7c7.0307150130.36e7bd61@posting.google.com>`

```
"Paulo Vieira" <pvieira@emporsoft.pt> wrote in message
news:b5b8d7c7.0307150130.36e7bd61@posting.google.com...
> After browsing thru MSDN, I decided that ODBCCP32.DDL was the module
> to use, since it has built in all the functionality I need.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> error: unresolveld external symbol SQLCONFIGDATASOURCE

That function is exported from  ODBCCP32.DLL (I assume your "ODBCCP32.DDL"
is a typing error) as "SQLConfigDataSource" in mixed case as shown. Your
missing external is in all upper-case as "SQLCONFIGDATASOURCE".  Import and
export names ARE case-sensitive.

The error message indicates the import table of the executable is using the
wrong case; so the problem is wherever the compiler/linker is getting its
import definition (the LIB file?).

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu's NETCOBOL: Any luck linking with ODBCCP32.LIB

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T13:34:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0307151234.149793c5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b5b8d7c7.0307150130.36e7bd61@posting.google.com>`

```
pvieira@emporsoft.pt (Paulo Vieira) wrote in message news:<b5b8d7c7.0307150130.36e7bd61@posting.google.com>...
> Hi,
> For a few hours now, I've been trying to create a program in Fujitsu
…[105 more quoted lines elided]…
>  END PROGRAM TSTODBC.

If you are not totally married to the ODBC approach, consider the
following...

I found the entire ODBC and Fujitsu "Environment Setup" approach to be
possible yet overly complex. In Chapter 11 of my book ("COBOL and
Visual Basic on .NET: A Guide for the Reformed Mainframe Programmer"),
I actually discourage this approach. A much simpler, more straight
forward and more ".NET" oriented way would be to do something like
this:

000010* Sample Code using ADO.NET Technology 
000020 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000030 PROGRAM-ID. MAIN.
000040 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000050 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000060 REPOSITORY.
000070* .NET Framework Classes
000080	   CLASS SqlConnection  AS
"System.Data.SqlClient.SqlConnection"
000090     CLASS SqlDataAdapter As
"System.Data.SqlClient.SqlDataAdapter"
000100     CLASS SqlCommand As "System.Data.SqlClient.SqlCommand"
000110     CLASS SqlDataReader As
"System.Data.SqlClient.SqlDataReader"
000120     CLASS DataSet    As "System.Data.DataSet"
<snip>
000250* .NET Framework Properties 
000260     PROPERTY PROP-ConnectionString AS "ConnectionString"
000270     PROPERTY PROP-Connection       AS "Connection"
000280     PROPERTY PROP-CommandText      AS "CommandText"
<snip>
000410 DATA DIVISION.
000420 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000430   77 mySqlConnection  OBJECT REFERENCE SqlConnection.
000440   77 mySqlDataAdapter OBJECT REFERENCE SqlDataAdapter.
000450   77 mySqlCommand     OBJECT REFERENCE SqlCommand.
000460   77 mySqlDataReader  OBJECT REFERENCE SqlDataReader.
000470   77 myDataSet        OBJECT REFERENCE DataSet.
<snip>
000650 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000660
<snip>
001380************************************************
001390   1000-UseSqlDataAdapter.
001400   
001410*  Reference Data Provider Objects
001420		INVOKE SqlConnection "NEW"  RETURNING  mySqlConnection 
001430      INVOKE SqlDataAdapter "NEW" RETURNING  mySqlDataAdapter 
001440      INVOKE SqlCommand "NEW"     RETURNING  mySqlCommand
001450      
001460*  Prepare to Connect to SQL Server Database
001470*  using Connection String
001480      SET PROP-ConnectionString OF mySqlConnection TO
001490      "user id=sa;pwd=;Database=northwind;Server=(LOCAL)"
001500		
001510*  Associate the Command Object with the Connection Object
001520      SET PROP-Connection OF mySqlCommand TO mySqlConnection    
001530*  Associate the Command Object with intended SQL Statement
001540      SET PROP-CommandText OF mySqlCommand TO "Select * from
Categories"
001550*  Associate the DataAdapter Object with the Command Object
001560      SET PROP-SelectCommand OF mySqlDataAdapter TO mySqlCommand
001570
<snip>

Refer to lines 1480 and 1490. There you could add whatever information
(i.e. IP address, Server Name, Database, etc.) as you would normally
have used when establishing your ODBC String. Using this "ADO.NET"
type approach you DO NOT need to set up a ODBC connection at all. This
ADO.NET way, results in you having a more dynamic connection.

Optionally, (as I demonstrate in Chapter 18 of my book) rather than
hard code the connection string information in your program, you could
make use of the .NET XML Config file. Then, you would need to add a
few lines of code similiar to the following:

<snip>
019211 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
019212 01 MyNewConnectionString  PIC X(100).
019213 01 MyNewAppSettings  OBJECT REFERENCE
CLASS-NAMEVALUECOLLECTION.
<snip>
19983     SET MyNewAppSettings TO PROP-APPSETTINGS 
019984         OF CLASS-CONFIGURATIONSETTINGS
019985     INVOKE MyNewAppSettings "get_Item" 
019986         USING BY VALUE "MyCOBOLConnectionString" 
019987         RETURNING MyNewConnectionString
019988     SET PROP-ConnectionString OF mySqlConnection 
019989         TO MyNewConnectionString
<snip> 


-----------------------
Using this non-ODBC String / ADO.NET approach, I would think that you
will have more flexibility - particularly when developing your setup
package and planning for multiple deployment scenarios.

-----------------------

I hope this was helpful. 

-----------------------

Marketing:

If you are interested in my book, please visit either of the following
3 links:

http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/1590590481/eclecticsoftw-20

http://www.eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com

http://apress.com/book/bookDisplay.html?bID=112

Alternate link page:
http://www.eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com/HTMLPage3.html
```

## Thread: Did Microsoft Just Patent The PDS? — 3 msgs

### Did Microsoft Just Patent The PDS?

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (theodp)
- **Date:** 2003-07-15T14:23:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e7946e7b.0307151323.b01809c@posting.google.com>`

```
From the 'Summary Of The Invention' for U.S. patent no. 6,594,674,
'System and method for creating multiple files from a single source
file', which was issued today to Microsoft:

The invention overcomes the limitations of the prior art by allowing
applications and utilities to write several files to a disk as a
single file-write operation, yet, after conversion, to individually
access the several files.

--> Full Patent Available At

http://patft.uspto.gov/netacgi/nph-Parser?Sect1=PTO1&Sect2=HITOFF&d=PALL&p=1&u=/netahtml/srchnum.htm&r=1&f=G&l=50&s1=6,594,674.WKU.&OS=PN/6,594,674&RS=PN/6,594,674
```

#### ↳ Re: Did Microsoft Just Patent The PDS?

- **From:** Patrick Herring <ph@anweald.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-07-16T14:47:36+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F155778.479CBEEC@anweald.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<e7946e7b.0307151323.b01809c@posting.google.com>`

```

> 
> From the 'Summary Of The Invention' for U.S. patent no. 6,594,674,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> access the several files.

```

#### ↳ Re: Did Microsoft Just Patent The PDS?

- **From:** Steve Thompson <steve_nospam_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T11:55:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.198090d08cac885698968a@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`
- **References:** `<e7946e7b.0307151323.b01809c@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <e7946e7b.0307151323.b01809c@posting.google.com>, 
theodp@aol.com says...
<snip>
> The invention overcomes the limitations of the prior art by allowing
> applications and utilities to write several files to a disk as a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I guess the following sent to the PTO won't win me any friends 
at Micro$**t:

Dear Sirs:

I wish to challenge patent 6,594,674. I understand there is an 
informal way to do this.

I might bring to your attention that IBM (among others) 
specifically does this with the old PDS (Partitioned Data Set) 
file type (known as _PAM where PAM is Partitioned Access 
Method). IBM has also been doing this via PDS/E (based on the 
VSAM I/O principles used with their "MVS" operating system).

The "MVS" utility, IEBUPDTE has the ability to do what is 
claimed in the patent, as does IEBCOPY and the various versions 
of the Linkage Editor (now called Binder). 

Personally, I worked on such a file system for NASA in 1979 
using a Univac 1100/22 at the Goddard Space Flight Center while 
an employee of CSC. I designed the entire file system based on 
IBM's PDS methodolgy.

Lastly, Microsoft has their own software out there, that they 
market that builds something called DLLs. Those DLLs have within 
them executable code, of which each member is a file unto 
itself.

Regards,
Steve Thompson
```

<a id="t-1792f04d11"></a>
## Thread: 2 questions — 9 msgs

### 2 questions

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl>
- **Date:** 2003-07-16T19:23:56+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf41ks$c7v$1@kostnix.uvt.nl>`

```
Hi,

I've got two question for which I'm still seeking an answer, hope to find it
here! I work with NetCOBOL for Windows version 7.

1. Splash screen during runtime load

The NetCOBOL runtime including the one for PowerCOBOL can take quite a while
to load on some older machines (Pentium 200 @ 32mb RAM). I was wondering if
there's a way in which I can show the user a splash screen stating that the
application is loading. Now the only indication of the runtime loading is
the mouse pointer with the hourglass; user tend to overlook that...

2. Detect display settings

How can I detect (via API call?) the display settings of the user's monitor?
Settings of interest are the number of colors (16, 256, 16k).

Thanks in advance!

Rik Hendriks

---------------------------------
Don't think you are, know you are.
```

#### ↳ Re: 2 questions

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-16T17:02:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TDmdnYlbQb0cVoiiXTWJkg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bf41ks$c7v$1@kostnix.uvt.nl>`

```
R. Hendriks wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> monitor? Settings of interest are the number of colors (16, 256, 16k).

For a list of most api calls and invocation from VB (which can translate
most of the time) see

http://www.mentalis.org/agnet/apiguide.shtml

I couldn't find one for number of colors, but you might.
```

#### ↳ Re: 2 questions

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T13:42:05+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f15ff8c_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bf41ks$c7v$1@kostnix.uvt.nl>`

```

"R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl> wrote in message
news:bf41ks$c7v$1@kostnix.uvt.nl...
> Hi,
>
> I've got two question for which I'm still seeking an answer, hope to find
it
> here! I work with NetCOBOL for Windows version 7.
>
> 1. Splash screen during runtime load
>
> The NetCOBOL runtime including the one for PowerCOBOL can take quite a
while
> to load on some older machines (Pentium 200 @ 32mb RAM). I was wondering
if
> there's a way in which I can show the user a splash screen stating that
the
> application is loading. Now the only indication of the runtime loading is
> the mouse pointer with the hourglass; user tend to overlook that...
>

Do this by using a script to start the application. The script displays a
bitmap with whatever you want to say on it, then calls the PowerCOBOL
application.

I have a number of PowerCOBOL applications and, after reading your post, I
tested some of them on a Pentium 333 running under Win98 with 32Megs of
memory.  There is no detectable difference between running in this
environment and running on a 2.5 GHz P4 (512 Megs). (It takes around 10
seconds for the first screen to appear, running the identical application on
both systems. After that (the RTS is loaded), responses are immediate).

If you are experiencing long load times, I suspect the system needs some
optimizing. Try defragging, and you might also check the swapfile size.

(The P1 has had the original  3 GIG HDD replaced with a Fujitsu 20 GIG,
which probably has a much faster seek time than the original).

> 2. Detect display settings
>
> How can I detect (via API call?) the display settings of the user's
monitor?
> Settings of interest are the number of colors (16, 256, 16k).
>

You could use a component I have (ActiveX - drop it on your PowerCOBOL form
and invoke its methods  and properties (one of which is number of colours)
from PowerCOBOL), but it isn't free...sorry.

Alternatively, use the API to build your own.

Here is some sample VB code (courtesy of KPD Team in Belgium - who produce
the downloadable "ALLAPI" Guide that is extremely useful...) that shows the
API calls you will need...

Const CCDEVICENAME = 32

Const CCFORMNAME = 32

Const DM_PELSWIDTH = &H80000

Const DM_PELSHEIGHT = &H100000

Const CDS_TEST = &H4

Private Type DISPLAY_DEVICE

cb As Long

DeviceName As String * 32

DeviceString As String * 128

StateFlags As Long

DeviceID As String * 128

DeviceKey As String * 128

End Type

Private Type DEVMODE

dmDeviceName As String * CCDEVICENAME

dmSpecVersion As Integer

dmDriverVersion As Integer

dmSize As Integer

dmDriverExtra As Integer

dmFields As Long

dmOrientation As Integer

dmPaperSize As Integer

dmPaperLength As Integer

dmPaperWidth As Integer

dmScale As Integer

dmCopies As Integer

dmDefaultSource As Integer

dmPrintQuality As Integer

dmColor As Integer

dmDuplex As Integer

dmYResolution As Integer

dmTTOption As Integer

dmCollate As Integer

dmFormName As String * CCFORMNAME

dmUnusedPadding As Integer

dmBitsPerPel As Integer

dmPelsWidth As Long

dmPelsHeight As Long

dmDisplayFlags As Long

dmDisplayFrequency As Long

dmICMMethod As Long 'NT 4.0

dmICMIntent As Long 'NT 4.0

dmMediaType As Long 'NT 4.0

dmDitherType As Long 'NT 4.0

dmReserved1 As Long 'NT 4.0

dmReserved2 As Long 'NT 4.0

dmPanningWidth As Long 'Win2000

dmPanningHeight As Long 'Win2000

End Type

Private Declare Function ChangeDisplaySettingsEx Lib "user32" Alias
"ChangeDisplaySettingsExA" (lpszDeviceName As Any, lpDevMode As Any, ByVal
hWnd As Long, ByVal dwFlags As Long, lParam As Any) As Long

Private Declare Function EnumDisplayDevices Lib "user32" Alias
"EnumDisplayDevicesA" (Unused As Any, ByVal iDevNum As Long, lpDisplayDevice
As DISPLAY_DEVICE, ByVal dwFlags As Long) As Boolean

Dim OldX As Long, OldY As Long, T As Long

Private Sub Form_Load()

'KPD-Team 2000

'URL: http://www.allapi.net/

'E-Mail: KPDTeam@Allapi.net

Dim DD As DISPLAY_DEVICE, DevM As DEVMODE

DD.cb = Len(DD)

OldX = Screen.Width / Screen.TwipsPerPixelX

OldY = Screen.Height / Screen.TwipsPerPixelY

'First retieve some display info

If EnumDisplayDevices(ByVal 0&, 0, DD, ByVal 0&) Then

'and show it

Me.AutoRedraw = True

Me.Print "Device String:" + Left$(DD.DeviceString, InStr(1, DD.DeviceString,
Chr$(0)) - 1)

Me.Print "Device Name:" + Left$(DD.DeviceName, InStr(1, DD.DeviceName,
Chr$(0)) - 1)

Me.Print "Device Key:" + Left$(DD.DeviceKey, InStr(1, DD.DeviceKey,
Chr$(0)) - 1)

Me.Print "Device ID:" + Left$(DD.DeviceID, InStr(1, DD.DeviceID, Chr$(0)) -
1)

Else

Me.Print "Error while retrieving Display Information"

End If

DevM.dmSize = Len(DevM)

'we want to change the horizontal and the vertical resolution

DevM.dmFields = DM_PELSWIDTH Or DM_PELSHEIGHT

DevM.dmPelsWidth = 640

DevM.dmPelsHeight = 480

'change the display settings

Call ChangeDisplaySettingsEx(ByVal 0&, DevM, ByVal 0&, CDS_TEST, ByVal 0&)

T = Timer

Do: DoEvents: Loop Until Timer > T + 5

DevM.dmPelsWidth = OldX

DevM.dmPelsHeight = OldY

'change the display settings back to the old settings

Call ChangeDisplaySettingsEx(ByVal 0&, DevM, ByVal 0&, CDS_TEST, ByVal 0&)

End Sub

Hope this helps,

Pete.
```

What you describe is exactly what I mean, the 10 seconds delay. After the
runtime has loaded, there is problem with the speed of execution of the
application. It's those first 10-12 seconds during which I'd like to show a
splash screen. It's also a fact that after launching an app for the first
time it takes 10-12 seconds, but any consecutive launch takes about half the
time. Too bad Fujitsu didn't think of a standard solution for this...

>
> > 2. Detect display settings
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

form
> and invoke its methods  and properties (one of which is number of colours)
> from PowerCOBOL), but it isn't free...sorry.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the downloadable "ALLAPI" Guide that is extremely useful...) that shows
the
> API calls you will need...
>
[...]

I've found the call in the SDK from Microsoft, thanks for the tip! Yet I
can't seem to get it to work. The program compiles and links without an
error, but when executed the call always returns zero (aka some error
occured). What am I doing wrong? Here's the code for those that want to help
me out:

000010 @OPTIONS ALPHAL(WORD)
000100 IDENTIFICATION  DIVISION.
000110  PROGRAM-ID. pr-enumdisplaysettings.
000120 ENVIRONMENT     DIVISION.
000130 DATA            DIVISION.
000140 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000150 01  ws-lpszDeviceName PIC X.
000151 01  ws-iModenum  PIC S9(9) comp-5.
000152
000160 01  ws-lpDevmode.
000161   03  dmDeviceName PIC X(32).
000163   03  dmSpecVersion PIC S9(4) comp-5.
000164   03  dmDriverVersion  PIC S9(4) comp-5.

000209
000211 01  ws-api-returnvalue  PIC S9(9) comp-5.
000212   88  ws-api-fout  value 0.
000213
000214 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
000215
000216
000217     move X"00" to ws-lpszDeviceName.
000218     move -1 to ws-iModenum.         *> ENUM_CURRENT_SETTINGS = -1
000220     move 1024 to dmSize.
000221     move 0 to dmDriverExtra.
000222
000223     call "EnumDisplaySettingsA" with STDCALL using by reference
ws-lpszDevicename
000224                                                    by value
ws-iModenum
000225                                                    by reference
ws-lpDevmode
000230                                              returning
ws-api-returnvalue.
000256
000257     display "Returnvalue : " ws-api-returnvalue.
000258     display "ws-lpDevmode  : " ws-lpDevmode.
000320
000330 pr-99.
000340     exit program.

Some remarks:

-. I want to query the current display and therefor ws-lpszDeviceName has
value X"00"  (aka NULL)
-. For the same reason ws-iModenum has value -1 (aka ENUM_CURRENT_SETTINGS)

This should do the trick according to the SDK docs, yet it doesn't...

>
> Hope this helps,

It sure did! Now just to get is working ;-)

>
> Pete.

Rik Hendriks

R. Hendriks wrote:
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3f15ff8c_6@news.athenanews.com...
…[151 more quoted lines elided]…
> This should do the trick according to the SDK docs, yet it doesn't...

� iModeNum
Index value that specifies the graphics mode for which information is to be
obtained.
Graphics mode indexes start at zero. To obtain information for all of a
display device's graphics modes, make a series of calls to
EnumDisplaySettings, as follows: Set iModeNum to zero for the first call,
and increment iModeNum by one for each subsequent call. Continue calling the
function until the return value is FALSE.
When you call EnumDisplaySettings with iModeNum set to zero, the operating
system initializes and caches information about the display device. When you
call EnumDisplaySettings with iModeNum set to a non-zero value, the function
returns the information that was cached the last time the function was
called with iModeNum set to zero.

If I were a betting man, I'd say the -1 for the first (only) call is
wrong: -1 says, according to the above, give me the information from the
LAST call. Since there was no last call, the function fails.
```

!SOLVED!

With many thanks to Luc Kumps, the solution is to change ws-lpDeviceName
into a PIC S9(9) comp-5, setting it's value to zero and passing it by
value -> summed up: set the pointer to NULL.

See newsgroup microsof.public.win32.programmer.kernel if interested.

Rik Hendriks

## Thread: how to tranfer the object handler between two form? — 20 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### how to tranfer the object handler between two form?

- **From:** "fulvio" <nospam@abuse.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-16T19:20:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it>`

```
I'm using Powercobol V7.
I have a project with two forms.

In the first one I have create an instance to an object of one class
that I have created.

INVOKE my-class-name "NEW" RETURNING handler.

Now i need to perform some action in a child form, and
I want to use the same object, so i need to know how to
transfer the handler (USAGE OBJECT REFERENCE) from the
parent form to the child.

Any Ideas?

Fulvio
```

#### ↳ Re: how to tranfer the object handler between two form?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-16T15:33:22-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cOhRa.4188$eP6.606647@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it>`

```
"fulvio" <nospam@abuse.com> wrote in message
news:oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it...
> I'm using Powercobol V7.
> I have a project with two forms.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

An object reference can be passed in subroutine argements etc., the same as
any other data name.  You can create a new reference with the SET comand.

    SET object-REFERENCE-TWO TO object-reference-one.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: how to tranfer the object handler between two form?

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T11:38:45+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f15e6e6_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it>`

```
Fulvio,

I hit this same problem a few days ago.

You have an instantiated object that you need to pass down to another form.

Normally, you would expect to be able to make the Object Reference EXTERNAL.

You can't use EXTERNAL if you are using OO COBOL as the scripting language.

Bugger!

I tried everything I could think of (including redefining the object
reference), but nothing worked.

I don't know what the "official" solution is (my support with Fujitsu has
lapsed) but I solved it by doing the following:

1. Create an ActiveX control that has the Second form included in it.

2. Add a custom Property  to the ActiveX control, that is the Object
Reference for the Class you want passed to the form.

3. Drop the new ActiveX onto the Main form.

Now you can instantiate the Class within the Main Form, set the Object
Reference property in the ActiveX control, then invoke a Method of the
ActiveX that displays the second form (or whatever you want to do with it.).

It means thinking outside the square and NOT invoking the second form with
"CallForm" or "OpenForm".

I see no reason why you couldn't have any number of forms included on your
Main form as ActiveX controls in this way.

Hope this helps,

Pete..


"fulvio" <nospam@abuse.com> wrote in message
news:oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it...
> I'm using Powercobol V7.
> I have a project with two forms.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: how to tranfer the object handler between two form?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T02:51:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F1603C2.3BE0578C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it>`

```


fulvio wrote:

> I'm using Powercobol V7.
> I have a project with two forms.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fulvio

I don't know Fujitsu PowerCOBOL so adapt accordingly, particularly the
class and method names :-

[Class/Program - Business Logic]---------->[Customer Dialog]
[...................,,...........................]---------->[History
Dialog]

Business Logic :

Working-storage section.
01 os-Parent                                    object reference.
01 os-Dialog                                    object reference.
01 os-CustomerDialog                      object reference.
01 os-HistoryDialog                         object reference.

Procedure Division.

invoke "getDesktop ????" returning os-Parent     *> ParentWindow

(A)
invoke CustomerNames "new" using os-Parent returning os-CustomerDialog
invoke os-CustomerDialog "createDialog ????"

(B)
invoke CustomerSalesHistory "new" using os-Parent returning
os-HistoryDialog
invoke os-HistoryDialog "createDialog ????"

*>........ Assume each dialog has a Pushbutton ( CustomerDialog <Show
History> and
*> ........HistoryDialog <Show Name/Address>

I can think of four possibilities, and with thought there are others  :-

-------------------------------------------------
1. Pass each as a handle to the other :-

invoke os-CustomerDialog "passHandle" using os-HistoryDialog
invoke os-HistoryDialog    "passHandle" using os-CustomerDialog

Now when the Pushbutton is accessed in either Dialog, the event could
instruct to "show" the alternate Dialog, i.e., within CustomerDialog you
would see an event(callback) :-

Method-id. "showHistory".
invoke os-HistoryDialog "show".
----------------------------------------------

2. Return the pushbutton event to the Business Logic and either :-

   (returned event from Customer) - invoke os-HistoryDialog "show"
   (returned event from History)     - invoke os-CustomerDialog "show"
--------------------------------------------------------------

3. An adaptation of # 2 (and my own preference) :-

Having done (A) and (B) above, now show CustomerDialog :-

set os-Dialog to os-CustomerDialog
invoke os-Dialog "show"

*>..... we get returned Pushbutton event from CustomerDialog saying
"OnEventShowHistory"

Method-id. "OnEventShowHistory"

set os-Dialog to os-HistoryDialog
invoke os-Dialog "show"

My reasons for #3 - *IF* Business Logic is written as a CLASS (with
Methods) as opposed to a Procedural program, by setting either of the
dialogs to os-Dialog there are some common Business Logic methods which
would apply to both Dialogs. Your Business logic is also ONLY working on
one Dialog at a time (that currently designated as os-Dialog).
-----------------------------------------------------------
4. Just to give you another option :-

invoke CustomerNames "new" using self (= Business Logic), os-Parent
         returning os-CustomerDialog
invoke os-CustomerDialog "createDialog ????"

Class : CustomerNames :
FACTORY.
Method-id. "new"
Linkage Section.
01 lnk-BusinessLogic                    object reference.
01 lnk-Parent                                object reference.
01 lnk-self                                    object reference.

Procedure Division using lnk-BusinessLogic, lnk-Parent
                             returning lnk-self.

invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
invoke lnk-self "passHandles" using lnk-Businesss Logic, lnk-Parent
END FACTORY.

OBJECT SECTION.
Working-Storage Section.
01 os-Caller                                 object reference.
01 os-Parent                                object reference.

Method-id. "passHandles".
Linkage section.
01 lnk-BusinessLogic                    object reference.
01 lnk-Parent                                object reference.
Procedure Division using lnk-BusinessLogic, lnk-Parent

set os-BusinessLogic to os-Caller
set os-Parent           to lnk-Parent

*>Above now allows DialogX to return individual events(callbacks) direct
to os-Caller *> (Business Logic) as appropriate

END OBJECT
*>-----------------

Above may not "jive" with how you create Dialogs in F/J - but at least
it shows you some options.

PS: avoid syntax like :-

    INVOKE my-class-name "NEW" RETURNING handler.

Substitute something more meaningful for 'handler'.

HTH

Jimmy
```

…[39 more quoted lines elided]…

Oops ! Screwed up on the "passHandles" method - IT SHOULD READ :-

OBJECT SECTION.
Working-Storage Section.
01 os-Caller                                 object reference.
01 os-Parent                                object reference.

Method-id. "passHandles".
Linkage section.
01 lnk-BusinessLogic                    object reference.
01 lnk-Parent                                object reference.
Procedure Division using lnk-BusinessLogic, lnk-Parent

set os-Caller   to lnk-BusinessLogic    <************
set os-Parent  to lnk-Parent

Mea culpa !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to tranfer the object handler between two form?

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T22:43:00+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f167e57_3@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it> <3F1603C2.3BE0578C@shaw.ca> <3F162358.EFAA4E02@shaw.ca>`

```
Ha! Knew you'd be back and glad to see it, Jimmy!

Now, if we can only get Werner to reconsider also...

I thought your post was interesting as an OO COBOL example, but it is
totally inapplicable to a PowerCOBOL environment.

There are NO handles to windows  or anything else involved so there is no
passing of them required. Neither is there any invoking of GUI Classes
(Windows are defined grahically by point and click, not by code). The whole
environment is miles away from the approach you would use with OO COBOL. All
of the nitty gritty is under the covers and not readily accessible. You can
argue whether this is a benefit or not; most people who use PowerCOBOL like
to be spared the bottom level. It is a "Quick Build" tool and a very good
one. It is possible to build applications along the lines you posted using
OO COBOL  (Fujitsu NetCOBOL) and in this case your post is very pertinent.

PowerCOBOL is a different ball game.

Your comment about it not jiving is, sadly,
 true.

Fulivio simply needs to pass an Object Reference to a subform. You'd think
it would be straightforward and it should be, unfortunately, it isn't. The
normal way to pass data between GUI forms in PowerCOBOL is by means of
EXTERNAL data reference. Object References can't do that.

There are simple (in PowerCOBOL) alternatives and I posted one.

Pete.

<snipped OO COBOL code>
```

"fulvio" <nospam@abuse.com> wrote in message

>

That is outrageous. Somebody needs a kick for that. They obviously have said

#### ↳ Re: how to tranfer the object handler between two form?

- **From:** fulvio <member33769@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T09:49:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3133156.1058780941@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it>`

```

OK. I am successful to solve my problem!

My problem:

I need to create a class that manage the DataBase
connection for my applications.
So I need to creat it one time, on program start, and share this
object-instance inside my run unit and between forms.

In this test-class I'm trying to use the MyVbQL.dll to connect to
MySQL data_base with InnoDB that support the row-level locking.

In a previous test where I have used the ESQL, the problem already
had been resolved passing the connection name between forms with
EXTERNAL clause and set it with the "SET CONNECTION", but
unfortunately the FJ don't perform the FETCH PRIOR, FIRST, LAST
on cursors.


Solution:

implement the "Singleton" when create a new object-instance of
the class.

How to:

1. create a new class called "singleton" that inherit FJBASE.
2. put the "singleton" class inside the repository of the class
   that you want instantiate only one time.

That's all.

The "singleton" class:

**************************************************
 IDENTIFICATION  DIVISION.
 CLASS-ID. SINGLETON INHERITS FJBASE.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SPECIAL-NAMES.
 REPOSITORY.
     CLASS FJBASE.
 FACTORY.
 DATA DIVISION.

 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 CTRL-OBJ OBJECT REFERENCE SELF.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

 METHOD-ID. NEW OVERRIDE.
 DATA DIVISION.

 LINKAGE SECTION.
 01 L-CTRL-OBJ OBJECT REFERENCE SELF.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION RETURNING L-CTRL-OBJ.

     IF CTRL-OBJ = NULL
        SET CTRL-OBJ  L-CTRL-OBJ TO SUPER :: "NEW"
        INVOKE  CTRL-OBJ "INIT"
     ELSE
       SET L-CTRL-OBJ TO CTRL-OBJ.

 END METHOD NEW.
 END FACTORY.
 END CLASS  SINGLETON.
**************************************************

This small class has one variable that is a pointer to itself.

In the implementation of the "new" method, there is a test that checks
if
the variable has been initialized, and if not, creates a new instance.

This way of initialization in the control block, means that the
Singleton instance
is initialized, or created, only on the first invoke to the "new"
method.

In this way I can invoke the "new" method on my own class in the
parent form and
"create" the instance, and invoke the "new" method again in the sub
form (child)
and instantiate the same object.

I hope that it can be useful.

Fulvio.

P.S.

Many thanks to the LinuxJournal that, with a its article, it has
given the right inspiration to me.


(I'm sorry for my English...)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how to tranfer the object handler between two form?

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T23:00:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1bc87f_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it> <3133156.1058780941@dbforums.com>`

```
Thanks for posting this solution.

You asked me for my comments, so here goes...

It means invoking "new" in two places when there is no need.

As it is for a database connection, it makes sense for this to be a separate
Class, as you have shown.

You could invoke it once and set the reference obtained as a custom
property. (It would be the "last instantiated instance").

This avoids the overhead of a Method invoke from the subform. (or any other
sub form that needs to use this database connection...)

Causing the new method to ensure that the same instance is passed back on
every subsequent invoke (after the first one) may be suspect and is not
really what the "new" Method is for. (Constructors should construct, not
"construct sometimes <G>")

Still, if it works and you are happy with it, good job!

Pete.


"fulvio" <member33769@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3133156.1058780941@dbforums.com...
>
> OK. I am successful to solve my problem!
…[98 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to tranfer the object handler between two form?

- **From:** fulvio <member33769@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T18:30:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3134905.1058812210@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<oAhRa.76451$qa5.1570213@news2.tin.it> <3133156.1058780941@dbforums.com> <3f1bc87f_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```

In a more elegant way we can create a new method called "GETINSTANCE"
like this one and leave unchange the "NEW" method from FJBASE:

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
 IDENTIFICATION  DIVISION.
 CLASS-ID. SINGLETON INHERITS FJBASE.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SPECIAL-NAMES.
 REPOSITORY.
     CLASS FJBASE.
 FACTORY.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 CTRL-OBJ OBJECT REFERENCE SELF.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

 METHOD-ID. GETINSTANCE.
 DATA DIVISION.

 LINKAGE SECTION.
 01 L-CTRL-OBJ OBJECT REFERENCE SELF.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION RETURNING L-CTRL-OBJ.

     IF CTRL-OBJ NOT = NULL
         SET L-CTRL-OBJ TO CTRL-OBJ.

 END METHOD GETINSTANCE.


 END FACTORY.
 END CLASS  SINGLETON.
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::


and inside our main-proc take the right steps to test if the
class is already instanciaded or not and decide what we want to do:
```

Christian Lutz wrote:
> little question: i am looking for a small fast & simple editor for
> cobol-programs. any suggestions? thanks a lot.
>
> christian

If your compiler package came with an editor, it's ususally best to use the
editor supplied. The trade-off is lack of some feature you love vs. the
integration with the compiler (such as jumping to the source line with the
error).
```

#### ↳ Re: Editor for Cobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T07:43:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf6uit$p52$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<1470b7c0.0307170132.4fade84d@posting.google.com>`

```
Christian Lutz wrote:

> little question: i am looking for a small fast & simple editor for
> cobol-programs. any suggestions? thanks a lot.

Well the smallest, fastest and simplest would be NotePad.  But I don't 
think that you will like it, you probably need to add more criteria such as 
syntax highlighting, auto-indenting, block indenting, key style you would 
be happy with.

Editors are something that you have to train yourself to fit, or vice 
versa. You need to get several and see which you like and which you can 
make do what you want.

I use TSE (www.semware.com) because I have made it fit my needs with syntax 
highlighting, tabbing, and key short cuts (eg F9+P -> PERFORM).

On Linux I use SETEdit (setedit.sourceforge.net) which is free and I can 
get used to it.  There is a version for DOS which will run in Windows.
```

ch_lutz@hotmail.com (Christian Lutz) wrote:

>little question: i am looking for a small fast & simple editor for
>cobol-programs. any suggestions? thanks a lot.

You could write your own, as I did. I  modeled it after TSE and Brief, 
with hints of SPF, E!, EDIX, EMacs and  IBM Professional. I added features not
found in any of them, such as 'hot grep' and hyperlink.  Writing took less than
a month of evenings and weekends.
```

## Thread: ADD v COMPUTE — 25 this month / 39 total

### ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T11:37:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
What is the difference between ADD and COMPUTE in terms of performance? If I 
want to add two values and store the result in a variable how to decide 
whether I should select ADD or COMPUTE?


-- ISLAM: Winning the hearts and minds of the world, one bomb at a time.
```

#### ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T12:41:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XPwRa.59203$3o3.3938332@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```

"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
news:bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com...
| What is the difference between ADD and COMPUTE in terms of performance? If
I
| want to add two values and store the result in a variable how to decide
| whether I should select ADD or COMPUTE?
|

It's sometimes a matter of using what you prefer.

Using the COMPUTE sometimes causes the compiler to store intermediate
results.
The precision of the intermediate results could affect the results of the
calculation.

Don't worry about performance, concentrate on accuracy.
```

#### ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T08:43:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-A05FFA.08434317072003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
In article <bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com>,
 weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote:

> What is the difference between ADD and COMPUTE in terms of performance? If I 
> want to add two values and store the result in a variable how to decide 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> -- ISLAM: Winning the hearts and minds of the world, one bomb at a time.

That is a very compiler dependant question.  In general there should be 
no decernable performance differernce between:

   Add A to B

   or 

   Compute B = B + A

But that does not account for the various oddities of compiler vendors.

I think, as a general rule, when you have something like this you should 
just use the one that 'fits' the best with the program and only concern 
yourself with relative performance if it appears to be a problem.
```

#### ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T07:56:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pz6dnR8bfJxkAYuiXTWJkg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
Ubiquitous wrote:
> What is the difference between ADD and COMPUTE in terms of
> performance? If I want to add two values and store the result in a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> time.

ACCEPT  BEG FROM TIME.
PERFORM 1000000 TIMES
   ADD A TO B
   END-PERFORM.
ACCEPT FIN FROM TIME.
COMPUTE DUR = FIN-SEC - BEG-SEC.

DISPLAY "Add =  " DUR

ACCEPT BEG FROM TIME
PERFORM 1000000 TIMES
   COMPUTE B = B + A
   END-PERFORM
ACCEPT FIN FROM TIME
COMPUTE DUR = FIN-SEC - BEG-SEC
DISPLAY "Compute = " DUR.
COMPUTE DUR = FIN-SEC - BEG-SEC
```

#### ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T15:30:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jSZ306ESMrF$EwZN@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
With regard to IBM compilers, It used to be that ADD was more efficient 
than COMPUTE. That is no longer the case. I use COMPUTE when I have a 
non-trivial equation to evaluate and ADD when the calculation is 
trivial.

In message <bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com>, Ubiquitous 
<weberm@polaris.net> writes
>What is the difference between ADD and COMPUTE in terms of performance? If I
>want to add two values and store the result in a variable how to decide
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>-- ISLAM: Winning the hearts and minds of the world, one bomb at a time.
```

:>Actually the code generated for an add,subtract,whatever is the same no
:>matter how you code the arithmetic. In terms of machine language.

Not necessarily.

The ADD compiler function is quite simple while the COMPUTE compiler function
is much more complex.

It is quite possible that the COMPUTE will generate more object code.

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T15:15:18-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf9rhn$jv3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
I can state with a fair degree of certainty that for Unisys MCP/AS dialects
of COBOL, so long as all other things are equal, the performance of the
COBOL statement "ADD A TO B" is absolutely identical to the performance of
"COMPUTE B = B + A" on that system.  Though the object code may differ
slightly between COBOL *dialects*, the object code is *the same* for these
two *statements* within a given dialect.

If you're trying to add exactly two variables together and store exactly one
sum into exactly one of them, either ADD A TO B or COMUTE B = B + A is OK.
But if you're trying to add three variables together and add the sum of
those three into each of four variables in turn *in a single statement*,
between ADD and COMPUTE the choice is clearly ADD.

The capabilities available to ADD are different from those available to
COMPUTE, according to the various COBOL standards. While
    ADD A, B, C   TO  D, E, F, G
is both legal and meaningful,
    COMPUTE D, E, F, G = D, E, F, G  +  A, B, C
 is neither.

To do something similar to ADD A, B, C to D, E, F, G using COMPUTE you end
up with something like
    COMPUTE temp = A + B + C
    COMPUTE D = D + temp
    COMPUTE E = E + temp
    COMPUTE F = F + temp
    COMPUTE G = G + temp
That's FIVE statements, not ONE, and requires an extra temporary to boot.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Ubiquitous" <weberm@polaris.net> wrote in message
news:bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com...
> What is the difference between ADD and COMPUTE in terms of performance? If
I
> want to add two values and store the result in a variable how to decide
> whether I should select ADD or COMPUTE?
>
>
> -- ISLAM: Winning the hearts and minds of the world, one bomb at a time.
```

>standards. While
>     ADD A, B, C   TO  D, E, F, G
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now that is an interesting point. Words have a very different way of
describing data than mathmatical abstractions do. I'd suggest that all the
mathmatical operators, looked at in the same manner, have capabilities well
beyond a simple compute statement. Computes were just indroduced as a sop to
the Fortran dolts, and no true Cobol affectionado would stoop to such
insiduous deviation from the teachings of the First Church of Fortress
Cobol.

Donald

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T01:48:29-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-7D02F0.01482919072003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <bf9rhn$jv3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
In article <bf9rhn$jv3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
 "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> I can state with a fair degree of certainty that for Unisys MCP/AS dialects
> of COBOL, so long as all other things are equal, the performance of the
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens


The temp approach is cute, but hardly realistic...you can always write:

    COMPUTE D = D + (A + B + C)
    COMPUTE E = E + (A + B + C)
    COMPUTE F = F + (A + B + C)
    COMPUTE G = G + (A + B + C)

and any non-toy compiler will fix up that intermediate result of (A+B+C) 
once and add it to D, E, F & G for you.

That is one of the easier optimizations to recognize.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T12:58:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg3v8k$12ei$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <bf9rhn$jv3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-7D02F0.01482919072003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger->

> The temp approach is cute, but hardly realistic...you can always write:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> That is one of the easier optimizations to recognize.

No, you cannot "always write".  In some few cases yes, but by no means can
you do so in the general case.  I made no statements about the relationship
among the descriptions of D, E, F and G, and scale factor or precision
requirement differences among the destinations could, and almost certainly
would, render such an approach dead wrong for the general case.

Giving equal weight to all possibilities, the percentage of cases in which
such an optimization would be suitable in a given implementation is rather
limited, I would contend.   While the multiple-destination ADD statement
would likely produce a result the same as the suggested COMPUTE
optimization, it's not clear to me that the author of the four COMPUTE
statements intended for a single intermediate result with a single precision
and a single scale factor to be applied to all four destinations,
particularly when the scale factors of the four destinations differed.

For that reason, I do not accept the assertion that "any non-toy compiler
will fix up that intermediate result ... once ...".  I personally would
mistrust a compiler that violated the programmer's instructions so
egregiously, and would be rather more inclined to trust one that did not!

Even if the precision requirements imposed by the operands A, B and C and
their interactions with the operators (in this case, only "+") in the
arithmetic expression do not exceed the precision required by the standard
(18 digits in the case of '85) or the implementation, there is no guarantee
that once the alignment of the decimal point of the sum is rectified against
the scaling requirements of each or any of the destinations that that limit
will not be exceeded resulting in precision loss.  I would regard any
compiler that failed to account for that possibility and blindly optimized
the four calculations into a single one a "toy compiler" indeed.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

I'll wager the attributes of D,E,F, & G are immaterial in any non-toy
compiler.

>
> Giving equal weight to all possibilities, the percentage of cases in
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> factors of the four destinations differed.

I'll wager the scale factor of D-G are immaterial to any non-toy compiler.

>
> For that reason, I do not accept the assertion that "any non-toy
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> trust one that did not!

All non-toy compilers optimize as stated above.

>
> Even if the precision requirements imposed by the operands A, B and C
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> precision loss.

You're right. But that is a failing of the programmer, not the compiler.

> I would regard any compiler that failed to account
> for that possibility and blindly optimized the four calculations into
> a single one a "toy compiler" indeed.

Check your compiler manual under the section "Intermediate Results." You'll
see the compiler does what it damn well wants to, irrespective of the
instructions or intentions of the programmer. It's the programmer who must
make allowance for the actions of the compiler, not the reverse.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T16:23:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg448b$c44$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <bf9rhn$jv3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-7D02F0.01482919072003@corp.supernews.com> <bg3v8k$12ei$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <mEmdnQM40Ne-ELiiXTWJjg@giganews.com>`

```
I'll wager that you would loose your WAGER <G>

Assuming that you will accept that the IBM Mainframe COBOL compiler is *not*
a "toy compiler", I suggest you read the information at (and following):


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3PG10/APPENDIX1.1

and see exactly HOW much difference there can be between:

     COMPUTE D = D + (A + B + C)
        and
     COMPUTE E = E + (A + B + C)

depending on EXACTLY how D versus F is defined (scalling, usage, etc).

As far as your statement
  "All non-toy compilers optimize as stated above"

You might want to read the information at:
 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/7.1.1.5

which says (in part),

 "When several components of different expressions are duplicates, make sure
the compiler is permitted to optimize them. For evaluating arithmetic
expressions, the compiler is bound by the left-to-right evaluation rules of
COBOL. Therefore, either move all the duplicates to the left side of the
expressions or group them inside parentheses."

which DOES say that they would optimize THESE examples as you state, but
indicate (as I read it) that it would NOT if the code were (instead):

     COMPUTE D = D +A + B + C
     COMPUTE E = E + A + B + C
     COMPUTE F = F + A + B + C
    COMPUTE G = G + A + B + C
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T08:14:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg631j$2j0e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <bf9rhn$jv3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-7D02F0.01482919072003@corp.supernews.com> <bg3v8k$12ei$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <mEmdnQM40Ne-ELiiXTWJjg@giganews.com> <bg448b$c44$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <P0qdnSxU_tJXMriiXTWJkw@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:P0qdnSxU_tJXMriiXTWJkw@giganews.com...

> I stand corrected - if the receiving field is floating-point, which makes
> sense given the potentially huge scaling differences involved.

No, Jerry, the same issue obtains for standard COBOL when multiple
destinations have wildly disparate scale factors.  It might -- *might*  -- 
be reasonable to make such an optimization if the destinations all had *the
same* scale factors and precisions; it gets really risky otherwise.

Standard COBOL requires multiple-destination COMPUTE and
multiple-destination ADD to behave as if a temporary field contains the
(single) sum that is used for all destinations.

That isn't the case for single-destination ADD or single-destination
COMPUTE.  If the compiler is able to deduce for the single-destination case
that certain portions of the argument values are not reflected in the
destination, the implementor is not precluded from choosing to truncate
high-order digits in the arguments of an expression to ensure a more precise
result; the rules for multiple-destination variants of these statements
*require* the production of a single result applied to all the destinations.

A compiler that sacrifices speed for accuracy and precision in cases like
this is not necessarily a "toy compiler".   An implementor that chooses not
to "optimize" four COMPUTEs with the same expression into four different
destinations into a multiple-destination COMPUTE with a single temporary
expression value may not be doing so out of ignorance or laziness, but
rather out of a desire to give his customers the most precise value
available in the particular context of argument and destination
characteristics.  .

> For this compiler, true. Put parens around A+B+C and the optimization
> latches in.

*For this compiler*.  The rules for parentheses don't even *suggest* the
production of a common temporary in this case.

> Which illustrates the point about the programmer conforming his
> intentions to the actions of the compiler - not vice-versa.

It's a good idea for the programmer to know, first and foremost, which
approach is most likely to produce the *most accurate* answer, and why, for
a given compiler.  The *standard* requires the production of a single result
to be applicable to all destinations of a multiple-destination ADD or
COMPUTE; in the single-destination case the implementor is free to tailor
the expression evaluation process to match the characteristics of the
destination, because there's only one destination to contend with.  There
*may* be multiple-destination cases in which this is also possible, but it
cannot be said that it is possible for *all* multiple-destination cases, and
that's true regardless of whether any or all of the destinations are
floating-point.

Producing a less-accurate result on the grounds that it can be produced
faster than a more-accurate one looks a whole lot more like the choice an
implementor of a "toy compiler" would make than the approach of ensuring
that the customer receives the most accurate value for the *particular*
context of the *particular* statement as the programmer has coded it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T08:54:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-E23AC6.08543931072003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <bf9rhn$jv3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-7D02F0.01482919072003@corp.supernews.com> <bg3v8k$12ei$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
In article <bg3v8k$12ei$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
 "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> "Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
> news:joe_zitzelberger->
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens


In some special cases a programmer might try to out-think the 
compilier/optimizer -- but why?

The first rule of optimization is "guarentee identical results, fall 
back to no optimization if that is not possible".  So your arguement 
about not trusting a compiler that violates programmer intentions 
strikes me as a bit wierd.

If you can't trust your tools, then you might as well go back to hand 
coding machine language.  

This is a clear case of common subexpress reuse, as the values of A, B 
and C never change in the course of the four statements it is impossible 
for scale, precision, datatype to mess it up.

Just for fun I ran this through the IBM OS/390 v2.2.2 Cobol compiler 
with the fields defined as S9(4) binary -- 16-bit integer (used because 
it looks nice and clean and I won't have to explain floating point 
instructions to financial programming folks reading the thread).

Compute D = D + (A + B + C)
Compute E = E + (A + B + C)
Compute F = F + (A + B + C)
Compute G = G + (A + B + C)

The compiler spit out this:

000264  COMPUTE
    00021C  4830 A002   LH   3,2(0,10)     B
    000220  4A30 A000   AH   3,0(0,10)     A
    000224  4A30 A004   AH   3,4(0,10)     C
    000228  1843        LR   4,3
    00022A  4A30 A006   AH   3,6(0,10)     D
    00022E  1033        LPR  3,3
    000230  4030 A006   STH  3,6(0,10)     D
000265  COMPUTE
    000234  1834        LR   3,4
    000236  4A40 A008   AH   4,8(0,10)     E
    00023A  1044        LPR  4,4
    00023C  4040 A008   STH  4,8(0,10)     E
000266  COMPUTE
    000240  1843        LR   4,3
    000242  4A30 A00A   AH   3,10(0,10)    F
    000246  1033        LPR  3,3
    000248  4030 A00A   STH  3,10(0,10)    F
000267  COMPUTE
    00024C  4A40 A00C   AH   4,12(0,10)    G
    000250  1044        LPR  4,4
    000252  4040 A00C   STH  4,12(0,10)    G

I changed the fields to floating point and packed decimal and the same 
optimizations occurred.

Just for fun I mixed up the datatypes of A, B, C, D, E and F the 
generated code got a bit more complex because of all the type 
promotions, but it still computed the result of (a+b+c) once and added 
it to the four target variables, sometimes with type conversion of the 
intermediate result.

Since the values of A, B and C are never changed in this run of 
statements it is an ideal candidate for common subexpression 
elimination.  I don't have a Cobol for my desktop (yet), but my C and 
Pascal compilers (Metrowerks, $99) are equally skilled at reusing 
intermediate results.  For just about any compiler, this is the first, 
and easiest, place to look for optimizations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T15:01:47+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.00000160.01e6b766@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <bf9rhn$jv3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-7D02F0.01482919072003@corp.supernews.com> <bg3v8k$12ei$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-E23AC6.08543931072003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe,

> So your arguement 
> about not trusting a compiler that violates programmer intentions 
> strikes me as a bit wierd.

It's a fact of life. Programmers make mistakes - even compiler programmers. 
And the ANSI definition isn't clear on subtleties like this, so any 
implementor can do what he wants.

> For just about any compiler, this is the first, 
> and easiest, place to look for optimizations.

Yes, but optimization is outside the scope of the language, and is by 
definition implementor-defined.

You only notice stuff like this either when you write a compiler or when 
you stumble across an arithmetic expression that simply doesn't work.

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T22:16:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AygWa.888$kP6.634@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <bf9rhn$jv3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-7D02F0.01482919072003@corp.supernews.com> <bg3v8k$12ei$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-E23AC6.08543931072003@corp.supernews.com> <VA.00000160.01e6b766@ieee.org>`

```
Sad but true ...

There is NOTHING in the '85 ANSI/ISO Standard (and when ARITHMETIC IS
NATIVE - the default - in the 2002 Standard that says, that if you have
(with no other code in-between)

 Compute A = B + C
 Display A
Compute A = B + C
 Display A

that the same value will be displayed for A.  The '85 Standard definition of
an "arithmetic expression" (the thing after the equal sign in a compute
statement) is TOTALLY implementer defined.  This means that the implementor
may

A) return a "reasonable" evaluation of the arithmetic expression
B) a random value
C) a constant (counting the number of characters in the statement)
D) the time of day
E) anything else they "want"

Having SAID that,  some interpretations are more likely to  keep the vendor
in business than others.

P.S. I have told this story before, but ...

A major COBOL vendor (whose name shall remain unnamed in THIS thread) had an
"interesting feature" for several years after they first produced a '74 then
'85 Standard compiler.  When doing fractional exponentiation, they TRUNCATED
the exponent (not even rounded) and did the exponentiation, i.e.

  Compute = Field-with-value-4 ** (1 / 2)
     would evaluate as
 Compute = Field=with-value-4 ** 0
    =
    1

This passed all the certification tests *AND* it was "fully conforming" to
both the '74 and '85 ANSI and ISO Standards.  Needless-to-say, this
"feature" was not well documented and was "FIXED" when the resources were
available to do so!!!
```

Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> In article <bg3v8k$12ei$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
>  "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

>>   I would regard any compiler that
>> failed to account for that possibility and blindly optimized the
>> four calculations into a single one a "toy compiler" indeed.

…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> intermediate result.

Not to put words in your mouth, but the original statement,

>>>
>>>     COMPUTE D = D + (A + B + C)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> (A+B+C) once and add it to D, E, F & G for you.

then, turned out to be empirically true.

Thanks for going to the trouble.

## Thread: Procedure Division — 6 msgs

### Procedure Division

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T13:16:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3121415.1058447768@dbforums.com>`

```

Respected Gentlemen,

I was reading the documentation of Cobol for Mainframe of Unisis and I
found that:

LINKAGE SECTION.

77 WVARIAVEL1 PIC X(01).

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING WVARIAVEL1
                                              FILE-1
                                              FILE-2.

Where:
FILE-1 and FILE-2 they are native files COBOL.

The mainframe compiler Unisys allows to pass FD of the file as argument.
How does it work? You receive the "pointer" of the file. If the file be
opened in the CALLER program, he will be open in the receiver too. If
you records registrations or to move in the pointer of the file (to walk
for front, to re-position the key), the CALLERprogram had noticed. It is
the SAME file...

Does Microfocus Netexpress support that resource type? How would
it use that?

Respectfully
```

#### ↳ Re: Procedure Division

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T08:53:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf6gqd$1746$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3121415.1058447768@dbforums.com>`

```
I can't speak to the Unisys 2200 side of the fence, or to whether MicroFocus
products have this extension.

But I can say that the ability to pass a file as a parameter to a Unisys
MCP/AS library written in COBOL (which is distinguished from a COBOL "called
program" under ANSI rules only by the capabilities of Unisys extensions) is
indeed a Unisys MCP/AS extension to ANSI COBOL.  It dates back to at least
the COBOL(68) implementation on the Burroughs Large System.

Yes, the "open state" of the file is retained across the CALL.  Remember
that COBOL ANSI IPC calls, as well as COBOL library calls, are
"single-threaded"; while a "called" program is executing, the program that
called it is waiting for the called program to execute an EXIT PROGRAM.  So
long as everybody using the file is sensitive to the possibility that others
may also be using it, and thus accounting for verification of the file's
current state on each call, the answer to the question "how does it work?"
is "Just fine, thank you.  Been working fine for some decades now."

    -Chuck Stevens


"mfcobol2002" <marcos_as@terra.com.br> wrote in message
news:3121415.1058447768@dbforums.com...
>
> Respected Gentlemen,
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Procedure Division

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-17T11:39:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf6jfn$ugi$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3121415.1058447768@dbforums.com> <bf6gqd$1746$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Passing a file to a "subprogram" of some sorts is a medium common extension
to PRE-'85 Standard compilers.  (Once the EXTERNAL attribute was introduced,
I think that few - if any - NEW vendors added this.  Others, such as Unisys
may well have kept it for upward compatibility).

In the IBM mainframe world, the OS/VS COBOL compiler allowed one to pass
either a sequential (QSAM) or Indexed (VSAM) file to a subprogram.  What was
ACTUALLY passed was a pointer to the DCB or FCB, so only an Assembler
subprogram could do anything "useful" with this.  In VS COBOL II and later,
you could pass a QSAM/DCB - but not a VSAM/ACB.  (Using USAGE POINTER data
and a LOT of "systems internal" information, you can even process this in a
semi-useful way.)

In Micro Focus, you should look at the FCDREG compiler directive to see what
it "gives" you.  I don't think you will be able to "port" a Unisys
application (from the information Chuck provided below) DIRECTLY using this.
However, I think (but am not positive) that you should be able to emulate
much of the Unisys FUNCTIONALITY with this Micro Focus feature.
```

#### ↳ Re: Procedure Division

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T07:49:24+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf6utg$p52$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3121415.1058447768@dbforums.com>`

```
mfcobol2002 wrote:

> The mainframe compiler Unisys allows to pass FD of the file as argument.
> How does it work? You receive the "pointer" of the file. If the file be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> it use that?

Declare the file as EXTERNAL in the two programs where one calls the other 
and it will be the same file.  If it is OPENed in the calling program then 
the CALLed program can just READ.

      SELECT filename ASSIGN name EXTERNAL ...
```

#### ↳ Re: Procedure Division

- **From:** hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T02:50:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3124834.1058496652@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3121415.1058447768@dbforums.com>`

```

Richard's suggestion is the correct one in my opinion as well. You might
have to add the "EXTERNAL" phrase to the 01 Level Record definition in
the FD as well (Richard - do you know for sure?). This is how we deal
with UNISYS code int he Fujitsu Compiler....
Thanks,
-Howard
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Procedure Division

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T17:06:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf7vb6$7el$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3121415.1058447768@dbforums.com> <3124834.1058496652@dbforums.com>`

```
hhinman wrote:

> Richard's suggestion is the correct one in my opinion as well. You might
> have to add the "EXTERNAL" phrase to the 01 Level Record definition in
> the FD as well (Richard - do you know for sure?). This is how we deal
> with UNISYS code int he Fujitsu Compiler....

Oops, the EXTERNAL goes on the FD, I should have checked my programs 
instead of glancing at the manual.

The EXTERNAL on the SELECT ASSIGN makes the actual name of the file come 
from a source external to the program, ie from an environment variable.

EXTERNAL on the FD is what is required to make the one file accessible in 
several programs in the same run unit.

      FD   filename EXTERNAL.

      01   ...

I don't use EXTERNAL on the record areas, and don't know if the same record 
areas are used for the file in the two programs, I guess not.  It is 
important that the file attributes be identical in the programs that use 
this.  I was confused when I was getting a run time error in Fujitsu when 
the programs worked fine in MF.  Turns out that Fujitsu requires the file 
name data name to be defined:

      SELECT file ASSIGN data-name ...

In MF the 'data-name' is implicitly defined if it has not been explicitly 
defined, this is how MF has been since CIS Cobol.  Fujitsu requires 
data-name to be explicitly defined and gives a compiler error when it finds 
MOVE something TO data-name.

Of course I had declared the data-name in the program that set up the name 
and OPENed the file, but I had not done so in the program that merely READ 
the file and had no use for the data-name item there.

Fujitsu failed at run-time because the file wasn't OPEN.  Actually it 
treated the two instances of the file, both declared EXTERNAL, as if they 
were two different files instead of the one being shared. It worked fine in 
MF, In the program that OPENed the file it could READ, in the CALLed 
program it failed 'file not open'.

It was very annoying because I had another example where it worked 
perfectly in both systems.  Eventually I tried adding an OPEN into the 
failing program, noticed the missing data-name when setting up the name of 
the file and added this.  _Then_ it complained the file was already open.  
Adding the data-name fixed the problem because now all the attributes were 
the same.
```

---
