```
even
> > though they may be past the mid point of their careers, to acquire new
> > skills and try to improve their value. Sadly, they are a minority.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have been in places where these people (the ones you could do without) are
offered redundancy and a golden handshake to encourage them to leave, while
the ones who are bright are not allowed redundancy because they aren't
supposed to leave. So the dead wood takes the package, goes down the road
and repeats the performance over a 5 year period. Meanwhile, the ones who
move to further their careers are considered traitors and get as little as
the company can get away with giving them, despite many years of sterling
service.

I guess if the dead wood is removed, as you say, some good may come of it.

> > 5. Legacy Management is perceived as a "dead end" management role and
the
> > bright young managers don't want to be there. The ones who are there
tend to
> > be doing a holding action. Keep the costs right down, keep the machines
> > running until we can replace them. Investment in this area cannot be
> > justified, but don't tell the staff that.... (We have seen many examples
of
> > short-sighted management action posted in CLC...usually from the point
of
> > view of the people affected by it and not specifically as an example of
bad
> > management, but easily interpolated to be such.)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Really pleased to hear it. I am appalled when I read some of the posts in
CLC, to think that there are managers out there who appear to have no idea
about managing or motivating. I discussed this with several Senior managers
I know both here and in Europe (I don't know any in the US, but I'm sure
those of you who do can do your own research) and they independently agreed
(although not unanimously, so maybe it depends on the organisational
"culture" also...) that most of the rising middle managers don't want to
manage Legacy systems. It seems that the less able managers end up in
"Legacy Management" because the "high flyers" see it as a bad career move
and bypass it on the way up. Obviously, this is a generalization, as are all
the conclusions from the thread, and I'm sure there are exceptions. Glad to
see that your site is one.

> We have a "pet project" in the organization that all object-oriented,
> Oracle/Java based, blah blah blah.  It's had some major problems (mostly
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Can't over-stress the importance of the Pilot projects. This should be
monitored very carefully and if it is going off the rails, they should get
help.

> These young officers actually listen to us, even come to us and ask us
> what we're doing, what we think we should be doing, where we should be
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Excellent!

> It's actually a pretty exciting time - of course, getting the go-ahead
> to make this happen is making us put our money where our mouth is...  :)
>
> > The factors that nurture the Fortress mentality are going to be with us
for
> > some time (about 12 years, I reckon <G>) so individuals may want to
think
>
> There's that magic number again...  12-level data items, 12 years left
> for FC (fortress COBOL) - Would your favorite number happen to be 12?
>

Nope. I don't get any more emotional about numbers than I do about
Programming Languages.

The 12 levels in my code go back to the days of punch cards. If cards got
minced in the reader (and they did quite often) the girls would have to
rebuild the source deck from a listing.  Lineflo was not always clear, and
it certainly gave no indication of what columns things were in. It helped
them if the level number showed what column they should start the line in.
Hence, start in column 12 and indent 3 columns from there, so 15, 18, 21,
etc. They used to punch up a card with "tabs" on it so they could tab to the
various columns for a COBOL listing. The fact was that punching COBOL was
not part of their normal jobs and so they wouldn't have a standard "tab temp
late" for it.  They all knew that 01 levels were in column 8 because we
wrote that on the listing.  Over the years, I found that this numbering
scheme is a very useful one for COBOL, and now I do it as a kind of respect
to the people I worked with then who taught me so much, and also from force
of habit. There is no deep significance to it <G>.
I have seen several other people use this scheme as well. I figure they are
old-timers like me...

As for the twelve years ... that would bring us to 2015. I am on record as
predicting that by then COBOL will not be a generally used programming
language and "computer programming" as we understood it for the decades from
1960 to 2000 will not be around. This is based on trends which started
emerging in the early-mid 90s. I am also expecting at least one major change
to the way we develop commercial software within the next 10 years.
Actually, the closing down of "in-house" COBOL computer programming is
running pretty close to this schedule. But it is not life and death. If I
get it wrong I'll be embarrassed (and deserve to be)...

So there is nothing special about "12" (at least, not in my universe; the
number has various mystical associations in other universes, which I won't
go into here...) and two swallows do not a summer make <G>.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> "LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> I guess if the dead wood is removed, as you say, some good may come of it.

I've seen some people stick around that you wonder how in the world they 
pulled it off...

>>We have a "pet project" in the organization that all object-oriented,
>>Oracle/Java based, blah blah blah.  It's had some major problems (mostly
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> help.

The problem they ran into was that they had one person writing 
requirements, and when they came to acceptance milestone 1, they brought 
a group of folks in from the field who completely rejected what this 
person had written.  So, these folks rewrote requirements and went back 
to their base.  When the next milestone came around, a different group 
came back, and basically did the same thing the first group.

Our management finally sat down and said "You need to poll your folks 
and come up with the requirements so that we can build the system to 
those specs."  They also set up a Configuration Control Board (CCB) for 
new requirements, and defined an accepted look-and-feel, that absolutely 
-would not- be changed for the duration of this initial project.  I 
think they've about completed it now, and it's going smoother than it 
did in the beginning.  I still can't believe it's taken that long.

>>There's that magic number again...  12-level data items, 12 years left
>>for FC (fortress COBOL) - Would your favorite number happen to be 12?
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hence, start in column 12 and indent 3 columns from there, so 15, 18, 21,

You know, I didn't get that until you said it.  That's actually pretty 
cool (I indent 4, so I'd use 16, 20, etc.) - I might just adopt that. 
(The only standard at work is that you can't have them sequentially 
numbered (01, 02, 03, etc.).)

> As for the twelve years ... that would bring us to 2015. I am on record as
> predicting that by then COBOL will not be a generally used programming
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> get it wrong I'll be embarrassed (and deserve to be)...

But you can be grateful to be around to be embarrassed...  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T08:24:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> "LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> OUCH!

Yes - things were not happy around our office for a while...  Even 
though they were in a different office, their manager was also our 
manager, so we got some of the stress by proxy.

> I'd go for Joint Development Workshops. Why have one poor sod writing the
> requirements? They should be derived from a Workshop (or workshops) where
> ALL interested parties get to input. (You need a good Chairperson/Project
> Manager to keep things on track, otherwise it gets a bit like CLC <G>)

They thought it would be efficient - have one person working directly 
with the programmers (actually collocated) would give great feedback, 
etc.  The workshop idea would have been a much better way for initial 
requirements gathering.

But then, on the other hand, our customers have a severe lack of vision 
about what they want.  We constantly find that they'll ask for 
something, we'll build it, and they decide that it's not really what 
they want or need.

There was one specific case that comes to mind.  In this project, we've 
changed security from "you can do everything except what we say you 
can't" to "you can only do what we say you can".  We're doing this 
through profiles, and there is a screen that lets you delete an entire 
profile.  The user said that when you deleted a profile, if there were 
any users using that profile, you had to provide a replacement profile 
for them.  We told them that that didn't make any since, but they were 
insistent.  Well, when testing time came around, all of a sudden they 
understood, and that code actually was backed out before it was ever 
released.

>>>As for the twelve years ... that would bring us to 2015. I am on record
[snip]
>>> If I get it wrong I'll be embarrassed (and deserve to be)...
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> changing his poultice...>  (Thanks for that, Daniel...<G>)

Heck, -I'll- be grateful to be around then - that wasn't a dig at your 
age...  :)  We're not guaranteed tomorrow, you know...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T09:46:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfbi3i$b1$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com>,
LX-i  <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:

[snip]

>But then, on the other hand, our customers have a severe lack of vision 
>about what they want.

Ummmm... folks complain about COBOL-coders being 'too wordy' and I think I 
might see a typical example of this in the sentence above.  Would any 
truth be lost or any accuracy disturbed were 'have a severe lack of vision 
about' (seven words) to be replaced with 'don't know'?

>We constantly find that they'll ask for 
>something, we'll build it, and they decide that it's not really what 
>they want or need.

*This* is one of the reasons why, e'er-so-long-ago, I was taught that 
Signoff is Sacred as it prevents the wasting of corporate resources.

Imagine going to buy a custom-made car and when the product is delivered
saying 'Yes, that's my signature on the purchase-order next to 'install
hot-pink vinyl seat covers'... but now that I see it I've reconsidered my
options and find that this particular choice will damage my
mission-effective posture so I'm going to refuse delivery, demand
alteration and refuse to accept that the delivery-date has changed.'

I'll not elaborate at this point any further.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T09:50:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhimlu765e2983@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bfbi3i$b1$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <bfbi3i$b1$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> In article <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com>,
> LX-i  <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> about' (seven words) to be replaced with 'don't know'?

Well, I'm the first to admit that sometimes I use more words than 
necessary - but, I did that long before I learned COBOL.  :)  What I was 
trying to convey was that they had a concept (they -did- know what they 
wanted), they just didn't have a vision of what it would look like 
completed.  Maybe they do, but they just can't (for whatever reason) 
articulate it to the developers.  They certainly seem to know right off 
the bat if "that ain't it"...

>>We constantly find that they'll ask for 
>>something, we'll build it, and they decide that it's not really what 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll not elaborate at this point any further.

That's what we've been fighting for the last two months.  Our deadline 
was 8 July, and we had 2 new control numbers (requirements) added on 3 
July.  It never seems to end.  (The 2 new requirements were simple 
tweaks, so we were able to do them, but others are not so trivial.)  We 
finally had to start saying "no" when they asked us to do things, and 
tell them to write it up (if it's a defect) in the testing phase, which 
started yesterday afternoon.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T17:08:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhjgad9tff6lf8@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bfccmf$k1l$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <bfbi3i$b1$1@panix1.panix.com> <vhimlu765e2983@corp.supernews.com> <bfccmf$k1l$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> In article <vhimlu765e2983@corp.supernews.com>,
> LX-i  <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Be prepared for some... heated interactions.

There's the catch - although it's easy to say "I told you so" in a lot 
of these circumstances, I also welcome the opportunity to tweak this 
product so it'll be the best it can be before it hits the field.  I hope 
they don't find a whole lot wrong with my stuff, though, as I'm in the 
midst of some "research programming" (sounds better than "playing"), 
trying to get a concept to work.

If I can make it work, we can place all our subprograms in one place, 
and if one changes, we swap it out without having to statically relink 
every program.  I have a feeling this is going to be like my trying to 
get our shop to COBOL 85.  I'll work for a few months, figure it out, 
convert a few of my programs, and get resistance from nearly everyone. 
Then, the next time a change has to occur to one of these subroutines, 
and we have to go through and compile -every- program in the system, all 
of a sudden I won't look so crazy after all.

The thing I can't really get some people, who feel that "that's the way 
we've always done it, and it's always worked", to embrace anything new. 
  In fact, we have a major file contention problem in the field, which 
we cannot fix without releasing every online program in the system. 
Getting this to work would allow us to respond rapidly to required 
changes, instead of leveling a system-wide test every time one of these 
modules changes...
```

```
I often receive specfications from users who have no understanding fo how
database keys work. They want data displayed from multiple database tables,
but there is usually at least one that  cannot be reached because there is
no data available to build the key that is needed. It usually take over an
hour to convince them that the requirements are impossibe to implement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T21:30:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfcrbn$9n3$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhimlu765e2983@corp.supernews.com> <bfccmf$k1l$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c34e55$c4c77ae0$59caf943@chottel>`

```
In article <01c34e55$c4c77ae0$59caf943@chottel>,
Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:
>I often receive specfications from users who have no understanding fo how
>database keys work. They want data displayed from multiple database tables,
>but there is usually at least one that  cannot be reached because there is
>no data available to build the key that is needed. It usually take over an
>hour to convince them that the requirements are impossibe to implement.

I used to try to explain such things... but things have changed.

'Hey, I want these data.'

'Sorry, it can't be done.'

'What do you mean 'it can't be done'?'

'Just what I said... it can't be done.'

'Why can't it be done?'

'It cannot be done for technical reasons.  Are you 'technical'?'

NOTE:  For some reason being 'not technical' is worn by many as a badge of 
pride.  At any rate the question of 'Are you 'technical'?' may be 
recognised by some as a tactic called (in chess) a 'fork'.  Consider:

'Yes, I am 'technical'.'

'Good!  Then you will find it easy to accept that no data exist to build 
the relationship which you would rely on to associate these data; these 
relationships were not considered when the system's architecture was 
established.  Sorry about that, better luck next time!'

... or ...

'No, I'm not 'technical'.'

'Well, as I said, the reasons are 'technical'; you would need to develop, 
at the very least, the vocabulary necessary to begin the explanation.  
Feel free to come back, though, when you have!'

DD

>
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote in article <bfccmf$k1l$1@panix1.panix.com>...
…[81 more quoted lines elided]…
>>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T20:44:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhjt05glai46c6@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<01c34e55$c4c77ae0$59caf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <bfbi3i$b1$1@panix1.panix.com> <vhimlu765e2983@corp.supernews.com> <bfccmf$k1l$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c34e55$c4c77ae0$59caf943@chottel>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:

> I often receive specfications from users who have no understanding fo how
> database keys work. They want data displayed from multiple database tables,
> but there is usually at least one that  cannot be reached because there is
> no data available to build the key that is needed. It usually take over an
> hour to convince them that the requirements are impossibe to implement.

This is interesting.  We have a situation right now where (in the field 
version) we write aircraft maintenance to a set of tapes when it's 
archived, and flight and sortie data to another set.  Of course, once 
the data is on the tape, there's no linkage between the two, but while 
they're in the database, there's a manual set (DMS) that links a 
particular maintenance action with a particular sortie.

In our current project, we made relational tables to hold the data 
that's now being pushed to tape.  Our user have just figured out that 
there's -still- no linkage (as the same data that was on the tapes is 
now in the database).  They couldn't understand why not...  After 
several iterations, we finally all got together around a table and 
explained it to all of them.

It's probably going to be one of those things we get to "fix" during 
"testing"...  :)  Heck, it keeps us employed and out of trouble...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T01:51:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c34f2a$9f8637a0$44ccf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <bfbi3i$b1$1@panix1.panix.com> <vhimlu765e2983@corp.supernews.com> <bfccmf$k1l$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c34e55$c4c77ae0$59caf943@chottel> <ZOoSa.63225$0v4.4252713@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Well it depends on who has the power, the user or the IT department.  Since
that time things have changed a little. We seldom talk directly to users
anymore. Now we have "subject matter experts" (SME) translate what the
users want into something programmers can implement.  Of course if the SME
doesn't understand database keys then you can have the same problem.
Luckily the one I deal with now was a programmer in a former life.

The data base key problem was the least of it.  That person also did not
understand that the screen had a limited number of lines with a limited
number of characters on each line, much less what an attribute byte was.
She was good at telling management what they wanted to hear and did resort 
to lies to cover her ass. Management was NOT interested in documentation
would confirm/expose those lies. The head guy had hired her, therefore she
could not be wrong, otherwise that might reflect negatively on "he who must
appear to always be right".

It is a highly varied world out there and not everyone get to work with
great people all the time like Pete Dashwood does :-)

Hugh Candlin <no@spam.com> wrote in article
<ZOoSa.63225$0v4.4252713@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>...

how

> tables,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T22:13:42+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1bbd7b_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <bfbi3i$b1$1@panix1.panix.com> <vhimlu765e2983@corp.supernews.com> <bfccmf$k1l$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c34e55$c4c77ae0$59caf943@chottel> <ZOoSa.63225$0v4.4252713@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <01c34f2a$9f8637a0$44ccf943@chottel>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c34f2a$9f8637a0$44ccf943@chottel...
>
> It is a highly varied world out there and not everyone get to work with
> great people all the time like Pete Dashwood does :-)
>
Yes, Charles, my IT career has been a simple tiptoe through the tulips...

I have always had understanding Management, highly motivated subordinates,
helpful and sympathetic peers, sweet talking, light hearted, positive go
getters who just make you want to jump out of bed in the morning and get
into work so you can spend more time around them.

(That, of course, is exactly why I am so at home in CLC...)

In 37 years and countless installations, across 7 countries and cultures, I
never met a colleague (or a Manager) I didn't like.

And everybody LOVED me.

(puts down hash pipe and dons yellow robe...

A traveller came to the gates of a great City and enquired of the beggar at
the entrance...

"What are the people in this city like?"

The beggar replies: "What were they like in the place where you came from?"

"Oh, they were villains. Self-seeking, scum sucking rip-off merchants who
would have the fillings out of your teeth while you slept."

The beggar sighs..."I'm afraid they're very much like that here..."

The traveller kicks his weary horse and moves on away from the City.

A few minutes later another traveller arrives at the Gates and addresses the
beggar:

"What are the people in this city like?"

The beggar replies: "What were they like in the place where you came from?"

The travellers eyes light up and enthusiasm creeps into his voice as he
says:

"They were the salt of the earth! Kind, decent people, who'd go out of their
way to help a stranger. Industrious, honest, fun-loving and helpful. I was
sorry to leave, and retain many goods friends there..."

"They're exactly like that here..." smiles the beggar.

The traveller walks his horse into the City and refreshes himself and the
animal at the well...

(takes out ping pong ball eyes, removes yellow robe, and returns to problem
of getting odbcconf.exe to behave itself  and configure ODBC DSNs from
inside the Wise Installer...)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T14:46:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c34e04$8cd04140$3a91f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Plsease see below.

LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in article
<vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com>...
> Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>requirements, and when they came to acceptance milestone 1, they
brought
> >>a group of folks in from the field who completely rejected what this
> >>person had written.  So, these folks rewrote requirements and went back
> >>to their base.  When the next milestone came around, a different group
> >>came back, and basically did the same thing the first group.
> >>

We had a similiar situation where one user representative determined the
requirements and one specialist wrote the requirements. There were actually
two related systems to be developed. After 2.5 years the subsystem I worked
on was complete but the other one was not finished. The user rep left for
another job and the new user rep wanted a PC based system. The whole thing
( approx 125 programs accessing 70+ databases) was dropped and they worked
on the PC based system for another year, before that was abandoned. Not
sure if that was for politics or if they just could not get it done. This
is the only real failure I have ever been associated with.

<snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T15:28:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e04$8cd04140$3a91f243@chottel> <vhin2v512lq289@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Yes, it was really hard to take. Originally they started out with only two
programmers and added me later. Then the lead programmer left due to a
difficult pregnancy. When I took over there was no written project plan at
all. I created a PERT chart and detailed plan that showed it would take 3
programmers 10 years to complete the project. Then they added more
manpower. I think that after 2.5 years for the completion of our subsystem
we missed my time estimate by 2 months. This was maily due to constant
changes to the requirements. There were a lot of complicated politics and
personal relantionships involved. Some in management were delighted because
they never wanted to replace the existing system in the first place. We
ended up making some enhancements to it which was what they wanted in the
first place. Now the whole thing has been replaced by another system
developed by another group. Their first release left out some 1099 tax
credit processing and I helped the user manually create the data that they
needed to meet the IRS requirements. I received an award from the user
organization.  When the assistant commisioner of my organization presented
it to me he had no idea what it was for. Since it wasn't coming from him
directly it meant nothing to him. I felt so validated.

<vhin2v512lq289@corp.supernews.com>...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T15:44:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhjbdn7uqal361@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e04$8cd04140$3a91f243@chottel> <vhin2v512lq289@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:

> I created a PERT chart and detailed plan that showed it would take 3
> programmers 10 years to complete the project. Then they added more
> manpower. I think that after 2.5 years for the completion of our subsystem
> we missed my time estimate by 2 months. This was maily due to constant
> changes to the requirements.

When I went through training for my last job upgrade (3C052 to 3C072, 
which is the highest level for programmers in the Air Force), the 
material said that the most costly and most prevalent errors found in 
software systems were not design errors or construction errors, but 
requirements errors.  In the flow of designing a project, as much time 
should be given to gathering requirements (for example, like Pete 
suggested elsewhere in this thread, a working group of target end users) 
as is given to design -and- code.  Then, testing should get just as much.

Our users don't want to hear that.  The one thing they have built into 
the schedule is a pretty long testing phase, which is good - we'll need 
it since we didn't have solid requirements for most of the project.  :)

> There were a lot of complicated politics and
> personal relantionships involved. Some in management were delighted because
> they never wanted to replace the existing system in the first place.

I wonder sometimes if that's what happening to us.  I really think it 
was, then management changed.  The person that was doing this was 
actually promoted up to the next level.  Now, he's re-evaluating what he 
(and other like him) did and seeing that it didn't make a lot of sense. 
  Additionally, the person he replaced is now over our sub-organization 
(the programmers), and now -he- is seeing even more of the light.

> needed to meet the IRS requirements. I received an award from the user
> organization.  When the assistant commisioner of my organization presented
> it to me he had no idea what it was for. Since it wasn't coming from him
> directly it meant nothing to him. I felt so validated.

At least something good came of it...  I know that's not much 
consolation, but sometimes you take what you can get.  I can't tell 
whether that last sentence was said sarcastically or seriously, but I'd 
be validated.  One of the biggest things I've done is make an online 
help system for our database, and every time I run into someone who 
tells me how useful that is, and how much it helps them, I'm good for a 
week...  :)
```

- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e04$8cd04140$3a91f243@chottel> <vhin2v512lq289@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel> <vhjbdn7uqal361@corp.supernews.com> <vhjh84m3hne627@corp.supernews.com> <vhjn40hatvcff6@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vhjn40hatvcff6@corp.supernews.com...
> Rick Smith wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> This Glenford Meyers fellow sounds pretty astute...  :)

Unfortunately I misspelled his name. It should have been
Glenford Myers.

Myers, like many of his contemporaries, might now be
considered a Waterfallian; that is, one who suggests that
requirements, specifications, and design precede coding.
He was a co-author of the paper titled "Structured Design"
that appeared in IBM Systems Journal, Vol 13, No. 2, 1974.
Myers called his method "composite design."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T15:38:26+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1a0f58_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e04$8cd04140$3a91f243@chottel> <vhin2v512lq289@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel> <vhjbdn7uqal361@corp.supernews.com> <vhjh84m3hne627@corp.supernews.com> <vhjn40hatvcff6@corp.supernews.com> <vhjqfatg5q1d34@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:vhjqfatg5q1d34@corp.supernews.com...
>
<snip>
> Myers, like many of his contemporaries, might now be
> considered a Waterfallian; that is, one who suggests that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That wouldn't make him a Waterfallian; that would simply make him sensible.

Requirements, specification, and design, precede coding on my projects also,
but they are not Waterfall projects.

The difference is that this process is iterated through a timebox with
evaluation and re-prioritisation following coding and testing.The
specification  and design are informal, and coding is allowed to evolve (by
iteration) towards a solution acceptable to all parties. This approach is
fluid and dynamic and copes very well with change, even while the system is
being developed. (The general approach is Goal oriented, and positive.)

It is SIGNING OFF (so it cannot easily be revisited) and refusing to start a
phase until the previous one IS signed off, that identifies the
Waterfall.(The general approach is task oriented and the prime criterion is
"that no blame shall attach itself to us")

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T02:53:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhkf657fcjig85@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e04$8cd04140$3a91f243@chottel> <vhin2v512lq289@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel> <vhjbdn7uqal361@corp.supernews.com> <vhjh84m3hne627@corp.supernews.com> <vhjn40hatvcff6@corp.supernews.com> <vhjqfatg5q1d34@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a0f58_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```

Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f1a0f58_5@news.athenanews.com...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> That wouldn't make him a Waterfallian; that would simply make him
sensible.

I often wish that I had expressed things differently in my posts.
That post was no exception. How about: proceed through
requirement, specification, and design steps before coding?

Recall that Waterfall began in 1970. It was more common
before Waterfall, as I understand from Boehm, to 'code and
fix'. Myers work with 'composite design' was through 1974,
when the 'structured design' paper was published, and
appears to have begun, from various referenced papers,
prior to the adoption of Waterfall. For comparison, elements
of the 'structured design' paper contributed by Constantine
were acknowledged to have been developed over a ten year
period.

Many authors, primarily in the 70's, concentrated on various
aspects of Waterfall. Some, like Tausworthe and Gunther,
presented complete formal systems--some presented parts
of the Waterfall, formally. Myers concentrated on formal
descriptions of design.

To complete the correction; Myers, like many of his peers,
might now be considered as having been a Waterfallian.
This would be understood as in contrast to the later
Prototyper or RADian. <g>

> Requirements, specification, and design, precede coding on my projects
also,
> but they are not Waterfall projects.
>
> The difference is that this process is iterated through a timebox with
> evaluation and re-prioritisation following coding and testing.The
> specification  and design are informal, and coding is allowed to evolve
(by
> iteration) towards a solution acceptable to all parties. This approach is
> fluid and dynamic and copes very well with change, even while the system
is
> being developed. (The general approach is Goal oriented, and positive.)
>
> It is SIGNING OFF (so it cannot easily be revisited) and refusing to start
a
> phase until the previous one IS signed off, that identifies the
> Waterfall.(The general approach is task oriented and the prime criterion
is
> "that no blame shall attach itself to us")

I'll disagree that 'signing off' provides identity to Waterfall
because it is the arrangement of the steps that give Waterfall
its name! It is the fomalism attached to Waterfall that requires
'signing off'. Waterfall may be used informally in small projects.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T01:10:11+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1a9558_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```

"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
news:vhkf657fcjig85@corp.supernews.com...
>
>
> I'll disagree that 'signing off' provides identity to Waterfall
> because it is the arrangement of the steps that give Waterfall
> its name!

No, not only that. It wouldn't be a Waterfall if the steps flowed into each
other fluidly. It is the signoff that separates the phases and creates the
Waterfall.

>>>
 It is the fomalism attached to Waterfall that requires
> 'signing off'.
<<<
Yes, I agree that is so. We are probably not so far apart on this to make it
worth arguing.

>>>
Waterfall may be used informally in small projects.
> >>
I have never seen it so. My take on that is that "informal waterfall" is as
oxymoronic as "military intelligence" or "political honesty".

Pete.
>
>
```

- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e04$8cd04140$3a91f243@chottel> <vhin2v512lq289@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel> <vhjbdn7uqal361@corp.supernews.com> <vhjh84m3hne627@corp.supernews.com> <vhjn40hatvcff6@corp.supernews.com> <vhjqfatg5q1d34@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a0f58_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhkf657fcjig85@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a9558_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```

Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3f1a9558_7@news.athenanews.com...
>
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
> news:vhkf657fcjig85@corp.supernews.com...
[snip]
> Waterfall may be used informally in small projects.
> > >>
> I have never seen it so. My take on that is that "informal waterfall" is
as
> oxymoronic as "military intelligence" or "political honesty".

Requirement - what are the goals?
Specification - how will we know the goals were met?
Design - how do we structure (partition) the solution?
Code - Duh! <g>
Test - were the goals met?

I want a component that does this.
These are its interfaces (inputs and outputs) and processes.
These are its classes (data and methods) and collaborations.
This is the finished component.
Test it.

Is there a trail of paperwork?
Does not each step need to be substantially complete before
the next step is begun?
Informal Waterfall?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T09:46:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfe6fr$qlg$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhjn40hatvcff6@corp.supernews.com> <vhjqfatg5q1d34@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a0f58_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```
In article <3f1a0f58_5@news.athenanews.com>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>It is SIGNING OFF (so it cannot easily be revisited) and refusing to start a
>phase until the previous one IS signed off, that identifies the
>Waterfall.(The general approach is task oriented and the prime criterion is
>"that no blame shall attach itself to us")

Assessing responsibility, Mr Dashwood, can be just as much a matter of
'giving credit' as 'attaching blame'... curious that you approach the
circumstance of signoff from such a negative side.  When it comes to
signoff I, for one, do not view it as a matter of 'attaching blame' but as
a matter of someone saying 'I am pleased enough with the results to attach
my signature to it.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T02:34:50+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1aa930_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhjn40hatvcff6@corp.supernews.com> <vhjqfatg5q1d34@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a0f58_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfe6fr$qlg$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bfe6fr$qlg$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <3f1a0f58_5@news.athenanews.com>,
> Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >It is SIGNING OFF (so it cannot easily be revisited) and refusing to
start a
> >phase until the previous one IS signed off, that identifies the
> >Waterfall.(The general approach is task oriented and the prime criterion
is
> >"that no blame shall attach itself to us")
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

LOL!

And that would be on Planet Cyberia where everybody loves computers...

Ah, but then I forgot...you ARE the King of England...

Nope.  Here on Earth, Signature ("sign off") simply indicates that the
agreed deliverables were delivered. ("Deliverables" in this sense could be a
set of requirements...) It carries neither praise nor blame, approval nor
disapproval. It has never, in my experience, been a statement of  "I am
pleased enough with the results to attach my signature to it."

It is more of the nature  of..."If I sign this will you please get on with
the next phase because we really need this system..."

However, if things go badly wrong, or the user complains that required
functionality has not been delivered, this will be used by IT as a
justification. ("We did what you agreed and here's your signature to prove
it."  Hence, no blame attaches to us. We knew very well that the screen
layouts you signed off were missing some essential fields but YOU signed it
off... Of course we'll add them now, but it will take time and cost
money...then we have to regression test and revisit the previous phases you
signed off, to ensure the new inclusions don't upset anything... Oh, and
we'll need a couple fo contractors to ensure our existing committments are
met while we deal with this newly imposed unforseen workload. Just sign here
to indicate you do want these fields, and we'll get started (as soon as we
have your signature on the budget approval...)...

Not a bit like Cyberia, really...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T11:50:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfedo9$7kv$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <3f1a0f58_5@news.athenanews.com> <bfe6fr$qlg$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f1aa930_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```
In article <3f1aa930_5@news.athenanews.com>,
Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bfe6fr$qlg$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>And that would be on Planet Cyberia where everybody loves computers...

No, Mr Dashwood, it would be *any* place where people are expected to 
verify that-which-has-been-delivered is to be applied to 
that-which-was-requested.

You requested a box containing 17 blue, left-handed widgets in good 
working order.  Here is a box containing 17 blue, left-handed widgets in 
good working order.  Please feel free to inspect and test the contents... 
but before you take posession of them for your own full and unfettered 
used you are to sign this sheet saying that what was delivered was what 
you ordered.

>
>Ah, but then I forgot...you ARE the King of England...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>disapproval.

This sort of neutrality seems to be contradicted by your previous
assertion (quoted above) that 'the prime criterion is "that no blame shall
attach itself to us")'.

>It has never, in my experience, been a statement of  "I am
>pleased enough with the results to attach my signature to it."

Your experiences differ from mine, of course... when *my* work pleases me 
I sign and release it.

>
>It is more of the nature  of..."If I sign this will you please get on with
>the next phase because we really need this system..."

This also appears to contradict that 'the prime criterion is "that no 
blame shall attach itself to us")'... in a few short paragraphs you have 
moved from defensiveness to neutrality to continuity.

>
>However, if things go badly wrong, or the user complains that required
>functionality has not been delivered, this will be used by IT as a
>justification. ("We did what you agreed and here's your signature to prove
>it."  Hence, no blame attaches to us.

No, Mr Dashwood, 'hence what you received satisfied your requirements for 
that-which-was-requested that you certified it with your signature.'

>We knew very well that the screen
>layouts you signed off were missing some essential fields but YOU signed it
>off...

This does not seem to differ from 'Hey, there are only 12 widgets in this 
box!'; it is up to the recipient to say 'This is what I requested', last I 
looked.

The Anciente Formulation is 'By my signature affixed I do hereby 
acknowledge that...'; how is any work to be done, Mr Dashwood, in absence 
of an acknowledgement?

DD
```

```
> set of requirements...) It carries neither praise nor blame, approval nor

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

here

Peter, what if it is a customer, and they are asking *you* to quote a fixed
price ... *everything* they have not thought of is to come out of your own
pocket?  And they also want your signature on the other side of the
document?  Those are the conditions I have been working under for
twenty-five years, at least. You can call is ass covering till the cows come
home, and all I'll say is "damned right".

 I'm a contractor.  I build to blueprint. You want me to draw up some
blueprints, fine.  I charge for that.  I can give you what I think you want,
and explain exactly what you are getting, or I can build exactly what you
ask for. I cannot do both at the same time, and telll you how much it will
cost before I start.  For that, I can only give you a rate on a time basis.

Donald



Donald
```

```
"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:TRASa.505$1I5.74370@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3f1aa930_5@news.athenanews.com...
> >
>
> Peter, what if it is a customer, and they are asking *you* to quote a
fixed
> price ... *everything* they have not thought of is to come out of your own
> pocket?  And they also want your signature on the other side of the
> document?  Those are the conditions I have been working under for
> twenty-five years, at least. You can call is ass covering till the cows
come
> home, and all I'll say is "damned right".
>

Totally understand that, Don. I've been there many times.

This "discussion" is not about never signing anything. There are cases where
an agreement must be struck and this typically requires a signature from
BOTH parties.

My whimsy in this thread is about the use of SIGN OFFs in the Waterfall
development Methodology. That's all.

I am not suggesting nothing should ever be signed by anybody or that there
are no legitimate cases where a signature protects BOTH parties; my
objection is to a specific mis-use of this process within certain Corporate
hierarchies.

>  I'm a contractor.  I build to blueprint. You want me to draw up some
> blueprints, fine.  I charge for that.  I can give you what I think you
want,
> and explain exactly what you are getting, or I can build exactly what you
> ask for. I cannot do both at the same time, and telll you how much it will
> cost before I start.  For that, I can only give you a rate on a time
basis.
>

No Argument.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <vhihkbn87th413@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e04$8cd04140$3a91f243@chottel> <vhin2v512lq289@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel> <vhjbdn7uqal361@corp.supernews.com>`

```


LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in article
<vhjbdn7uqal361@corp.supernews.com>...
<snip>
> At least something good came of it...  I know that's not much 
> consolation, but sometimes you take what you can get.  I can't tell 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Well I was happy that the user was so appreciative. I was just doing my job
and did not think it was anything particulary special. I was quite surprise
to learn of the award as I had no idea what I could have done to earn it. 
I thought my management should at least be glad that I had done something
that made our organization look good. The guy presenting the award was an
SESer and his office had a table that could seat 40 people and it had
another area with a sofa and several chairs. He seemed to me to be a pretty
gruff person. He asked me if I had any issues that I wanted to raise, but I
declined because he was a very powerful person that I did not know very
well and I have learned that in these types of situations there may well be
hidden motives at work.

Awards and bonuses are funny. Often when I thought I deserved them I did
not receive them. A product I helped develop named Code1 earned the company
6.5 million the first nine months. They sold one copy for only 8000 less
than my yearly salary. Over the years it has earned them multiple millions.
 If they had kept the original bonus system that I was hired under I would
have become pretty well off. Before I was hired I asked if they planned to
change it and they said no. Three months after I started they changed it
completely. My total bonus was zero. Now days I find I am receiving more
awards including share awards (money) and commissioner unit citations. I
think it is because the team I am on is all contractors except for me and
the project leader. The contractors are not eligible for the awards but
management wants to give at least one award to each group so it looks like
good things are happening within their empire.
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

```
Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:

[snip]

>There were a lot of complicated politics and
>personal relantionships involved. Some in management were delighted because
>they never wanted to replace the existing system in the first place.

I can never find the original of this... but all over the web one sees 
quoted "There is nothing more difficult to plan, more doubtful of success, 
nor more dangerous to manage than the creation of a new system. For the 
initiator has the enmity of all who would profit by the preservation of 
the old system and merely lukewarm defenders in those who would gain by 
the new one." --Machiavelli, 1513

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T18:13:45-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhjgoapp71jr09@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c34e04$8cd04140$3a91f243@chottel> <vhin2v512lq289@corp.supernews.com> <01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel> <bfcd21$m1i$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bfcd21$m1i$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <01c34e0a$739e4ea0$3a91f243@chottel>,
> Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >personal relantionships involved. Some in management were delighted
because
> >they never wanted to replace the existing system in the first place.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the new one." --Machiavelli, 1513

Niccolo Machiavelli, "The Prince," The Oxford University
Press "World's Classics" translation by Luigi Ricci,
Chapter 6, "Of New Dominions Which Have Been
Acquired by One's Own Arms and Ability",
"Those who by exercise of abilities such as these become
princes, obtain dominions with difficulty but retain them easily,
and the diffuculties which they have in acquiring their dominions
arise in part from the new rules and regulations that they have
to introduce in order to establish their position securely. It must
be considered that there is nothing more difficult to carry out,
nor more doubtful of success, nor more dangerous to handle,
than to initiate a new order of things. For the reformer has
enemies in all those who profit by the old order, and only
lukewarm defenders in all those who would profit by the new
order, this lukewarmness arising partly from fear of their
adversaries, who have the laws in their favour; and partly from
the incredulity of mankind, who do not believe in anything new
until they have actual experience of it."
```

```
Colliers states that Machiavelli wrote numerous letters to a
friend in Rome in 1513-1514 and a number of topics were
discussed. It may be that the quote is actually a translation
from one of those letters. Colliers also states that 'The Prince'
was written in 1513. I don't know that I can add more.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T09:26:07-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfe59f$l0b$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhjgoapp71jr09@corp.supernews.com> <bfcrt2$c3u$1@panix1.panix.com> <vhkup2t85n1074@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <vhkup2t85n1074@corp.supernews.com>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bfcrt2$c3u$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
>was written in 1513. I don't know that I can add more.

I can, but just a bit.  The text from the version you cited appears to 
match that given on 
<http://www.adams.edu/academics/art_letters/hgp/civ/111/1machiavelli_the_prince.html>

... which is an online course at Adams State College and it has been my 
experience that professors frequently use handouts which accurately 
reflect the contents of the work.  One may also consider the Project 
Gutenberg sample at http://www.ibiblio.org/gutenberg/etext98/tprnc10.txt 
(downloadable at http://www.ibiblio.org/gutenberg/etext98/tprnc10.zip ); 
this is presented as a copy of the Marriott translation (c. 1916, 
MacMillian Company) and rendered in HTML at 
<http://www.ilt.columbia.edu/Publications/Projects/digitexts/machiavelli/the_prince/chapter06.html>

... in what appears to be identical wording; Chap VI, para 5, sent. 3 - 5 
shows as:

--begin quoted text:

And it ought to be remembered that there is nothing more difficult to take
in hand, more perilous to conduct, or more uncertain in its success, then
to take the lead in the introduction of a new order of things. Because the
innovator has for enemies all those who have done well under the old
conditions, and lukewarm defenders in those who may do well under the new.
This coolness arises partly from fear of the opponents, who have the laws
on their side, and partly from the incredulity of men, who do not readily
believe in new things until they have had a long experience of them.

--end quoted text

Although I do not have access to a copy of the original text (and I am not 
fluent in the Italian in which it was written) the variations between the 
texts do not seem, to my view, to be irreconcileable with other 
differences in translations with which I am more familiar.

DD
```

```
the

of

> >> >
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<http://www.adams.edu/academics/art_letters/hgp/civ/111/1machiavelli_the_pri
nce.html>
>
> ... which is an online course at Adams State College and it has been my
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<http://www.ilt.columbia.edu/Publications/Projects/digitexts/machiavelli/the
_prince/chapter06.html>
>
> ... in what appears to be identical wording; Chap VI, para 5, sent. 3 - 5
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> differences in translations with which I am more familiar.

While 'The Prince' was written in 1513, it was not published
until 1532. Machiavelli died in 1527. If one wanted to attribute
the quote to Machiavelli, one would need to use 1513. If one
wanted to attribute the quote to 'The Prince', the date seems
irrelevant. Speech is often attributed by occasion and date.
It seems to me that 'Machiavelli, 1513' is on the order of
'I was there when ol' Nicco said this.' Any approaching the age
of 500 might have some difficulty with their recollection and
the subsequent translation. Or, perhaps it was a paraphrase.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **Date:** 2003-07-20T12:23:32+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f19e1a9_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <vhh0ognov16g35@corp.supernews.com> <3f189659_7@news.athenanews.com> <vhha2dkudsiae6@corp.supernews.com> <3f18eeb4_5@news.athenanews.com> <dtdSa.62940$3o3.4140847@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:dtdSa.62940$3o3.4140847@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> | > requirements, and when they came to acceptance milestone 1, they
brought
> | > a group of folks in from the field who completely rejected what this
> | > person had written.  So, these folks rewrote requirements and went
back
> | > to their base.  When the next milestone came around, a different group
> | > came back, and basically did the same thing the first group.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> You will get this with any development methodology when the designers
don't
> communicate with the user community.
> I've even seen a case where developers of different components of the same
> system didn't communicate. Integration wasn't a pretty sight.
>

OK.

But other approaches don't ALLOW designers NOT to communicate or be involved
in the identification of requirements.

I accept what you say, but what Project Manager worth his salt would allow
different component developments not to communicate (or fail to ensure that
they actually did...)?

Reading what Daniel has posted in this thread simply spells the classic
mistakes that I have seen time and again. It's all very well to get the
"sacred sign off", but all that does is cover IT's arse against the
inevitable failure. The users will get a system "something like" what they
actually require, about a year after they started requesting it. (By which
time the requirements have changed so much that it is only just "fit for
purpose". The delivered system will NEVER be really useful and the
credibility of IT will be affected adversely for a long time to come.)

The Users simply see that IT systems don't do what they "should".

The IT people see that the Users "don't know shit". They feel
underacknowledged, having "sweated blood" to deliver, then had a lukewarm
reception and the old: "Thank you. It's very nice but can we just have this
one small change..."

Now, you can say what you like about the merits of the Waterfall and the
advantages of sign offs, but I KNOW that the above scenario happens more on
Waterfall projects than it does when DSDM (Dynamic System Design
Methodology) type approaches are implemented. The keyword here is "dynamic".
Translate that as "responsive to change".

Without digressing into a summarised lecture on DSDM, consider just the
following:

1. Change is a natural part of the Business environment.

2. IT can deal with it in one of two ways:

(a).  Control the flow of change into the project by requiring signoff of
clear deliverables. (This is what usually happens and is the basis of the
Waterfall.)

(b). Work WITH the Business to identify and prioritise change. Use technical
approaches that accommodate change. All development is timeboxed. Timeboxes
cannot slip or be extended. When the Business identify a new (not previously
recognised) requirement, it is prioritised and accommodated just like all
the other requirements. BUT, all parties know that when the timebox is up,
that's it. You get what was prioritised. No attempt is made to deliver
EVERYTHING unless this can be done easily, within the allocated time. Within
a given timebox priorities are iterated and the requirements re-visited to
ensure that what was top priority is still top priority, and changes have
been properly assimilated and prioritised, as development proceeds.
Priorities are driven by the Business, not by IT.

Under option (a), a system is delivered that matches the Requirements
Gathering all those months ago. It may or may not be useful.

Under option (b), the current priorites are delivered. The system may not
have EVERY requirement implemented, but what it does have will be what the
Business wants NOW, not what they thought they might want 9 months ago.
Useful functionality will have been trickled out of the timeboxes as
development proceeded, rather than a Big Bang "delivery" at the end of the
project.

Having worked together with IT throughout the development (mainly in JDWs)
the Business have "buy in" and accptance, even if the result is not perfect
functionally. It is seen as OUR system, not another system being thrust upon
them by IT...

Having spent 20 years doing it both ways, I am persuaded that option (b) is
better.

But the chance of getting approval for a project using this approach on a
Fortress site is about the same as finding evidence for
gravitons...(extremely remote, difficult to set up the right conditions, but
not impossible).

Even if you do get approval for a pilot project, it takes a skilled Project
Manager to hold all the threads (Business and IT) together. The selection of
the project team is critical to the success of this approach. Neverthelees,
I have managed and implemented around a dozen such projects and every one
was a success.

What amazes me is that we see the same failed approaches being repeated over
and over, with the same symptoms emerging, and we have no answer, other than
"Well, we delivered what they signed off...".

Personally, success on paper is not good enough for me. If the systems I
develop don't make a difference, I'm wasting my own and everybody else's
time, and I'd rather be doing something else.

If you are interested in exploring this further please follow this link:

http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/archives/v3/v30501.asp

I'll happily debate points raised from this article.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T02:38:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

Please see below.

> 
> Really pleased to hear it. I am appalled when I read some of the posts in
> CLC, to think that there are managers out there who appear to have no
idea
> about managing or motivating. I discussed this with several Senior
managers
> I know both here and in Europe (I don't know any in the US, but I'm sure
> those of you who do can do your own research) and they independently
agreed
> (although not unanimously, so maybe it depends on the organisational
> "culture" also...) that most of the rising middle managers don't want to
> manage Legacy systems. It seems that the less able managers end up in
> "Legacy Management" because the "high flyers" see it as a bad career move
> and bypass it on the way up. Obviously, this is a generalization, as are
all
> the conclusions from the thread, and I'm sure there are exceptions. Glad
to
> see that your site is one.
> 

My official supervisor has been my boss for over three years now.  We have
never spoken to each other except a couple of times over the phone. I have
also sent him an occassional memo. I have a project leader who does my
evaluation and my official boss just signs it.

> > We have a "pet project" in the organization that all object-oriented,
> > Oracle/Java based, blah blah blah.  It's had some major problems
(mostly
> > due to bad/changing requirements), and the amount of code they've got
> > versus the amount of time they've spent isn't too great.  Our
management
> > is also over this project, although they've got someone actually
running
> > it day-to-day for them.

We had a pilot project, in another group, using Oracle that took 9 months.
The deputy to the assistant commissioner for IT asked why we were not using
COBOL and Datacomm/DB since we have in-house experts who could have
accomplished the project in a month. I thought he had a  pretty good point
but then no one ever did anything about it. At least he wasn't being swayed
by the fad of the day.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

- **From:** hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T17:48:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3129972.1058636883@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```

Pete,
    Good Post. I see one missing point regarding this from your post. I
    glanced down through some of the responses and apologize in advance
    if someone else already noted it - It the fact that very few
    colleges and universities are teach COBOL anymore. There is little
    "fresh blood" coming into the COBOL programming community. And IT
    managers do consider this. We were talking to a CIO of a large
    enterprise this past week (who is looking to migrate all of their
    systems to the PC) who said he's had a CICS programming position
    advertised for over a year and can't fill it (and sorry - I can't
    disclose the customer).
     And I think this is kind of killing COBOL from the roots - no
     new roots are growing to support the foundation and tree that
     COBOL became.
Just a thought....
-Howard
```

```
Embroideries:

When the CIO was mentioning to you (and those accompanying you during this 
mentioning) that he had been unable to fill this CICS programming position 
did you, or anyone in your hearing, suggest to him that one of the 
difficulties in filling this position might lie in the salary being 
offered and that better results might occur were the salary offered 
increased?

was mentioned focused on the sale of a product or an analysis which might 
lead to a sale?
```

```
DD,
   I'm not sure if your powers of comprehention need some tuning or
   what... I'm not interested in trying to repeatedly answer the same
   question over and over in your exact words. Now, as I previously
   stated, it is clear to me that the CIO understood quite well why he
   was having a problem finding a CICS programmer, and the discussions
   were about migrating mainframe applications to the PC - which one
   could easily infer would require the acquisition of both software and
   possibly consulting services... so please try to figure this one out
   on your own.. It's really not that difficult... :-)
   And I am not going to discuss any more of the details - this is my
   last posting on this subject. I hold customer confidentiality to a
   very high standard unless it's been published by a respectable third
   party source or permission has been given by the customer and I'm
   afraid I'm not motivated to ask the customer for such permission in
   this particular circumstance.
Thanks,
-Howard
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T15:58:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfevpl$fk5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <3130707.1058675291@dbforums.com> <bfe5k7$mev$1@panix1.panix.com> <3131561.1058721781@dbforums.com> <bfeq26$lc4$1@panix1.panix.com> <3131869.1058732138@dbforums.com>`

```
Just to clarify why DD (and I) am a little confused by exactly WHAT the
point is, let me go back to how this part of the thread began.  It was a
statement from Howard that said (and I quote),

"There is little
    "fresh blood" coming into the COBOL programming community. And IT
    managers do consider this. We were talking to a CIO of a large
    enterprise this past week (who is looking to migrate all of their
    systems to the PC) who said he's had a CICS programming position
    advertised for over a year and can't fill it (and sorry - I can't
    disclose the customer).
     And I think this is kind of killing COBOL from the roots - no
     new roots are growing to support the foundation and tree that
     COBOL became."

Given posts after this, I think it is more accurate to state, that THIS
particular case demonstrates that when you don't offer the "going reasonable
wage" for mainframe COBOL/CICS programmers, that you won't find takers.
Any other conclusion, seems to be "stretching" the evidence - at least that
presented to us in CLC.
```

```
Bill,
   I think the point is that 10-15 years ago, being located in a small
   town off the beaten path and not offering a top salary may not have
   precluded them from finding someone - particularly a young,
   unmarried, willing to relocate and wanting to further a career type
   of individual. With all due repect, I think it is more than fair to
   state that there are significantly fewer such individuals coming into
   the job marketplace anymore.... Would you disagree...? And what's
   left are the older more highly experienced life long programmers who
   expect a much higher wage for their experience - and who want to jump
   on such a situation as an insult to them.
   And regarding DD's comments, its pleasing to see he apparrently is
   able to deduce something on his own with forcing others to agree to
   his exact wording... :-)
Regards,
-Howard
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Message-ID:** `<bfcqoo$6rf$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <3129972.1058636883@dbforums.com> <bfcdde$nln$1@panix1.panix.com> <3130370.1058655136@dbforums.com>`

```
In article <3130370.1058655136@dbforums.com>,
hhinman  <member30421@dbforums.com> wrote:
>
>DD,
>    I don't know the answer to your question, but as I responded to Bill
>    earlier, I think their location may have a lot to do with this...

I responded to Mr Klein a moment ago as well... isn't it amazing how often 
folks bewail a lack of talented professionals willing to work for 
sub-optimal wages in areas described by my Drill Sergeant as 'Bumfuck 
Egypt'?

There was a credit-card mill I did a stint with in South Bend, IN... they 
wanted to relocate to Houston and sent down an installation-team of thirty 
folks, a mixture of systems and apps jockies.  This original team lasted 
about two months in Texas before realising 'Hey, there are other shops in 
this town that need our skills... and they pay Real Money, too!'.

Upon seeing the cream of their data-processing crop evaporate into the 
desert sands Management decided to back off on its decision... so while 
all of the users in the shop got moved to Houston the computer operations 
*stayed* in South Bend, working at The Only Game in Town.

DD
```
