#### ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-22T21:59:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ztiTa.50084$k85.1874762@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
> They feel they are under attack and watch in dismay as their jobs are
> moved off-shore.

But this is the natural ebb and flow of the corporate/political world.
If you don't like it then you have to do something about it - get educated
and read Stiglitz, read Greg Palast - and for the Americans quit watching
FOX and read an international newspaper.  Read Private Eye..don't vote
Republican....and most importantly quit thinking you're a special human
being. CEOs don't care, the govt doesn't care...and before you get
defensive - ask if you stood up for Dock Workers, Car engineers, steel
workers.....and while we're at it...the Native americans, gays & lesbians,
women, ethnic minorities..

> (I was surprised by the defensiveness exhibited. I made a
> tongue-in-cheek comment about response times under TSO and it prompted
> a number of serious posts defending something that didn't need to be
> defended...It was a joke, but it seems people are losing their sense
> of humour...)

This is one thing I cannot figure out.  At my work we talk about migrating
mainframe platforms and people throw a fit because we can no longer use
CA-Intertest (though the IBM Debug tool works fine and the support is better
for us).   I cannot wait until the day when someone asks them to implement a
Websphere solution....The Fortress you talk about is DEFINITELY real.


> 2. Management are perceived to be not interested in furthering the
> careers of mainframe people.

As far as I can tell, managerment are not interested in furthering the
careers of any people.  I have first line/second line managers that I can
see daily.  I think they worry more about their own jobs than their
employees...it's not until CIO/CEO that you are safer and at that point they
don't worry about you when they drive (execuse me, get driven) home at night
after working at Pebble Beach.


> Training is not being implemented for mainframers. But the same seems
> not to be true for Client/Server people who mostly get what they need.
> This is part of the management perception that they need this
> technology only until such time as it can be replaced.

Recently I knew personally about 30 people that got laid off.  Client Server
programmers are not being rehired.  The mainframers are - albeit with a
salary reduction of 40% - but still higher than any local schoolteacher.

I think most mainframers thing young kids are snotty nosed know it alls.

What surprises me is that people think Cobol hasn't changed since 1985.


> 3. There is little new or "sexy" happening in the mainframe arena.
> (Note: moves to Linux and Java, although technically mainframe
> activities, are viewed with suspicion by many.) Nobody knows what
> Websphere is about...<G>.(We haven't had a single thread on this in
> CLC...)
Call me crazy...but are business rules ever "sexy"...?!?

Enterprise Cobol is all XML'ized (a little but it's where the development
is).  Web services on the mainframe are all the rage.  Z/VM has just been
saved by Linux....Personally it is beginning to bore me...but I don't know
what other industry has more go...IBM is investing serious dollars in the
mainframe...it's not going anywhere (the fact they market these things like
they are servers *is* interesting).  It's like a bait and switch...buy our
servers.....I mean *cough* mainframe.  I remember when MPPs were going to
take over the world...whatever happened there...

What surprises me is that people think Cobol hasn't changed since 1985.


> 4. Maybe as a result of the general low morale, there is a high degree
> of inertia being exhibited by many mainframers. Motivation to expand
> the skill set has never been more necessary than at the moment, yet
> the majority are doing nothing about increasing their value.

I knew a second line manager who was new who walked into an office of an
employee and said "Hmmmm...do you work on Application A? I heard it sucks."
(A is a green screen internal app).  This was LITERALLY his
introduction...no "Hi" was spoken... So being human, my coworked feels
useless, old and pathetic (and he is none of these...pathetic arguably ;0))
.
 Unfortunately he isn't old enough to not care...Most people I know are
holding on with fingernails at that piece of pension left over trying to
live out the next 5-10 years til they can retire.  They still believe this
even though we've had 3 rounds of sell offs where the new company
said....hmmmm....we're going to reset your pension and vacation.  I think
it's hard to care.

I think a lot of people don't see what the point of things are...We get a
requirement to 'web-enable' a system that is used by 10 internal employees
that are perfectly happy.  You recommend "green screen on the web" and you
get nasty looks...the hidden requirement is to look cool too.  We can only
quickly make it work and functional which makes us useless...again, who
wants to get educated to go through hoops to web enable something of little
value.

The web that we see in Legacy systems is typically customer fronting and
"neato" add ons.  Development is in the high $$$ for something that has no
business justification anymore. Companies spend a fortune making everything
real time....when do you think the last time a Pepsi account cared if his
network usage for Bob Smith in A/R is going over his plan.....

For god's sake, we are putting GAMES on phones?  If I wanted to play games
I'd get a GBA...but it's the STUFF differentiator.


> the "old" days), others feel that the job ends at 5pm (as long as they
> still have a job...). On the other hand, there are some who are doing
> everything they can, even though they may be past the mid point of
> their careers, to acquire new skills and try to improve their value.

In the US the issue is bigger.  There is an employment crisis.  Overtime is
expected, support is expected. 24x7 customer access means 24x7 pagers.
People don't get an opportunity to not care about their job.  This in turn
leads to employees who don't care about their job.  Client Server jobs are
worst of all paying less here and working more...who needs that?  I'm
thinking of supermarket bagging as my next career...Let's face it, that
instant messaging solution is just awesome....and best of all I can
customize the incoming message with my own "Oh crap, another one" wav file.

> Sadly, they are a minority.
Why sadly? People with no drive for a job in A don't need a job in A.
Besides it's less competition for Job A.  It's like the University system in
England...in 1993 they had a crisis..all the Science positions filled out,
so some of the more underqualified people interested in Science, ended up in
Practical Study of Fine Arts just to get a degree...I guess if you mean get
reskilled in other jobs in area B then I understand your point and it is
sad...it's like giving up because they beat the life out of you.

> Given all of the above, it is hardly surprising that people dig
> themselves into entrenched positions. The result is the "Fortress",
> where change is fiercely resisted and anything that challenges the
> Status Quo is attacked overtly and covertly.

That's mostly due to the prevailing strategy "If I understand it they cannot
get rid of me."  The best analogy I have heard is the bucket of water....if
someone has their hand and it and it is taken away...there are some ripples
but it doesn't take long before you don't even know anything was there.  In
other words, if they need to chop the change, they don't care what you know,
just what you make.

> So what is the outlook? Can the war be won? Can the Fortress withstand
> the tides of change sweeping through the industry?
>
> In a word: No.

I disagree - kinda.  Ask yourself the following question....if I were
offered a job making enough to feed me and my family, take care of my
parents but it was 1000 miles away would I do it?
This is what Indians going to Bangalore do every day....don't think they all
grew up their in pretty little suburbs there...If you grew up in Goa and
needed a job you go to Bangalore - your family doesn't necessarily. Ask any
one of them if they would want to work for IBM/EDS/Dell/Oracle in the US and
they would probably tell you they would in a second...
If all you do is code COBOL you're sh*t out of luck...if you are an
architect/analyst role...you have a chance.

If people make that sacrifice you can still work...want to make $100k and
work 35 hours a week....forget it.  Of course I'm still waiting for some
small country to come in and say "We have no technical skills or systems;
however, we have 1,000,000 man hours at $1 a piece".

Interestingly I hear that India is going to face competition from Russia and
China.....and internally they are trying to "privatize" (they being non govt
outfits)...ah ..I love the smell of free enterprise in the morning....


> The computer boom is over. (It started ending in the 1980s). The days
> when experts were required to get computers to do anything are
> finished.

And luckily for our economy they created almost as many jobs as they
destroyed or we'd be really in trouble....my phone has games!

> The population is becoming more and more computer literate.
> Kids are growing up with computer technology that they take for
> granted just like all the other technology of the 21st century.

People have replaced values with "THINGS".  People are ok without a job,
with huge amounts of debt..coz look at my phone...it plays depeche mode's
Just Can't get enough.
Of course they are more computer literate, but certainly more illiterate.
They read Harry Potter once...(this is littered with grammar/sp errors so
don't go looking...it's late here)


> veils of mystery have been ripped away and what was once a secret
> arcane art is now the preserve of spotty adolescents.

Was all that green screen a secret covenant? Cool..shame I missed out on it


> There is no longer a secure job for life in being possessed of some
> specialist knowledge (like, how to write COBOL).

How to make a Nike sneaker, build a tv.  I don't think kids even know what a
lettuce that doesn't come in a bag looks like. Give a 10 year cheese that
isn't Kraft and they freak out.   People think Chorizo is that cuban
sausage......


> (If it's any consolation, there isn't in knowing how to write any
> procedural language. There might not be in anything computer
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the future it had in the 1960s and 70s...))

Their is always something.  Teaching the Chinese english..Teaching the
english english....street cleaning....the wonderful thing about life is that
it begins and it ends and in the middle sh*t happens and you get on with it.


> But that doesn't mean that we should all chuck it in <G>.

But you've made such a great case for it.

> It simply means an adjustment to our expectations.

Ding Ding..Give this man a Pepsi.

> The factors that nurture the Fortress mentality are going to be with
> us for some time (about 12 years, I reckon <G>) so individuals may
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> is simply "waiting to migrate".

Lower salary expectations, save for your kid's education now, don't close
your eyes while 40% of the population has a 12th grade reading level but yet
50% go to College. Don't let people continue to devalue the industries and
the values of hard work......don't ignore the arts, don't rip of Latin
America.

You're part of a global economy, you aren't special anymore.

WORK TO BE HAPPY NOT TO GET STUFF.  You spend half your waking life working,
you may as well enjoy it.  Quit identifying your WORK as part of
YOURSELF.....be creative and alive.

> Personally, I'm considering a career change...looked at Bank Robbing
> but I can't run fast enough and I never liked firearms even when I was
> in the army... (noisy, dangerous, things...).

What about burglary?  Have you visited those "Gated" communities - many have
little sliding glass windows held together with sticky back plastic.  Some
of them even leave their doors/garages open for you...Thoroughly confuse
them by living IN the community....it's a bit of a mind fu*k when they find
out that the gates only keep out the criminals that aren't successful.


> I'm a bit too old for "Would you like fries with that?" so I am coming
> to the conclusion that the only safe option for me is a rich widow or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Many rich widows down in Florida...come down, flash some charm, sell them a
public telephone, or how to buy a Condo with no money down and you're ready
to retire.

BTW I am youngish developer fringin on both mainframe/client server stuff.

JCE
```

```
jce wrote:
>> They feel they are under attack and watch in dismay as their jobs are
>> moved off-shore.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I watch FOX. I read international newspapers (the Economist). I have never
knowingly voted for anyone BUT a Republican. To me, I am special. I don't
give a fig what anyone else thinks.

CEOs and the government may not care about me; I'm supremely indifferent to
their opinion. I am in charge of my destiny.

I have never stood up for dock workers, car 'engineers,' Native Americans,
gays, lesbians, or ethnic minorities. If they can't take care of themselves,
they deserve to go extinct. Bunch of indolent sloths.

If someone wants to subsume their identity in a holy cause, he can join a
mass movement that is actually beneficial: Abolish brassierres.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>

- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <ztiTa.50084$k85.1874762@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <JcudnfTBM_8_f4CiXTWJgA@giganews.com>`

```
> I watch FOX. I read international newspapers (the Economist).
I too Fox. King of the Hill, The Simpsons and other fictional shows - like
their news.

> I have never knowingly voted for anyone BUT a Republican.
Well it is hard to tell these days....

> I have never stood up for dock workers, car 'engineers,' Native Americans,
> gays, lesbians, or ethnic minorities. If they can't take care of
themselves,
> they deserve to go extinct. Bunch of indolent sloths.

don't understand why indolent IT sloths believe that anyone should stand up
for them.  Jobs are leaving......care about me.....err ok.
```

```
LX-i wrote:
> jce wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> stuff)?

Well, I can give you a couple for CNN. Remember the "Posion Gas in Viet Nam"
special? Got Peter Arnett (apologist-in-chief for Saddam Hussein) fired. In
May, CNN put on a bogus "Assault Weapon" demonstraton and the president of
the NRA ripped them a new orfice the next night. More repercussions
followed:

"When CNN aired the first "assault weapons" story that faked the difference
between so-called illegal and legal rifles, not only did the NRA membership
come unglued but folks who are not NRA members were also outraged.

"The conduct of CNN and Ken Jenne, a Florida Sheriff, was so egregious that
it could not go unanswered. There is no doubt in my mind that CNN and
Sheriff Jenne and his staff, plotted, lied, faked and willfully perpetrated
a fraud with that story." Marion P. Hammer, Executive Director, Sportsmen of
Florida

Story at:
http://www.nramemberscouncils.com/hammer/

Oh, yeah. Remember a few years ago CBS put explosives in a pickup's gas tank
and made it look as if the truck blew up when in a traffic collision?

 FOX *cannot* lie. The people who watch FOX would spot it in a heartbeat.
Whereas, CNN, CBS, . . . .

Nielson rankings - 22 July 2003

O'REILLY 2.7 (FOX)
HANNITY/COLMES 2.2 (FOX)
SHEP SMITH 2.0 (FOX)
GRETA 1.6 (FOX)
BRIT HUME 1.6 (FOX)
PUNK'D 1.5 (MTV)
LARRY KING 1.3 (CNN)
PAULA ZAHN 1.2 (CNN)
AARON BROWN 1.0 (CNN)
ABRAMS 0.8 (MSNBC)
BUCHANAN/PRESS 0.5 (MSNBC)
SCARBOROUGH 0.4 (MSNBC)
HARDBALL 0.4 (MSNBC)
OLBERMANN 0.4 (MSNBC)


Based on the figures above, one notes the MTV "newscast" (? I guess. I've
never seen it.)beats any of the CNN offerings.

Assuming, just for a moment, there is no preconceived editorial bias at FOX,
they may have looked at the demographics and said: "Hmmm, 40% of the public
is conservative and 100% of the national TV space is liberal. If we lean
even just a teeny bit conservative, we'll get that 40% leaving the alphabet
networks to divvy up the remaining 60%."

Sound business decision. No doubt made by COBOL programmers.
```

```
  Look at Buchannan/Press's ratings - I'd venture a guess that Pat
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T13:04:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfp39r$8gu$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <ztiTa.50084$k85.1874762@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <JcudnfTBM_8_f4CiXTWJgA@giganews.com> <8koTa.1675$BB6.55475@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <vhu6fb8vmride3@corp.supernews.com> <8PqcnS-nmJP104KiXTWJkg@giganews.com> <vhvl6vkhleic8f@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vhvl6vkhleic8f@corp.supernews.com...
> :)  That wasn't the question.  (I appreciate these, though.)  If I've
> learned anything from the local royalty, it's that generalizations must
> be substantiated, or they may be dismissed.
Generalizations, in general, cannot be simply substantianted or dismissed.

> This (new ?) guy came in here on a tirade, and rather than try to refute
what he said, I thought
> I'd ask him to prove it.
Let's start with the announcement that George W Bush won the election....
Not after he "won" it....but before that.  Fox was the first to report this
fiction.

However, learn to read between the 7-80.   I apologize to FOX for picking on
them, but my point was more about making people read and watch more than
just primetime news.  There are ten sides to each story, and unfortunately
the one side that is reported on primetime FOX (and MSNBC) generally has a
very limited view.  I know that not all Americans are represented accurately
by their government - and so as well as reading the Guardian/Independent in
the UK I will read the NY Times, watch the BBC and ABC..and even FOX.  I
don't think any one of them is perfect; however, I think in general you will
find that Fox/MSNBC news are shallow in global coverage - and I would argue
that lack of coverage constitutes a denial of world opinion and that this
denial leads to a fictionalized account of the world.   Ask the average
person to talk to you for 5 minutes about Venezuala/Liberia ...then ask them
to talk for 5 minutes about Kobe Bryant or Dennehy....That's my point.

Further between the lines is that fact that people have a tendency to
complain about the world around them and don't get involved in anything (I
don't - I'm lazy but that's my prerogative).  But all these people that just
complain and whine need to find out why people protest the WTO....they
aren't all just hippy tree huggers that make the police use force...that's
what my "tirade" is for.

That's all I will say on this as this is WAYYYYYYYYY off topic :-)

JCE
```

```
> Further between the lines is that fact that people have a tendency to
> complain about the world around them and don't get involved in anything (I
```

```
We already know why they protest the WTO.  It is a combination of 
several factors:

   - They learned monetary policy from Jimmy Carter.

   - They live in a society that has not known "poverty" for almost a 
century -- and thus have way too much time, and money, on their hands.

   - A night in a jail cell is cheaper than a night in a Holiday Inn.

   - They flunked out of the Fisher-Price School of Economics.

   - Riots are fun.  It doesn't matter if the reason is a Soccer (uk 
xlation "football") match or a trade meeting.

   - The have never visited a third world country starved for economic 
activity.

   - Bono is k00l dude!


Did I miss any?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ [WAY OT] Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T17:15:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5eydnbaasc8BYL-iU-KYvA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <ztiTa.50084$k85.1874762@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <JcudnfTBM_8_f4CiXTWJgA@giganews.com> <8koTa.1675$BB6.55475@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <vhu6fb8vmride3@corp.supernews.com> <8PqcnS-nmJP104KiXTWJkg@giganews.com> <vhvl6vkhleic8f@corp.supernews.com> <bfp39r$8gu$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`

```
jce wrote:

>
>> This (new ?) guy came in here on a tirade, and rather than try to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> first to report this fiction.

"Fiction?"
1. Of course Bush didn't actually "win" until the Electoral College voted on
January 6th, 2001, and I trust you'll forgive the shorthand use of the word
before that.
2. If, however, you're speaking of Florida, Bush won the certified vote, and
two court mandated recounts. A consortium of news organizations recounted
all the ballots over the next several months using a variety of criteria.
Bush came out ahead in those recounts also.
3. News organizations report or 'project' winners long before all the votes
are tabulated. They all do.
4. But most importantly, it is possible that FOX knew something the others
didn't.

>
> However, learn to read between the 7-80.   I apologize to FOX for
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Dennehy....That's my point.

Your point is twisted - you're griping about the symptom, not the disease.
There are people who like opera but infinitely more people who like hip-hop.
Most people don't care about Venuzuala or Liberia but do care about Bryant.
The networks simply give them what they want.

The BBC is funded by tax dollars with absolutely no oversight by
politicians. The BBC doesn't have to work for its money and can report on
whatever liberal cause it fancies. Not so in the U.S. The networks here have
to EARN their money by producing a sellable product.

In the U.S. economy, the way to get more world news on TV is to inculcate a
desire amongst the masses for that data. This won't happen easily. Most
Americans, to their credit, don't give a shit what's going on in the rest of
the world.

Unless it directly affects us.

9/11 caused us to begin draining the swamp in the Middle East. And we won't
stop until we've killed all their leaders and converted the rest to
Christianity.


>
> Further between the lines is that fact that people have a tendency to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> the police use force...that's what my "tirade" is for.

We know why people protest the WTO. They are losers who've subsumed their
identity in a 'cause.' They take their minds off their own meaningless
affairs by minding other people's business. The anti-WTO is a mass-movement
and, as such, is indistinguishable in membership from other mass movements
(membership is interchangable). The goals of the anti-WTO are irrelevant;
membership in the movement is all. Look at the signs carried by the
protestors. Many have zilch to do with the WTO. These people just want to
party, to find some meaning in their drab existence, experience some - any -
emotion.

They should try opera.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T19:16:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IQUVa.77153$3o3.5303845@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <ztiTa.50084$k85.1874762@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <JcudnfTBM_8_f4CiXTWJgA@giganews.com> <8koTa.1675$BB6.55475@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <vhu6fb8vmride3@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vhu6fb8vmride3@corp.supernews.com...
| jce wrote:
|
| >>I watch FOX. I read international newspapers (the Economist).
| >
| > I too Fox. King of the Hill, The Simpsons and other fictional shows -
like
| > their news.
|
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
|   Oh wait - it's ours again!"  They -all- ran that kind of stuff)?

Fox was reporting that WMDs were discovered in Iraq, while the other
networks were reporting suspected WMDs were found.
The suspected WMDs were just that.

I'll bet that if you ask some Fox viewers whether WMDs were found, they
would answer YES.

I stopped watching Fox after this.
"We report, you decide" should have been "We report the truth, you decide".

Fox definitely slants their reporting.
I've even seen their correspondents augment responses from their guests,
when the guest didn't give the answer they wanted.
The other US networks also slant their reporting, but not as much as Fox
does.
I usually try to watch the BBC World News.
If you want the truth, you can't depend on a single news source.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T08:08:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1e21b7.37364175@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <ztiTa.50084$k85.1874762@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

>> They feel they are under attack and watch in dismay as their jobs are
>> moved off-shore.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>and read Stiglitz, read Greg Palast - 

Greg Palast wrote:

"The purpose of every industrial revolution .. is to make craft and skill
obsolete, thereby making people interchangeable and cheap."

Big Macs, small horizons, "The Guardian",  January 1, 2000

They've been unsuccessful at doing that with programmers for forty years. Looks
like India is accomplishing what CEOs, methodologies and OO could not.

Palast goes on to say In the same article:

"Between 1983 and 1997, 85.5 per cent of the vaunted increase in America's
wealth was captured by the richest 1 per cent of the population. In that time,
the country's income rocketed - yet 80 per cent of American families received 0
per cent of that increase. The gilded 1 per cent owns $2.9 trillion of US stocks
and bonds out of a total $3.5 trillion." 

The good news is no more class warfare. The bad news is we lost.

>.Most people I know are
>holding on with fingernails at that piece of pension left over trying to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>it's hard to care.

People who rest their security in the goodwill of one employer are fools. 
Better security lies in the vibrancy of the marketplace, but even that is
vulnerable.  When the market stops vibrating, what are they to do?

>I think a lot of people don't see what the point of things are...We get a
>requirement to 'web-enable' a system that is used by 10 internal employees
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>value.

Once tools and skill are in place, how long does it take to drop some widgets on
a screen? If it takes longer than a few hours, the tools are below average. How
long to write event handlers that send transactions through some middleware? A
few days if you have to do it manually; one day if you have a
prototype/framework.

The ability to do that makes you valuable, not useless. It's a transferrable
skill.

>For god's sake, we are putting GAMES on phones?  If I wanted to play games
>I'd get a GBA...but it's the STUFF differentiator.

Sales of stuff are easier to account for than dissemination of values. Demand
for stuff is higher. Hint: programmers who write those games are well paid and
have fun at work. Why not be one of them?

>In the US the issue is bigger.  There is an employment crisis.  Overtime is
>expected, support is expected. 24x7 customer access means 24x7 pagers.
>People don't get an opportunity to not care about their job.  This in turn
>leads to employees who don't care about their job. 

Unemployment is a sign that people care, and hope for higher wages. When people
stop caring and take any job they can get, unemployment plummets. 

> Ask yourself the following question....if I were
>offered a job making enough to feed me and my family, take care of my
>parents but it was 1000 miles away would I do it?

We US contractors move further once a year. If we're playing the per diem game,
Uncle Sam requires it. The best part is shedding all the stuff accumulated in a
year. Move with computer, Gap uniforms and what little else fits in the station
wagon. 

>This is what Indians going to Bangalore do every day....don't think they all
>grew up their in pretty little suburbs there...If you grew up in Goa and
>needed a job you go to Bangalore - your family doesn't necessarily. Ask any
>one of them if they would want to work for IBM/EDS/Dell/Oracle in the US and
>they would probably tell you they would in a second...

They wouldn't be so eager if they knew how Dell and EDS treat contractors.

>If all you do is code COBOL you're sh*t out of luck...if you are an
>architect/analyst role...you have a chance.

Wrong. Those jobs go to American employees. Indian contractors get the shit jobs
nobody wants -- production support, test team, help desk. 

>If people make that sacrifice you can still work...want to make $100k and
>work 35 hours a week...

Some have been doing exactly that for years.

>> The computer boom is over. (It started ending in the 1980s). The days
>> when experts were required to get computers to do anything are
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>destroyed or we'd be really in trouble....my phone has games!

Exactly. Out with general ledger, in with sexy graphics.  Namencloture changes
but human nature stays the same.

>People have replaced values with "THINGS".  People are ok without a job,
>with huge amounts of debt..coz look at my phone...it plays depeche mode's
>Just Can't get enough.

If it develops an attitude, it'll display Jane Fonda's famous quote from the '68
Democratic convention: "We're all n*****s now."

>Was all that green screen a secret covenant? Cool..shame I missed out on it

The covenant was in the lingo.  It reminds me of movies made in the '30s, in
which everyone who mattered lived in Manhattan, men wore suits and women talked
with a faux cultured accent. If you didn't know Bowery or Uptown, you were a
worthless hayseed. In the computer world, if you didn't know FBA or CKD, if you
called them disks rather than DASD, you weren't a Professional Programmer. The
attitude is still in place, but creativity has moved elsewhere.

>WORK TO BE HAPPY NOT TO GET STUFF.  You spend half your waking life working,
>you may as well enjoy it.  Quit identifying your WORK as part of
>YOURSELF.....be creative and alive.

Street theater? Greeter at McDonald's? Web page design ..  too late. I've got it
-- lottery tickets on cellphones. The network is in place. Can have a drawing
every 30 minutes. 

BTW, I'm a youngish developer too. I'm only 178.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T10:12:21+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1f08f6_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <3141989.1058948581@dbforums.com>`

```

"hhinman" <member30421@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3141989.1058948581@dbforums.com...
>
> Here's a link discussing the outsourcing of IT jobs. It's a bit sad the
> quote at the very end...
> http://money.cnn.com/2003/07/22/news/economy/jobless_offshore/index.htm
>
I read this with interest. They are talking a 15 year exodus of jobs from
the US. It makes me wonder what effect this influx of prosperity will have
on the countries quoted. Maybe the smart thing to do would be to buy a
holiday property in India, now... (I've spent time there and some of it is
really beautiful.)

The 49 year old losing her house after battling all her life is not an
inspiring example, but I fear it will not be the last.

And what effect will the resentment engendered by this have on the domestic
economy in the US? Are we likely to see a rise in civil unrest, industrial
sabotage against firms who do it? Disgruntled ex-employees have a history of
causing trouble.

It is hard to believe that the outflow of such a vast amount of capital can
take place with no effect (even on an economy as giant as the US.).

You can't blame the companies. It is a competitive market and everybody
wants cheap goods and services.

30 years ago I lived in Nepal for a while. It cost around $US7 a day to live
very well.(three meals a day, accommodation, and safe-to-drink ice
water...). (In Kathmandu it would have been $US8 a day...I was in a village
in the foothills of the Himalayas about 25 miles from there.). India at the
same time would have been $US10, depending on where you were, of course.

Today, I understand Kathmandu has its own casino and is thriving on tourism.
India is also building a vibrant tourist industry. I have no idea what the
per diem would need to be today to match the same standard as then, but I'm
sure it will be considerably more than the figures quoted. So what is it
likely to be in the next 15 years?

I have to wonder about some of the highly skilled, high tech, people in
companies like IBM and MicroSoft who may be affected by this. (The Companies
protest that they will keep the core of their business in the US, and it is
easy to believe that only the shit work will be offloaded, but I happen to
know about one project currently going on in India that is "cutting edge"
and financed from the US...(you can bet there are others)).

Don't be surprised if we see some disruption to the technical infrastructure
in the next few years... Look for an upsurge in Internet viruses and general
attacks on technology and commerce. I wouldn't exclude the phone network
either.

Hope it doesn't happen, but don't be surprised if it does.

Pete.
```

```
> protest that they will keep the core of their business in the US, and it
is
> easy to believe that only the shit work will be offloaded, but I happen to
> know about one project currently going on in India that is "cutting edge"
> and financed from the US...(you can bet there are others)).

Not sure how much IBM is protesting...
http://www.nytimes.com/2003/07/22/technology/22JOBS.html?ex=1059451200&en=0c
92e3397e0523e7&ei=5062

Note also the jobs - they are talking "design" jobs.  These are the sh*t
jobs?


Why I should have studied economics at school.....

What I don't understand is that if you reduce Cost you in turn reduce
Revenue (no company will pay the same for your work if they know you are
outsourcing it on the cheap).  Most people don't buy american clothing
anymore......though some consumer goods retain value through
"fashion/social standing/belonging"...which is not *as* true in the business
world.

So you reduce cost, you reduce prices and your profit margin stays roughly
the same.   Your IT consumers are predominantly US.  India doesn't have an
infrastructure yet to consume IT resources and is just a producer (which
won't change anytime soon).  So my question is simple....all you've done is
remove 2% of all jobs and the associated monies to another nation who
benefits here?  I don't see India as a nation growing infrastructurally as a
result of this boom.....they would potentiall get more money from the US by
"fighting" terrorism.....poverty is still rife, living conditions in general
still suck.

I'd still rather be an unemployed poor european than a poor person in India.

JCE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T12:24:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f20795d_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <3141989.1058948581@dbforums.com> <3f1f08f6_8@news.athenanews.com> <bfp1j2$7nk$1@news.btv.ibm.com>`

```

"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:bfp1j2$7nk$1@news.btv.ibm.com...
<snip>

> What I don't understand is that if you reduce Cost you in turn reduce
> Revenue (no company will pay the same for your work if they know you are
> outsourcing it on the cheap).  Most people don't buy american clothing
> anymore......though some consumer goods retain value through
> "fashion/social standing/belonging"...which is not *as* true in the
business
> world.
>

I'm not sure that that follows. First off, the consumer probably doesn't
know that jobs are being outsourced and how many "business consumers" know
the costs and sourcing of the businesses they deal with?

Even if you're right, it would simply be a negotiation point when purchasing
services. EVERYTHING these days is negotiable...


> So you reduce cost, you reduce prices and your profit margin stays roughly
> the same.

Or not....<G>

> Your IT consumers are predominantly US.  India doesn't have an
> infrastructure yet to consume IT resources and is just a producer (which
> won't change anytime soon).  So my question is simple....all you've done
is
> remove 2% of all jobs and the associated monies to another nation who
> benefits here?  I don't see India as a nation growing infrastructurally as
a
> result of this boom.....they would potentiall get more money from the US
by
> "fighting" terrorism.....poverty is still rife, living conditions in
general
> still suck.
>
Yes, the poverty of Asia in general is awesome. Nevertheless, the cash has
to go SOMEWHERE. India is very good at "making do" with what they have but
an influx of capital on this scale, I believe, will have an effect. There
are some very bright and innovative people there. When you combine ideas and
vision with cash, the results are usually worth watching.

> I'd still rather be an unemployed poor european than a poor person in
India.

That is apples and oranges. Unemployed Europeans usually have a roof over
their heads and get to eat regularly. There is a Social Welfare system that
works to at least some extent (it is far from perfect...)

Poverty is not much fun wherever you have to suffer it.

I've been poor (like, no food for days and no prospects, no shelter, no
friends, in a strange country, flat broke with no recourse or possibility of
getting out...) and I've been "rich". (All basic necessities easily covered
and enough left over to do the things you want...)

"Rich" is better. <G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T13:01:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oS9Ua.151839$ic1.3439377@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <3141989.1058948581@dbforums.com> <3f1f08f6_8@news.athenanews.com> <bfp1j2$7nk$1@news.btv.ibm.com> <3f20795d_5@news.athenanews.com>`

```

>I'm not sure that that follows. First off, the consumer probably doesn't
>know that jobs are being outsourced and how many "business consumers" know
>the costs and sourcing of the businesses they deal with?
They know through competitive - cheaper - costs.  Consumer market is driven
by greed but it impacts the individual. The business market is driven
through different greed that impacts the employees...Large companies will
outsource deals and rather than pay $xx for employees, they will require
$xx - $yy and dissolution of the unions.  I don't see where the POSITIVE
revenue comes in...it's just cost reduction and it's not pure reduction
(like just laying off the inept people and replacing them with functioning
humans).  I remain positive knowing that I am (a) Not dumb and (b) Not
afraid of reasonable graft and (c) Have no kids.   They need me for the
illusion that they still hire locals :-)

> I'd still rather be an unemployed poor european than a poor person in
> India.
>
> That is apples and oranges. Unemployed Europeans usually have a roof over
> their heads and get to eat regularly. There is a Social Welfare system
that
> works to at least some extent (it is far from perfect...)
Why I'd rather be an apple.

> Poverty is not much fun wherever you have to suffer it.
Agreed.

> I've been poor
I was a student but that was understood to be short term...though they did
tell me when I graduated - there are no jobs they just laid everyone
off....so there's still hope for a turnaround here still.

> "Rich" is better. <G>
It makes me sad that so many millions of people can't know this.

JCE
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fortress COBOL - some conclusions

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T10:12:21+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1f6d72_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3f173df9_3@news.athenanews.com> <3141989.1058948581@dbforums.com>`

```

"hhinman" <member30421@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3141989.1058948581@dbforums.com...
>
> Here's a link discussing the outsourcing of IT jobs. It's a bit sad the
> quote at the very end...
> http://money.cnn.com/2003/07/22/news/economy/jobless_offshore/index.htm
>
I read this with interest. They are talking a 15 year exodus of jobs from
the US. It makes me wonder what effect this influx of prosperity will have
on the countries quoted. Maybe the smart thing to do would be to buy a
holiday property in India, now... (I've spent time there and some of it is
really beautiful.)

The 49 year old losing her house after battling all her life is not an
inspiring example, but I fear it will not be the last.

And what effect will the resentment engendered by this have on the domestic
economy in the US? Are we likely to see a rise in civil unrest, industrial
sabotage against firms who do it? Disgruntled ex-employees have a history of
causing trouble.

It is hard to believe that the outflow of such a vast amount of capital can
take place with no effect (even on an economy as giant as the US.).

You can't blame the companies. It is a competitive market and everybody
wants cheap goods and services.

30 years ago I lived in Nepal for a while. It cost around $US7 a day to live
very well.(three meals a day, accommodation, and safe-to-drink ice
water...). (In Kathmandu it would have been $US8 a day...I was in a village
in the foothills of the Himalayas about 25 miles from there.). India at the
same time would have been $US10, depending on where you were, of course.

Today, I understand Kathmandu has its own casino and is thriving on tourism.
India is also building a vibrant tourist industry. I have no idea what the
per diem would need to be today to match the same standard as then, but I'm
sure it will be considerably more than the figures quoted. So what is it
likely to be in the next 15 years?

I have to wonder about some of the highly skilled, high tech, people in
companies like IBM and MicroSoft who may be affected by this. (The Companies
protest that they will keep the core of their business in the US, and it is
easy to believe that only the shit work will be offloaded, but I happen to
know about one project currently going on in India that is "cutting edge"
and financed from the US...(you can bet there are others)).

Don't be surprised if we see some disruption to the technical infrastructure
in the next few years... Look for an upsurge in Internet viruses and general
attacks on technology and commerce. I wouldn't exclude the phone network
either.

Hope it doesn't happen, but don't be surprised if it does.

Pete.
```

```
Yes, there is a hotkey sequence....

Ctrl+alt+O, Shift-F2, Z, alt-W, ctrl-+(must use numeric keypad), P,,j,
"Windows"+G, right-click, <Enter>.
```

```
Pete If you're using Outlook Express this is what I got from their Help.

To cancel a posted message

In the Folders list, select the newsgroup in which you posted the message,
and then select your message.
On the Message menu, click Cancel Message.
 Notes

You can only cancel messages that you posted.
After you cancel a message, it may take some time for it to be removed from
your server and other servers on the Internet. Canceling a message does not
remove it from another user's computer if the user downloaded the message
before it was canceled.
```

```
> > You can't blame the companies. It is a competitive market and everybody
> > wants cheap goods and services.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

It will certainly be profitable to ship operations overseas that currently
produce products to be exported.

But in the DP industry, what do the software companies import?   Lines of code?
 Then produce a bunch of CDs and books here that aren't imported.

The solution is to be competitive.   Isolation means no more exports.
```

## Thread: MF Cobol/AIX/MQDISC problem — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol/AIX/MQDISC problem

- **From:** paul.davis@watkins.com (paul davis)
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T06:47:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<83569b3d.0307180547.319b3c28@posting.google.com>`

```
We created the object file, TEST6.o , with the following compilation
command on our Unix box (without using NetExpress):

cob -cx ./WMTEST6.cbl -o ./WMTEST6.o

We then create the executable, TEST6, with the following compilation
command on our Unix box.

cob -x -o ./WMTEST6 -e "WMTEST6" ./WMTEST6.o  -lmqmcb_r

Now, if we take out the debugging statement

 DISPLAY '*** AFTER CONNECT'.

which resides between the MQCONN and MQDISC statements, then error 114
appears.  If we leave the DISPLAY statement where it is, then the
error does not appear.  The same thing occurs if we use an ACCEPT
statement in place of a DISPLAY statement.

Is there some kind of alignment problem or linkage problem in our
compilation statetements?  Do we need to set some kind of memory
alignment before issuing the MQDISC call, and how?  Do we need to
change our compilation statement options?  Is the MQ option -lmqmcb_r
correct?  What do all those letters in the option mean?

Thanks for your help.

-----------------  Original post -----------------------------------

We are getting the following error message in our AIX 4.3.3
environment when we run a Micro Focus v 2.2 COBOL program and do a
MQDISC (MQ v 5.3) in the program:

Execution error : file 'WMTEST6'
error code: 114, pc=0, call=1, seg=0
114     Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal 11)

We are compiling the program using Net Express v 3.1.11 and publishing
the executable to AIX.

Could you please cut and paste the following program and compile and
run it in your environment and see if you get the same error message?

Here is the test program I am using:

      PROGRAM-ID.               WMTEST6.
       AUTHOR.                   John Doe.
       INSTALLATION.             ABCDEF Co.
       DATE-WRITTEN.             July 14, 2003.
       DATE-COMPILED.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.
       SOURCE-COMPUTER.  IBM-AIX.

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

       DATA DIVISION.

      *================================================================*
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      *================================================================*

       01  MQAPI-HCONN                 PIC S9(09).
       01  MQAPI-COMPLETION-CODE       PIC S9(09).
       01  MQAPI-REASON-CODE           PIC S9(09).

      *================================================================*
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *================================================================*

           DISPLAY '*** BEFORE CONNECT'.

           CALL 'MQCONN' USING 'AQD2',
                               MQAPI-HCONN
                               MQAPI-COMPLETION-CODE
                               MQAPI-REASON-CODE.

           DISPLAY '*** AFTER CONNECT'.

           CALL 'MQDISC' USING MQAPI-HCONN,
                               MQAPI-COMPLETION-CODE,
                               MQAPI-REASON-CODE.

           DISPLAY '*** AFTER DISCONNECT'.


Thanks for your help.
```

## Thread: vutil question — 6 msgs

### vutil question

- **From:** "nospam" <bruceradtke@REMOVEspamREMOVE.earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T15:53:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BJURa.9624$Mc.684883@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Hello all -

I need help in extracting data from AcuCobol database files into a readable
format.  I'm not a Cobol programmer and could use your help.

I've tried  "vutil -unload -v -t  MYFILE > myfile.txt".    This does dump
the file except I don't recognize the numeric format.

I'm not sure what the "-extract" option does or how it works.  It asks me
the index key and number of records to dump.   If I just carriage return
past these prompts,  the program appears to be idle.

How can I use the vutil utility to dump some AcuCobol proprietary database
files to an ASCII text file?

Thanks in advance,

bruce
bruceradtke@earthlink.net.NOSPAMPLEASE
```

#### ↳ Re: vutil question

- **From:** gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T06:18:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3129209.1058595495@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<BJURa.9624$Mc.684883@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```

Hi

Do you need to extract data from an indexed file or a database?

Vutil.exe can be used to extract records in line sequential format from
an indexed file..the command is:

VUTIL -E myindexed_file  > myfile.txt

If you have a line sequential file and you want to store its data on an
empty indexed file you can reverse the command like:

Vutil -load -t -r mydata.txt  myindexed.inx

Hope in this help

Gianni
```

- **From:** "nospam" <bruceradtke@REMOVEspamREMOVE.earthlink.net>

```
bruce
```

#### ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T16:31:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u8Scnerf94ik-oWiXTWJjg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <3iPRa.78928$sY2.35667@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <oekfhv4ocpt0cr6ni4ek8lslafg754se7m@4ax.com> <m20ghvsb36sm46flce74fhktgqojtp0e89@4ax.com> <e0YRa.61464$0v4.4104258@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:
> "Ron" <ron@address.below> wrote in message
> news:m20ghvsb36sm46flce74fhktgqojtp0e89@4ax.com...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> COMPUTE vs ADD.

ACCEPT  BEG FROM TIME.
PERFORM 1000000 TIMES
   ADD A TO B
   END-PERFORM.
ACCEPT FIN FROM TIME.
COMPUTE DUR = FIN-SEC - BEG-SEC.

DISPLAY "Add =  " DUR

ACCEPT BEG FROM TIME
PERFORM 1000000 TIMES
   COMPUTE B = B + A
   END-PERFORM
ACCEPT FIN FROM TIME
COMPUTE DUR = FIN-SEC - BEG-SEC
DISPLAY "Compute = " DUR.

(maybe I ought to patent this idea just in case someone else discovers it)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T17:28:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf9sac$ark$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <3iPRa.78928$sY2.35667@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <oekfhv4ocpt0cr6ni4ek8lslafg754se7m@4ax.com> <m20ghvsb36sm46flce74fhktgqojtp0e89@4ax.com> <e0YRa.61464$0v4.4104258@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <u8Scnerf94ik-oWiXTWJjg@giganews.com>`

```
I have created my OWN "test" for this issue.  (I know there is ONE bug in my
logic - for cases where the "start" and "finish" times are not within the
same minute.  I am not "correctly" dealing with only 60 - not 100 - seconds
in a minute).

My test (with Fujitsu V7) shows a 1/100 of a second difference between the
two statements.   Any comments from others? (I could "send" a text file to
anyone who wants it that way.  Also "GoBack" could be changed to STOP RUN -
and then I think it should compile with any '85 Standard compiler)

    ****


       Identification Division.
        Program-ID. TESTARTH.
       Data Division.
        Working-Storage Section.
       01  Num-Fields.
           05  NF1    Pic S9(05)V99.
           05  NF2    Pic V9(10) Packed-Decimal.
           05  NF3    Pic 9(5)V9(3) Binary.
           05  NFA    Pic 9(5)V9(5).
           05  NFB    Pic S9(8)V9(8) Binary.
           05  NFC    Pic S9(03) Packed-Decimal.
       01  Time-Table.
           05  Second occurs 16 times.
               10  EachSecond   Pic 9.
       01  Info-Times.
           05 Beg-Time    Pic 9(8).
           05 Fin-Time  Pic 9(8).
           05 Dur  Pic z(7)9.
       Procedure Division.
        Mainline.
           Accept Time-Table (1:8) from Time
           Accept Time-Table (9:8) from Time
           If EachSecond (1) = Zero
            Move 1 to EachSecond (1)
           End-If
           If EachSecond (2) = Zero
            Move 1 to EachSecond (2)
           End-If
           If EachSecond (3) = Zero
            Move 1 to EachSecond (3)
           End-If
           If EachSecond (4) = Zero
            Move 1 to EachSecond (4)
           End-If
           If EachSecond (5) = Zero
            Move 1 to EachSecond (5)
           End-If
           If EachSecond (6) = Zero
            Move 1 to EachSecond (6)
           End-If
           If EachSecond (7) = Zero
            Move 1 to EachSecond (7)
           End-If
           If EachSecond (8) = Zero
            Move 1 to EachSecond (8)
           End-If
           If EachSecond (9) = Zero
            Move 1 to EachSecond (9)
           End-If

           Compute NF1 = (EachSecond (1) * EachSecond (2)) / EachSecond (3)
           Compute NF2 = (EachSecond (4) * EachSecond (5)) / (EachSecond (6)
* 100)
           Compute NF3 = (EachSecond (7) * EachSecond (8)) / EachSecond (9)
           Display "Time is in 100ths of a second - 'wall time'"

           ACCEPT  Beg-Time FROM TIME
           Accept Beg-Time from Time
    PERFORM 1000000 TIMES
           ADD NF1 to NF2 Giving NFA
           Add NF2 to NF3 Giving NFB
           Add NF3 to NF1 Giving NFC
           END-PERFORM
           ACCEPT Fin-Time FROM TIME
           COMPUTE DUR = FIN-Time - BEG-Time
           DISPLAY "    Add =  " DUR
           Display "              " Beg-Time
           Display "              " Fin-Time
           Display " "
            .

           ACCEPT Beg-Time FROM TIME
           PERFORM 1000000 TIMES
               Compute NFA = NF1 + NF2
               Compute NFB = NF2 + NF3
               Compute NFC = NF3 + NF1
           END-PERFORM
           ACCEPT Fin-Time FROM TIME
           COMPUTE DUR = FIN-Time - BEG-Time
           DISPLAY "Compute = " DUR
           Display "              " Beg-Time
           Display "              " Fin-Time
           Display " "
            .

           GoBack
            .

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ADD v COMPUTE

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-18T19:35:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hgydnQ6FJNT4D4WiXTWJkw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bf61p6$6m$1@news.utelfla.com> <3iPRa.78928$sY2.35667@rwcrnsc51.ops.asp.att.net> <oekfhv4ocpt0cr6ni4ek8lslafg754se7m@4ax.com> <m20ghvsb36sm46flce74fhktgqojtp0e89@4ax.com> <e0YRa.61464$0v4.4104258@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <u8Scnerf94ik-oWiXTWJjg@giganews.com> <bf9sac$ark$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> I have created my OWN "test" for this issue.  (I know there is ONE
> bug in my logic - for cases where the "start" and "finish" times are
> not within the same minute.  I am not "correctly" dealing with only
> 60 - not 100 - seconds in a minute).

You know, of course, that this technique is "Patent Pending."
```

```
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
> news:u8Scnerf94ik-oWiXTWJjg@giganews.com...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jerry...<G>

It's already accurate to the nearest hundredths of a second - assuming the
test takes no longer than a minute.

01 BEG-TIME.
       02  BEG-HOURS    PIC 99.
       02  BEG-MIN          PIC 99.
       02  BEG-SEC          PIC 9999.

In the words of all those famous authors: "Further refinement is left as an
exercise for the reader."
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T10:12:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1058609520@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

```
Status:  Full Time, Temporary/Contract/Project     Reference Code:
BG-4237382

Job Location: Minneapolis

Analysts International is a national IT consulting company with over 30
offices and 3,500 employees. Our Minneapolis office is seeking 10+ technical
resources with current COBOL, IMS and DB2 skills to work on a large project
for our client. Qualified candidates will have recent work experience with
all skills (Cobol, DB2 and IMS), be avail. ASAP and currently located in the
Twin Cities.

These contract positions are scheduled to be 6-12+ months in length and we
prefer to hire W2 employees.

Qualified candidates should send a Word formatted resume to
mpls.jobs@analysts.com for immediate consideration.
```

```
You're all so pessimistic...!

Yeah the goldrush has passed, and now our market is like any other job
market. If we demand megabuck wages for a job which is not so taxing IMO,
then companies will continue to source overseas. I blame the greed around
Y2K personally...

Nobody owes anybody a living. There's jobs out there for the business minded
amongst you. Cobol platforms and technologies are changing - best change
with it, then...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Job situation in Twin Cites, MN?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jed" <jed@jed.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T13:41:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfe2ne$doj$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<bfc185$b9g$1@newsg3.svr.pol.co.uk> <HpKdnSUohq2LOoSiXTWQlg@giganews.com> <3130871.1058684317@dbforums.com> <bfdoc6$juu$1@titan.btinternet.com>`

```

> >
> > All the jobs are in India and Russia. Forget it.
> >
> China is next on the list I think
>

Incorrecto! Russia isn't ever likely to be a huge outsourcing location
either. COBOL and mainframe technologies are western alphabetic languages,
obviously, and english at that. Not much chance of a mandarin compiler being
developed - communication in general would be far too much of an obstacle,
never mind the lack of a skills base.

That's why India is really the only place this is going to in any quantity
that effects us. English is actually the Indian first language, and
companies are exploiting that left right and centre - such a vast swathe of
English speakers, educated and cheap. That's why call centres, support
centres, financial houses and all other forms of business support services
that aren't dependent on the transfer of manufactured goods are being
outsourced there. It's not just us techies who are suffering - many other
business support functions are feeling the pinch also.

The demand for our skills has actually gone through the roof in the last few
years. Yes, displacement has happened due to outsourcing most certainly to
cut costs, but also to meet ever spiralling demand across the globe. How do
you think that India is ever going to modernise it's economy if it hasn't
got some kind of central mainframe technology to hold health, crime,
education, financial etc etc records on it's 1 billion inhabitants? Via
mainframes. India has always long adopted western technology that has
matured but is still wholly useful.
```

```
it is difficult to find good jobs.
```

```
Both are software emulators. Ten years ago there were hardware emulators. For
about $2K you could buy a mainframe on a card that plugged into your PC bus.
Speed was said to be comparable to a 4381 -- a low-end mainframe that served the
IT needs of medium-sized companies in the '80s. Those hardware solutions are now
gone, thanks I'm sure to IBM licensing roadblocks. 

Rather than pretending to be a mainframe, a better solution is porting source
code to an OS outside the control of IBM.
```

<a id="t-98862e8ab7"></a>
## Thread: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage — 18 msgs

### Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T19:08:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Good evening.

I've been toying around with nested subprograms here lately.  Our 
current "standard" common-storage section has certain information in it. 
  My question is this - without specifying "Global" in common-storage 
within the main program, do nested subprograms automatically have 
visibility of this common-storage, or does it have to actually be 
defined (via copy procs and such ilk) to have the items visible in the 
nested subprogram?

I've read and re-read the manual a couple of times on this, and it just 
doesn't say.  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-19T22:28:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfd2c0$437$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
If I understand your question correctly:

1) If data in an outermost program is defined WITHOUT the GLOBAL attribute,
then it is not "automatically" visible to a nested program.

2) Data without the GLOBAL attribute may be passed (by reference or by
content) to a nested program - just as  it is to a separately compiled
program.

3) Data WITH the GLOBAL attribute is automatically visible to all nested
(within that program) program.

    ***

P.S.  I am not familiar with the term "common-storage section".  Is this a
(Unisys?) extension?  If so, I have no PERSONAL knowledge of how it is
handled or what its "scope" is.  (Possibly Chuck or another Unisys person
will help out.)

P.P.S.  And before someone asks, EXTERNAL is totally separate from GLOBAL.
A data item, may be Global, External, neither, or both.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T05:01:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xmpSa.63256$0v4.4256017@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <bfd2c0$437$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bfd2c0$437$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net...
| If I understand your question correctly:
|
| 1) If data in an outermost program is defined WITHOUT the GLOBAL
attribute,
| then it is not "automatically" visible to a nested program.
|
| 2) Data without the GLOBAL attribute may be passed (by reference or by
| content) to a nested program - just as  it is to a separately compiled
| program.

Data WITH the GLOBAL attribute can also be passed.
(It looked like you might be implying a restriction.)
There is no restriction on using GLOBAL data in a calling parameter list.
GLOBAL exposes the data name, and allows reference to the name.
A parameter list would allow the called program to use an alias (in
Linkage), even though the GLOBAL name is visible to the nested program. (A
stealth REDEFINES.)

|
| 3) Data WITH the GLOBAL attribute is automatically visible to all nested
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
|
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T07:04:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhl1ape6ajdc04@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bfd2c0$437$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <bfd2c0$437$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> P.S.  I am not familiar with the term "common-storage section".  Is this a
> (Unisys?) extension?  If so, I have no PERSONAL knowledge of how it is
> handled or what its "scope" is.  (Possibly Chuck or another Unisys person
> will help out.)

I didn't think it was...  It's a section in the Data Division where you 
can put data items that are visible to subprograms, provided that these 
subprograms have defined this data the same way.  Copy books are 
recommended for that reason.  We currently use it for our database 
control area, so we can write subprogram that have visibility of the 
existing database connection, and not have to make their own.

That would be a shame if it's an extension - but maybe a nice suggestion 
for consideration in 2008...  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T08:54:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg3gvp$nkd$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <bfd2c0$437$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <vhl1ape6ajdc04@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vhl1ape6ajdc04@corp.supernews.com...

> I didn't think it was...  It's a section in the Data Division where you
> can put data items that are visible to subprograms, provided that these
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> existing database connection, and not have to make their own.

It's not standard, and it's not in any MCP/AS dialect of COBOL. that I know
of.  Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85 does have the $COMMON Compiler Control Option,
which turns all areas under its control into COMMON data items.  This is
almost identical to the operation of the MCP/AS COBOL $GLOBAL option and is
intended for (and functional, so far as I know, only in) the bound
subprogram environment.   Is that perhaps what was meant?

> That would be a shame if it's an extension - but maybe a nice suggestion
> for consideration in 2008...  :)

One of the reasons it took nine years to produce the 2002 standard (dated
from the publication of the Corrections Amendment in 1993) (and seventeen
years from the publication of the base standard itself) was that so many new
"gee, wouldn't it be great if ..." pet features kept creeping into the
draft, along with all the "I won't vote to adopt this standard unless it has
..." pet features.

Both committees recognize the danger of "feature creep".   In order for the
first draft of the next standard to be in shape for beginning the
international ballot process by December 2004, which is pretty much a
requirement for getting the standard out in 2008, WG4 has asked that the new
feature content of that draft be limited to that list that was established
at their June meeting, and J4 wishes to conform to that request.  That
feature content "wish list" from WG4 for the 2008 standard has been
published (see document 03-0175 on the J4 web site,
http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/ , resolutions 2 and 3) , and revisions will
take convincing the National Standards Bodies of several countries of the
need for these features in this standard.  J4 continues to maintain a "wish
list" for upcoming revisions to the standard, but at this point anything
that goes on that list will be considered for  the revision *after* the one
currently under development.

I'm not sure what "COMMON-STORAGE" provides that the GLOBAL in the data
declaration entry, or for that matter $GLOBAL in the Unisys MCP/AS extension
to ANSI-85 COBOL, does not.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:vhl1ape6ajdc04@corp.supernews.com...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> subprogram environment.   Is that perhaps what was meant?

No - on the 2200, you actually have a "common-storage" section (comes 
right after the working-storage section, I believe), and it can be used 
as a common area among subprograms.  The 2200 COBOL compiler doesn't 
have any of the $ directives (as I found out also in my research on 
nested subprograms).  :)

> I'm not sure what "COMMON-STORAGE" provides that the GLOBAL in the data
> declaration entry, or for that matter $GLOBAL in the Unisys MCP/AS extension
> to ANSI-85 COBOL, does not.

Global is only subprograms within the main program, right?  Global 
External would do it (both nested and linked subprograms).  The only 
issue we had was with the DMS database - I have yet to find a way to 
tell it to make the subschema copy with either of those on the DMCA and 
record declarations.
```

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

```
You missed the point Daniel.

You shouldn't place it in the COMMON SECTION.

It goes in the WS of the main program with GLOBAL in it's 01.

Your question was whether you then need to define it also in the called
nested sub-program.

The answer is "No".

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T12:03:13-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<vhliqn24l1cc79@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3f1a9337_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a3530.102716111@news.optonline.com> <vhl1mpa8gn9s99@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a9337_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> You missed the point Daniel.
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> The answer is "No".

I'm on a different problem now.  I got that one.  :)  This problem comes 
down to the Unisys DMS and RDMS control areas.  (I know you've seen 
this, since you've seen part of the code I'm working on, but I'm putting 
here for others' benefit.)  Our current programs have an Invoke 
statement in the Data Division, Subschema Section (which I -am- pretty 
sure is Unisys-specific) that follows...

Invoke Subschema CAMS-USUB7R1
     in File      CAMSDBG-7R1
     of Schema    CAMS-SCH7R1
     Copying Records             into Common
     Copying Data-Names          into Common
     DMCA And Run-Unit-Statistics are Common
     [more program-specific stuff]

That last line is really the significant one.  DMCA is not the Digital 
Millennium Copyright Act in this case ;), it's the DMS Control Area.  By 
placing them in "Common", statically-linked non-nested subprograms can 
utilize the existing connection to the DMS database.  The program 
"copies" the database into the program right after the "common-storage 
section" header.

Also, the first copy proc (which comes after the stuff the Invoke puts 
there) that we use is the RDMS control area.  Unisys RDMS is 
interesting, in that if you haven't explicitly begun a thread and you do 
a SQL statement, it begins an implicit one for you.  This isn't good for 
several reasons, which is why I'm trying to avoid it.  I'm just seeing 
if I'm going to have to have an invoke and copy the RDMCA into every 
nested subprogram, or if they have visibility of common-storage items by 
default.

The "Global" think is working great - I've just encountered my first 
case where I need to go to DMS, and I'm trying to figure out if there's 
a nice way to do it.

(I think I could construct a well-crafted Replace statement, like 
"Replace == [each 01-level-item] ==  by  == [each 01-level-item] is 
Global ==.", if that would make them visible.  Even if I changed the 
schema to come into working-storage instead of common, I'd still have 
the problem of all that text being inserted in the program without the 
"is Global" clause.)

Just didn't want you to think that all your e-mails were being 
misinterpreted that badly.  Thanks to you, I completely understand "is 
Common" and "is Global".  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T13:29:47+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1b42b1_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a3530.102716111@news.optonline.com> <vhl1mpa8gn9s99@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a9337_8@news.athenanews.com> <vhliqn24l1cc79@corp.supernews.com>`

```
OK, Thanks for that. I am now better informed <G>.

Have you considered a separate layer for RDB access?

Your general "facilities" (like Date) would simply call out, passing a
single communication block  with a data  and control buffer (the same
concepts as the one in the Main program) but the DB connection and sharing
would be in a module that ONLY deals with DB.(Communication is through the
shared buffer, by reference).

There are a number of benefits to using an n-tiered structure like this.

It means you can have somebody writing the DB access stuff (maybe who's had
a fair bit of DB experience (?)) while someone else is writing the Business
logic.

Separating DB access from Business logic means that both of them can focus
on what they specifically need to achieve and changes to one are less likely
to impact the other.

Suppose you move to a different RDB system? (unlikely in your environment,
but not impossible...). N-tier design really facilitates the move.

Although embedded SQL for data access is probably OK in the Business layer,
when it comes to updating, n-tier is much better. Given the use of common
store by Unisys and access to the sub-schema I would definitely move this to
a "specialist" module.

Pete.
<snip>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T06:03:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhni44crcqkof6@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3f1b42b1_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a3530.102716111@news.optonline.com> <vhl1mpa8gn9s99@corp.supernews.com> <3f1a9337_8@news.athenanews.com> <vhliqn24l1cc79@corp.supernews.com> <3f1b42b1_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> OK, Thanks for that. I am now better informed <G>.
> 
> Have you considered a separate layer for RDB access?

That's sort of where I'm wanting to go eventually.  Like I mentioned 
before, our shop is extremely proc-heavy.  We have what we call R-procs 
for each table in the database.  These procs have a working-storage 
entry point, an "inquiry" entry point (for SELECT commands), and an 
"update" entry point (for INSERTs, UPDATEs, and DELETEs).

This allows for more experienced folks to write the database code, and 
the application guys to just copy in the proc and perform the paragraph. 
  It's not ideal, and the idea was originated when I was out of the 
office for 5 weeks, but it works.  Eventually, it would be nice to have 
these as components as well.

The DMS side (network database) is really as you describe already - it's 
just that the database tier is provided by Unisys.  The DMCA (described 
in the last post) is a 01-level item with lots of information.  In COBOL 
74, there was a pre-processor called ADMLP, which actually converted 
"statements" to "calls".  The COBOL 85 compiler does the same thing, but 
it doesn't replace it in the source listing.

> Suppose you move to a different RDB system? (unlikely in your environment,
> but not impossible...). N-tier design really facilitates the move.

They're still talking about Oracle...  And that's been one of my 
arguments for even pursuing "fixing" (componentizing) something that 
"works".  (Yes, what we have now works, but I've detailed in previous 
posts how it isn't easily modifiable in some cases.  That's what I'm 
trying to "fix".  :>  )

At least I know someone really sharp agrees with me...  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T23:45:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uW_Sa.84880$EQ5.61214@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I haven't tested any of this but I'll tell you what I think should happen.
Data in the common-storage section is defined to exist once for the entire
run-unit, that would include nested subprograms. However, the nested subs
have no way of knowing the structure of common-storage without either a
local definition (i.e. including the subschema section) or getting something
with GLOBAL on it. I'm not sure how you could get that on the DMS
definitions. It seems that the last time I tried a REPLACE on DMS data it
did not work. There is always the WORKING or LINKAGE options but they
typically have their own downsides.

Having read a couple of your other posts, I would try to keep the RDMCA and
program specific entities out of common-storage. Let the DMS stuff live
there by itself. I've seen too much grief doing it any other way. The RDMCA
definition (and whatever else you need) are easily accommodated by putting
the code in a proc and including GLOBAL in its definition.

You commented that you liked the idea of the common-storage section. Having
used it (and seen it misused) for a long time, I prefer using EXTERNAL
instead. Gives the same effect as common-storage (particularly when an item
is declared EXTERNAL and GLOBAL) but has the added benefit of being able to
define multiple "common-storage" sections.



"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com...
> Good evening.
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage
