```
Douglas Gallant wrote:

> You commented that you liked the idea of the common-storage section. Having
> used it (and seen it misused) for a long time, I prefer using EXTERNAL
> instead. Gives the same effect as common-storage (particularly when an item
> is declared EXTERNAL and GLOBAL) but has the added benefit of being able to
> define multiple "common-storage" sections.

So, if I declare something like

        01  RDMCA  External Global.
            03  Error-Status2             Pic 9(04).
            03  Aux-Info                  Pic S9(09) Usage Binary.

I could have a subprogram that just started using RDMS, and it would 
already have visibility of an existing thread?  That's great.

I tried several different things, and none of them worked.  I guess I'm 
going to have to promote the nested subprograms that I wanted to access 
DMS with, and make them actual paragraphs in the main program.

Thanks for the advice.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T03:32:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<alnTa.23974$wk4.458@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <uW_Sa.84880$EQ5.61214@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <vhp279dnvlg4b2@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> I could have a subprogram that just started using RDMS, and it would
> already have visibility of an existing thread?  That's great.

Should work fine. Just remember the difference in visibility that the two
different clauses produce. GLOBAL only affects the program(s) in which it
appears. EXTERNAL makes item available to the whole run-unit. Whether you
only need one or both of these clauses I'm not sure. I don't think you have
the same issues with declaring RDMCA that you do with DMCA, i.e. RDMCA can
be local to any program. The same fields do not have to be used for all
access because it only contains status info. With that being the case, a
simple proc with neither GLOBAL nor EXTERNAL may be sufficient. It becomes a
matter of how you want to handle that data.

I will warn you there a few other subtleties in a multi-LDM program (DMS and
RDMS here). One that I particularly like is if a SQL statement forces a
thread rollback, UDS will also automatically rollback the DMS updates
(reasonable) as well as depart you from the schema so the next DMS command
you execute will get a E$21 (not imparted). Of course, if you have
bullet-proof error handling, not a problem<g>.

"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vhp279dnvlg4b2@corp.supernews.com...
> Douglas Gallant wrote:
>
> > You commented that you liked the idea of the common-storage section.
Having
> > used it (and seen it misused) for a long time, I prefer using EXTERNAL
> > instead. Gives the same effect as common-storage (particularly when an
item
> > is declared EXTERNAL and GLOBAL) but has the added benefit of being able
to
> > define multiple "common-storage" sections.
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T17:09:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhu1tetebdjc86@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<alnTa.23974$wk4.458@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <uW_Sa.84880$EQ5.61214@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <vhp279dnvlg4b2@corp.supernews.com> <alnTa.23974$wk4.458@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Douglas Gallant wrote:

>>I could have a subprogram that just started using RDMS, and it would
>>already have visibility of an existing thread?  That's great.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> matter of how you want to handle that data.

So, even if I don't have direct visibility of the RDMCA, when I do SQL 
in a subprogram, it'll utilize the existing thread?

> I will warn you there a few other subtleties in a multi-LDM program (DMS and
> RDMS here). One that I particularly like is if a SQL statement forces a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> bullet-proof error handling, not a problem<g>.

Ah yes - we actually pitched this as "run unit integrity" to our 
customer to get their buy-in into putting some of our data in RDMS in 
the first place.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Douglas Gallant" <no@spam.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T01:58:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s3HTa.175928$8B.92190@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <uW_Sa.84880$EQ5.61214@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <vhp279dnvlg4b2@corp.supernews.com> <alnTa.23974$wk4.458@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <vhu1tetebdjc86@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> So, even if I don't have direct visibility of the RDMCA, when I do SQL
> in a subprogram, it'll utilize the existing thread?

>"LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
news:vhu1tetebdjc86@corp.supernews.com...
>

That should be the case. RDMS is much more forgiving/flexible in its
programming model than DMS. Maybe the thing to keep in mind is that your
run-unit only has one thread of control active in UDS (I don't think you can
get multiple threads). Therefore, anything you do will get associated with
that single thread. With RDMS, that's really all you have to think about.
The DMS rules, as you know, are much more convoluted, partially because it
maintains more information directly in your program. RDMS maintains
everything external to the program (although the RDMS work area is still in
your address space). The RDMCA, as far as I know, is only used to
communicate status information on SQL calls. Using the same RDMCA address
for the entire run-unit should not be neccessary. At worst, one per
compilation unit may be required but I remain convinced (until you find out
otherwise<g>) that a RDMCA definition per program should work.

Douglas Gallant
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Subprograms & Common-Storage

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T21:58:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhuirc1bap9425@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<s3HTa.175928$8B.92190@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<vhjnc3o6cn9r7a@corp.supernews.com> <uW_Sa.84880$EQ5.61214@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <vhp279dnvlg4b2@corp.supernews.com> <alnTa.23974$wk4.458@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <vhu1tetebdjc86@corp.supernews.com> <s3HTa.175928$8B.92190@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Douglas Gallant wrote:

> That should be the case. RDMS is much more forgiving/flexible in its
> programming model than DMS. Maybe the thing to keep in mind is that your
> run-unit only has one thread of control active in UDS (I don't think you can
> get multiple threads).

That makes sense.  So, I'm still skrewed if I want to do DMS in a nested 
subprogram, but RDMS should be fine, even if the main program's RDMCA is 
in the common-storage section.  I can overcome that...  I'll just make 
the DMS stuff be paragraphs in the main.

I still wish I'd known about External when I started this project - I 
just would have added them to the stuff I mandated everyone put in 
common-storage.  (The MCB packet and RCB are common, along with our 
monster date/time proc's working-storage.)

> At worst, one per
> compilation unit may be required but I remain convinced (until you find out
> otherwise<g>) that a RDMCA definition per program should work.

Time to play...  :)  Thanks!
```

## Thread: Stretching it a bit By Chance....? — 11 msgs

### Stretching it a bit By Chance....?

- **From:** hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-20T06:51:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3130870.1058683881@dbforums.com>`

```

Well, this will probably cause a bit of controversy, but couldn't help
noting it... Someone drew my attention to a press release on the Micro
Focus site which purportedly quotes a ComputerWorld article dated May
28th of this year giving Micro Focus credit for producing the first
COBOL compiler that allows users to create COBOL Web Services without
using any other 3rd party software... Here's the link:

http://www.microfocus.com/press/news/20030519.asp

Now given that Fujitsu have been doing this for about 2 years in .NET,
and even for a year prior to that using their Version 5 and 6 compilers
with the standard free SOAP toolkit (does this count as third party
software maybe?), I find this to be more than a bit of a stretch.... in
my humble opinion. Am I missing something here...?

And I was informed last week that Fujitsu have been compelled to place a
statement on their www.netcobol.com website clarifying that their .NET
compiler has always and does indeed produce MSIL code (how could it be a
NET compiler any other way..?) because a competitior has been
purportedly informing customers otherwise in an effort to inapropriately
discredit Fujitsu....

Seems like things are starting to heat up a bit. Should be
interesting... :-)
Regards,
-Howard
```

#### ↳ Re: Stretching it a bit By Chance....?

- **References:** `<3130870.1058683881@dbforums.com>`

```
You don't consider the ".NET" framework to be a 3rd party product.

If Party 1 is the application developer

If Party 2 is the compiler vendor

who is the *owner* of ".net"?  (I know that a version comes "bundled" with
the .NET Fujitsu product, but they are NOT its "developer").  I do *not*
consider the operating system to require a 3rd party (although those in a
mainframe environment where the OS and the compiler vendor are the same,
might).
```

#### ↳ Re: Stretching it a bit By Chance....?

- **From:** "Gary" <IDontThink@so.com.so>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T02:06:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OUHSa.108371$ye4.81967@sccrnsc01>`
- **References:** `<3130870.1058683881@dbforums.com>`

```
"hhinman" wrote:
> Well, this will probably cause a bit of controversy, but couldn't help
> noting it... Someone drew my attention to a press release on the Micro
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> my humble opinion. Am I missing something here...?

Yes.

First, you've paraphrased the announcement. Corrected, it reads:

"...Micro Focus International Ltd., which last week announced the first
product enabling Cobol code to be deployed directly as a Web service without
requiring third-party software."

The key part of this statement is "deployed directly", not "without
requiring third-party software". Most SOAP toolkits generate "wrapper" code
(typically in Java/C#) to handle the SOAP request and to make the call to
the "interfaced" code (i.e. your COBOL application). This approach does not
scale well.

With Net Express V4 (and Server Express later this year), that extra layer
is no longer required. Execution of the Web service is handled directly by
COBOL and its transactional (scaleable) execution environment.

Note: Since March 2002 Net Express V3.1 has also supported the "indirect"
approach; notably through a partnership with Cape Clear software.

> And I was informed last week that Fujitsu have been compelled to place a
> statement on their www.netcobol.com website clarifying that their .NET
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> discredit Fujitsu....

I've not heard anyone ever suggest otherwise.

Obviously, customers should fully evaluate the available offerings (of which
product is a key component) and make their decisions from there.

Gary.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Stretching it a bit By Chance....?

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T21:39:26+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1bb572_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3130870.1058683881@dbforums.com> <OUHSa.108371$ye4.81967@sccrnsc01>`

```

"Gary" <IDontThink@so.com.so> wrote in message
news:OUHSa.108371$ye4.81967@sccrnsc01...
>
> First, you've paraphrased the announcement. Corrected, it reads:
>
> "...Micro Focus International Ltd., which last week announced the first
> product enabling Cobol code to be deployed directly as a Web service
without
> requiring third-party software."
>
> The key part of this statement is "deployed directly", not "without
> requiring third-party software". Most SOAP toolkits generate "wrapper"
code
> (typically in Java/C#) to handle the SOAP request and to make the call to
> the "interfaced" code (i.e. your COBOL application). This approach does
not
> scale well.

Gary,

I, for one, couldn't care less about your ongoing battle with Howard or the
history of what happened or didn't happen at MicroSoft...no problem, I just
ignore it, and have done up until now.

But the above is just rubbish.

I use SOAP to prepare Web Services and others here have been doing so for
years.

The wrapper does NOT generate Java/C#, it generates XML, which is all that
is needed to ensure that messages are sent and received across the Network.
Why would it "not scale well"? There is nothing to scale. XML is a TRANSPORT
mechanism, not a programming language.

And using the free SOAP toolkit is a very viable way of converting
ActiveX/COM/OLE Components into Web Services WITHOUT moving to .NET or
buying the MicroFocus OR Fujitsu .NET products.

I haven't used Fujitsu's .NET compiler but I would venture to suggest that
it was providing access to .NET (using MSIL) at least 18 months ago, without
requiring "third party software". In fact, Fujitsu have been providing OO
support, Component support, and .NET support while MicroFocus were still
dreaming about it.

This is a Marketing statement by MicroFocus and should be regarded with the
same scepticism we regard marketing statements from ANY company... OK, most
of us will cut some slack to Marketing and understand that very few salesmen
have actually used the products they are trumpeting about or even understand
the finer points, so some hyperbole can be forgiven.

BUT, if you are going to pronounce on things technical please do some
homework and get it right.

Pete.
<snipped more bollocks about MicroFocus>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Stretching it a bit By Chance....?

- **From:** "Barry Rosetti" <barry.rosetti@microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T11:53:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfhcjc$cck$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3130870.1058683881@dbforums.com> <OUHSa.108371$ye4.81967@sccrnsc01> <3f1bb572_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter and all,
I rarely post to comp.lang.cobol and certainly don't want to get involved in
any of the Fujitsu/Micro Focus rivalry that is going on here - however I
feel the need to clarify some points here.

To state the obvious and set a background: when you talk about Web Services,
strictly speaking, you are talking about services made available over the
Web that are invoked by a sending a Soap encoded message either directly or
indirectly (through Soap intermediatories) to an ultimate Soap receiver.
The key things here are that the messages are encoded in Soap and, for 'Web
Services', the protocol and transport for transmission are http over
tcp/ip - of course the Soap encoding can and is used with lots of other
protocols and transports.

Of course, a Soap message is just a message, the actual invocation and
implementation of the Web Service must be written in some programming
language (whether it be C# or C++ or COBOL).   In order to realize a binding
to implementation, there is a technology/software stack on both sides of the
equation (ie. on the sending side and on the receiving side).  These
software stacks takes care of conversion of the data types and data
representations from/to the source(target) language (C#, C++ or COBOL)
to/from the data encoding used in the Soap message.

The point of the Micro Focus announcement is that on both the
sending/receiving side the software stack required to handle the creation of
a Soap message or the reception/execution of a Soap message is 1 level deep.
The Micro Focus Soap handler directly converts from Soap data types and
representations to/from  COBOL data types and representations and then
directly invokes the COBOL implemented Web Service.  There is no
intermediate language layer (such as C# or Java) and no third-party software
is required (ie. no further Soap toolkits, no further Soap servers and no
further infrastructure).

So you see that marketting statement was correct technically.

Barry Rosetti,
Sr. Director of Development, Product/Technology Architecutre
Micro Focus
```

<a id="t-5a2df7bae0"></a>
## Thread: Replacement for JCL on a (Windows) server — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Replacement for JCL on a (Windows) server

- **From:** "The COBOL Frog" <Huib.Klink.Spam@Blocker.MicroFocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T10:08:44+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl>`

```
I'm involved in migrating a batch application to a Windows 2000
server environment. Now, important part of the deployment was
(on the mainframe) and still will be, of course, Job Control and
a JOB scheduler.

Are there tools available to do these things on a server?
Compose JOBs, schedule, JOB dependencies, triggering, you know
the stuff ...

Any rating what's the best JCL-replacing product?

I would very much appreciate to hear names, web site URL's,
experiences etc.

Thanks, regards,

+------------------------------------------------------+
| H.M. (Huib) Klink  also known as  * The COBOL Frog * |
|------------------------------------------------------|
| Mail me @ the address below, remove the spam blocker |
|------------------------------------------------------|
| Huib.Klink.Spam@Blocker.MicroFocus.com               |
+------------------------------------------------------+

P.S: The app. has been written in COBOL, but I think that's not
so important for this question.
```

#### ↳ Re: Replacement for JCL on a (Windows) server

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T21:20:07+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1bb0f0_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl>`

```
Huib,

all of the behaviour obtained by JCL (and RPFs and REXX) on the mainframe
can be achieved by scripting on the PC. The only thing is that it requires
some different approaches.

It is no good trying to "think mainframe" and "translate" JCL.

You need to "think functional" and this easily translates into equivalent
functions in Windows scripting. It is certainly possible to set up jobs to
run in batch and check condition codes and execute other jobs depending on
what happened...even at the most basic level of Batch Procedures using DOS
commands.

Having said that, there are implementations of REXX that run on the PC...

You need to look at Batch files for the PC and or Windows Scripting (try
MSDN).

Good Luck!

Pete.

"The COBOL Frog" <Huib.Klink.Spam@Blocker.MicroFocus.com> wrote in message
news:bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl...
> I'm involved in migrating a batch application to a Windows 2000
> server environment. Now, important part of the deployment was
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Replacement for JCL on a (Windows) server

- **From:** "The COBOL Frog" <Huib.Klink.Spam@Blocker.MicroFocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-22T22:11:30+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfm0g7$c17$1@news.hccnet.nl>`
- **References:** `<bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl> <3f1bb0f0_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote #
in message news:3f1bb0f0_6@news.athenanews.com...
| Huib,
|
| all of the behaviour obtained by JCL (and RPFs and REXX) on
the mainframe
| can be achieved by scripting on the PC. The only thing is that
it requires
| some different approaches.
|
| It is no good trying to "think mainframe" and "translate" JCL.
|
| You need to "think functional" 8<

  8<.

|
| Good Luck!
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| "The COBOL Frog" <Huib.Klink.Spam@Blocker.MicroFocus.com>
wrote in
| message news:bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl...
| > I'm involved in migrating a batch application to a Windows
2000
| > server environment. Now, important part of the deployment
was
| > (on the mainframe) and still will be, of course, Job Control
and
| > a JOB scheduler.
| >
| > Are there tools available to do these things on a server?
| > Compose JOBs, schedule, JOB dependencies, triggering, you
know
| > the stuff ...

  8<

All,

Thanks for all your answers. I summarised all feedback I got
(including
from sources outside CLC) at the bottom of this message.

Pete,

I am fully aware of the need to a mind shift when moving from
one type
of platform to another. Some things that are "usual" or come in
"handy"
have to be done (although functionally still required) in a way
that
applies and is more "at home" on the target platform. It is a
matter
of changing the "how" (i.e. the implementation), not the "what"
(i.e the
functionality). Insofar (is that good English?) I agree with
you.

However, still the "what" remains for this batch app. on the new
Win2K
machine: (1) the need to run "jobs" (related series of programs)
and
(2) the need to schedule these jobs.

Composing jobs can indeed easily and effectively be done via
shell
scripting, in the form of batch files (*.bat or *.cmd, whatever
one
likes). Scheduling however requires some more tooling than is
available
in a Windows OS. Using the native AT command one could schedule
at
times and dates, but job-dependencies are not easily set-up by
AT and
other native DOS-like commands. Running a job (only) when a
previous one
succeeded, another (only) in case the first one abended, firing
a job
at the moment a triggering file is created and closed by a
preceding job
(etc. etc.) is a whole other type of thing. It needs a scheduler
tool /
program that enables to setup such (more or less complex)
schemes of
batches and that enables to execute your jobs as defined.

Of course one could write this myself (in COBOL f.e.) but it's
not
easily done and probably more effective to be bought.

One of my colleagues answered my question by starting of with
the
sentence: "You're right, that is a key area to all IBM migration
tasks".
I believe him, especially when huge mainframe applications have
to be
migrated (batch oriented).

In other words, translating single jobs to scripts
(bat/cmd-files) is
one relatively simple step, but a full-functional scheduling is
still
needed and not that easy made. Better buy, I think.

At last the promised collection of answers I got:

1) - Get the cgywin UNIX compatibility layer and a bash shell.
2) - UC4   - http://www.uc4.com/
3) - TIDAL - http://www.tidalsoft.com/
4) - MKS   - http://www.mkssoftware.com/ (UNIX scripts on
Windows)
5) - "Launchpad" of Cypress Technologies
http://www.cypressnet.com/
6) - Some person mailed me privately (after reading my initial
     question here in CLC):
     "Hi Huib,
      I saw your posting in Comp.Lang.Cobol. We have a parser
that
      converts JCL into MSDos Batch files for one of our
clients.
      It must be ok because they've gone live on it.
      If you want any further details please get in touch."
     Not knowing his reasons not to share this with all of us in
CLC,
     I deliberately left out his name/company here. When he
wants to,
     he'll no doubt post a reply when he reads this.

I shared all this results with our customer. Thank you all.

P.S: I hope 1) my use of 'one' and 'you' is correct and 2) my
     generalizations are no source of huge reply-threads like
others
     recently posted to CLC. Apologies beforehand :))

Regards,

+------------------------------------------------------+
| H.M. (Huib) Klink  also known as  * The COBOL Frog * |
|------------------------------------------------------|
| Mail me @ the address below, remove the spam blocker |
|------------------------------------------------------|
| Huib.Klink.Spam@Blocker.MicroFocus.com               |
+------------------------------------------------------+
```

#### ↳ Re: Replacement for JCL on a (Windows) server

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T06:18:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhnj1230ktvh44@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl>`
- **References:** `<bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl>`

```
The COBOL Frog wrote:

> I'm involved in migrating a batch application to a Windows 2000
> server environment. Now, important part of the deployment was
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> the stuff ...

You've got several options.  You can write a .cmd (.bat) file and 
actually "shell it" (open a command-line shell and start it) - this 
might be the best for dynamic processes.  You can also set up "Scheduled 
Tasks" on the box itself - these are good for your hourly/daily/etc. 
processes.

Depending on what relational database you're using with this product, 
you can write database procedures, and either schedule them on a 
recurring basis, or execute them dynamically through an application program.

> Any rating what's the best JCL-replacing product?

Unless someone knows of an "emulator" or sorts, I'd have to say, for 
Windows 2000 Server, it's DOS.

> I would very much appreciate to hear names, web site URL's,
> experiences etc.

I don't have web sites (although I'm sure http://msdn.microsoft.com is a 
good place to start), but I can share an experience.  One of our 
requirements is to back up the database and web site daily.  On the 1st 
and 15th, we make a special copy that gets burned to CD.  We have a 
batch file that utilizes WinZip's Command-Line interface (WZZIP - free 
to download from http://www.winzip.com ) to ZIP directories of files 
into one file.  This batch file is kicked off by a scheduled task on the 
server.  We also have scheduled database tasks in SQL Server that do the 
actually database backup for us.

There are a lot of automation tools out there by default (I haven't even 
touched on Windows Scripting Host).  You should be able to come up with 
something pretty easily.  :)  Hope that helps...
```

#### ↳ Re: Replacement for JCL on a (Windows) server

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T09:18:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-550332.09183921072003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl>`

```
In article <bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl>,
 "The COBOL Frog" <Huib.Klink.Spam@Blocker.MicroFocus.com> wrote:

> I'm involved in migrating a batch application to a Windows 2000
> server environment. Now, important part of the deployment was
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> so important for this question.

The easy answer is to use the DOS shell COMMAND.COM with .bat or .cmd 
files to replace your procs and jobs.

As an alternative, you might want to get your hands on the cgywin unix 
compatibility layer and a bash shell.  It is much more functional so you 
should not lack for JCL work-alike concepts.

You can schedule with the native AT command or buy/build/develop a 
scheduler reasonably easily.
```

#### ↳ Re: Replacement for JCL on a (Windows) server

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T16:40:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<-M-dnXcusPxdwIGiXTWQlg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bfg72d$a2h$1@news.hccnet.nl>`

```
A pretty good scheduler I've used is "Launchpad" from
Cypress Technologies. www.cypressnet.com   It has more
features than the simple Windows task scheduler such as
starting a task based on the presence of a file and many
others. I used it quite a bit on my last job. It worked
flawlessly.
```

## Thread: PDF output — 13 msgs

### PDF output

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T09:18:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pMRSa.1154$1I5.224176@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Platform ... PC, MicroFocus.

We want to use PDF to store archival reports.  We have two possible sets if
software we are looking at, but neither provides exactly what we want, and I
wonder if anyone has something better.

The simplest seems to be a print driver ... the biggest problem with that is
we have no control over where the PDF file is put ... we open it, print to
the driver, and like magic it appears.  Unfortunately, it places it into a
directory specified at installation and we would like to be able to choose
the directory. It also seems to require that we use a print dialogue, so we
have no assurance that the users will do it right.

The second is a DLL that also appears it would do the job ... unfortunately
it apears it has never been used with Cobol before,and the examples are very
obscure C++.

Does anybody have any other options?

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: PDF output

- **From:** gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-21T22:20:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3136000.1058826043@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<pMRSa.1154$1I5.224176@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

Hi Donald

The Pdf995 driver, as Joe Hunter has mentioned, in the simple and easy
way to obtain automatically a .pdf file (at low cost)

I use it with Netcobol for Windows and what you have to do is only to
pass before run the printing, the print name that must be assumed the
job, like "Invoice_report"..It will create a .pdf file called
"Invoice_Report.pdf" and, if you did set the pdf995 options, you can see
the preview automatically after the print is executed.

If you use Netcobol for Windows, you can create your own .prt file
information, like below:

*

*    Printer Information File
*

PRTDIALG N                       * Print Dialog : YES
PRTID    "Invoice_Report"
PRTDRV   PDF995
FORMKIND C                       * Kind of form : cut paper
PRTFONT  FAC                     * Font face specification
FONTACE  "Arial"                 * Default font face name

It costs about $20 for the complete suite...
You can also set in the PDF995 options, the directory where to store the
pdf generated.

Hope in this help.

Gianni
```

#### ↳ Re: PDF output

- **From:** dan@davince.com (Dan Cogliano)
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T08:01:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2d0837c4.0307280701.1e534dab@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<pMRSa.1154$1I5.224176@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
You can take the report text and convert it to PDF with the shareware
application "DaVince Tools" (http://www.davince.com). "DaVince Tools"
supports column 1 carriage control and can even produce the reports
with the green bar background. Specify the txt2pdf converter and one
of the legacy profiles when using it with COBOL reports (it also
includes TIFF and JPEG converters).
```

- **Date:** 2003-07-22T04:29:11-07:00

```
LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message news:<vhp1c2lqa4jt5a@corp.supernews.com>...
> Carlos lages wrote:
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Olï¿½ Carlos,
Primeiro, obrigado pelo cï¿½digo que me enviou.
Acerca da questï¿½o que coloca, eu defino sempre os campos "data" como
PIC X(23) o que permite acomodar uma data no formato 'AAAA-MM-DD
HH:mm.sss.ccc'. Embora MS SQL Server guarde as datas sob o nome
DateTime ou SamllDateTime que sï¿½o inteiros e representam o nï¿½mero de
"ticks" que passaram desde uma data base (1 de Janeiro de 1584 ou 1900
dependendo do tipo de coluna), nunca percebi qual a relaï¿½ï¿½o que
existia entre estes inteiros e os dados pelas funï¿½ï¿½es "data" do COBOL.
Por isso limito-me a passar as datas como caractere e assegurar-me que
o "parser" da base de dados conhece o formato em que lhe sï¿½o enviadas.

Now in English:
Hi Carlos,
First, thank you for the code you sent.
As to your question, I always define date fields as PIC X(23) which
can acommodate a date in the format 'YYYY-MM-DD hh:mm:ss.ccc'.
Although MS SQL Server stores dates as DateTime or SmallDateTime which
are integers and represent the amout of ticks elapsed since a given
base date, it wasn't clear to me how those integers related to the
COBOL date functions, so I simply pass them as char data and in the
stored procedure make shure that the format is well understood by the
parser. For example:

CREATE PROCEDURE dbo.something
@char_dt char(23)
AS                                     
set dateformat ymd 
declare @w_dt  datetime
set @w_dt=@char_dt
INSERT ...

Cumprimentos
best regards

Paulo Vieira, Emporsoft
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: LU6.0 vs LU6.2

- **From:** robertwessel2@yahoo.com (Robert Wessel)
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T21:12:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bea2590e.0307232012.5568726@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<LdecnfjhyryyoYCiXTWc-g@speakeasy.net> <bfjiu0$lop$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <4sycnV_Z3-ilF4CiXTWc-g@speakeasy.net>`

```
"csira" <csira@speakeasy.net> wrote in message news:<4sycnV_Z3-ilF4CiXTWc-g@speakeasy.net>...
> No, that is what we had first thought.  It is definitely LU6.0.  Looking at
> the commands that they are using, it seems to be a subset of the
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Bill


I think you probably mean LU6.1

LU6.1 was a predecessor to LU6.2 (originally called just "LU6", so
that may be where the "6.0" comes from).  It was implemented in IMS/DC
and CICS only, and I believe there's still support in both systems for
it, so CICS and IBM systems can use it to communicate.  You *could*
implement LU6.1 code with native VTAM (or a comparable low level SNA
API on another platform), but you do it at the raw RU level as there's
nothing like the APPCCMD interface, and it would be quite painful.

To the best of my knowledge, LU6.1 is not directly compatible or
interoperable with LU6.2, but as you've already noticed, most
functions in LU6.1 are supported in LU6.2.  In CICS, many applications
can be converted from LU6.0 to LU6.2 with modest changes, although a
few deleted features will likely cause grief if they're used (for
example, embedded FMHs).  My knowledge of IMS/DC is quite limited, so
no comments there.
```

<a id="t-1bd25ee7a1"></a>
## Thread: COBOL integration with TCP/IP and/or Java (long) — 20 msgs

### COBOL integration with TCP/IP and/or Java (long)

- **From:** clvrmnky <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-07-22T11:58:55-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bdTa.44959$PD3.4478855@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
Greetings,

I am a complete COBOL novice, so please be patient.  I learned a small 
amount of COBOL in high school computer science, but have had little 
opportunity to explore it any further.  I'll admit to a certain 
chauvinism regarding the language.  After a few days reviewing the COBOL 
world, I now see the error of my ways.  It looks like COBOL is still a 
vibrant part of true enterprise computing.

Anyway, because of a small amount of OS/390 experience I have, I'm 
suddenly in charge of figuring out how to hook up a client-server Java 
API to a third-party app that runs on the 390.  This third-party tool 
has a public event and API mechanism we intend to hook into as part of 
the integration.  These events are intended to be accessed via 390 
assembler or COBOL.

We chose COBOL, so I needed to do a crash-course in COBOL, and figure 
out how to make sense of this public API.  I have all the API docs, as 
well as the relevant IBM PDFs describing the language.

Anyway, I'm not intending on this newsgroup to do my work for me.  I've 
mapped out a pretty high-level view of how we intend to get the COBOL 
information into a Java "adapter" that speaks to our own Java API.

I was hoping to treat the stuff we get out of the COBOL side as fixed 
strings, and send them to the Java adapter in some manner.  The problem 
is that I'm not entirely sure how to go about this.  I understand that I 
can invoke Java methods from IBM's 390 COBOL, but I was hoping to be 
able to just send the strings via TCP/IP to the adapter, which then does 
not have to run in a local JVM.  The adapter may have to, as part of the 
event model, call back to the COBOL API to complete it's work.

I just don't know how or if COBOL can speak TCP/IP.  Heck, I'm not sure 
how to get it to talk to a file at this point (I've played around with 
the Fujitsu COBOL compiler on Win32, and can at least get a "Hello 
World" working).  My assumption is that COBOL completely abstracts i/o 
and depends on the underlying environment for resources of this kind, 
but I'm unsure how that mechanism works.  I'm further assuming I have to 
do this in the DATA DIVISION.

Is this the wrong approach?  Can I depend on TCP/IP services at some 
level?  Is JNI more appropiate for this use?  I don't really need tight 
intercommunication between the COBOL and Java adapter -- I'm hoping to 
create one big COBOL program that just grabs the strings I need and 
fires them off to the Java adapter, which will do the rest.

I notice that IBM COBOL supports JNDI, but I _really_ don't want to use 
EJBs or ORB.  The idea is to make this initial adapter part cheap and 
fast -- it doesn't have to be smart at all, as our own API has all the 
intelligence.

I'm continuing to work through a book I found ("Fundamentals of 
Structured COBOL Programming") and reviewing the terse IBM docs.  As 
I've said, I'm a complete COBOL novice, so please be kind if my 
assumptions are off base.  Any ideas or suggestions appreciated.

-- cm
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL integration with TCP/IP and/or Java (long)

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-22T17:17:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfkgmm$f0abh$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<3bdTa.44959$PD3.4478855@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
clvrmnky<clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid> 07/22/03 09:58AM >>>
>Greetings,
>
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>assumptions are off base.  Any ideas or suggestions appreciated.

While COBOL as a language in and of itself does not support TCP/IP, neither
does C.  Both use calls to the appropriate TCP/IP API.  What I'm getting at
is, yes, you can do TCP/IP programming with COBOL.  I have done so myself,
to great success.

Anyway, in the MVS world you would want to use what are called EZASOKET
calls.  The EZASOKET calls are fairly similar to the standard BSD/C TCP/IP
calls (socket, bind, connect, send, receive, close, etc.)  A good manual for
this is the IP Application Programming Interface Guide.  Try:
http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/epubs/pdf/f1af2031.pdf.  There's probably
more recent versions out there, but I don't feel like looking at the moment!
 :-)

Anyway, one big question I have is will the COBOL application on the
mainframe be a batch program or a CICS program?  I hope it's CICS, because
it seems to me that doing it under batch would require some sort of
multi-threading, and I don't know that COBOL supports that.

Well, actually, maybe not.  Is the OS/390 COBOL program going to be the
client or the server?  If the client, it should be fairly easy whether it's
CICS or batch.  Just have the COBOL program create a socket and do a connect
to the server; then send the request, receive the response and close the
socket.  Of course you'd need to agree on a 'message format'.  Meaning, are
the messages to be in EBCDIC or ASCII?  (Probably the latter... you can use
some subroutines from the above manual to do the conversion).  Are you
always going to send messages of the same size?  If not, you need to agree
on how you determine the end of the message.  Myself, I prefer a 'message
header' which gives the length of the message.  You can also use some sort
of delimiter (CR/LF/NL/NUL whatever...).

Anyway, hope this has helped a bit.  Feel free to ask more questions. 
Basically, if you are able to do Java to Java calls using the regular Socket
and ServerSocket classes, then using COBOL EZASOKET calls should be at least
somewhat similar.  In fact, since Java is your main language you may want to
write a simple Java client to connect to your Java server and simulate what
a "COBOL to Java" session would do.  This way you could make sure that your
Java server (I'm assuming that the Java application will be the server) is
working.  Then you can code the COBOL client to interact with a Java server
that you know is working.

Oh, as for file access, yes COBOL has 'built in' access to several file
types (sequential, indexed, etc.).  See FILE-CONTROL and FILE SECTION in
your manuals.  (Well, manuals won't really teach you COBOL, but anyway...)


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL integration with TCP/IP and/or Java (long)

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T04:54:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ByoTa.1798$BB6.56442@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3bdTa.44959$PD3.4478855@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <bfkgmm$f0abh$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
> Anyway, one big question I have is will the COBOL application on the
> mainframe be a batch program or a CICS program?  I hope it's CICS, because
> it seems to me that doing it under batch would require some sort of
> multi-threading, and I don't know that COBOL supports that.

IBM Enterprise COBOL does support running in threads under USS.  I don't
believe COBOL is multithreaded itself but will not have a problem running in
a C or JAVA process ..there is a new THREAD compile option and certain
criteria that must be met such as being reentrant with attention paid to
working versus local storage (obviously).

JCE
```

```
 clvrmnky <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid> wrote:

> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> homework required.

CICS fits the same problem space as the http server does on little 
computers.  It is a transaction server that is tuned to satisfy many 
small, concurrent, requests from various sources.

It manages all machine resources used within said problem space, to 
include threads, files and database connections, for maximum throughput.
```

```
Well, I've determined that we have TCP/IP libraries available to us on 
the target system, and I now have Redbook details on using the 
Communication Server extended sockets.

Our COBOL is going to be driven by, and will run in the environment of, 
the third-party tool we are integrating with.  It will take care of 
loading and invoking my COBOL programs for us.  This will allow us to 
open up a socket to a Java listener on demand and pass a string to the 
Java part, which will do the necessary magic to talk to our API.

This is really a server-to-server integration, but our server is 
essentially slaved to the 390 application.  I've got an example COBOL 
app that simulates being called from the event, and writes it's data out 
to a file (well, the display right now, as I may not be able to count on 
a "file" as I'm used to talking about it).

Lots of work to do yet, and I'm still fuzzy on "levels" and how I need 
to use them to describe my various file bits, but I'm learning.

Thanks to the group for all your help.  I have to put my head down now 
and hack up a prototype.

-- cm
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL integration with TCP/IP and/or Java (long)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T17:27:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfpq0k$ggsn1$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<3bdTa.44959$PD3.4478855@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <bfkgmm$f0abh$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <1CzTa.45388$PD3.4483594@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <4%QTa.70534$0v4.4710245@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bfoup9$hajf9$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <BAVTa.45747$PD3.4488810@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
clvrmnky<clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid> 07/24/03 12:29PM >>>>Frank Swarbrick
wrote:
>
>> Harley<dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> 07/24/03 07:16AM >>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> mainframe COBOL application will act as a client, not a server, and thus
the
>> CICS listener would not fit in to the equation.
>> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>and hack up a prototype.

Well, it's server to server in that both applications are servers.  But in
your specific case, one of the servers must act as a client.  Speaking
strictly within the TCP/IP paradigm, two server processes cannot talk to
each other.  This is because a TCP/IP server process by definition waits
passively for connections, while clients actively attempt to establish a
connection (to a server).  There is nothing to say that a process cannot be
both a client and a server (I have such a process).

To give a specific example, we have an internet banking server.  When this
server needs information from the mainframe it functions as a client and
establishes a connection to a server process running on the mainframe.  It
then sends a request and gets a response.  This doesn't make the internet
banking server any less of a server.  It just also makes it a client as
well.

As for your logic, you probably can think of it in terms of writing to a
file.  In the file situation the COBOL program would do the following:

OPEN the file
WRITE the record to the file
CLOSE the file

Your socket stuff will be pretty much the same:

Create a SOCKET
CONNECT the socket to a server
(these first two are basically your OPEN step)
SEND the record on the socket
CLOSE the socket




--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL integration with TCP/IP and/or Java (long)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T12:24:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg6e59$lm5uh$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<3bdTa.44959$PD3.4478855@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <bfkgmm$f0abh$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <1CzTa.45388$PD3.4483594@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <4%QTa.70534$0v4.4710245@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bfoup9$hajf9$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de> <7ItVa.75565$3o3.5164924@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley<dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> 07/29/03 06:24AM >>>
>
>"Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com> wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>| >| > Anyway, one big question I have is will the COBOL application on
the
>| >| > mainframe be a batch program or a CICS program?  I hope it's CICS,
>| >because
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>| >| This is the part I'm unclear on.  I don't know (yet) if the
third-party
>| >| app runs under CICS or JCL (if that is the correct reference).  I'm
>| >| hoping to find this out tomorrow.  I've read about CICS, but I'm
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>Guess what handles the reply message coming from the server.

Umm, the CICS client task that started the conversation.  Not the CICS
Listener.  We may be getting our terminology mixed up.  As far as I know,
all the CICS Listener does is waits for connections *from* clients and then
STARTs a new CICS task to handle the actual conversation.  Well, I guess it
does 'listen' for an initial message so it knows which CICS task to START.

If a CICS task is a TCP/IP client then it would do the connect, sends,
receives, and close all within the same task.  The CICS Listener would not
be involved.

>AND I said the CICS Listener is a good place to start research.

Perhaps, but I think in this case it would just be confusing.  You state in
your original message "CICS has a TCP/IP listener to receive incoming
messages".  It might be better to say "CICS has a TCP/IP listener to receive
incoming requests".  An incoming would consist, in this case, of both the
initial connection request and the initial request message.  

It is quite possible for the task started by the listener to consist of many
messages flowing in both directions.  In other words, a client could connect
to the CICS listener and then send "ABCD".  The CICS listener would start
tran ABCD.  ABCD might then send something like "OK" and then do a receive,
waiting for the client to send some more data.  The client might send
"2929292" and the CICS task (not the listener but tran ABCD) would receive
it and maybe send back the current balanace of 2929292.  The client, if it
had another account it wanted the balance on, would send another account
number.  This could go back and forth for many accounts.  Finally the client
would determine it's done and might send "DONE" instead of an account
number.  The server might then send "OK BYE" and close the connection.  Then
the client would receive this and close its side of the connection.

Yes, I've gone beyond the original issue.  I just wanted to clarify
(hopefully!) what the CICS Listener does and does not do.  The Listener is
only involved in the original connection establishment and receipt of the
first 'request'.  After that it is not involved in the remainder of the
conversation.  And it is not at all involved when a CICS task behaves as the
TCP/IP client. 

This is my understanding from what I've read in the documentation.  Of
course you may have an example that proves me wrong.  I'd like to hear it,
if that's the case.





--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL integration with TCP/IP and/or Java (long)

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T05:42:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lfpTa.2174$BB6.59324@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3bdTa.44959$PD3.4478855@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
> world, I now see the error of my ways.  It looks like COBOL is still a
> vibrant part of true enterprise computing.
Woo hoo....

> We chose COBOL
Why? Isn't assembler that much easier ;-)

> I was hoping to treat the stuff we get out of the COBOL side as fixed
> strings, and send them to the Java adapter in some manner.  The problem
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> event model, call back to the COBOL API to complete it's work.
You can write a cobol program that is called from Java
You can write a cobol program that calls Java.
This would require Enterprise Cobol and writing OO Cobol to run under USS.
There is also limited XML support which might be appropriate here depending
on what you are doing...it might not.

The new COBOL reference is here (type in COBOL in the search ....bookmark
this page):
http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/LIBRARY

If you have a nice middleware like MQ Series then you could use that as a
message transport which would give you a nice easy way to get disparate
systems to communicate without the hassle of TCPIP.

> I'm continuing to work through a book I found ("Fundamentals of
> Structured COBOL Programming") and reviewing the terse IBM docs.
The COBOL Application guide has a lot of detail I thought.
You could try looking at redbooks.  Most of these focus on Websphere
products but may help a little.

> Any ideas or suggestions appreciated.
Having rethought this I am confused by your overall architecture...what's
the client and what's the server...are you calling the S390 program
remotely? Who initiates what?

If you are trying to get information out of the cobol program from the Java
API on a remote system - you could look at the Redbook called Websphere
Version 5 Web Services Handbook for ideas of how to access legacy data (even
with its Websphere slant)

JCE
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL integration with TCP/IP and/or Java (long)

- **From:** clvrmnky <clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T13:21:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OuzTa.45376$PD3.4483628@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<lfpTa.2174$BB6.59324@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3bdTa.44959$PD3.4478855@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <lfpTa.2174$BB6.59324@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
jce wrote:

[...]
>>Any ideas or suggestions appreciated.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Well, the client-server stuff is pretty remote from what I'm doing.

We have a third-party app that runs on the 390 which has an event model. 
  When something of interest happens in the app, it has the facility to 
run external COBOL or 390 assembler routines.

The plan is to make pretty simple (read: stupid) COBOL routines that 
simply take the data available to it (lots of hand-waving here) from the 
third-party app (when run via the event model), and send that to a Java 
adapter, which will properly speak with our Java API.  The Java adapter 
does all the real work.  Our API talks with our server app, and can act 
as a "proxy" client in the absence of our true client (this facilitates 
server-server integration, which is all that is required in this case).

I'm not sure about the other way: the third-party app has a public API 
that is probably also accessed via COBOL, but I haven't gone down that 
path, yet.

I'm trying to stay away from JNI and/or EJBs; mostly to keep things 
simple and fast.  We also do not want to depend on a JVM on the 390 
side.  This approach also makes it dead easy to setup a test harness 
without having the third-party app or the COBOL pieces present, as we 
can just feed strings into the Java adapter that correspond to the 
application events.  To be perfectly honest, COBOL is a minimal part of 
what we need to do, but it is required.  We are trying to minimize the 
COBOL we have to develop and maintain, as we are not a very COBOL smart 
shop.

Hope this clarifies what I'm trying to do.  The pointers in all the 
replies have given me a lot to chew on.  The next step is finding out 
the environment (if that is the correct word) the third-part app runs 
in.  I understand that "CICS" provides socker communication, but I'm 
unsure how CICS fits in to our situation, or if it is even present.

-- jdv
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL integration with TCP/IP and/or Java (long)

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T12:30:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfmk8t$gcues$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<3bdTa.44959$PD3.4478855@nnrp1.uunet.ca> <lfpTa.2174$BB6.59324@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <OuzTa.45376$PD3.4483628@nnrp1.uunet.ca>`

```
clvrmnky<clvrmnky@coldmail.com.invalid> 07/23/03 11:21AM >>>
>jce wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Having rethought this I am confused by your overall
architecture...what's
>> the client and what's the server...are you calling the S390 program
>> remotely? Who initiates what?
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>unsure how CICS fits in to our situation, or if it is even present.

CICS does not provide socket communication in and of itself.  The socket API
can be used within CICS applications.  The socket API also can be used with
regular batch applications.

It sounds to me like you would want to third party app to function as a
client.  When an event occurs your 'external routine' would connect to your
Java adapter, send the data, perhaps wait for some sort of acknowledgement
from the Java adapter (or perhaps not), close the socket and return.

Your Java adapter (I don't even know what that means, honestly) would have
to be related to some sort of server application.  The server would wait for
a connection from your 390 client.  When one is received it would receive
the incoming message, do whatever it needs to do, close the socket and
return.

I don't think it makes much difference if your 390 application runs under
CICS or not.



--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation
```

## Thread: Where do they use WANG COBOL? — 47 msgs

### Where do they use WANG COBOL?

- **From:** b.sosnow@whitridge.com (Blake Sosnow)
- **Date:** 2003-07-22T13:22:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I am a recruiter with a client in need of a consultant to do some WANG
COBOL and Microfocus COBOL programming on a project near Boston. I
have not worked too much on requirements like this of late and am
wondering if anybody might point me in a direction as to where I may
find a professional with this type of current expertise. Thanks a lot
for your help! b.sosnow@whitridge.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-22T23:34:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com>`

```
>I am a recruiter with a client in need of a consultant to do some WANG
>COBOL and Microfocus COBOL programming on a project near Boston. I
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>for your help! b.sosnow@whitridge.com

Wang Labs went out of business around 1990. It's doubtful you'll find anyone
with _current_ Wang COBOL experience. The good new is you don't need to. 
Wang COBOL conformed to the '85 standard, same as Micro Focus and every other
COBOL compiler. Wang COBOL is not a different language nor dialect; it's
standard COBOL. Its only non-standard extension is in writing data on text mode
screens using DISPLAY and reading it therefrom using ACCEPT. Many COBOL
compilers have similer extensions. 

It sounds like the client is converting from Wang to Micro Focus. In any
conversion project, skills with the target environment are far more important
than detailed knowledge of the old one. The converter needs only to read and
understand the old code long enough to get it converted; (s)he's not developing
new Wang code. Since Wang is so similar to Micro Focus, any COBOL programmer
could understand it. I recommend you advertise for Micro Focus, and just mention
that it's a Wang conversion. 

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

- **From:** "Jim Morcombe" <jim@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T11:57:34+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com>`

```
Robert

Wang Cobol was very different from Cobol 85, even after it claimed to be 85
compatable.

To make matters worse, the Wang "Cobol 85" was so much slower in compiling
than its predecessor that many companies stuck to the normal Cobol compiler.

The actual syntax of Wang Cobol was different.  For example, the word "LOCK"
was used instead of "HOLD" in READ statements, etc.

The Wang Screen Handling and the Wang file handling were totally
non-standard.

But it was a rock-solid, extremely usable compiler.

Porting from Wang Cobol to MicroFocus Cobol is a non-trivial task.  I did
this for about thirty clients and eventually automated the conversion.

So did Sticky Software and a few other companies.

Jim


Jim
```

```
I agree.  The conversion from Wang COBOL to Micro Focus COBOL is *far* from
a trivial task. There are a number of companies in this business.
(Personally, I don't know of any automated tools to do it - but there may be
some).

However, I also agree that finding "current" Wang COBOL developers (much
less support for the underlying WANG software) will be a "challenge".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T15:15:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`

```
"Jim Morcombe" <jim@byronics.com.au> wrote:

>Robert
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>than its predecessor that many companies stuck to the normal Cobol compiler.

The reference below shows before-and-after -- 74 vs. 85 -- compile speed to be
about the same, with a slight advantage to 85.

http://www.dsbarnes.com/atw/cobol85.htm

>The actual syntax of Wang Cobol was different.  For example, the word "LOCK"
>was used instead of "HOLD" in READ statements, etc.

LOCK is 85 standard; HOLD isn't. You got 'em backwards. Converting HOLD to LOCK
is trivial. See the READ statement on page 185 of the Wang COBOL manual cited
below.

>The Wang Screen Handling and the Wang file handling were totally
>non-standard.

Screen handling, yes; file handling, no. While VS XDMS file formats are unique
to Wang, COBOL syntax is standard. Go to the Wang COBOL manual at the reference
below and scroll down to the SELECT statement on page 55. You'll see syntax is
standard (with a few minor extensions). File formats are sequential, relative
and indexed .. same as Micro Focus, same as the 85 standard. 

If you're referring to Wang's PACE database, the program talks to it in SQL,
same as current databases. That's an easy conversion.

http://softdevl.biz/docs/cobol_85.pdf
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jim Morcombe" <jim@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T10:54:49+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfnhan$miu$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com>`

```
Robert


Robert, you should bother to read articles before you quote them.

> The release documents clearly indicate that along with this sophistication
comes greater overhead. For example, a table of
> recommended Modifiable Data Area (MDA; a.k.a. Segment 2) space is
included, showing typical user needs according to
> the number of lines of code and whether the optimizer is used. Due to a
larger need for memory, smaller VS models may not
> even be able to compile programs that previously cleared the COBOL 74
compiler.

For comparisons of compilation speed, the options that restricted the code
to 74 were selected.  Of what relevence is a performance number such as
this?  If you are going to compile in 74 mode, you might as well stick to
the real 74 compiler.

I personally spent three months investigting the advantages/disadvantages in
74 vs 85.  Even though compile time for 85 was dreadful, we ended up going
with 85.

Compile speed was the main nad most controversial issue.  I guess we had a
bit of Fortress Cobol mentality, because it was a bitter fight -
particularly as we had already made a decision to move to Unix and MF Cobol
in the future.

I benchmarked the code as we had written it.  Hence it had no EVALUATES,
END-READs etc.  We were seeing programs take well over three times as long
to compile in some cases.

When reading any product reviews, you must learn to spot the "vendor bias".
This is present in all "independent" reports.

Jim
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T22:10:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> LOCK is 85 standard; 

No it isn't.  LOCK MODE IS .., READ WITH LOCK, etc are not part of the
'85 standard.  They are MF extensions (and Fujitsu etc) and may be
different on each implementation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T15:51:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>different on each implementation.

You're right. It's in the 2002 standard with the same syntax -- LOCK MODE IS
[AUTOMATIC/MANUAL] - found-in every compiler I worked on since '85 (except
Realia). Thus, I assumed it came from the '85 standard. 

Hypothetically, it could be different on each implementation; in practice it is
the same. One compiler, either IBM or MF, became the de-facto standard copied by
others and now ANSI.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T17:51:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> You're right. It's in the 2002 standard with the same syntax -- LOCK MODE IS
> [AUTOMATIC/MANUAL] - found-in every compiler I worked on since '85 (except
> Realia). Thus, I assumed it came from the '85 standard. 

There are many that do not have this. I worked on some '74 compilers
with LOCKing from the late '80s.  It certainly predates '85 by several
years and has nothing to do with it.
 
> Hypothetically, it could be different on each implementation; in practice
> it is the same. 

No. That is incorrect.  It is not 'the same' between, say, MF and
Fujitsu.  Fujitsu has no COMMIT (except for SQL) and cannot gain a
record lock on a REWRITE as MF can.  MF has READ WITH [NO / KEPT/
IGNORE] LOCK, Fujitsu doesn't have 2 of those options. MF also has
WITH WAIT to wait for the lock which Fujitsu does not do, and RM
always does (AFAIK).

It may be that for some compilers there is a simplistic subset of each
which more-or-less works the same.

That is: Hypothetically it could be the same, in practice it isn't.

> One compiler, either IBM or MF, became the de-facto standard
> copied by others and now ANSI.

As IBM does not have LOCKing - except for the rebranded MF systems,
then it wasn't that company that led the way.

RM had LOCKING from the late 70s at least. I used an MF CIS-Cobol
compiler with automatic locking (but no syntax) in 1981.  Locking
syntax was in the 'X-Open' standard, but has moved in different ways
since then.

MS also had different syntax in its own version 2 compiler (not MF).

A quick look on a google search brought up such gems as:

Compaq Cobol (Tandem ?):
""" --------------------
To use the REGARDLESS or ALLOWING options, the program must include 
  these entries:

APPLY LOCK-HOLDING clause of the I-O-CONTROL paragraph
   
ALLOWING option of the OPEN statement
--------- """

Wang Cobol:
"""  ------------------------
Figure 3: Typical COBOL open statement
    OPEN SHARED filename.
------------------------- """

The '02 standard is different again, but a subset may be similar
enough to subsets of others.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T00:10:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfqe3l$fk7$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com>`

```
The X/Open CAE included file-share and record-locking.  This definition was
used as the basis for what was adopted in the 2002 Standard (where it is in
the "processor dependent" list - so no implementor is REQUIRED to include
it).

The definition in the 2002 Standard is SIMILAR but certainly NOT identical
to what X/Open defined.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T23:37:01+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f211746_3@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com>`

```
I found this very interesting, Richard.

I can understand Robert's position because many people using COBOL have
never used LOCK or even HOLD.

As a mainframe programmer I don't recall EVER coding these constructs; as  a
"network programmer" I've always used RDBs for shared access and it is not a
consideration (COMMIT and ROLLBACK of course, DO bear thinking about...)

VSAM shareoptions (set externallly to the COBOL program) tend to take care
of this for mainframe programmers.

With ISAM files on a Network, I can see it becomes relevant, but AUTOMATIC
seems to habdle it OK in my experience (which is not extensive in this area;
I moved to RDB as quickly as i possibly could for shared data access...)

I'm wondering know how serious/important these options are for flat files
and who is actually using them?

Pete.

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com...
> robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
> > You're right. It's in the 2002 standard with the same syntax -- LOCK
MODE IS
> > [AUTOMATIC/MANUAL] - found-in every compiler I worked on since '85
(except
> > Realia). Thus, I assumed it came from the '85 standard.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Hypothetically, it could be different on each implementation; in
practice
> > it is the same.

> enough to subsets of others.
```

```
I do, and I expect it is very common usage on a PC.

One of the most common uses of Cobol on PC's is for the small PC network ...
two or three doctors/lawyers doing standard accounting on a small VAR
system.  For such stuff, a DBS is overkill. Lots of my systems involve one
operator on several machines ... again file level/record level lock out is
common.

Donald
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f21d6af_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com> <3f211746_3@news.athenanews.com> <L59Ua.8269$ys2.443743@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f216ddf_8@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0307251345.314792@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0307251345.314792@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote
>
> > 1. A fully fledged RDB like ACCESS, that can be easily scaled to
anything
> > else is NOT an overkill these days.
>
> Give us a break.  ACCESS may be better in more recent times, but it
> has had significant 'issues' in the past, especially after a BSOD.

Hmmm... not like you to advocate hanging on to the past Richard.

ACCESS 2003 uses the SQL Server engine, is reasonably priced and performs
extremely well. I am using it for several small-to-medium client/server
applications. (So are several million other users World-wide, but I know you
don't rate anything with a MS label on it.) I see ACCESS as a "modelling
tool" that allows DB designs to be fiddled with and tested. You can use it
on a development machine, have complete control over it and immediate access
to it without affecting other users. You can then model the transactions you
are building across it. develop your SQL in situ, and, finally, deploy it,
or scale it to SQL Server or Oracle. (Or MySQl, or Firebird, or Pervasive or
ANY other RDB that is Standard SQL compliant), depending on your workload
and your throughput.

How can that be bad?

Sorry, you lost me on "BSOD"?

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T20:27:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<61WdndMLPr6JRLyiXTWJjg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com> <3f211746_3@news.athenanews.com> <L59Ua.8269$ys2.443743@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f216ddf_8@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0307251345.314792@posting.google.com> <3f21d6af_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>
> Sorry, you lost me on "BSOD"?

"Blue Screen Of Death (obs)" Windows would hold its breath until it turned
the screen blue when confronted by incompetent application programs -
usually writing in C - that allowed unchecked or runaway pointers to cause
corruption in the operating system's kernel ("You're trying to write in a
protected space!" -- "I don't care! Write anyway!").

Now extremely rare; not because C programmers became competent, but because
Micros~1 has innoculated its operating system to protect against renegade
application programs. ("Because you tried to write in a protected space,
here's a little gift: BANG!")
```

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote

> Hmmm... not like you to advocate hanging on to the past Richard.

> ACCESS 2003 

Aha, they finally got it right this year then    ;-)

> uses the SQL Server engine,

I use PostgreSQL.  Better priced and solid - and no CAL issues.

access or direct.  I also have PostgreSQL on another machine on my
development network and MySQL on my laptop.

> are building across it. develop your SQL in situ, and, finally, deploy it,
> or scale it to SQL Server or Oracle. 

With PosgreSQL I don't need to 'scale' it up from a 'toy', it is
already on a full sized RDM.

Well you run XP.  This no longer 'BSOD's [as often], instead of
telling you that it has broken and must be rebooted it silently
reloads itself while pretending that all is well   ;-)
```

```
> With PosgreSQL I don't need to 'scale' it up from a 'toy', it is
> already on a full sized RDM.
>

There is nothing "toy" about modern ACCESS.

Don't believe everything you hear...<G>.

These are good non-MicroSoft solutions, which is what I would expect from
you.

As a matter of interest, assuming you HAD to use Microsoft and deploy under
Windows instead of Linux or a Unix lookalike, what would YOU do?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Access Versions (Was: Where do they use WANG COBOL?)

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T18:06:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vi62at9d377a85@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3F22F5B6.4F5B9B35@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com> <3f211746_3@news.athenanews.com> <L59Ua.8269$ys2.443743@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f216ddf_8@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0307251345.314792@posting.google.com> <3f21d6af_4@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0307252153.6d6d29b8@posting.google.com> <3f228086_8@news.athenanews.com> <ECqdnVfEir29Zb-iU-KYgg@giganews.com> <3F22F5B6.4F5B9B35@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> Just one cautionary question - an answer from Jerry or Pete, or both will do.

How bout me?

> I'm currently dabbling with MS Access 2000, but latest message from Pete, he
> is talking Access 2003.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> next few years ?

Your assumption is slightly flawed.  You can read and write data to 
different versions of Access just fine.  (I've done it, and I know it 
works.)  The problem comes when you have to manipulate the "objects" in 
the database (tables, views (oh wait - they call them "queries"), etc.). 
  That's where you've got to "convert" the database to the new "format" 
so that you can then change the objects.

I'm not sure if this is how Access 2003 works or not, but this was how 
97 to 2000 and 2000 to XP worked.  Your solution is simple, though - if 
you keep a copy of the lowest revision of Access you're planning to 
support, then use it to manipulate your database objects, you can simply 
release the database, and all versions forward of Access should be able 
to read/write the data.  (I would imagine you wouldn't want your users 
modifying your table structure, would you?)

I actually did this in one scenario, and it worked fine.  I had Access 
2000 and Access 97 on the same machine - the default (and the 
application) used Access 2000, but if I had to change the database, I 
fired up Access 97.
```

```
Thanks Daniel,

Let's see if I've got this right.- I'm assuming "Moi" and also the user have only
one version of MS Access :-

- If I have 2000 and user has 2000 - no problem
- If I have 2003 and user has 2003 - no problem
- If I have 2000 and user has 2003 and I provide him with an initial empty DB, but
        with sample data  from 2000 - he will see "needs to be converted" before he

        starts, (i.e.,  if he looks at the DB directly from Access)
- If I have 2003 and user has 2000 - then we have a problem ?
- If I have 2000 and user has 2003 - He comes up with some obscure problem on
    the mdb file; the only solution - send me a copy. He can't, because I can't
read it.
    He will have to export and send me CSVs.

Hopefully that covers all possibilities.

Jimmy
```

```
Actually, on #3, you don't have to provide him an empty database (unless 
that's how the app is designed).  And, make sure he knows to answer "no" 
when it asks if he wants to convert.  (Then, #5 doesn't become an issue, 
because although he's executing under 2003, it's a 2000-format database).

And yes, on #4, you're up the creek.  That's why I suggested maintaining 
at least one copy of the lowest possible revision you planned to support.
```

```
format as exists on the face of the planet, and versatile enough to pass
source code. Fully fledged RDB's like access are fortress cobol mentality at
```
