###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T13:15:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307251215.1c273a58@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com> <3f211746_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote 

> As a mainframe programmer I don't recall EVER coding these constructs; 

Batch programs tend not to need record locking as they tend to have
exclusive access for updating.  I suspect that CICS uses some form of
serialisation to ensure atomic updates.

> as  a
> "network programmer" I've always used RDBs for shared access and it is
> not a
> consideration (COMMIT and ROLLBACK of course, DO bear thinking about...)

Actually it _is_ a consideration, but it may be that you have just not
considered it.

There is a SELECT .. FOR UPDATE in some systems which should produce a
row lock(s) (or in some systems a page or even a table lock).

My understanding of the normal behaviour of SQL systems is that rows
that are UPDATEd are locked until the COMMIT or ROLLBACK and that
SELECTs may read those locked rows.

This makes the system work for most purposes.  But it is not suitable
for some applications - such as Order entry for distribution, or not
for how my customers want to do it interactively.

If their customer rings and asks availability of a product then the
stock level should be locked until the customer confirms the quantity
required. If this is not done with a WITH LOCK or FOR UPDATE then the
stock may 'disappear'.

It is also useful to report back when records are locked, rather than
just waiting until they come free.  In the situation above where the
customer is deciding how many he wants (or whether to take something
else instead), the other users will be told that stock item is locked
(while displaying the details) rather than 'hanging'.

Of course we also want to avoid the problem where two users find that
there are 10 items available and both take 1 and issue UPDATE ... SET
stock = 9 ..
SQL will carefully wait for one to complete then apply the other -
oops.

One can avoid this by adding WHERE ... AND stock = 10 .. This will
fail for one of them and have to go back for reentry.

One could also arrange for the UPDATE to do the subtraction to the
existing figure whatever it is, but then the user may not find out
that the stock is now overallocated (gone negative) and that will
cause an exception at despatch - which order gets the stock is now
dependent on how fast each warehouseman shuffles around (technically a
'race condition', but I've seen them work) rather than on the sales
order taker.

Different solutions are required for different situations.  If there
are lots of products, most in stock, and few concurrent orders then it
doesn't matter how it is done.  Where stock levels often run down
resulting in back ordering as a normal process then locking on
read/select may be best.

At the other end there are order systems with few products and lots of
interactive orders where locking of the stock rows for anything would
cause a disaster.  In that case the initial read must not lock in any
way and the update must be done based on the current stock level by
reREAD WITH LOCK or UPDATE doing a subtraction.
 
> I'm wondering know how serious/important these options are for flat files
> and who is actually using them?

Absolutely vital.

(I assume that by 'flat files' you are actually referring to indexed. 
Sequential files are 'flat', indexed files are not.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T13:36:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f21dbb9_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com> <3f211746_3@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0307251215.1c273a58@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0307251215.1c273a58@posting.google.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That is certainly a possibility... however, as I read on through your post I
realised that every scenario you describe has been part of my career at some
time or another. I think I have considered these things at a system design
level, rather than a programming one.

For example, even in the "old days" using hiererchic databases, we designed
locks into segments so that that level and everything beneath it would be
held when necessary, to fit the scenarios you described, rather than using
the system supplied support which was very often "too broad a brush". (Your
post explains this better than I would've.)

> There is a SELECT .. FOR UPDATE in some systems which should produce a
> row lock(s) (or in some systems a page or even a table lock).
>
Yes, I am aware of that. (I worked for 18 months as a DBA on DB2).

> My understanding of the normal behaviour of SQL systems is that rows
> that are UPDATEd are locked until the COMMIT or ROLLBACK and that
> SELECTs may read those locked rows.
>

That depends on the environment. In an IBM mainframe environment it is
governed by the VSAM SHAREOPTIONS.

 ((1,3) I think, it is years ago and I am relying on memory. That option
does indeed allow 1 update thread  and any number of "read-only" threads,
BUT, it is NOT the only option. If you are prepared to take responsibility
yourself to get exactly what you want, you can have multiple update threads
concurrently.)

> This makes the system work for most purposes.  But it is not suitable
> for some applications - such as Order entry for distribution, or not
> for how my customers want to do it interactively.
>
Absolutely.

> If their customer rings and asks availability of a product then the
> stock level should be locked until the customer confirms the quantity
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This is where judicious use of COMMIT is required. AND considerable
attention to system design as well as programming.

> One can avoid this by adding WHERE ... AND stock = 10 .. This will
> fail for one of them and have to go back for reentry.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
 All of the above is a very useful and clear explanation of some of the
various scenarios. As I read it, it brought memories of various systems
flashing into my head.

I don't disagree with any of this, Richard. My point was that if systems are
designed carefully, it puts less strain on the programmer. That was one
reason why I queried who is actually using LOCK and the need for MANUAL
rather than AUTOMATED locking.


> > I'm wondering know how serious/important these options are for flat
files
> > and who is actually using them?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Sequential files are 'flat', indexed files are not.)

No, my definition of flat files is the same as yours, although I was using
the term broadly. While we "expect" sequential files to be locked on
opening, such is not always the case. Again it depends on the environment.
VSAM supports non-indexed files (Entry sequenced datasets - ESDS) that can
also have SHAREOPTIONS applied to them.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T10:28:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f2254e5.119443990@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com> <3f211746_3@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0307251215.1c273a58@posting.google.com> <3f21dbb9_1@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> ((1,3) I think, it is years ago and I am relying on memory. That option
>does indeed allow 1 update thread  and any number of "read-only" threads,
>BUT, it is NOT the only option. If you are prepared to take responsibility
>yourself to get exactly what you want, you can have multiple update threads
>concurrently.)

Share option 1: Multiple readers OR one writer.
Share option 2: Multiple readers AND one writer.
Share option 3: Anything goes, no file system support, you're on your own, very
fast.
Share option 4: Full support for multiple update threads, automatic (or manual)
record locking, slow. Gotcha: CA split is a fatal error. 

Two share options (x, y)
x = two regions on the same machine (system) or members of GRS ring
y = cross system (semi-obsolete because SMS does not share with non-SMS)

>VSAM supports non-indexed files (Entry sequenced datasets - ESDS) that can
>also have SHAREOPTIONS applied to them.

ESDS files can also be indexed! This is useful for applications that insert
records into a 'collection bucket' file. They can open the ESDS file for APPEND
and quickly write the records sequentially at its end. If a KSDS file is used as
a collection bucket, every write is random and further delays are caused by
CI/CA splits. After building an (alternate) index around the ESDS file (using
IDCAMS), it can be read in key order or randomly, just like a KSDS file.
```

```
responsibility

threads
> >concurrently.)
>
> Share option 1: Multiple readers OR one writer.
> Share option 2: Multiple readers AND one writer.
> Share option 3: Anything goes, no file system support, you're on your own,
very
> fast.
> Share option 4: Full support for multiple update threads, automatic (or
manual)
> record locking, slow. Gotcha: CA split is a fatal error.

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thanks for the details, Robert.

> >VSAM supports non-indexed files (Entry sequenced datasets - ESDS) that
can
> >also have SHAREOPTIONS applied to them.
>
> ESDS files can also be indexed!

Yes, I did know that, but it wasn't pertinent to my argument.

Burroughs (now Unisys) had an access method called "indexed random" that
operated in a similar way. Records were appended to the primary dataset and
chained into the current index. On close, the indexes were sorted. It was
pretty cool for the time and a complete departure from "indexed sequential".

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T06:26:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f221006.101810424@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com> <3f211746_3@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0307251215.1c273a58@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>serialisation to ensure atomic updates.

CICS has explicit READ and READ WITH LOCK on VSAM. The syntax is that of CICS
commands rather than COBOL, but functionality is the same.

>My understanding of the normal behaviour of SQL systems is that rows
>that are UPDATEd are locked until the COMMIT or ROLLBACK and that
>SELECTs may read those locked rows.

Yes. The return code warns you the data may be stale. UPDATEs are held in memory
until COMMIT.

>If their customer rings and asks availability of a product then the
>stock level should be locked until the customer confirms the quantity
>required. If this is not done with a WITH LOCK or FOR UPDATE then the
>stock may 'disappear'.

What if a clerk goes to lunch and leaves it locked for an hour? Just because the
customer inquired doesn't give him an 'option' on the merchandise. 

I never lock data on first read. When it is time for update, I reread it to make
sure it hasn't changed, then rewrite it and release the lock. The lock persists
for a millisecond. 

On databases I avoid the overhead of rereading and locking by putting a WHERE
clause on the UPDATE. With that technique, you don't need locks at all. The
technique will work on any file system, including lock-less ones like the old
Realia.

>One can avoid this by adding WHERE ... AND stock = 10 .. This will
>fail for one of them and have to go back for reentry.

Not reentry, recalculation of the quantity available. If the quantity has
changed, I don't make the clerk reenter the order. If there is still sufficient
quantity to fill the order, I silently update it. The order taker is notified
only if the quantity has become insufficient, and sometimes not even then (see
below). 

>One could also arrange for the UPDATE to do the subtraction to the
>existing figure whatever it is, but then the user may not find out
>that the stock is now overallocated (gone negative)

That would be done in a trigger -- a stored procedure which CAN abort the update
and return an error. 

>At the other end there are order systems with few products and lots of
>interactive orders where locking of the stock rows for anything would
>cause a disaster.  In that case the initial read must not lock in any
>way and the update must be done based on the current stock level by
>reREAD WITH LOCK or UPDATE doing a subtraction.

It can also happen when the number of products is large. There may be a
chi-squared distribution such that the top ten products appear in 80% of the
orders. Imagine a supermarket warehouse with 25,000 items on hand. Nearly every
order will include empty sacks, bags of sugar and flour, etc. There is no reason
to make the customer and order taker wait when quantity on hand is 100 times a
normal order.

------------------------------------------
A related issue is rationing. Suppose you don't have enough quantity to satisfy
the week's demand. Should the early birds get all they want and the late-comers
get none? To make it worse, customers will often be aware there is a shortage
and go into hoarding mode, ordering more than they need to avoid being out next
week.

It's bad business to have some stores out of a staple such as onions or lettuce
while other stores have a surplus. The order entry program should record the
quantity requested. A batch program can later ration the quantity available so
that every store gets some, even though it's less than they asked for. Stores
might catch on and ask for twice the quantity they really want. To avoid that,
rationing might be based on order history rather than quantity requested. 

Rationing is common practice with mass merchandisers and supermarkets,
especially on seasonal and promotional items.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T16:20:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307261520.1d7ddce6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bfl0kf$iq6$1@yeppa.connect.com.au> <3f1e982c.67693889@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307232110.79e5de48@posting.google.com> <3f1ff198.45612495@news.optonline.com> <217e491a.0307241651.ad3a545@posting.google.com> <3f211746_3@news.athenanews.com> <217e491a.0307251215.1c273a58@posting.google.com> <3f221006.101810424@news.optonline.com>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> What if a clerk goes to lunch and leaves it locked for an hour? 

Then the clerk gets fired when he does return.

It is the reason why systems (such as SQL does) should not 'hang'
waiting for the lock to become fre but should return a status (as
Cobol READ WITH LOCK does on many systems).  Preferably it should be
able to report _who_ holds the lock, but this is relatively hard to
do.

In practice it is not an issue.

> because the
> customer inquired doesn't give him an 'option' on the merchandise. 

That is a Business Manager's decision, not the programmers.
```

```
In article <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>>I am a recruiter with a client in need of a consultant to do some WANG
>>COBOL and Microfocus COBOL programming on a project near Boston. I
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>compilers have similer extensions. 

Mr Wagner, WANG used something that I recall as 'DISPLAY AND READ' for 
screen-io; this had a few... whacky variations like 'DISPLAY AND READ 
MODIFIABLE'.  The last version of the compiler I worked with (I think it 
was 4.3) did not support reference modification, scope delimiters or 
inline performs.

Then again, the last time I worked with WANG was on a VS10000 in 1991; 
when was the last time you did so, yourself?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T15:15:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1ea4bf.70912705@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bflpco$rm8$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>>standard COBOL. Its only non-standard extension is in writing data on text
mode
>>screens using DISPLAY and reading it therefrom using ACCEPT. Many COBOL
>>compilers have similer extensions. 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>when was the last time you did so, yourself?

I've not had my hands on Wang; my information came from Wang manuals. I
acknowledged DISPLAY is non-standard. The '85 compiler does support reference
modification, scope delimiters and inline performs because (duh) those featues
are in the '85 standard. Anyway, conversion problems are not caused by missing
features, they are caused by non-standard features. 

The COBOL manual lists Wang extensions on page 17. You can see they are limited
to screen and file sharing. 

http://softdevl.biz/docs/cobol_85.pdf
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Where do they use WANG COBOL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jim Morcombe" <jim@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T11:04:25+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfnhsn$mnr$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`
- **References:** `<c1cbd0ed.0307221222.74476de0@posting.google.com> <3f1dc5e2.13851165@news.optonline.com> <bflpco$rm8$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f1ea4bf.70912705@news.optonline.com>`

```
Robert

I have certainly worked on a lot of Wang VS systems.  I have also read all
the manuals so often I have nightmares about them.

I have never seen a Wang VS Cobol program use reference modification.

There were significant overheads in Opening a VS file - so you never closed
it, just left it open for the next program to use - the Wang file sharing
was so good you never used FILE STATUS - and so on.

As I said before, I could never understand how VS cobol managed to claim to
be 85 compatible.  Even if a compiler has a feature, that doesn't
necessarily mean it is "usable".

Jim
```

```
I've recently used the Fujitsu compiler on some Wang code. As previously
noted, there are a few differences in the File I/O area, but he screen
I/O area is quite different. You should ascertain how much is batch and
how much is on-line. You might get lucky and have llittle or no on-line
and the batch is far easier to convert.
Good Luck,
-Howard
```

<a id="t-d9a1ac2a1c"></a>
## Thread: OT: General Host Environment Skills Growth Question — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: General Host Environment Skills Growth Question

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-22T22:18:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3LiTa.50097$k85.1877584@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
Is the following a common truth or a misconception on my part.  I guess I
thought about this reading the Fortress thread and the "digging in" and lack
of skills upgrading.

Client server (by this I mean Unix/Windows client) developers need to get an
application online and working.  They log onto one of  5 test AIX/Unix/Linux
boxes, install Websphere/JBoss and ship some demos and test files over
there...tweak it and get a prototype....then they change settings, add some
more ....code/develop/test.....they need a database they install MySQL,
UDB...they test, they install a JMS implementation and they .....until they
are fully educated in the world.

Host developers need to get an application online and working.  They log
onto the1 test machine LPAR and see if Websphere is running.  It isn't, they
request support to install....wait.....they ship some demos and test
files......they try and run, RACF won't let them.   They get support to fix
RACFt.  They try again,  it doesn't work, they cannot change setting.  They
tweak. They need DB2/MySQL they call the DBA who calls support, they
wait.....etc etc etc

Is it me or does the environment support structure difference on the two
platforms

1) Encourage poorly structured managed applications where people manage more
than they "really understand"....(i.e. using JDBC doesn't mean your database
subsystem is performance tuned)

2) Inertia that prevents the ability to really "investigate" and try out new
things.

JCE
```

#### ↳ Re: General Host Environment Skills Growth Question

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T12:31:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1dd808_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3LiTa.50097$k85.1877584@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
Excellent and pertinent comment.

All to often getting things done on Fortress sites is like wading through
treacle.

I've
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3LiTa.50097$k85.1877584@twister.tampabay.rr.com...

> thought about this reading the Fortress thread and the "digging in" and
lack
> of skills upgrading.
>
> Client server (by this I mean Unix/Windows client) developers need to get
an
> application online and working.  They log onto one of  5 test
AIX/Unix/Linux
> boxes, install Websphere/JBoss and ship some demos and test files over
> there...tweak it and get a prototype....then they change settings, add
some
> more ....code/develop/test.....they need a database they install MySQL,
> UDB...they test, they install a JMS implementation and they .....until
they
> are fully educated in the world.
>
> Host developers need to get an application online and working.  They log
> onto the1 test machine LPAR and see if Websphere is running.  It isn't,
they
> request support to install....wait.....they ship some demos and test
> files......they try and run, RACF won't let them.   They get support to
fix
> RACFt.  They try again,  it doesn't work, they cannot change setting.
They
> tweak. They need DB2/MySQL they call the DBA who calls support, they
> wait.....etc etc etc
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1) Encourage poorly structured managed applications where people manage
more
> than they "really understand"....(i.e. using JDBC doesn't mean your
database
> subsystem is performance tuned)
>
> 2) Inertia that prevents the ability to really "investigate" and try out
new
> things.
>
> JCE
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: General Host Environment Skills Growth Question

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T01:25:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f1ddd42.19836162@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3LiTa.50097$k85.1877584@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Is the following a common truth or a misconception on my part.  I guess I
>thought about this reading the Fortress thread and the "digging in" and lack
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>are fully educated in the world.

Isn't that how we learned most of what we know .. by trying things?

>Host developers need to get an application online and working.  They log
>onto the1 test machine LPAR and see if Websphere is running.  It isn't, they
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>platforms?

The platforms aren't all that different. The difference is the culture of people
working on those platforms. 

>1) Encourage poorly structured managed applications where people manage more
>than they "really understand"....(i.e. using JDBC doesn't mean your database
>subsystem is performance tuned)

Every database doesn't NEED to be performance tuned. I liken this to old-time
COBOL programmers who pre-optimized every statement, who worried about whether
COMPUTE is faster than ADD when writing new code. We now know that's a waste of
time. One should post-optimize only proven bottlenecks. The same principle
applies to databases. If the application is widely used AND the database is
causing a performance hit THEN it needs to be optimized by professionals. 

>2) Inertia that prevents the ability to really "investigate" and try out new
>things.

Inertia is not just lack of personal initiative, it's institutionalized. 

Prog:      I'd like to get MySQL installed on the test Linux.
Support: Why not use DB2?
Prog:      Its for a project I'm designing. I want to see whether it's faster 
             than DB2. 
Support: Ok, who's the team lead and what's the charge code?
Prog:      It's not an actual project yet. I just want to play with it. 
             Charge your time to general support.
Support: General is for dumb users. I need a project code for developer support.
             Call back when you have an approved project and make sure your team

             lead will sign off on it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: General Host Environment Skills Growth Question

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T04:14:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GYnTa.1476$BB6.53582@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3LiTa.50097$k85.1877584@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <3f1ddd42.19836162@news.optonline.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f1ddd42.19836162@news.optonline.com...
> Every database doesn't NEED to be performance tuned. I liken this to
old-time
> COBOL programmers who pre-optimized every statement, who worried about
whether
> COMPUTE is faster than ADD when writing new code. We now know that's a
waste of
> time. One should post-optimize only proven bottlenecks.

Bad choice of words...I meant poorly structured...data propagated to
multiple tables, inconsistent etc...usually the reason for poor
performance...I talk to DBAs daily even when there is no table involved as
they have unique perspective on data strategy.

JCE
```

## Thread: OT: A litte humor to break up your day — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: A litte humor to break up your day

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-23T10:30:08-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<or6thvgtfct6gg5ft28f3rc84lvvb3sven@4ax.com>`

```

A tourist walked into a pet shop and was looking at the animals on
display. While he was there another customer walked in and said to the
shopkeeper "I'll have a C-monkey please". The shopkeeper nodded, went
over to a cage at the side of the shop, and took out a monkey.  He fit
a collar and leash and handed it to the customer, saying, "That'll be
$ 5000". 
 
The customer paid and walked out with his monkey. Startled, the
tourist went over to the shopkeeper and said, "That was a very
expensive monkey most of them are only a few hundred dollars.  Why did
it cost so much?" 

The shopkeeper answered, "Ah, that monkey can program in C - very
fast, tight code, no bugs, well worth the money." 

The tourist looked at the monkey in another cage. "That one's even
more expensive - $10,000! What does it do?" 

"Oh, that one's a C++ monkey; it can manage object-oriented
programming, Visual C++, even some Java. All the really 
useful stuff," said the shopkeeper. 
 
The tourist looked around for a little longer and saw a third monkey
in a cage of its own. The price tag around its neck read $50,000.  He
gasped to the shopkeeper, "That one costs more than all the others put
together !   What on earth does it do?" 
 
The shopkeeper replied, "Well, I haven't actually seen it do anything
yet, but it says it's a consultant." 

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Real Signs:
Hotel, Japan:  YOU ARE INVITED TO TAKE ADVANTAGE OF THE
CHAMBERMAID.
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

<a id="t-27916e7bbd"></a>
## Thread: XML/HTML Encoding Subprogram — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### XML/HTML Encoding Subprogram

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T08:01:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhvm5it660k4c4@corp.supernews.com>`

```
As promised earlier, here's the XML/HTML encoding subprogram I wrote. 
It will convert ASCII codes that HTML/XML don't like to their character 
code equivalent (&#60;, for example).  It doesn't handle character codes 
over 255 (simply because we didn't need it to).  If you've got access to 
other classes, you probably don't need it, but here 'tis...  Enjoy, use 
at your own risk, blah blah blah...

-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-=-

        Identification Division.
          Program-ID.  IMDS-XML-Class.
       *>________________________________________________________________
       *>
       *>      IMDS XML/HTML ENTITY ENCODING / DECODING SUBROUTINE
       *>
       *> This subroutine accepts a 500-character text field and
       *> either replaces characters which XML finds offensive with their
       *> entity equivalents, or replaces these entities with their
       *> characters.
       *>
       *> WARNING - The same parameter is used for input and  output, so
       *>           truncation will occur if the expanded text is
       *>           greater than 500 characters.
       *>________________________________________________________________
       *>
       *> INPUT REQUIRED
       *> --------------
       *>  - Input Parameter packet, consisting of...
       *>    - Encode / Decode switch - "E" for encode, "D" for decode
       *>    - Text - 500 position alphanumeric field
       *>    - Length - 3 position numeric field (output only)
       *>
       *> OUTPUT EXPECTED
       *> ---------------
       *>  - The text parameter will be converted according to the
       *>    encode/decode switch
       *>  - The length parameter will be filled with the length of the
       *>    converted field
       *>________________________________________________________________
       *>
       *> Author:        SSgt Daniel J. Summers
       *> Date Written:  July 2003
       *> Security:      Unclassified
       *>________________________________________________________________

        Data Division.
          Working-Storage Section.

       *>   Hold areas for strings
        01  WS-In-String        is Global Pic X(500) Value Spaces.
        01  WS-Work-String      is Global Pic X(500) Value Spaces.

       *>   Indices used to step through the strings
        01  WS-Input-Idx        is Global Pic 9(10) Binary Value 0.
        01  WS-Work-Idx         is Global Pic 9(10) Binary Value 0.

       *>   Length of the input string
        01  WS-Input-Length     is Global Pic 9(10) Binary Value 0.
       *>________________________________________________________________

          Linkage Section.

        01  LS-XML-Class-Parameters.
            12                            Pic X(01).
                88  LS-Encode               Value "E".
                88  LS-Decode               Value "D".
            12  LS-String                 Pic X(500).
            12  LS-Length                 Pic 9(03).
       *>________________________________________________________________

        Procedure Division Using LS-XML-Class-Parameters

        . IMDS-XML-Class.
       *>***************************************************************
       *>* Process this string
       *>***************************************************************

       *>   Move the input to global WS, find the length of the input
            Move LS-String to WS-In-String
            Move 0         to WS-Input-Length
            Move 1         to WS-Input-Idx     WS-Work-Idx
            Move Spaces    to WS-Work-String

            Inspect Function Reverse (WS-In-String)
              Tallying WS-Input-Length for Leading Space

            Compute WS-Input-Length = 500 - WS-Input-Length

       *>   Which method do we choose?
            Evaluate True
              When LS-Encode  Call "XML-Encode"
              When LS-Decode  Call "XML-Decode"
            End-Evaluate

       *>   Move the string back to the linkage section
            Move WS-Work-String to LS-String

       *>   Compute the length of the output
            Compute LS-Length = WS-Work-Idx - 1

            Exit Program.
       *>________________________________________________________________
       *>________________________________________________________________

        Identification Division.
          Program-ID.  XML-Encode.
       *>________________________________________________________________
       *>
       *> This subprogram codes necessary entities for XML and HTML
       *> documents.
       *>________________________________________________________________

        Data Division.
          Working-Storage Section.

       *>   Hold area for character code
        01  WS-Char-Code                  Pic 9(03) Value 0.
        01  WS-Char-Code-X           Redefines WS-Char-Code.
            12                            Pic 9(01).
            12  WS-Char-Code-LT-100       Pic 9(02).
       *>________________________________________________________________

        Procedure Division

        . XML-Encode.
       *>***************************************************************
       *>* Encode the input string with XML character entities
       *>***************************************************************

            Perform Until  WS-Input-Idx > WS-Input-Length
                        Or WS-Work-Idx  > 500

       *>       This function returns a number that represents a
       *>       character's ordinal position in the character set.
       *>       However, the character set begins with 0, and this
       *>       function returns at least 1.  For example, the space
       *>       character is 33 from Function Ord, but 32 in the ASCII
       *>       character set.  This is why we then subtract 1 from the
       *>       code this function returns.
                Move Function Ord (WS-In-String (WS-Input-Idx:1))
                  to WS-Char-Code

                Subtract 1 from WS-Char-Code

                Evaluate WS-Char-Code

       *>         The following When clause catches leading and trailing
       *>         special characters, quote (34), ampersand (38),
       *>         apostrophy (39), less than (60), and greater than (62)
                  When   0 Thru 31
                  When  34
                  When  38
                  When  39
                  When  60
                  When  62
                  When 127 Thru 255

                    Move "&#" to WS-Work-String (WS-Work-Idx:2)
                    Add  2    to WS-Work-Idx

                    If WS-Char-Code <= 100
                        Move WS-Char-Code-LT-100
                          to WS-Work-String (WS-Work-Idx:2)
                        Add  2 to WS-Work-Idx
                    Else
                        Move WS-Char-Code
                          to WS-Work-String (WS-Work-Idx:3)
                        Add  3 to WS-Work-Idx
                    End-If

                    Move ";" to WS-Work-String (WS-Work-Idx:1)

       *>         This character is OK
                  When Other
                    Move WS-In-String   (WS-Input-Idx:1)
                      to WS-Work-String (WS-Work-Idx:1)

                End-Evaluate

                Add 1 to WS-Input-Idx  WS-Work-Idx

            End-Perform

            Exit Program
       *>________________________________________________________________

        . End Program XML-Encode.
       *>________________________________________________________________
       *>________________________________________________________________

        Identification Division.
          Program-ID.  XML-Decode.
       *>________________________________________________________________
       *>
       *> This subprogram decodes entities encoded by the above
       *> subroutine from XML / HTML to regular text documents.
       *>________________________________________________________________

        Data Division.
          Working-Storage Section.

       *>   The length of the ASCII code in the line
        77  WS-Code-Length                Pic 9(01) Value 0.

       *>   The character code that we'll convert
        77  WS-Char-Code                  Pic 9(03) Value 0.
       *>________________________________________________________________

        Procedure Division

        . XML-Decode.
       *>***************************************************************
       *>* Replace XML/HTML entity codes w/ their character equivalents
       *>***************************************************************

            Perform Until  WS-Input-Idx > WS-Input-Length
                        Or WS-Work-Idx  > 500

                If WS-In-String (WS-Input-Idx:2) = "&#"
       *>           This is a character entity - determine if it's 2
       *>           or 3 digits
                    If WS-In-String (WS-Input-Idx + 4:1) = ";"
       *>               Entity is 0 - 99
                        Move Function
                         NumVal (WS-In-String (WS-Input-Idx + 2:2))
                          to WS-Char-Code
                        Add  1 to WS-Char-Code
                        Add  4 to WS-Input-Idx
                    Else
       *>               Entity is >= 100
                        If WS-In-String (WS-Input-Idx + 5:1) = ";"
                            Move Function
                             NumVal (WS-In-String (WS-Input-Idx + 2:3))
                              to WS-Char-Code
                            Add  1 to WS-Char-Code
                            Add  5 to WS-Input-Idx
                        Else
       *>                   This will not handle character codes over 3
       *>                   positions, as these codes aren't part of the
       *>                   ASCII character set, and Function Char
       *>                   doesn't support Unicode
                            Move Function
                             Ord (WS-In-String (WS-Input-Idx:1))
                              to WS-Char-Code
                        End-If
                    End-If

       *>           At this point, WS-Char-Code has the proper code for
       *>           the character we need to output
                    Move Function Char (WS-Char-Code)
                      to WS-Work-String (WS-Work-Idx:1)

                Else
       *>           Just move the character - no entity found
                    Move WS-In-String   (WS-Input-Idx:1)
                      to WS-Work-String (WS-Work-Idx:1)
                End-If

                Add 1 to WS-Input-Idx  WS-Work-Idx

            End-Perform

            Exit Program
       *>________________________________________________________________

        . End Program XML-Decode.
       *>________________________________________________________________
       *>________________________________________________________________

        End Program IMDS-XML-Class.
```

#### ↳ Re: XML/HTML Encoding Subprogram

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T18:11:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307241711.43f22fb9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vhvm5it660k4c4@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote 

> As promised earlier, here's the XML/HTML encoding subprogram I wrote. 
> It will convert ASCII codes that HTML/XML don't like to their character 
> code equivalent (&#60;, for example).  

Wouldn't it be preferable to use the standard character entities such
as: &quot; &amp; &lt; &gt; etc rather than the hex values ?

It may also be useful to use character literals rather than ordinal
values to make it usable regardless of the specific character set.

Decoding certainly requires processing any &name; as well as &#nn;.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: XML/HTML Encoding Subprogram

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T05:52:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vi22uvgtvmplc3@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0307241711.43f22fb9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vhvm5it660k4c4@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0307241711.43f22fb9@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:

> LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote 
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> as: &quot; &amp; &lt; &gt; etc rather than the hex values ?

That's why the comments clearly state that that's what they do.  Plus, 
the "standard character entities" to which you refer above don't work in 
XML (without some gyrations).  That's the way I was headed, but when I 
found the &#nnn; format, and tested it under both XML and HTML, and 
found that it worked, it seemed simpler to just do it the same way for 
both formats.

> It may also be useful to use character literals rather than ordinal
> values to make it usable regardless of the specific character set.

This should work with whatever character set you've got - the ordinal 
positions in the character set will still be there, right?  Again, XML 
was what drove the #nn versus &gt; and the like.

> Decoding certainly requires processing any &name; as well as &#nn;.

Yes, the decode isn't perfect.  It was written in a hurry, and is only 
designed to decode items that were also encoded with this routine.  I'll 
probably go back at some point and put all the named entities in there, 
just to make it a little more robust.  :)

Thanks for your comments!  :)
```

---

<a id="t-8217610c6a"></a>
## Thread: inspect reverse — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### inspect reverse

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T10:53:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfp2u3$gsjuj$1@ID-184804.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
In a previous message on the subject of determining the 'length' of a string
(where length is defined as the number of characters before trailing spaces)
I suggested the following:
INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE(MY-STRING) TALLYING MY-LEN FOR CHARACTERS AFTER
LEADING SPACES.

Unfortunately this is not valid COBOL.  I hadn't tested it before posting
the message!  Ah well.  I guess we're still stuck with this:
INSPECT FUNCTION REVERSE(MY-STRING) TALLYING MY-LEN FOR LEADING SPACES.
COMPUTE MY-LEN = LENGTH OF MY-STRING - MY-LEN.

Of course I still have my wish list method of:
INSPECT MY-STRING TALLYING MY-LEN FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE TRAILING SPACES.

Oh well.  We almost have it in:
INSPECT MY-STRING TALLYING MY-LEN FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE INITIAL SPACE.
But this would not properly handle strings with embedded spaces.



--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: display diacritical characters in a screenset

- **From:** stage.charmes@allevard-rejna.com (stage88)
- **Date:** 2003-07-24T23:36:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dbbdc9c.0307242236.305b7399@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<4dbbdc9c.0307240121.195fa9e3@posting.google.com> <3F203B0B.4A753E02@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy,

Thanks for your answer.
I use dialog system to migrate a legacy ERP.Every strings within the
programs are OEM set caracters (and in the IDE they are still OEM
because the Workbench use the OEM set caracters contrary to
Dialog-system which use ANSI set caracters)
I've solved the problem yesterday by putting above the
SCREENSET-INITIALIZED event the following:
CALLOUT-PARAMETER 1 CONFIG-FLAG $NULL
CALLOUT-PARAMETER 2 CONFIG-VALUE $NULL
MOVE 16 CONFIG-VALUE
CALLOUT "dsrtcfg" 3 $PARMLIST

and it's working.
Concerning NLS alternatives, it's ok because i have now severals
libraries used at run-time according to the language used by the
system

Fabrice, FR
```

```
stage.charmes@allevard-rejna.com (stage88) wrote:

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>How can I do?

The nice thing about standards is that there are so many to choose from.

1.  Try this:
     display '8859-1       ' x'E9'
     display 'PC             ' x'82'
     display 'UTF-7        ' x'A9'
     display '646-France ' x'7B'

2.  If none of those work, write a brute-force test program:

     perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > 255
         display n space char(n)
     end-perform

After you find the character,
  inspect MSG replacing all '�' by [x'??' or char(???)]
When the environment/platform/terminal emulator changes,  it may stop working.
```

```
literals" are stored in a reference file - so when 'German' he picks up on the
```

```
Top-posted response to "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

Given that Dialog System distinguishes between countries with a Latin alphabet,
we can infer that DS is playing by either ISO-646 or Unicode rules. What if your
speculation about switching characters sets is incorrect, the set is Latin-1?
What if the display adaptor, terminal emulator or middleware doesn't understand
those standards? What if the program or reference file editor doesn't? 

In an ideal world, the development environment would be synchronized with the
execution environment so that the developer sees what the user will see. For
example:

evaluate language-level
    when 'French'
        move 'Libell� repr�sentant' to MSG
    when 'German'
        move 'Das Wort f�r' to MSG
    when 'English'
    when other
        move 'The word for' to MSG
end-evaluate
 
If we had integration like that, we could all play by the rules. When the
evidence shows lack of integration, we are forced to work outside the box. My
suggestions my be inelegant but they will get the job done. 

 --------------------- history below ------------------
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: display diacritical characters in a screenset

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T19:51:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F217F95.ADFD8216@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<4dbbdc9c.0307240121.195fa9e3@posting.google.com> <3f205911.72105996@news.optonline.com> <3F20618A.F1CDFA18@shaw.ca> <3f20e423.25027511@news.optonline.com>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:

> Top-posted response to "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> suggestions my be inelegant but they will get the job done.

Robert,

See his reply with his solution. Agreed your example above DOES work - BUT - now you
need to add Spanish and Portuguese ???  A year down the road, you are now trading in
Scandinavia. ?. From memory, I don't think you would disagree but the above shows
the merit of three-tier system design, segregating UI from Business Logic and DBI.
Strictly speaking you have to start out thinking of multi-language in your system
design if that is where your application is going.

In an OO environment I use the following  :-

En Anglais :-

if ThisNumber = zeroes
   set DiscountError to true
   invoke self "errorMessages"
end-if

*>------
Method-id. "errorMessages".
*>------
01 T1.
    05 pic x(xx) value "This is an invalid discount rate".     *> DiscountError
    05 pic x(xx) value etc...
01 T2 redefines T1.
    05 ErrorMessage occurs 10 pic x(xx).

Procedure Division.

 string the message and set flags for the messagbebox types
 invoke MyErrorMessages "new" using ws-parameters returning ls-response
*> MyErrorMessages is sub-classed to MessageBox to display the actual
*> messagebox

*>----------

Off the top of my head if I wanted to add German or French - two possibilities.
(1) Up front in the application you set a flag that you will be using French or
German instead of English. (2) Alternatively set the flag in the individual Business
Logic program - but I think #1 is preferable, if possible, and #1 would ALSO allow
me to pass a method-name as a literal, so back to that first example :-

if ThisNumber = zeroes
   set DiscountError to true
   invoke self CountryErrorMethod
end-if

so we would have methods titled as follows :-

*>------
Method-id. "errorMessagesEnglish".
*>------
01 T1.
    05 pic x(xx) value "This is an invalid discount rate".

Method-id. "errorMessagesFrench".
Method-id. "errorMessagesGerman".
Method-id. "errorMessagesSpanish".
Method-id. "errorMessagesPortugese".

Whether you hold these in the Business Logic program which is generating the error
or sub-class them by language, is debatable. I'd have to give it a lot more thought
before deciding.

As to the application of character sets, I don't have a clue. I know one M/F user
wants to use Slavic languages, and there's a problem, (but can probably be solved by
invoking OO classes).

Similarly on the language kick, either here or in Answer Exchange a name like 'Haas'
in Norwegian (I haven't indicated characters over the 'aa's ), when being sorted for
a Listbox, the double 'aa' has to be treated as a singular 'a'.

Multi-language is a challenge all by itself. I know that Pete has produced routines
for dates, certainly in Spanish etc. Some five years back I was dabbling with OO
date routines and I wanted to get the equivalents of the following :-

JanFebMarAprMayJunJulAugSepOctNovDec
SunMonTueWedThrFriSat

I got the French equivalents from Jean in Quebec. Then I wrote to Volker Bandeke. Ah
ha ! Not so easy in German. The full word for Saturday in southern Germany is
different from that used in the north ! And strictly speaking in German, there isn't
a clear-cut set of abbreviations as I show above in English. Then it gets further
complicated - Volker was not prepared to commit himself about Austria - saying there
were subtle differences that he wasn't aware of !

I think I can stick with English. There aren't too many oil fields that I'm aware of
in Quebec <G>.

Jimmy
```

```
Jimmy,

what you posted is a very good multi-lingual solution, in my opinion.

At the same time, I can see where Robert is coming from. He wants a solution
NOW!

And it better be quick, because he has other things to do... (It's very much
an "extreme programming" solution).

There is merit in both approaches, but in the long run, and given that time
can be taken to do some designing and thinking,
yours is superior.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: display diacritical characters in a screenset

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T04:42:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f22042f.98778724@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<4dbbdc9c.0307240121.195fa9e3@posting.google.com> <3f205911.72105996@news.optonline.com> <3F20618A.F1CDFA18@shaw.ca> <3f20e423.25027511@news.optonline.com> <3F217F95.ADFD8216@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
>> Top-posted response to "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>>
>> Given that Dialog System distinguishes between countries with a Latin
alphabet,
>> we can infer that DS is playing by either ISO-646 or Unicode rules. What if
your
>> speculation about switching characters sets is incorrect, the set is Latin-1?
>> What if the display adaptor, terminal emulator or middleware doesn't
understand
>> those standards? What if the program or reference file editor doesn't?

>See his reply with his solution. Agreed your example above DOES work -

His reply says the problem was caused by development software using "OSI" aka
NLS aka ISO-646, whereas the execution software is using "ASCII", which I take
to mean ISO-8859-1. The fix was to disable device-independent 8859 in the
execution environment, then develop a machine-dependent 'library' for each
language. 

That's a step backward to the way we did it before standards. A modern solution
would be an 8859 driver between Workbench and the hardware. With that in place,
the developer would see the same thing the user sees WITHOUT installing
language-specific hardware. 

> BUT - now you
>need to add Spanish and Portuguese ???  A year down the road, you are now
trading in
>Scandinavia. ?. From memory, I don't think you would disagree but the above
shows
>the merit of three-tier system design, segregating UI from Business Logic and
DBI.

The above doesn't show that; it shows the opposite -- messages hard-coded in the
program (business logic) tier. But I DO agree. I like the Dialog System approach
of putting messages in a file rather than in the program. For example, the text
editor I wrote has all prompts in a file named DIALOG. Changing to another
language is as simple as replacing that file. It can be edited with the text
editor. 

>Strictly speaking you have to start out thinking of multi-language in your
>system design if that is where your application is going.

Yes. 

>In an OO environment I use the following  :-
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>*> messagebox

No no .. your messages are hard-coded in a program. Adding a language, or simply
changing a message, requires recompilation and redistribution. Put them in a
file that you or the user can edit. 

>Off the top of my head if I wanted to add German or French - two possibilities.
>(1) Up front in the application you set a flag that you will be using French or
>German instead of English. (2) Alternatively set the flag in the individual
Business
>Logic program - but I think #1 is preferable, if possible, and #1 would ALSO
allow
>me to pass a method-name as a literal, so back to that first example :-
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Method-id. "errorMessagesPortugese".

I picture CountryErrorMethod as:

evaluate country-flag
    when French
        invoke self "errorMessagesFrench"
    when German
        invoke self "errorMessagesGerman"
   etc. 

That's not OO, that's old wine in new bottles. One way to do it OO would be to
overload the errorMessages method. That's not easy in COBOL because program-
pointers cannot be used for methods (unlike C++) and method names cannot be
variables (unlike dynamic calls). If you think about it, overloading is not what
you want anyway. The method is the same for all languages; the only thing
different is T1 -- the message values. Why not create a class named Messages,
create one instance named CurrentMessages filled with the appropriate text by
New, then say simply

 move CurrentMessages::GetErrorMessage to MSG

That looks to me like a truly OO approach. 

>Whether you hold these in the Business Logic program which is generating the
error
>or sub-class them by language, is debatable. I'd have to give it a lot more
thought
>before deciding.

Black or white alternatives. The correct answer is neither; they should reside
in a file and be temporarily held in a data object, not a method.

>As to the application of character sets, I don't have a clue. I know one M/F
user
>wants to use Slavic languages, and there's a problem, (but can probably be
solved by
>invoking OO classes).

The cobbler's children have no shoes. OO classes cannot be applied to the
compiler nor Workbench. If you mean messages on the target machine, sure, there
are several choices -- ISO-8859 and ISO-10646 aka Unicode. Between the two, 8859
is the better choice because it doesn't require 'double byte' support from the
compiler. Because the heavy lifting is done by device drivers rather than OO
classes, Workbench will show the correct graphics. 

>Similarly on the language kick, either here or in Answer Exchange a name like
'Haas'
>in Norwegian (I haven't indicated characters over the 'aa's ), when being
sorted for
>a Listbox, the double 'aa' has to be treated as a singular 'a'.

That's what the COLLATING SEQUENCE clause of the SORT verb is for. That would
handle the problem if only the compiler offered table sort. If it's not MF and
you can't wait a few years, use the CONVERTING option of the INSPECT verb for
single-byte replacements. There is no COBOL solution for double-byte, as in the
above example; you have to do it yourself. 

>Multi-language is a challenge all by itself. I know that Pete has produced
routines
>for dates, certainly in Spanish etc. Some five years back I was dabbling with
OO
>date routines and I wanted to get the equivalents of the following :-
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I got the French equivalents from Jean in Quebec. Then I wrote to Volker
Bandeke. Ah
>ha ! Not so easy in German. The full word for Saturday in southern Germany is
>different from that used in the north ! And strictly speaking in German, there
isn't
>a clear-cut set of abbreviations as I show above in English. Then it gets
further
>complicated - Volker was not prepared to commit himself about Austria - saying
there
>were subtle differences that he wasn't aware of !

The answer is simple -- don't use abbreviations (the computer's not a lazy
writer) and don't use fixed-length for ANY string. Variable length strings are
easy to work with in COBOL. 

>I think I can stick with English. There aren't too many oil fields that I'm
>aware of in Quebec <G>.

Why limit it to Canada? There are oil fields all over the world, especially
Russia, China and the Persian Gulf. Can YOU sell them computer software?

I've heard some of the oil in Alberta and NW Territories is too viscous to pump
without heating. Is that true? Do they inject steam or use fire flood?
```

#### ↳ Re: display diacritical characters in a screenset

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T17:01:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Dear Sir,

I use Dialog together System 3.1 with Win98 here in Brazil.
The characters �, � �, � when used in a PIC X they work perfectly.
Verified is the problem is it is not in your PC.

NLS is just recommended when we want a same GS to be used in more than a
language at the same time.For instance, instead of us to have a GS in
English and a same GS in French, we can have an only GS that supports
the two languages.

Respectfully
```

<a id="t-26c5a4cb6f"></a>
## Thread: Copyleft Cobol Manual — 6 msgs

### Copyleft Cobol Manual

- **From:** "Jim Morcombe" <jim@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T13:32:27+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfqeuc$dj$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`

```
Is anyone aware of any Cobol documentation for Cobol 85 that can legally be
copied as a basis of a new free Cobol manual?

Jim
```

#### ↳ Re: Copyleft Cobol Manual

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-25T13:05:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfrrgf$u7t$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bfqeuc$dj$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`

```
The ANSI Standard itself meets this requirement.  It includes the following
statement in it,

"ACKNOWLEDGMENT
Any organization interested in reproducing the COBOL standard and
specifications in whole or in part, using ideas from this document as the
basis for an instruction manual OI for any other purpose, is free to do so.
..."

Read the full acknowledgement and act upon it as your legal advisor
recommends.

OF COURSE, it is also true that this is now an "OBSOLETE" Standard and any
documentation that you produce should indicate that.
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote 

> OF COURSE, it is also true that this is now an "OBSOLETE" Standard and any
> documentation that you produce should indicate that.

It is only obsolete for the purposes of using it as a 'standard', not
for the purposes of understanding what a '85 compiler will do and how
to program that.
```

```
You can get a FREE copy of the final draft of the 2002 Standard at:
   http://www.techstreet.com/ncitsgate.html

You can purchase (for $100) a downloadable copy of the '85 Standard from:
   http://www.techstreet.com/cgi-bin/detail?product_id=56218

If you do the latter, you may also want to purchase the two published
amendments to it from:
  http://www.techstreet.com/cgi-bin/new_results

ALTERNATIVELY,  I would be happy to send you (if you can accept LARGE
attachments) copies of the '85 Standard and 2 amendments.  (Send me an
email - don't reply to this post)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Copyleft Cobol Manual

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T08:02:47-07:00

```
From what I can see, you can now order a downloadable copy of the official
INCITS/ANSI 2002 COBOL standard from ANSI's standards store at
http://webstore.ansi.org/ansidocstore/ for $18.    I did a Standards Search
for the keyword COBOL to get to it.

It is (or it darned well better be!!) identical in technical content to the
ISO/IEC version also downloadable from ANSI as well as ISO for $273.

ANSI X3.23-1985 (the '85 standard itself), ANSI X3.23a-1989 (the Intrinsic
Function Amendment) and ANSI X3.23b-1993 (the Corrections Amendment) are
also downloadable from the ANSI web site for $18 apiece.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
I recall that a few years back the telecomm companies were moving to 
newly-constructed data centers in Colorado Springs; pimps were burning up 
the lines trying to find folks to whom living in such an area would 
compensate for the abysmally low rates being offered.

Me: 'That's an awfully low rate you're offering for that job.'

Pimp: 'Well, it's a beautiful place to live... and the Cost of Living is 
so much lower, too!'

Me: 'The Cost of Living is lower?  Does that mean when I try to send my 
kid to Princeton I'll get a letter saying 'Oh, you live where there's a 
low Cost of Living... for you the tuition is cheaper!'?'

Pimp: 'Uhhhhhh...'

DD
```
