#### ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T23:25:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r5EUa.75820$0v4.4970838@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com>`

```

K Phelan <karl.phelan@prudential.com> wrote in message
news:6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com...
> I have recently been appointed to a committee responsible for rolling
> out coding standards for my organisation.  My current thoughts on this
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> appreciated.

I wouldn't do this.  There is nothing optional about a "Standard".

Consider producing 2 separate documents:

A  A Standards Manual (your "Essential" items)
B  A Style Guide (your "Desirable" and "Cosmetic" items).

You will eliminate confusion between the Essential items
and everything else, while keeping the Standards Manual
lean-and-meaningful.
```

#### ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T23:29:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f22f595.160586149@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com>`

```
karl.phelan@prudential.com (K Phelan) wrote:

>I have recently been appointed to a committee responsible for rolling
>out coding standards for my organisation.  My current thoughts on this
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>appreciated.

The essentials are consistancy with other programs and quality. Desirable is
beauty. Cosmetic is, what?, personal idiosyncrasies, which by definition are
non-standard. 

I dislike your model because it contains a veiled threat -- Essential is
non-negotiable, Desirable would make management happy, Cosmetic are your
boundaries of self-expression. 

My advice is:

.. Make the standard positive rather than negative. Make it a list of mostly DOs
rather than DON'Ts. In other words, provide leadership rather than repression.

.. Emphasize structure rather than grammar. Structure is where the most
expensive programming errors are made. Nit-picking grammar rules DO NOT correct
structural flaws.

.. Write top-down -- general principles first, technical details at the end. If
you organize it like a COBOL manual, you're doing it wrong, your thinking is
bottom-up. 

.. Provide lots of examples. A structure point cannot be made in a 5-10 line
code snippet. One precept may require 100 lines of non-trivial code. Writing an
adequate standard is not a weekend project.

.. Write the standard so it encourages growth rather than retarding it. 

.. Make it a consensus of programmers' style rather than tablets handed down
from the clouds. If you do this, it will be self-enforced by the programming
staff, as described below. If you impose it on them, you'll foster justified
resentment and disinclination to cooperate.

We discussed that at length in May, in a thread titled "A Style Question." At
the time I wrote:

I have contracted to shops where programming standards were nearly all about
grammar. They were followed rigorously. But the structure of those
shops' programs ranged from poor to horrid. Grammar controls do not produce
quality programs. I dislike them for two reasons:

1. They give the illusion of quality control without the substance.

2. They stifle growth. We should be empowering programmers, not
disabling them.

How to achieve reasonably consistent style across a shop? During the
25 years I managed programming shops, I made it very clear that nobody
'owned' a program. If you write a program today and it needs
maintenance three months hence, the task will NOT be assigned to you.
You will be working on someone else's code. Code is all community property;
we're all team players.

In that environment, a consensus style is developed by the programmers
themselves, out of self-interest. They'd be challenged if programs
looked radically different from each other .. in structure OR grammar.
When someone wants to depart from the norm, he discusses it with other team
members informally. Not at a conference table where egos are at risk.
They each have standing to voice an opinion because they will be
maintaining his code next month.

I, as a team member, voice my opinion, which carries a 'little' more
weight, but is not decisive. In the rare event of deadlock ... that's
where leadership comes into play.

In that environment, style can grow in a evolutionary fashion. When
grammar is formalized, it grows much more slowly .. too slowly to keep up with
changing technology and BUSINESS NEEDS that its champions claim to be concerned
about.

There is an example of what the first page might look like:

* Write a procedure like a story -- with a beginning, middle and end.
In the beginning you clear counters, acquire memory, construct
objects, open files, etc. In the middle, you loop through whatever
you're doing. In the end you print out totals, release memory, destroy
objects, close files, etc. Initialization means 'done at the
beginning'. Programs that 'clean up' at the end of a procedure rather
than the beginning are poorly designed. As a formatting convention,
leave a blank line between the beginning, middle and end.

* Design from the top down; program from the bottom up. When you are
writing a program, part of the time you will be designing and part of
the time you will be programming. Learn to tell the difference so you
can employ the correct mental process. Mediocre programmers do
everything bottom-up.

* Use the company as a design model for your program or system. High
level code should make high level decisions; low level code should do
the grunt work and refer exceptions to higher levels for handling. If
the first code in your program opens files, you have the model
upside-down, you have a file clerk running the company. If low level
code can abort the run due to an exception, that's like giving a file
clerk authority to declare the company bankrupt. For real-time
applications, use the network model, in which modules pass messages to
each other in response to events. Think of each module as a company
department, within which there can still be a hierarchy.

* Write function-like programs of reasonable size and simplicity. Glue
them together to form 'programs'. A reasonable size is 25-100 lines of
procedure. File IO is an especially good candidate for a callable
program.

* Use descriptive data and procedure names that anyone can understand,
not abbreviations or jargon. Run a spell checker on your program. A
program is written once; it is read hundreds of times. If the purpose 
of some code or data is not obvious, explain it with a comment written
in complete sentences which avoid jargon.

* Pass data between programs in memory, not by writing it to a
temporary file. Files are for transport through time, not between
programs. Top down design makes this easy; bottom up design creates
temporary files.

* Handle errors. Do not ignore return codes. Do not say you'll get
around to it later.

* Check limits on arrays, pointers for null, loops for progress. This
is called Defensive Programming. Your programs should never abend nor
go into an endless loop.

* Write one statement per line. Indent a consistent number of spaces
under data structures, if, evaluate and perform.

*Do not duplicate (cut and paste) the same procedure in more than one
place. If you feel the need to do so, your design is poorly
structured. Redesign so the code appears once. If that is not
feasible, due to scope of project, at least make it a callable
program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T05:22:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com>`

```
karl.phelan@prudential.com (K Phelan) wrote in message news:<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com>...
> I have recently been appointed to a committee responsible for rolling
> out coding standards for my organisation.  My current thoughts on this
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> appreciated.

Hello Karl,

I suggest your standards should first be issued as a discussion
document for general input, so people will be more likely to think
that they collectively "own" them after all suggestions have been
considered.

The consistent use of compilation options would be high on the list,
with allowance for variations between environments and programs under
test and ready for implementation.

I think that pre-existing programs should be maintained in the same
style in which they have been written, unless there are good reasons
to the contrary.

I would be inclined to produce some good examples for people to
follow, with explanations of why they are considered to be good. 
Separate examples for programs in different environments would be
appropriate, e.g. online, hatch, windows, mainframe, etc.

If you know of a site that uses the HUON insurance package, then I
suggest that you ask nicely to have a look at it, I was very
impressed.  For that matter, looking at the standards applied by
several different organisations using a variety of platforms and
techniques including both "structured" in its various forms and "OO"
would be desirable, to pick the features you like best that are
appropriate to your circumstances.

Regards, Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** karl.phelan@prudential.com (K Phelan)
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T07:29:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com>`

```
robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones) wrote in message news:<fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com>...
> karl.phelan@prudential.com (K Phelan) wrote in message news:<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com>...
> > I have recently been appointed to a committee responsible for rolling
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> Regards, Robert



Thanks all for your input.  I appreciate that standards are not an
essential part of good coding practice for many organisations, however
I am mainly interested in ensuring consistency of layout.  So far
these are the areas we have been concentrating on.

Variables, literals, counts, accumulators should have a similar
appearance throughout programs.  All should be tagged in such a way as
to make the code readable to the next maintenance person.  e.g
Switches prefixed SW-END-0F-FILE with 88 levels tagged
SW-88-END-OF-FILE-Y etc.

Comments should be flowerboxed (*) before each paragraph detailing the
purpose and scope of the code.

Abends should be handled consistently, so that say the previous 5 or
10 paragraphs called, the abend code and abend message should be
displayed on a sysout with the abend.  Anyone who's ever received a
5:30 call to fix a production error will know the benefit of having as
much information at hand as quickly as possible.

Banning internal COBOL sorts.  SYNCSORT for JCL is far more efficient,
and given the volume of records we normally process, efficiency is a
major consideration.

Each Paragraph to contain only one logical unit of work/function.

Numbering standards on paragraphs, and a rule that no paragraph call a
lesser numbered paragraph.  To ensure top-down structure.

I am enlisting the help of all interested mainframers in my
organisation, but find these groups useful for a different point of
view, so any further comments are welcome.

Thanks
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T20:35:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_TAVa.79497$0v4.5267163@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com> <6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com>`

```

"K Phelan" <karl.phelan@prudential.com> wrote in message
news:6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com...
<<snip>>
|
| Comments should be flowerboxed (*) before each paragraph detailing the
| purpose and scope of the code.

Why?
Isn't COBOL supposed to be readable?
I've seen this in action, 30 lines explaining what a search is doing, or a
detailed explanation of how the PARM is passed from the JCL.
Be careful what you ask for, you may get it.
Be flexible.
If a programmer can read it, and understand it, you don't need to comment
it.

The Working-Storage section is a good place to explain things also.
Comments there may tie things together better than snippets in the Procedure
Division.
* This area is used to ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T14:31:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0307291331.66e1aea5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com> <6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com>`

```
Comments interspersed amongst snipped text

karl.phelan@prudential.com (K Phelan) wrote in message news:<6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com>...
> 
> Thanks all for your input.  I appreciate that standards are not an
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> SW-88-END-OF-FILE-Y etc.

For files and screens, I would be inclined to use file-ids and
screen-ids as the prefixes for data-names, for example ACCT-FILE
datanames would all have a prefix of ACCT.

In working-storage, I would be inclined to have several group items,
one each for switches, counters, subscripts, file-statuses, etc, the
group levels being something like WA-Switches, WB-counters,
WC-subscripts, WD-file-statuses, etc.  The subordinate constituent
data items would have names that are all prefixed by the group name
prefix, e.g. WA-acct-status-sw, WB-acctfile-rec-counter,
WC-acct-table-sub, WD-acct-file-status.  Prefixes up to about WI-
might be reserved for common types of data-item, such as switches,
etc, while those above WI- would be used for application-specific data
constructs.  You might prefer to use indexes instead of subscripts,
but sometimes subscripts are more helpful, particularly when used to
refer to more than one table.  Conditional variable names would be of
the form WD-acct-file-OK, WD-acct-file-at-end,
WD-acct-file-key-not-found, etc, where the file name follows the
prefix and the individual condition description follows the file name.
 Sometimes it is better to associate subscripts more closely to the
tables for which they are used.

In the linkage section, I would be inclined to group parameters into
as few group items as possible consistent with ease of use, e.g. one
group for control parameters followed by data parameters, or to put
very large or variable length data parameters into separate group
items.

Local-storage data items would follow similar conventions for
working-storage.

Common forms of abbreviation when forming data-names can be an asset.
> 
> Comments should be flowerboxed (*) before each paragraph detailing the
> purpose and scope of the code.

I would prefer to do this for sections and to use procedural names
that substantally describe the function in most cases, making such
comments necessary only for more abstruse code.  I would tend to use
just a line of asterisks before each section.  But I would certainly
encourage the use of comments where the meaning wasn't clear.  For
example, in a procedure to read a particular file and do appropriate
file-status error checking, it would normally suffice to use the
procedure name "xxx-read-filexxx", so a comment stating that this
procedure reads filexxx would just be superfluous.  Many COBOL
statements are also reasonably self documenting.
> 
> Abends should be handled consistently, so that say the previous 5 or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> much information at hand as quickly as possible.

Hand coding what is effectively trace information is quite unwieldy
and relatively inefficient.  ABENDAID is a package for handling abends
that has been around for over 20 years and can do this sort of job
very much more efficiently and effectively, providing a mass of
information that can be formatted very helpfully.  Abendaid can be
forced for user abends, by such techniques as calling ILBOABN0, a very
old mainframe IBM cobol compiler routine.  I would recommend having a
look at an installation that uses it in this manner, it can be
configured in a variety of ways.  As time has moved on since I last
did any programming, it may be that the vendor supplied software now
provides similar facilities that would serve the purpose.
> 
> Banning internal COBOL sorts.  SYNCSORT for JCL is far more efficient,
> and given the volume of records we normally process, efficiency is a
> major consideration.
> 
Internal COBOL sorts are neither necessarily more or less efficient
than external ones.  Depending on the compiler, internal sorts
normally use the same sort program as external sorts.  Using external
sorts permits greater flexibility when the sort sequence is to be be
varied between applications, or the same sorted file is to be used by
several other programs.  Using internal sorts can reduce the amount of
I/O that the system has to do, this applies when using either an input
procedure or an output procedure or both.  SYNCSORT and DFSORT both
provide powerful additional processing facilities for record selection
and manipulation, sometimes to the point where another program is
unnecessary.  These additional facilities can also be used when these
sort programs are used in internal sorts.

> Each Paragraph to contain only one logical unit of work/function.
> 
I agree, but would rather use sections, especially if not using COBOL
2002 standard with EXIT section and EXIT paragraph statements.

> Numbering standards on paragraphs, and a rule that no paragraph call a
> lesser numbered paragraph.  To ensure top-down structure.

I basically agree, but would rather use letters, there are more of
them (to accommodate larger more complex programs) and would try to
use them in the following manner:

A-Control
B-Initialise
BA-Start-files
BB-Parameter-validation
BC-setup-headings
C-sort-process
CA-input-phase
CAA-get-input
CAB-key1-process
CABA-get-key1
CABB-key2-process
CABBA-get-key2
CABBB-format-sortrec
CB-output-phase
CBA-Report-write
CBB-Headings
CBC-Format-1st-key
CBD-Format-2nd-key
D-End-of-job
DA-report-write

I agree about not wishing to perform upwards, but there would probably
be some common routines that would need to be used from a variety of
places and some may need to call each other, for example I-O handling
procedures.  These can be placed at the end of a program in groups
with common prefix groupings according to function, for example all
I-O routines could have prefixes starting with W, e.g. WAA-ACCT-OPEN,
WAB-ACCT-READ, WBA-REP-OPEN, WBA-REP-CLOSE.  common calculation
routines could have another set of prefixes beginning with X, e.g.
XA-tax-calc, XB-NI-calc.  Common abend routines could have prefixes
commencing with Z.

CICS programs in particular (if you use them) can sometimes benefit
from performing procedures defined earlier, though the HUON package,
which I recommended you look at if you can, manages very well without
doing so.

> I am enlisting the help of all interested mainframers in my
> organisation, but find these groups useful for a different point of
> view, so any further comments are welcome.
> 
> Thanks
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T16:04:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg6uh2$5aa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com> <6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com>`

```

"K Phelan" <karl.phelan@prudential.com> wrote in message
news:6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com...

There seem to be some "here's some good coding style suggestions" mixed in
with "here's some hints about maximizing efficiency in a particular vendor's
operating environment".    I think it'd be a good idea if these weren't
mixed in together; the suggestions you make should apply equally well
whether the system is IBM, Sun, Unisys, HP Non-Stop or whatever.  Separate
the "this is good IBM mainframe practice" suggestions from the "this is good
COBOL practice" recommendations.   I'm neither vouching for nor criticizing
either set, just noting that one set might well outlive a computer-supplier
change while the other would be rendered irrelevant by it.

E.g.:  ABEND and its direct handling makes no sense in, say, a Unisys MCP/AS
environment.  And in that environment pretty much anything you can do with a
program written for compilation by the SORT compiler you can do with a COBOL
sort verb or even with an interactive sort utility.  There's no reason to
avoid embedded sorts in the *general* case; it's the *IBM* environment in
which there are potentially-more-desirable alternatives.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T00:05:29+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f27b599_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com> <6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com> <bg6uh2$5aa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:bg6uh2$5aa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
>
> "K Phelan" <karl.phelan@prudential.com> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> with "here's some hints about maximizing efficiency in a particular
vendor's
> operating environment".    I think it'd be a good idea if these weren't
> mixed in together; the suggestions you make should apply equally well
> whether the system is IBM, Sun, Unisys, HP Non-Stop or whatever.

Why? If you are developing standards for a given installation, then the
standard applies to THAT installation. The particular hardware/software in
use on it is part of what the local standard must address.

The standards manual (and I have developed a number of them for various
different installations/environments, so I know whereof I speak...) is
primarily intended as a reference for new programmers and contractors, to
enable them to get up to speed quickly and write code that will be
maintainable by the rest of the team. Coding styles and optimization
(including references to vendor's on-line optimization/good practices) are
an essential part of it. I see the two as inextricably intertwined.

For example, if the installation requires subscripts to be in a certain
format it is worth noting that that is because it suits the hardware in use.
(IBM might prefer BINARY or COMP; MicroFocus miight prefer COMP-5. There is
no getting away from the fact that the hardware in use DOES have an effect
on how the language is best coded (certainly as far a sthe DATA DIVISION
goes...)

I would agree that there COULD be separate sections for a general discussion
of Coding styles considered "good" and optimization, but I don't agree that
they SHOULD be separate. It depends...


> the "this is good IBM mainframe practice" suggestions from the "this is
good
> COBOL practice" recommendations.   I'm neither vouching for nor
criticizing
> either set, just noting that one set might well outlive a
computer-supplier
> change while the other would be rendered irrelevant by it.
>
This is fair comment; most sites like to live in the belief that they have
the option to change their hardware vendor... (whether it is real or
not...). That fact is that if/when they do, the programming standards are
not even at the bottom of the list of things that must be considered...

The standards will be updated as experience grows in the new environment.

> E.g.:  ABEND and its direct handling makes no sense in, say, a Unisys
MCP/AS
> environment.  And in that environment pretty much anything you can do with
a
> program written for compilation by the SORT compiler you can do with a
COBOL
> sort verb or even with an interactive sort utility.  There's no reason to
> avoid embedded sorts in the *general* case; it's the *IBM* environment in
> which there are potentially-more-desirable alternatives.
>

But you certainly wouldn't be considering the IBM environment when writing
standards for a Unisys installation, so you wouldn't need to mention the
limitations in it. Instead, you would lay out as simply and clearly as
possible, how things like ABENDS and SORTS are to be handled on THIS
installation, using the facilities of the hardware available on THIS
installation, and the programming style acceptable on THIS installation.

Like I said before, local standards SHOULD be installation specific.

If they are to be of value, they need to inform people about the quirks of
working in THIS environment.

This is the ONLY time I can think of where I would NOT advocate a "general
solution"...<G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T19:42:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vie54detav767d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com> <6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com>`

```
K Phelan wrote:

(You said that comments were welcome...  :>  )

> Variables, literals, counts, accumulators should have a similar
> appearance throughout programs.  All should be tagged in such a way as
> to make the code readable to the next maintenance person.  e.g
> Switches prefixed SW-END-0F-FILE with 88 levels tagged
> SW-88-END-OF-FILE-Y etc.

I'm not so sure I agree with this one.  What I might suggest is use of 
the "Set [condition-name] to True" structure for setting various 
88-levels.  That way, you can actually eliminate the name of the data 
item, and use conditionals, such as...

  01  Pic X(01) Value "N".
      88  SW-Not-at-End-of-File   Value "N".
      88  SW-End-of-File          Value "Y".

This enforces the use of the Set construct to change the switch, and a 
quick search of the source code for that item will retrieve when it is set.

> Comments should be flowerboxed (*) before each paragraph detailing the
> purpose and scope of the code.

We have a similar standard in our shop, except that we mandate the 
right-hand "*" be eliminated.  That way, you can modify the comment 
without having to readjust the right-hand *.  It looks something like 
this...

        . Convert-Jul-to-TS.
       *>***************************************************************
       *>* Convert the input Julian format date to timestamp format
       *>***************************************************************

(I use the *> format comment because I'm trying to get the compiler that 
allows us to use free-format.  Most of our programs just have a single "*".)

> Abends should be handled consistently, so that say the previous 5 or
> 10 paragraphs called, the abend code and abend message should be
> displayed on a sysout with the abend.  Anyone who's ever received a
> 5:30 call to fix a production error will know the benefit of having as
> much information at hand as quickly as possible.

I've never worked on an IBM, so I'm not sure about this one.  We 
generate long link listings, and when we have a major program error, 
there's an address that corresponds to a bank in the link, so we can at 
least identify the module in which the error occurred.

> Banning internal COBOL sorts.  SYNCSORT for JCL is far more efficient,
> and given the volume of records we normally process, efficiency is a
> major consideration.

Again, my lack of experience on IBM comes into play here.  Would it be 
any more efficient to build an indexed file as the programs run and 
accrue the output data, then read this file back and write a sequential 
one as output?  That's one thing we do in some of our programs.  That 
also easily allows us to set up alternate keys, and provide different 
sort options that the user can select.

> Each Paragraph to contain only one logical unit of work/function.

This can be taken to an extreme.  And, depending on your use of scope 
terminators, this seems to be just a tad unnecessary.  I tend to group 
paragraphs by program option, combining where I can.  I'll sometimes set 
up paragraphs that edit each screen field, and instead of returning a 
reject, they'll set a switch.  (For example, on an inquiry screen, the 
"device" must be loaded to the database already, but on the load, the 
device cannot be loaded.  I have one "edit device" paragraph, that 
returns either "device loaded" or "device not loaded".  Then, I make the 
decision about whether to reject or not in the paragraph which performed 
the edit.

> Numbering standards on paragraphs, and a rule that no paragraph call a
> lesser numbered paragraph.  To ensure top-down structure.

We do this.  However, we don't have that second rule.  I don't know that 
we shouldn't, we just don't.  :)

> I am enlisting the help of all interested mainframers in my
> organisation, but find these groups useful for a different point of
> view, so any further comments are welcome.

Hope these have given you more food for thought.  Most of these are 
style, so there's really no "one" way to do them.  Personally, I much 
prefer using one period for each paragraph, and always using scope 
terminators.  I've found that this greatly aids in maintenance, as you 
can take big chunks of code that used to all be at column 12, and put 
them under a conditional without having to go back and make sure you 
delete every period.

Again, these style issues are going to vary from person to person - I 
just thought I'd share some of my experiences, and what I've found useful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T17:56:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg90sv$r0n$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com> <6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com> <vie54detav767d@corp.supernews.com> <bg8s3n$p77$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d8TVa.77033$3o3.5296235@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On 30-Jul-2003, "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> | > We have a similar standard in our shop, except that we mandate the
> | > right-hand "*" be eliminated.  That way, you can modify the comment
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> What if you delete a word?

Turn insert on.

> Keeping the right hand '*' aligned is a problem, you're better off without

I've never found a problem with it since I went to PC terminals.   The insert
key toggles real easily.

Where I have seen people irritated is in the opposite.   When you don't have
characters to the right and they use the cursor to paste some data in, they
press enter and everything moves to the left.    That is irritating, but doesn't
happen when you have an asterisk on the right.
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>

```
like
| > | > this...

| > | >
*>***************************************************************
| > | >        *>* Convert the input Julian format date to timestamp format
| > | >
*>***************************************************************
| > |
| > | Just turn insert off.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| Turn insert on.

The delete shifts the "*" on the right to the left.
So you're still playing games to keep the asterisks aligned.

|
| > Keeping the right hand '*' aligned is a problem, you're better off
without
|
| I've never found a problem with it since I went to PC terminals.   The
insert
| key toggles real easily.
|
| Where I have seen people irritated is in the opposite.   When you don't
have
| characters to the right and they use the cursor to paste some data in,
they
| press enter and everything moves to the left.    That is irritating, but
doesn't
| happen when you have an asterisk on the right.

Turn nulls off.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T01:25:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f272bea.954896@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com> <6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com>`

```
karl.phelan@prudential.com (K Phelan) wrote:


>Banning internal COBOL sorts.  SYNCSORT for JCL is far more efficient,
>and given the volume of records we normally process, efficiency is a
>major consideration.

Have you timed it? That's hard to believe since COBOL sort *uses* Syncsort.
COBOL sort should be faster because it eliminates two writes to and reads from
temporary files.
```

```
Doesn't COBOL use SYNCSORT in your shop?

But this brings up a question about your standards (I disagree with many of your
proposals):

Are these to be rigidly enforced, or do they mean that the programmer must
demonstrate a good reason for a COBOL sort for it to get passed the reviewers?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T18:31:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<taUVa.77100$3o3.5300565@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com> <6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com>`

```

K Phelan <karl.phelan@prudential.com> wrote in message
news:6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com...
>
> Abends should be handled consistently

....but should be an action of last resort.

You do NOT want to crash a batch application at 2 AM
if it is avoidable and can be handled in a manner
that will detect an anomalous volume of such errors,
at which point an abend may be sanctioned.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T20:27:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg99n2$164$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307270422.4e0040d8@posting.google.com> <6b6a16c3.0307290629.298a3280@posting.google.com> <taUVa.77100$3o3.5300565@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On 30-Jul-2003, "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

> > Abends should be handled consistently
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> at which point an abend may be sanctioned.

Unless that's what we want.   One advantage of an abend is that the operator
won't miss it, and will pull out his instructions to do what is necessary.  
Usually when this happens, the program already displayed the relevant data
before going to an abend.
```

#### ↳ Re: Rollout of Coding Standards

- **From:** Alain Feler <member15854@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T12:28:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3160707.1059481715@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<6b6a16c3.0307260733.58f88e1c@posting.google.com>`

```

I tried this twice in two different shops... the result was not
very useful.
Such documents are so annoying to read...
Also the way - even the wrong way - people program is deeply rooted in
them and internally cohesive, so changing a part of it makes it usually
worst. Changing all of it is hard, and requires leadership.
Maybe an idea would be to find a convenient reference book and buy a
copy for each programmer: usually the author spent more time on the
subject than you can afford.
A thing that must be stated to manage the transition is the range of the
rules : things to change in any program on which some work is done,
things to do only in new programs, things that can be local to the
change made, or that must be applied to the entire source affected by a
change (and then tested...).
<<<
From: K Phelan
Date: 07-26-03 15:33
Subject: Rollout of Coding Standards

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
----------
I have recently been appointed to a committee responsible for rolling
out coding standards for my organisation. My current thoughts on this
are to rollout the standards in three phases. Essential, desirable
and cosmetic standards. If you have had experience of this in your
organisation and can offer some advice it would be greatly
appreciated.
```

---

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Mainframe jobs none in AZ

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T18:17:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
rseid13@aol.com (RSeid13) wrote:

>There are no opening or jobs for COBOL DB2 Mainframe in AZ.  I have been
>looking.  It is that every Mainframe position has been sent outside the US.  

Some facts about the US job market:

"Computer programmers held about 585,000 jobs in 2000."
"There were 22,000 self-employed computer programmers in 2000. "

This statistic is programmers only. Total IT jobs are around 1,000,000.

http://www.bls.gov/oco/ocos110.htm

"Roughly 27,000 technology jobs moved overseas in 2000, according to a November
study by Forrester Research. It predicts that number will mushroom to 472,000 by
2015 if companies continue to farm out computer work at today's frenzied pace."

Technology jobs includes engineers, chemists, etc. I would guess 10-15K of the
27K are IT workers. How many of those are programmers? Well under half; a
disproportionate share of jobs moved overseas are call center (help desk). At
most, 5,000 programmer jobs (in 2000). 

That's only 1%.

http://www.cnn.com/2003/TECH/biztech/07/14/moves.offshore.ap/
http://tech.tbo.com/tech/MGAP1WDM3ID.html
http://www.forrester.com/ER/Press/Release/0,1769,780,FF.html

How many of the 585K programmers work on mainframes? Computerjobs' home page
shows the distribution of ads by sector. Of 12,000 IT jobs listed, 520 are in
"Legacy". That's 4%, which appears low to me. Applying 4% to 585K gives 24,000
IBM mainframe programmers. I believe the correct number is closer to 40,000.

How many IBM mainframe jobs are available in the US now? Searching the three
largest job boards for "CICS"  returned 236 on DICE and 335 on Monster and 119
on Careerbuilder (only 2-3 in Arizona). Approximately 25% of those jobs have
been filled or never existed (another topic), so about 250 IBM mainframe jobs on
public boards, which are the domain of independent recruiters filling mostly
contract positions. The number of internal company openings not posted on job
boards is at least three times that: 750.

http://computerjobs.com/homepage.aspx

Given average annual turnover of 12% (see below) and time-to-fill of two months,
we'd expect to see 1,000 IBM mainframe openings at any single point in time.

http://www.waldentesting.com/about/article6.htm

I believe statistics are distored by contractors, who appear to be unrepresented
in the 12% quoted above. If 15% of the 40,000 programmers are contractors
changing jobs once a year or more, we'd expect to see 250-400 ads on job boards
(supply normally exceeds demand). If the other 85% change jobs at a rate of 12%
per year and time-to-fill is two months, we'd expect to see 700 non-job-board
(internal company) openings. 

That's close to the number of present openings. 

FWIW, the number of openings for "C++" is 2,400 on DICE and 2,500 on Monster --
7 to 10 times the number returned for "CICS". Overlap is insignificant; a search
for both returned only 20. "Java" returned 3,600 and 3,900, respectively, of
which only 40 are in Arizona.

Based on a month of close observation, I'd say hot spots are NYC and DC; warm
spots are NC, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas and Austin; cold spots are California,
Arizona and Florida  (no reference to climate intended). Having spent time in
all those places, I'd rank them: 1. Chicago (esp. NW suburbs), 2. NC, 3. Austin,
4. Atlanta, 5. DC (Md), 6. Dallas, 99. NYC.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Mainframe jobs none in AZ

- **From:** "Jed" <jed@jed.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T18:31:01+01:00

- **References:** `<20030726144436.07745.00000472@mb-m14.aol.com> <3f23f745.226564050@news.optonline.com>`

```
Well said Robert. There's too much 'jobs to India!' banging of the drum
recently. No company would devolve their entire IT function offshore if it
meant losing quality control - unless they don't care about quality and
developing their products, systems and service that is - as Amex are finding
to their cost. A lot of the work I do at the moment - I often wonder what
would happen if half of the work I get to do was sent offshore. Yes the
system could be kept hobbling along for some time, but it would be
eventually shambolic.

It's a red herring - like the 'hot summers' we all remember when we were
kids, you remember the halcyon days. The real problem is the global economy
(soft as pig droppings), Enron, 9/11, dot.com bubbles bursting, dubyah, and
so on. If you can't find work, that's where you should be looking, not at
the percentage of jobs sent offshore. The days of A/Ps having a devine right
to a job is over - we're in the same boat as everyone else now.

Which is still a pain in the arse, mind.
```

<a id="t-4c7d72b530"></a>
## Thread: String Statement — 22 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### String Statement

- **From:** "TerryMac" <tmaccaro@rochester.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T19:33:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
I know you can use the String Statement to combine the contents of different
fields (as long as their are no spaces in between words in each field) with
Delimited By Size. But will it combined all the fields in one record if you
just name the Record (as long as their no spaces in between words in each
field) so you don't have to name each specific field in a record?

Thanks for your help!
```

#### ↳ Re: String Statement

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T15:09:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfun6l$8sm$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
I don't really understand either your question - or what you "know".   Can
you give an example of the record layout and what you want to accomplish (in
the receiving field).  I *think* that the simple answer is that you canNOT
use the STRING statement the way you are talking about.  On the other hand,
if you do a MOVE statement of a group item - (and all of the sub-items are
USAGE DISPLAY with no "S" or "V"  in the Picture clause) then it will move
all the fields (as a group) to a receiving item.  If this isn't what you
want, - then I need to see a better description of exactly what you want to
accomplish.

P.S.  "Delimited by size" works identically - whether their are embedded
spaces or not.  Only "Delimited by Spaces" would be impacted by embedded
spaces.
```

#### ↳ Re: String Statement

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T15:26:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vi5ovcsp0proe9@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
TerryMac wrote:

> I know you can use the String Statement to combine the contents of different
> fields (as long as their are no spaces in between words in each field) with
> Delimited By Size. But will it combined all the fields in one record if you
> just name the Record (as long as their no spaces in between words in each
> field) so you don't have to name each specific field in a record?

You're a bit off on your description.  Delimited by Size doesn't care 
about embedded spaces, Delimited by Space does.  :)

However, whatever you string will go if you say delimited by size.  So, 
given

  01  My-Record.
      12  My-Group-Item-1.
          16  My-Elementary-Item-1        Pic X(30).
          16  My-Elementary-Item-2        Pic 9(07).
      12  My-Elementary-Item-3            Pic X(45).

  01  My-String-Target                    Pic X(100).

, and My-Elementary-Item-1 contains "Now is the Time", 
My-Elementary-Item-2 contains "0000037", and My-Elementary-Item-3 
contains "for All Good Men", if you were to say

  Move Spaces to My-String-Target
  String My-Record  "** THE END **"  Delimited by Size
    into My-String-Target

, you would have "Now is the Time" starting in column 1, "0000037" 
starting in column 31, "for All Good Men" starting in column 38, and "** 
THE END **" starting in column 83.  Now, if you were to say

  Move Spaces to My-String-Target
  String My-Record, "** THE END **" Delimited by Space
    into My-String-Target

, you would have "Now**" in the first 5 columns of My-String-Target.

Now, if you were wanting to combine all the non-space characters in 
My-Record, that's pretty easy too.  Given these two additional variables...

  01  My-In-Idx                           Pic 9(03).
  01  My-Out-Idx                          Pic 9(03).

, you can now say

  Move Spaces to My-String-Target
  Move 1      to My-Out-Idx

  Perform Varying My-In-Idx from 1 by 1
    Until My-In-Idx > 100

      If My-Record (My-In-Idx:1) Not = Space
          Move My-Record        (My-In-Idx:1)
            to My-String-Target (My-Out-Idx:1)
          Add 1 to My-Out-Idx
      End-If

  End-Perform

, in which case, given the original values above, you would have 
"NowistheTime0000037forAllGoodMen" in My-String-Target.

> Thanks for your help!

I hope some of that did.  I didn't quite understand the question, so I 
may have answer a question or two that you hadn't intended to ask.  :)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: String Statement

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T12:41:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0307271141.58529ae4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <vi5ovcsp0proe9@corp.supernews.com>`

```
LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote 

> Now, if you were wanting to combine all the non-space characters in 
> My-Record, that's pretty easy too.  Given these two additional variables...
> 
>   01  My-In-Idx                           Pic 9(03).
>   01  My-Out-Idx                          Pic 9(03).

Perhaps if one wanted the all the words separated by single spaces one
could write a routine such as:

    01  Text-In         PIC X(80).
    01  In-Size         PIC 9(4) VALUE 80.
    01  Text-Out        PIC X(80).
    01  Text-Word       PIC X(80).
    01  In-Point        PIC 9(4).
    01  Out-Point       PIC 9(4).

    Process-Words.

   *    input is in Text-In as say: "  22    High St.,   Penrose.  "
   *    output is in Text-Out as    "22 High St., Penrose.         "
   *            with Out-Point indicating terminating position (22)

        MOVE SPACES      TO Text-Out
        MOVE 1           TO In-Point Out-Point
        PERFORM UNTIL In-Point > In-Size
            MOVE SPACES    TO Text-Word
            UNSTRING Text-In DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES
                INTO Text-Word
                WITH POINTER In-Point
            IF ( Out-Pout > 1 )
                ADD 1      TO Out-Point
            END-IF
            STRING Text-Word
                INTO Text-Out
                WITH POINTER Out-Point
        END-PERORM
        .
   
If a record contained fields of an address then this could be
processed by moving the record to Text-In and then the routine will
convert all the words to being separated by single spaces where the
record has them separated by single or multiple spaces.

HOWEVER, note that if a field has its last character non-space then
this will be concatenated to the first word of the next field.

Also make Text-In, Text-Out and Text-Word large enough for your needs
and have In-Size set to the size of Text-In.
```

#### ↳ Re: String Statement

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T16:45:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lqCdnUOSmdv-a7-iXTWJjA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
TerryMac wrote:
> I know you can use the String Statement to combine the contents of
> different fields (as long as their are no spaces in between words in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for your help!

Spaces do not have anything (necessarily) to do with a STRING statement.

The syntax is:

STRING
   data-field1 DELIMITED BY (delimiter-1)
   data-field2 DELIMITED BY (delimiter-2)
   ...
   INTO receiving-field.

(delimiter-n) can be SPACE (or 'b') or any elementary item, such as:
   "***" or "bbbbbb" (b=blank) or MY-TAB (X'09')

We routinely assign a file name thusly:

MOVE SPACE TO FILE-ID.
STRING DIRECTORY-NAME DELIMITED 'bb' (two spaces)
   FILE-NAME DELIMITED 'bb'
   LOW-VALUES DELIMITED SIZE INTO FILE-ID.

For bonus points, what would happen in the above example if DIRECTORY-NAME
did NOT have two consecutive spaces?

P.S.
    Yes, there's an extra step to squeeze out multiple spaces in both
DIRECTORY-NAME and FILE-NAME.
```

```
> MOVE SPACE TO FILE-ID.
> STRING DIRECTORY-NAME DELIMITED 'bb' (two spaces)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Statement

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T23:21:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F2453D9.C00FDAC5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <lqCdnUOSmdv-a7-iXTWJjA@giganews.com> <RMVUa.130896$EQ5.129526@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```


TerryMac wrote:

> I give up on the bonus point question.
> I'm just learning Cobol and I have a very difficult program to write (for me
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here's another variation on STRINGing, using 'Double spaces' and "Single
spaces", plus handling the situation where some of the fileds you wish to string
together are blank  :-

 01 VESSEL-record.
    05 VESSEL-PrimeKey        pic x(04).
     05 VESSEL-Prefix             pic x(20).  *> "Colt"
     05 VESSEL-NameKey       pic x(04).  *> "WIP "
     05 VESSEL-Suffix              pic x(12).  *> (north)
     05 VESSEL-etc....

 01 ws-Name30              pic x(30).

Note that Ves-Prefix and Ves-Suffix are an EITHER/OR situation, they can be
blank or contain data in the Vessel Record; also their sizes are sufficient to
allow for TwoSpaces at the end of each. The major description is VERY
repetititive so Ves-NameKey contains a menomonic code - do a File/DB Table
Lookup and get the description for Ves-NameKey which is put into ws-Name30.

Now String :-

*>----------------------------------------------------------
*> CONDITIONS :
*> Prefix = spaces                            y   y   n   n
*> Suffix = spaces                            y   n   y   n
*>---------------------------------------------------
*> ACTIONS :
*> move   name                                x   -   -   -
*> String name with Suffix                 -   x   -   -
*> String name with Prefix                 -   -   x   -
*> String name with Prefix & Suffix   -   -   -   -

Linkage section.
01 lnk-fullname                  pic x(50).
Procedure Division returning lnk-fullname.

  move spaces to lnk-fullname
  evaluate true also true
    when VESSEL-Prefix =  spaces also VESSEL-Suffix = spaces
         move ws-name30 to lnk-fullname

    when VESSEL-Prefix =  spaces also VESSEL-Suffix <> spaces
         string ws-name30       delimited by "  "
                " "             delimited by size
                VESSEL-Suffix   delimited by "  "
                into lnk-fullname
         End-string

    when VESSEL-Prefix <> spaces also VESSEL-Suffix = spaces
         string VESSEL-Prefix   delimited by "  "
                " "             delimited by size
                ws-name30       delimited by "  "
                into lnk-fullname
         End-string

    when VESSEL-Prefix <> spaces also VESSEL-Suffix <> spaces
         string VESSEL-Prefix   delimited by "  "
                " "             delimited by size
                ws-name30       delimited by "  "
                " "             delimited by size
                VESSEL-Suffix   delimited by "  "
                into lnk-fullname
         End-string
  End-evaluate
  .
*>------------------------------------------------------------

Result of above :-

    "Colt Water Injection Pump (north)"

Note - I've used "  " and " " as delimiters but you could hard code those as :-

 01 OneSpace    pic x(01) value space.
 01 TwoSpaces. pic x(02) value spaces.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Statement

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T18:27:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uqudnad0t9dNwrmiXTWJjw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <lqCdnUOSmdv-a7-iXTWJjA@giganews.com> <RMVUa.130896$EQ5.129526@twister.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
TerryMac wrote:
> I give up on the bonus point question.
> I'm just learning Cobol and I have a very difficult program to write
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> fields, you can use just:    '  ' Delimited By Size

Your book is wrong - way wrong.

Look at the example I used. Suppose:

DIRECTORY-NAME  PIC X(255) VALUE 'C:\Program Files\Wahoo\'.
FILE-NAME PIC X(255) VALUE 'MYFILE.DAT'.

What is wanted is: "C:\Program Files\Wahoo\MYFILE.DAT"

And the example:
String DIRECTORY-NAME delimited 'bb' (where 'bb' is 'space-space')...

If you 'Delimited By Size' the entire 255-byte "DIRECTORY-NAME" field would
be moved to the output field (including about 240 blanks).

If you 'Delimited By Space,' only "C:\Program" would be moved to the output
field.

Therefore, the delimiter must be 'bb' ("bb" = two spaces - they don't show
up as two in a proprotional font in most newsreaders).

>
>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>> DIRECTORY-NAME and FILE-NAME.
```

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>

```
Not sure if this is what you want or not.

01 field-1  pic x(10).
01 field-2  pic x(15).
01 ws-rec pic x(80).
01 i          pic s(4) comp.

move 1 to i

string field-1
  delimited by size
  into ws-rec
  pointer i
end-string

string field-2
  delimited by size
  into ws-rec
  pointer i
end-string
```

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c353d1$749fb600$4295f243@chottel...
> Not sure if this is what you want or not.
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> > just name the Record (as long as their no spaces in between words in
each
> > field) so you don't have to name each specific field in a record?
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: String Statement

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T22:50:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c35491$7619cfa0$6e8bf243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <01c353d1$749fb600$4295f243@chottel>`

```
I think you probably want something more like this:

 01  field-length pic s9(4) comp.
 01  space-cnt   pic s9(4) comp.
 01 field-1  pic x(10).
 01 field-2  pic x(15).
 01 ws-rec pic x(80).
 01 i          pic s(4) comp.
 
 move 1 to i
 
 move zero to space-cnt
 inspect function reverse(field-1)
    tallying space-cnt for leading spaces
 compute field-length = 10 - space-cnt
 string field-1(1:field-length)
   delimited by size
   into ws-rec
   pointer i
 end-string
 
move zero to space-cnt
inspect function reverse(field-2)
    tallying space-cnt for leading spaces
 compute field-length = 15 - space-cnt
 string field-2(1:field-length)
   delimited by size
   into ws-rec
   pointer i
 end-string
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Statement

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T22:53:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c35491$cf15a840$6e8bf243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <01c353d1$749fb600$4295f243@chottel> <01c35491$7619cfa0$6e8bf243@chottel>`

```
Now look up "LENGTH OF" and think about how you could write this as:
    1. a performed paragraph
    2. a CALLed subprogram
Details are left to the reader :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Statement

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T18:36:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6EKdndPul_qO_7miXTWJhg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <01c353d1$749fb600$4295f243@chottel> <01c35491$7619cfa0$6e8bf243@chottel>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> I think you probably want something more like this:
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>  end-string

Here's another solution:

01  Field-1   pic x(10).
01  Field-2   pic x(15).
01  WS-Rec95  pic x(95).
01  filler redefines WS-Rec95.
  02  WS-Rec pic x(80).
  02  filler pic x(15).

Move Field-1 to WS-Rec95.
INSPECT WS-Rec95 REPLACING First '(15 spaces here)' by Field-2.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: String Statement

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T10:55:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yi7Va.27439$BM.8887656@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<1IAUa.39735$wk4.8672@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <01c353d1$749fb600$4295f243@chottel> <01c35491$7619cfa0$6e8bf243@chottel> <6EKdndPul_qO_7miXTWJhg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:6EKdndPul_qO_7miXTWJhg@giganews.com...
> Charles Hottel wrote:
> > I think you probably want something more like this:

You might just want to use reference modification instead of STRING

inspect function reverse (fld) tallying leading-spaces for leading space
compute fld-length = function length (fld) - leading-spaces
move fld (1:fld-lenght) to output-buffer
add 1 to leading-spaces
move space to output-buffer (leading-spaces:1)
add 1 to leading-spaces
move  field-2 to output-buffer (leading-spaces:)

MCM
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

```
news:S-Cdncf3873yLbiiXTWJhw@giganews.com...
> Charles Hottel wrote:
> > Thanks. Yes there are many ways to skin the cat, but the original
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Taxi driver: "Practice, man, practice."

Thank you for bringing that up. So often I see in ng's and in other venues
posters who ask, "how do I use <technique> to solve this problem?"  Seems to
me many of those questions should be  rephrased as, "what technique would
you suggest to solve this particular problem?"  To do otherwise is to put
the cart before the horse.

Maybe 'force-fitting' some particular verb or function is a good way to
acquire skills; but IMO 'force-fitting' is a long-term loser  when creating
Real World Applications.

MCM
```

<a id="t-44e4e3800f"></a>
## Thread: String Statement - Advanced — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### String Statement - Advanced

- **From:** <rpalm01@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-26T21:08:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f23269c_4@news1.prserv.net>`

```
Here's a complex string statement for building a variable WHERE condition on
an SQL statement....JUST FYI to show you the possibilities:

033340*
033350* WHERE
033360*
033370     MOVE HIGH-VALUES            TO WS10-3000-WHEREAND.
033380     STRING ' WHERE'                DELIMITED BY SIZE
033390            HIGH-VALUE              DELIMITED BY SIZE
033400            INTO WS10-3000-WHEREAND.
033410*
033420     IF   WS10-3000-RGN-I        IS NOT EQUAL TO HIGH-VALUES
033430     IF   WS10-3000-RGN-I        IS GREATER THAN SPACES
033440          IF   WS10-3000-RGN-I
033450                                  = FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(WS-NULL)
033460               MOVE SPACES       TO WS10-3000-RGN-I
033470          END-IF
033480          STRING WS10-3000-SQLT     DELIMITED BY HIGH-VALUE
033490                 WS10-3000-WHEREAND DELIMITED BY HIGH-VALUE
033500                 ' REGION_SALE = '  DELIMITED BY SIZE
033510                 QUOTE              DELIMITED BY SIZE
033520                 WS10-3000-RGN-I    DELIMITED BY SPACE
033530                 QUOTE              DELIMITED BY SIZE
033540                 HIGH-VALUE         DELIMITED BY SIZE
033550                 INTO WS10-3000-SQLT
033560          MOVE HIGH-VALUES       TO WS10-3000-WHEREAND
033570          MOVE ' AND'            TO WS10-3000-WHEREAND (1:4).
033580*
033590     IF   WS10-3000-BRANCH-I     IS NOT EQUAL TO HIGH-VALUES
033600     IF   WS10-3000-BRANCH-I     IS GREATER THAN SPACES
033610          IF   WS10-3000-BRANCH-I
033620                                  = FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(WS-NULL)
033630               MOVE SPACES       TO WS10-3000-BRANCH-I
033640          END-IF
033650          STRING WS10-3000-SQLT     DELIMITED BY HIGH-VALUE
033660                 WS10-3000-WHEREAND DELIMITED BY HIGH-VALUE
033670                 ' BRANCH_SALE = '  DELIMITED BY SIZE
033680                 QUOTE              DELIMITED BY SIZE
033690                 WS10-3000-BRANCH-I DELIMITED BY SPACE
033700                 QUOTE              DELIMITED BY SIZE
033710                 HIGH-VALUE         DELIMITED BY SIZE
033720                 INTO WS10-3000-SQLT
033730          MOVE HIGH-VALUES       TO WS10-3000-WHEREAND
033740          MOVE ' AND'            TO WS10-3000-WHEREAND (1:4).
033750*
033760     IF   WS10-3000-CUSTTYPE-I   IS NOT EQUAL TO HIGH-VALUES
033770     IF   WS10-3000-CUSTTYPE-I   IS GREATER THAN SPACES
033780          IF   WS10-3000-CUSTTYPE-I
033790                                  = FUNCTION UPPER-CASE(WS-NULL)
033800               MOVE SPACES       TO WS10-3000-CUSTTYPE-I
033810          END-IF
033820          STRING WS10-3000-SQLT     DELIMITED BY HIGH-VALUE
033830                 WS10-3000-WHEREAND DELIMITED BY HIGH-VALUE
033840                 ' CUSTOMER_TYPE = '
033850                                    DELIMITED BY SIZE
033860                 QUOTE              DELIMITED BY SIZE
033870                 WS10-3000-CUSTTYPE-I
033880                                    DELIMITED BY SPACE
033890                 QUOTE              DELIMITED BY SIZE
033900                 HIGH-VALUE         DELIMITED BY SIZE
033910                 INTO WS10-3000-SQLT
033920          MOVE HIGH-VALUES       TO WS10-3000-WHEREAND
033930          MOVE ' AND'            TO WS10-3000-WHEREAND (1:4).
```

<a id="t-ff90804090"></a>
## Thread: Beginner User Question — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Beginner User Question

- **From:** "dayton" <dayton500@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T09:09:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg3hp4$k4uce$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
I am not familiar with Cobol so I ask you guy's a simple question. I write
some basic reports with the IQ report writer on our system. I've written
some reports with IF statements. My question is, I don't want to write a
hundred IF statements, how would I write this for an ARRAY? Below is a
sample of the code.


IF OH-ACCOUNT = 'A10100'
        THEN
        MOVE 10140 TO CODE.
IF OH-ACCOUNT = 'A10110'
        THEN
        MOVE 30640 TO CODE.
IF OH-ACCOUNT = 'B25325'
        THEN
        MOVE 60650 TO CODE.

T.I.A.
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner User Question

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-27T18:29:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg47pl$k5$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bg3hp4$k4uce$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
Probably the easiest thing to do would be to mate the "argument" with its
corresponding "answer" in an array and use the SEARCH verb to obtain this
answer.

If the table size remains less than 100 elements, then a sequential SEARCH
would probably be more efficient than a SEARCH ALL.

Regardless, the ARRAY _M U S T_ be kept in "ACCT" order.

03  WS-ACCT-CODE-TBL-REC.
      05  FILLER PIC  X(011) VALUE 'A1010010140'.
      05  FILLER PIC  X(011) VALUE 'A1011030640'.
      05  FILLER PIC  X(011) VALUE 'B2532560650'.
03  WS-ACCT-CODE-TBL REDEFINES WS-ACCT-CODE-TBL-REC
                                                OCCURS 3 TIMES
                                                ASCENDING KEY IS
WS-ACCT-ELEMENT
                                                INDEXED BY X-WS-ACT,

X-WS-ACT-MAX.
      05  WS-ACCT-ELEMENT  PIC  X(06).
      05  WS-CODE-ELEMENT PIC  9(05).
03  WS-SUB                              PIC  9(08) BINARY.
*
DIVIDE LENGTH OF WS-ACCT-CODE-TBL-REC BY
              LENGTH OF WS-ACCT-CODE-TBL (1)
              GIVING WS-SUB.
SET X-WS-ACT-MAX TO WS-SUB.
*
At this point, if index X-WS-ACT-MAX (for example) exceeds 99, then perhaps
a SEARCH ALL will be more efficient than a straight SEARCH.

If you code for both, then as the ARRAY becomes larger, your indexed
threshold logic will dictate to the program which lookup method (SEARCH or
SEARCH ALL) would be best.

HTH....

Bill

"dayton" <dayton500@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:bg3hp4$k4uce$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de...
> I am not familiar with Cobol so I ask you guy's a simple question. I write
> some basic reports with the IQ report writer on our system. I've written
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner User Question

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T16:44:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wpcVa.74649$3o3.5077304@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bg3hp4$k4uce$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```

dayton <dayton500@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:bg3hp4$k4uce$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de...
> I am not familiar with Cobol so I ask you guy's a simple question. I
write
> some basic reports with the IQ report writer on our system. I've written
> some reports with IF statements. My question is, I don't want to write a
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>         MOVE 60650 TO CODE.

Set up a corresponding table to hold the code values,
use the SEARCH verb to search the account array,
and then move the appropriate code from the code table
using logic in your SEARCH statement similar to that shown .

WHEN OH-ACCOUNT = ARRAY-ENTRY (ARRAY-INDEX)
             SET TABLE-INDEX TO ARRAY-INDEX
             MOVE TABLE-CODE (TABLE-INDEX) TO CODE

You can use table subscripting or indexing, as you choose.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner User Question

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T17:40:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hedVa.74697$3o3.5080366@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bg3hp4$k4uce$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de> <wpcVa.74649$3o3.5077304@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bg3l7c$kjrf5$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```

dayton <dayton500@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:bg3l7c$kjrf5$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de...
> Hugh,
>
> I'm following you so far. How would I set up the table, what would it
look
> like.

01  WS-CODE-TABLE.
       05  WS-CODE-A10100    PIC X(05) VALUE '10140'.
       05  WS-CODE-A10110    PIC X(05) VALUE '30640'.
       05  WS-CODE-B25325    PIC X(05) VALUE '60650'.

01  WS-CODE-SEARCH        REDEFINES    WS-CODE-TABLE.
       05  WS-CODE-ENTRY   OCCURS  3  TIMES
                                                 INDEXED BY CSX
                                                 PIC X(05).

Expand both of the above to suit your requirements.

That's one way of doing it.  It isn't the only way.


> "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
> news:wpcVa.74649$3o3.5077304@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

written
> > > some reports with IF statements. My question is, I don't want to
write a
> > > hundred IF statements, how would I write this for an ARRAY? Below is
a
> > > sample of the code.
> > >
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> > You can use table subscripting or indexing, as you choose.
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner User Question

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T22:14:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f259f88.335191109@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<bg3hp4$k4uce$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de>`

```
"dayton" <dayton500@hotmail.com> wrote:

>I am not familiar with Cobol so I ask you guy's a simple question. I write
>some basic reports with the IQ report writer on our system. I've written
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>        MOVE 60650 TO CODE.

This is simpler than an array:

 EVALUATE OH-ACCOUNT
    WHEN 'A10100' MOVE 10140 TO CODE
    WHEN 'A10110' MOVE 30640 TO CODE
    WHEN 'B25325' MOVE 60650 TO CODE
    ...
    WHEN OTHER  MOVE '?????' TO CODE.

This requires one line of code per account/code pair, same as an array. It
eliminates the need for messy loop control or SEARCH statement to find the
entry, nor do you need indexes or subscripts. 

I believe in the MISS approach -- Make It Simple, Stupid (even if it isn't).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Beginner User Question

- **From:** "Jed" <jed@jed.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T18:23:45+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg6aj4$sp7$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<bg3hp4$k4uce$1@ID-201461.news.uni-berlin.de> <3f259f88.335191109@news.optonline.com>`

```

> >        MOVE 60650 TO CODE.
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> I believe in the MISS approach -- Make It Simple, Stupid (even if it
isn't).
>

Depends on how robust it needs to be. I always avoid hardcoding anything
into a program - I would set up an initial procedure to load the data from
an input file (the accounts and corresponding code) to an array (with a
'DEPENDING ON' for the occurs) and then do a SEARCH.

If this is a one-off job then Rob's idea is right - but if it's
productionised or needs to be run regularly, the indexed table and input
file route is far less work in the long run.

God I'm sad.
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-07-28T08:49:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1059382114@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

## Thread: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !! — 47 this month / 58 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** "Jagster" <jnori@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T12:38:22+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
Hi there,

I am having a problem converting this monstrous query into cobol code as it
takes 14 hrs to run this QMF Proc. Could any one help me out pls ?? It would
be greatly appreciated. Thanx in advance for this help.

Here is the query  !!

SELECT FULL_TERM, A.REM_MONTHS, COUNT(*), SUM(A.BILL_BALANCE)
     , END_LMTH_CHAR
FROM CSTMT A, LCUST B, ACCPLN C
   , CSTMT D
   , MDATE
WHERE D.CUST_NBR      = A.CUST_NBR
  AND D.ACCT_NBR      = A.ACCT_NBR
  AND A.STMT_DATE     >= (SELECT STRT_2MTH
                           FROM MDATE )
  AND A.STMT_DATE     <  (SELECT STRT_LMTH
                            FROM MDATE )
  AND A.BILL_BALANCE  > 0
  AND A.REM_MONTHS    > 0
  AND D.STMT_DATE     >= (SELECT STRT_LMTH
                            FROM MDATE )
  AND D.STMT_DATE     <= (SELECT END_LMTH
                            FROM MDATE )
  AND D.BILL_BALANCE  < 0.01
  AND D.CREDITS       < 0

  AND A.CUST_NBR       = B.CUST_NBR
  AND A.ACCT_NBR       = B.ACCT
  AND B.ACCT_TYPE      = C.ACCT_TYPE
  AND B.REVL_PLAN_NBR  = C.PAY_PLAN
  AND B.ORG_ID         = '600555'
  AND B.ACCT_TYPE      = '30'
GROUP BY FULL_TERM, A.REM_MONTHS
       , END_LMTH_CHAR
ORDER BY 1,2


Jagster
```

```
something like this:

Me: 'All right... for three months of COBOL/CICS/DB2 work in Manhattan I 
can quote you a rate of US$nn/hr.'

Pimp: 'Uuhhhhhh... you're flexible on that, right?'

Me: 'Of *course* I am flexible... I'm *always* willing to go higher.'
```

```
> fixed bid contracts.


Not even.  For less that I would charge to fix the problem onshore I 
will fly to Jamacia and fix the problem.

You have to go where the jobs are, if I have to be offshore...oh well, 
I'll manage.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T09:36:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-04576E.09363629072003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
In article <vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net>,
 "Jagster" <jnori@bigfoot.com> wrote:

> Hi there,
> 
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Sure, add an "exec sql" at the beginning, and an "end-exec" at the end.

;-)

Seriously though, you should be able to do some explains on your plan to 
id the problem.  Changing to Cobol without fixing the problem is just 
going to give you a Cobol program that runs in 14 hours.

Off the top of my head, the inclusion of the CSTMT table twice in the 
from might do nothing, or confuse your DBMS.

Doing column functions like count and sum with an order by might be 
giving it coniptions as well.  It can put you into a bubble-sort-like  
behavior table space scan.

Make sure you have indexes on FULL_TERM and REM_MONTHS, and maybe 
END_LMTH_CHAR as well.

Just some ideas...
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T08:40:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vicu9ogjs7f71d@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
Jagster wrote:

> Hi there,
> 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> ORDER BY 1,2

Hmm...  how 'bout...

Exec SQL
     SELECT FULL_TERM, A.REM_MONTHS, COUNT(*), SUM(A.BILL_BALANCE)
           , END_LMTH_CHAR
     FROM CSTMT A, LCUST B, ACCPLN C
         , CSTMT D
        , MDATE
     WHERE D.CUST_NBR      = A.CUST_NBR
        AND D.ACCT_NBR      = A.ACCT_NBR
        AND A.STMT_DATE     >= (SELECT STRT_2MTH
                                 FROM MDATE )
        AND A.STMT_DATE     <  (SELECT STRT_LMTH
                                 FROM MDATE )
        AND A.BILL_BALANCE  > 0
        AND A.REM_MONTHS    > 0
        AND D.STMT_DATE     >= (SELECT STRT_LMTH
                                 FROM MDATE )
        AND D.STMT_DATE     <= (SELECT END_LMTH
                                 FROM MDATE )
        AND D.BILL_BALANCE  < 0.01
        AND D.CREDITS       < 0

        AND A.CUST_NBR       = B.CUST_NBR
        AND A.ACCT_NBR       = B.ACCT
        AND B.ACCT_TYPE      = C.ACCT_TYPE
        AND B.REVL_PLAN_NBR  = C.PAY_PLAN
        AND B.ORG_ID         = '600555'
        AND B.ACCT_TYPE      = '30'
     GROUP BY FULL_TERM, A.REM_MONTHS
            , END_LMTH_CHAR
     ORDER BY 1,2
End-Exec

?
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T13:57:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f266ec9.388256477@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
You could plug the statement into a Cobol program, but it wouldn't run any
faster. You'll have to turn it into a cursor and add FETCH .. INTO. Conversely,
if the solution turns out to be a temp table, you can do that with QMF as easily
as with Cobol. 

I would try these fixes:

Delete the sub-selects on MDATE. Move the three dates to the top and do a simple
compare. In the Cobol version, read them with a separate SELECT and store them
in host variables. 

Change COUNT(*) to COUNT(A.ACCT_NBR). You are wastefully asking it to read all
fields just to get a count.

Run an EXPLAIN PLAN to find out where it's spending all its time. Look for
missing indexes, especially on C.ACCT_TYPE. Make sure the optimizer is turned
on.

Try changing the self-join -- first 14 lines of WHERE -- into an INNER JOIN. The
optimizer should be smart enough to do that for you.

Try making the detail SELECT into a sub-select with DISTINCT on CUST_NBR. After
finding the first pair for a customer, it will stop looking for more. 


------- question below -----------------
"Jagster" <jnori@bigfoot.com> wrote:

>Hi there,
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
hope that Jagster recognises the value of your post.)

Pete.
```
