###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T13:51:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f27d4b0.15763820@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net> <3f266ec9.388256477@news.optonline.com> <3f2735fa_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>should be able to pay for commercial solutions.

In the Golden Age of Computers ('60-'70s) programmers belonged to a fraternity.
They helped each other for free. They did not help managers, only programmers
who uttered the secret handshake -- lots of acronyms used knowledgeably. Once
upon a time, a high-powered systems programmer working for another company
spent an entire weekend helping me out of a crisis. I recently did the same for
*a programmer*, not the company, at a Fortune 100 place where I had worked the
year before. Sure the company has lots of money, but the programmer didn't have
authority to spend it and he needed a fix NOW. (Being Russian, he didn't know
about the fraternity. It was a learning experience for him.)

I'm saddened to see that brotherhood lost in the commercial world. CLC is
intended  to be a global version of it -- of users helping each other and trying
out ideas. It's not supposed to be a marketplace. 

--- end of my comments --------------

>(Expertise and experience are something it is difficult for them to source
>offshore for peanuts.)
…[96 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T18:56:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f282291.15974179@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net> <3f266ec9.388256477@news.optonline.com> <3f2735fa_7@news.athenanews.com> <3f27d4b0.15763820@news.optonline.com> <bg8kv4$8fc$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3f27d4b0.15763820@news.optonline.com>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>should be able to pay for commercial solutions.

>>I'm saddened to see that brotherhood lost in the commercial world. CLC is
>>intended  to be a global version of it -- of users helping each other and
trying
>>out ideas. It's not supposed to be a marketplace. 
>
>I'm sorry, Mr Wagner, I seem to have missed something... where did the 
>person who originated the query show any programming ability or knowledge 
>outside of quoting an SQL query?

Most help requests to CLC lack enough information to figure out what the problem
is, much less how to solve it. Or they are stupid questions (yes, there is such
a thing). Jagster provided enough information to solve a fairly typical,
non-trivial database problem. His subsequent postings show him to be in the
trenches and making an effort. 

Many of us can empathize because we've been frustrated by database optimization
at some point. The suggestions I gave were basic stuff that can be found in most
any book. I didn't jeopardize your $25K guru fee  by talking about HINTs or
partitioning or parallel processing.

Peter's objection concerned free advice to a commercial shop, even if the poster
has expert knowledge. I disagree. I see requests like this as ex cathedra. If
management wanted expert advice, they'd call IBM or a DB2 guru. They would not
encourage the programmer to post to CLC.
```

```
>I read

>should be more free with your information'.  Most trencherfolks I know 
>would mention things like platform, OS, key/index availability,

Platform and OS are obvious. If he knew the effect of indexes on performance,
he'd be looking at an EXPLAIN rather than posting here. I agree table volumes
would have been appropriate. 

>Oh joy... there's hope for my extortionate request succeeding, then!

The art of contracting is all in finding a dumb-enough client. As a rule of
thumb, gullibility is directly proportional to arrogance. 

>Most managers I know would 'gently encourage' in-house talent to try to 
>find free advice before turning to the people they'd have to pay... but 
>that only speaks of the managers I know.

Most managers I know care about covering their butt. It's not their money; it's
the stockholders' money. But if they screw up, it's their job. In the world of
starched shirts, one ohshit trumps a dozen attaboys.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T02:47:22+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f27db31_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net> <3f266ec9.388256477@news.optonline.com> <3f2735fa_7@news.athenanews.com> <3f27d4b0.15763820@news.optonline.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f27d4b0.15763820@news.optonline.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
<snip>
>
> I'm saddened to see that brotherhood lost in the commercial world. CLC is
> intended  to be a global version of it -- of users helping each other and
trying
> out ideas. It's not supposed to be a marketplace.
>

That's right, it isn't. And many of us have been giving free advice for
years here. (Sometimes it is worth the price <G>, but more often than not,
people get helped.)

I know the brotherhood you speak of (I was there at the same time you were),
and there are still people I contact when I need some inside information on
something obscure or not easily referenceable. Similarly, others contact me
when they need something that lies in my area of expertise. Although I no
longer take COBOL students, I do help students, and others. The difference
is that when I request help from others they have no doubt that, were the
roles reversed, I would do the same for them.

Your sadness should be reserved for the fact that there is a rising tide of
freeloaders who EXPECT to get help for nothing (it is NOT a God given
right), don't even bother to say "Thanks" when they do get it, don't really
appreciate that they are getting something of value, and never contribute to
the forum again. They mistake kindness for weakness and have no respect or
regard for the people whose help they require. A few such experiences tend
to curdle the milk of human kindness...

Most of us, even having had such experiences, are prepared to contribute
anyway. (I come here because it is amusing, I enjoy the personalities, I
quite often leqarn something, and I feel that IT has been very good to me
over a lifetime so I ought to give something back. I'm sure others have
their own reasons). But few people actually enjoy having their good nature
taken advantage of, and when it looks like there is a risk that this could
be so, you will see the posts you did see.

Being of a naturally kind and generous disposition (not to mention modest
with it...<G>), my first impulse is normally to help if I can.

(Jagster was no exception; I spent some time looking at his SQL and would
have responded very much the way you did, (but not as well; your post was
excellent), but I'm always suspicious when people "just turn up", never
having contributed before, and post something that shows no attempt on their
own part to look at, decompose, or understand it, and then request us to do
their job for them (I think it was Doc who accurately made that
observation). If Jagster is a COBOL Newbie "interested in learning the
language" it raises another question. Who would task a COBOL Newbie with the
job of translating  a fairly convoluted QMF generated SQL statement into
COBOL? Did they do so because they figured he was "Web aware" and would
derive the answer from a group such as this?  Why is no help and support
forthcoming from the place where he works? I would need to know Jagster
better before I'd respond to that one. (And, from the petulance of his
subsequent posts and his failure to understand why the group was not falling
over itself to "help" him, I doubt that I'll be getting to know him better.)

I'll leave it to you to decide who's loss that is...

Pete.
```

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Your sadness should be reserved for the fact that there is a rising tide of
>freeloaders who EXPECT to get help for nothing (it is NOT a God given
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>to curdle the milk of human kindness...

Do they? Others cannot MAKE one indifferent, any more than broken domestic
relationships can MAKE one frigid. The only agonist who can introduce those
feelings is oneself. When you encounter a panhandler, does the experience make
you unsanguine about mankind?

>Most of us, even having had such experiences, are prepared to contribute
>anyway. (I come here because it is amusing, I enjoy the personalities, I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>be so, you will see the posts you did see.

Their threshold is inappropriately sensitive, like my yuppie neighbors' car
alarms.

>Being of a naturally kind and generous disposition (not to mention modest
>with it...<G>), my first impulse is normally to help if I can.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>observation). 

Regular posters rarely ask for help, thus nearly all such requests will come
from newcomers. If they find succor, they might return or even develop an
interest in CLC; if they find bluster, they will exit permanently  (present
company excluded).

>If Jagster is a COBOL Newbie "interested in learning the
>language" it raises another question. Who would task a COBOL Newbie with the
>job of translating  a fairly convoluted QMF generated SQL statement into
>COBOL? 

Surely you've seen illogicality in the IT biz on your many trips around the
block. Jagster more or less said he's in a technological backwater. Some may
find that hard to imagine in a COBOL shop. 

>Did they do so because they figured he was "Web aware" and would
>derive the answer from a group such as this?  Why is no help and support
>forthcoming from the place where he works?

His query is doing statistical research on dormant accounts. Research activities
are on the nadir of the IT horizon. IT doesn't care whether he sinks or swims,
they have statements to get out and meetings to attend. 

> from the petulance of his
>subsequent posts and his failure to understand why the group was not falling
>over itself to "help" him, I doubt that I'll be getting to know him better.)
>I'll leave it to you to decide who's loss that is...

If it is true that one man's loss is another man's gain then nobody has lost.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T15:55:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5URVa.76952$3o3.5291169@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net> <3f266ec9.388256477@news.optonline.com> <3f2735fa_7@news.athenanews.com> <3f27d4b0.15763820@news.optonline.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f27d4b0.15763820@news.optonline.com...
| "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
|
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
| In the Golden Age of Computers ('60-'70s) programmers belonged to a
fraternity.
| They helped each other for free. They did not help managers, only
programmers
| who uttered the secret handshake -- lots of acronyms used knowledgeably.
Once
| upon a time, a high-powered systems programmer working for another company
| spent an entire weekend helping me out of a crisis. I recently did the
same for
| *a programmer*, not the company, at a Fortune 100 place where I had worked
the
| year before. Sure the company has lots of money, but the programmer didn't
have
| authority to spend it and he needed a fix NOW. (Being Russian, he didn't
know
| about the fraternity. It was a learning experience for him.)
|
| I'm saddened to see that brotherhood lost in the commercial world. CLC is
| intended  to be a global version of it -- of users helping each other and
trying
| out ideas. It's not supposed to be a marketplace.

Robert,

I think the requests for money were tongue-in-cheek.

When students ask for help they have to provide proof that they actually did
something. Most of us have responded to requests to find a bug or two in a
posted program.
(I know both of US have.)

WAR STORY:
I worked with an individual who would slap a program together from
components he had in a library on his PC.
What he would do is: flatter people, and request their help.
"Can you look at this for me, I can't find the problem, you're so much
better at this than I am."
After he had enlisted the DBA to fix his SQL, and other programmers to get
his program running, he would spend hours on the phone talking to his
friends.
He got me ONCE, but never again.

Yes, there is a brotherhood, but some people just try to take advantage of
it.

There are sample programs in the DB2 manuals, and some of the books on the
market come with a CD that has samples.
What could it cost, a trip to a bookstore, and $25-$50 for a book?

If he had cut a program out, inserted his query, and asked for help, it
probably would have been given.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T03:10:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f2886f7.41680290@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net> <3f266ec9.388256477@news.optonline.com> <3f2735fa_7@news.athenanews.com> <3f27d4b0.15763820@news.optonline.com> <5URVa.76952$3o3.5291169@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>WAR STORY:
>I worked with an individual who would slap a program together from
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>He got me ONCE, but never again.

Give him bad advice.

My war story:

New programming manager who didn't know jack was writing Cobol style standards.
I recommended putting a NOTE as the first sentence in each paragraph, to explain
what the paragraph does. He actually published it! Programmers were giving me
high fives and rolling on he floor. A month later I was his replacement.

(In the Golden Age, before these newfangled PC things screwed up the business,
NOTE at the beginning of a paragraph commented the whole paragraph out.)

>There are sample programs in the DB2 manuals, and some of the books on the
>market come with a CD that has samples.
>What could it cost, a trip to a bookstore, and $25-$50 for a book?

That's easy once you can form reasonable questions. Haven't you ever found
yourself so lost you didn't know where to begin? Big Five contracting companies
put people in that position routinely. They hire 'interns' straight out of
college, people with degrees in biology and English who've never seen a computer
program. After a two week course that's Greek to them, they become cannon fodder
on the front lines of mainframe assembly language, poorly written C and tight
deadlines. That 50% survive is testimony to the resilience or recklessness of
youth. If they had more sense, they'd be terrified. When I see that, I give the
ones with aptitude a short course in How To Program for which they're VERY
grateful. 

It's a good deal for Big Five partners -- bill rate $125, pay rate $25. The few
times I've told that to interns, they said it was impossible, I didn't know what
I was talking about. 

>If he had cut a program out, inserted his query, and asked for help, it
>probably would have been given.

He wouldn't have known enough to put SELECT in a cursor and use FETCH.
Respondants would have beat him over the head with that rather than addressing
the real problem.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T04:52:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Fg1Wa.3961$K4.182304@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net> <3f266ec9.388256477@news.optonline.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3f266ec9.388256477@news.optonline.com...

> Delete the sub-selects on MDATE. Move the three dates to the top and do a
simple
> compare. In the Cobol version, read them with a separate SELECT and store
them
> in host variables.

Given the syntax
AND D.STMT_DATE     <= (SELECT END_LMTH FROM MDATE )
one can assume that there is only one row in the table MDATE (as it has to
resolve the subselect to 1 row)....we could change this to AND D.STMT_DATE
<=  MDATE.END_LMTH.   For some reason MDATE is his FROM and never used
anyway.  I think this is what you are saying but it looks easier done than
said :-)
Not sure why you would store them as host variables though.

> Run an EXPLAIN PLAN to find out where it's spending all its time. Look for
> missing indexes, especially on C.ACCT_TYPE. Make sure the optimizer is
turned
> on.
Given that this is 14 hours I think it would be quicker to query the
catalogue and see if any index even exists
If we have DB2 it would be SELECT * FROM SYSIBM.SYSINDEXES WHERE
TBNAME=<table name>.

Also I would assume that ACCT_TYPE has low ordinality so an index probably
wouldn't help that much...but with little info it's hard to tell.

> Try making the detail SELECT into a sub-select with DISTINCT on CUST_NBR.
After
> finding the first pair for a customer, it will stop looking for more.
To do that it would make more sense to do AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM CSTMT A
WHERE A.data = B.data and A=? ) and B is your outer table declaration.  I
think a distinct might actually force a sort and so forth....where an EXISTS
will just stop on the first; however, I don't see where a distinct even
comes into play here...this looks like a many to many join on the face of it
many As joined to many Bs....

I'm going to assume also that a DBA has ensured that the db is not paging
like a lunatic and there is enough buffer pool space or sort work space etc
etc...

JCE

The original query....
>SELECT FULL_TERM, A.REM_MONTHS, COUNT(*), SUM(A.BILL_BALANCE)
>     , END_LMTH_CHAR
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>ORDER BY 1,2
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T15:36:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f2934f5.89794661@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net> <3f266ec9.388256477@news.optonline.com> <Fg1Wa.3961$K4.182304@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Given the syntax
>AND D.STMT_DATE     <= (SELECT END_LMTH FROM MDATE )
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>said :-)

MDATE is the good old 'date card'. Surely you remember date cards.

>Not sure why you would store them as host variables though.

So the database isn't tempted to rerun the select for each customer. The date
card *could* change during the 23 hour run.

>> Run an EXPLAIN PLAN to find out where it's spending all its time. Look for
>> missing indexes, especially on C.ACCT_TYPE. Make sure the optimizer is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>catalogue and see if any index even exists

An EXPLAIN takes milliseconds, not 14 hours. 

>Also I would assume that ACCT_TYPE has low ordinality so an index probably
>wouldn't help that much...but with little info it's hard to tell.

The whole table is probably cached, and it probably does have an index. My main
point was to look at EXPLAIN, with emphasis on indexes.

>> Try making the detail SELECT into a sub-select with DISTINCT on CUST_NBR.
>After
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>many As joined to many Bs....

It's a one to one join per account. He's looking for this month's statement and
last month's. Having found them, it's a waste of time to look for others on the
same account.

-----------------------------------------------
>The original query....
>>SELECT FULL_TERM, A.REM_MONTHS, COUNT(*), SUM(A.BILL_BALANCE)
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T15:29:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rpwVa.176600$ic1.4017495@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
Running this in a program will not make it faster than it is in QMF.
Run an explain - you're probably not getting any index access.....
Post to a db2 (or oracle) group and provide *much more* info if you want an
answer.

This is *not* even remotely close to a monstrous query.

JCE
```

```
On Wed, 30 Jul 2003 07:24:45 +1200 "Jagster" <jnori@bigfoot.com> wrote:

:>Thanks to all of those who tried to give me genuine solutions, I will try to
:>put into practice.

:>May be people who want money to solve technical problems should log to job
:>sites and not to user groups. Sending messages like this offends the people
:>who are genuinely only interested to learn the language. Atleast if I were u
:>i wouldn't post messages asking for money  etc etc.

Your question had absolutely nothing to do with COBOL.

You were asking for performance help, which is OK.

You clearly did not even begin to attempt a solution, which is quite bad.

There is absolutely nothing wrong with someone asking for payment for such a
solution. Nobody is forcing you to pay.

You have never supplied any advice in this group. Those that you are
complaining about have, and often.

You do have the "right" to complain about it, but it is quite unlikely that
anyone will assign much value to your complaints.

:>"jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com> wrote in message
:>news:rpwVa.176600$ic1.4017495@twister.tampabay.rr.com...

:>> > Hi there,

:>> > be greatly appreciated. Thanx in advance for this help.
:>> >
:>> > Here is the query  !!
:>> >
:>> > SELECT FULL_TERM, A.REM_MONTHS, COUNT(*), SUM(A.BILL_BALANCE)
:>> >      , END_LMTH_CHAR
:>> > FROM CSTMT A, LCUST B, ACCPLN C
:>> >    , CSTMT D
:>> >    , MDATE
:>> > WHERE D.CUST_NBR      = A.CUST_NBR
:>> >   AND D.ACCT_NBR      = A.ACCT_NBR
:>> >   AND A.STMT_DATE     >= (SELECT STRT_2MTH
:>> >                            FROM MDATE )
:>> >   AND A.STMT_DATE     <  (SELECT STRT_LMTH
:>> >                             FROM MDATE )
:>> >   AND A.BILL_BALANCE  > 0
:>> >   AND A.REM_MONTHS    > 0
:>> >   AND D.STMT_DATE     >= (SELECT STRT_LMTH
:>> >                             FROM MDATE )
:>> >   AND D.STMT_DATE     <= (SELECT END_LMTH
:>> >                             FROM MDATE )
:>> >   AND D.BILL_BALANCE  < 0.01
:>> >   AND D.CREDITS       < 0
:>> >
:>> >   AND A.CUST_NBR       = B.CUST_NBR
:>> >   AND A.ACCT_NBR       = B.ACCT
:>> >   AND B.ACCT_TYPE      = C.ACCT_TYPE
:>> >   AND B.REVL_PLAN_NBR  = C.PAY_PLAN
:>> >   AND B.ORG_ID         = '600555'
:>> >   AND B.ACCT_TYPE      = '30'
:>> > GROUP BY FULL_TERM, A.REM_MONTHS
:>> >        , END_LMTH_CHAR
:>> > ORDER BY 1,2
```

```
I would guess most people are doing this type of stuff on the clock so it
isn't really free is it...

> You have never supplied any advice in this group. Those that you are
> complaining about have, and often.
A COBOL beginner who obviously has not looked at very much will not offer
much in the way of advice...doh..

In my short time on this group it seems that there are some very helpful
people such as Robert Wagner who gave a well thought out answer and others
who just well....aren't.  It comes with the territory and I'm probably the
latter...so won't say too much :-o

> You do have the "right" to complain about it, but it is quite unlikely
that

Yet he got a response......and a follow up...woo hoo...

JCE
```

```
you're a regular lurker, I can't tell.  Many times, people post to this 
newsgroup -not- looking for genuine solutions, but on a fishing 
expedition to see what they can get for nothing.
```

```
"Jagster" <jnori@bigfoot.com> wrote in message
news:vPzVa.8119$9f7.949427@news02.tsnz.net...
| Thanks to all of those who tried to give me genuine solutions, I will try
to
| put into practice.
|
| May be people who want money to solve technical problems should log to job
| sites and not to user groups.

People who solve the type of problem you posted are called Programmers.
Many of us work as consultants, that's how we make our living.

You didn't say you tried different solutions, and were still having
problems.
If you did, you might find that others were willing to help by pointing you
in the right direction.

If someone here fixed your problem, you would become the person who fixed
that horrible 14 hour query problem, and you would be called on again when
similar problems came up.
What would you do then, post another query to have someone else tune it?

Have you read a book, or manual?
I doubt it, because they tend to mention the Explain, and Plan Table.

| Sending messages like this offends the people
| who are genuinely only interested to learn the language. Atleast if I were
u
| i wouldn't post messages asking for money  etc etc.
|

If this is your job, as your post indicates, tell whoever gave you the
assignment that you need training in COBOL, and/or DB2 in order to handle
this type of assignment.
An alternative is to work with a consultant/mentor who has the necessary
skills, and get some on-the-job training.

The query you posted isn't complex or monstrous, it's relatively simple.
The monstrous adjective may apply to the DB2 resources it consumes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T03:10:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f289645.45599382@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net> <b7lfivk0hoegh7t4t4ujvqj6el9tao24c5@4ax.com> <5OVVa.8357$9f7.975320@news02.tsnz.net>`

```
"Jagster" <jnori@bigfoot.com> wrote:

>I am stuck at the point where Mr Robert wagner  asked me to convert the Self
>Join to Inner Join. I will work on that today and may be by the end of the
>day I will land some where near to the solution.

Don't spend a lot of time on it. It won't make any difference if the optimizer
did it for you, as it should have. EXPLAIN will tell you whether it did.

If you decide to do it in COBOL, open two cursors on CSTMT and do the self-join
with program logic. If CSTMT has PREVIOUS_BALANCE, use that in place of the
self-join to really speed it up.
```

```
By this time you should have some DB2 materials: manuals, and/or books.

As others have pointed out, DB2 is where you should be concentrating your
efforts.

Some of the items, among many, that may interest you are:

Sub Selects

Index Overloading

Stage 1 Predicates (sargeable)

Table Space Scans

(And maybe) Partitioning

You will probably want to set up some queries of the SYSIBM tables.

You should also have SPUFI as well as QMF at your disposal.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting a complex SQL query to COBOL pgm !!

- **From:** hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T03:59:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3168592.1059623982@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<vjjVa.7968$9f7.930843@news02.tsnz.net>`

```

The Following program will pre-process cleanly and compile under Fujitsu
NetCOBOL for Windows Version 7. Please note, however that it needs
further modification (like adding an INTO area to select the data into
or making it a DECLARE CURSOR and then adding OPEN CURSOR and FETCH
logic, although I didn't look close enough at what your doing to
determin if this could result in multiple rows being retrieved or if it
retrieves a unique row.
Thanks,
-Howard

 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. TESTSQL.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 DATA DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
     EXEC SQL BEGIN DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.

 01 SQLSTATE    PIC X(5)         Value Spaces.
 01 SQLCODE     PIC S9(9) COMP-5 Value 0.
 01 SQLMSG      PIC X(255)       Value Spaces.
     EXEC SQL END DECLARE SECTION END-EXEC.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.

     EXEC SQL
             SELECT FULL_TERM, A.REM_MONTHS, COUNT(*),
                    SUM(A.BILL_BALANCE),
              END_LMTH_CHAR
       FROM CSTMT A, LCUST B, ACCPLN C,  CSTMT D,  MDATE
       WHERE D.CUST_NBR = A.CUST_NBR
         AND D.ACCT_NBR = A.ACCT_NBR
         AND A.STMT_DATE >= (SELECT STRT_2MTH FROM MDATE )
         AND A.STMT_DATE 0
         AND A.REM_MONTHS > 0
         AND D.STMT_DATE >= (SELECT STRT_LMTH FROM MDATE )
         AND D.STMT_DATE <= (SELECT END_LMTH FROM MDATE )
         AND D.BILL_BALANCE < 0.01
         AND D.CREDITS < 0
         AND A.CUST_NBR = B.CUST_NBR
         AND A.ACCT_NBR = B.ACCT
         AND B.ACCT_TYPE = C.ACCT_TYPE
         AND B.REVL_PLAN_NBR = C.PAY_PLAN
         AND B.ORG_ID = '600555'
         AND B.ACCT_TYPE = '30'
       GROUP BY FULL_TERM, A.REM
       ORDER BY 1,2
     END-EXEC.
```

---

<a id="t-fe23bc05fc"></a>
## Thread: IDCAMS to load a HIGH VALUES record — 8 msgs

### IDCAMS to load a HIGH VALUES record

- **From:** studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer)
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T07:56:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0465864.0307290656.117872f0@posting.google.com>`

```
Is there a way that a IDCAMS REPRO step can load a VSAM file and then
put a standard HIGH-VALUES record as the last record on the file?

I have a file that I will have to access randomly using START, then
READ NEXT.  If I am beyond the last record with the START command with
the key not < dmr-key.  I get a 23 for a file status because it did
not find the record in question, an acceptable condition.  However the
read next command returns a 46, meaning that the read caused an 'at
end' condition.

I can sort in a high-values record with the data prior to the IDCAMS
step,  or I found an ASSEMBLER module that will do this for me, but
the overhead is not pretty.

Can IDCAMS do this??

Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: IDCAMS to load a HIGH VALUES record

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T12:09:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0307291109.53f94957@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0307290656.117872f0@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello

1) You could concatenate a file containing just the single record
containing high values with the file that you wish to process,
obviously this file would be concatenated after the other one.  You
could create the file containing the single record with a utility such
as file-aid, then keep it permanently for reuse.

2) Consider amending the program that creates the file to generate the
high values record as its last action in writing the file.

3) Consider just handling the file status 46 as an at end condition
and process accordingly.  Or better still, just act on the file status
23 from the START statement and don't do the READ NEXT when this
condition occurs.

For example:

01  ws-afile-status.
    88  ws-afile-key-not-found             value '23'.
    88  ws-afile-no-valid-next-record      value '46'.
    03  ws-afile-status-1              pic x.
        88  ws-afile-ok                    value '0'.
        88  ws-afile-at-end                value '1'.
    03  ws-afile-status-2              pic x.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IDCAMS to load a HIGH VALUES record

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T00:27:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vhEVa.28298$BM.9295776@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0307290656.117872f0@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0307291109.53f94957@posting.google.com>`

```
>standard HIGH-VALUES

No such thing. I asume from the rest of your comments you A) have a KSDS
VSAM file (not ESDS or RRDS) and B) want a record with a key of x'FFFFFF...'
inserted with the rest of that record space or zeros?

That said, just create the 'high-values' record with FileAid (or
SORT/IEBGENER it in a previous job step), concatenate your real file and
REPRO...

STEP   EXEC PGM=IDCAMS
INF         DD DSN=DSN.WITH.ONE.HIGH.VALUE.RECORD     << real or generated
in prior step
              DD DSN=REAL.DSN.WITH.REAL.DATA
OUTF     DD DSN=YOUR.VSAM.DATASET.NAME
 SYSIN  DD *
     REPRO INFILE(INF) OUTFILE(OUTF)
....
```

#### ↳ Re: IDCAMS to load a HIGH VALUES record

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.org>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T18:59:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<w_2dnVrRzuPUl7qiXTWQlg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0307290656.117872f0@posting.google.com>`

```
> Is there a way that a IDCAMS REPRO step can load a VSAM file and then
> put a standard HIGH-VALUES record as the last record on the file?
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Can IDCAMS do this??

Why don't you just concatonate a high-values record in the input?

//FILEIN  DD DSN=THE.DATA.FILE,DISP=OLD
//        DD DSN=THE.FF.RECORD,DISP=OLD
//FILEOUT DD DSN=THE.VSAM.FILE,DISP=OLD
```

#### ↳ Re: IDCAMS to load a HIGH VALUES record

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T00:54:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SGEVa.76277$3o3.5223434@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0307290656.117872f0@posting.google.com>`

```
Top post only.

You realize that if you put a high-values dummy record at the end of the
file, every program that accesses the file sequentially will have to process
it.
You may be creating more work than you save, people will have to program
around your solution.
You seem to have a handle on the file status, so it doesn't make much sense
to add a high-values record.
I hope this is a new development project.
If it's maintenance, you may wind up with egg all over your face when
currently working sequential access programs start to fail on the high-value
record.

"The Mean Farmer" <studlow1@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:e0465864.0307290656.117872f0@posting.google.com...
| Is there a way that a IDCAMS REPRO step can load a VSAM file and then
| put a standard HIGH-VALUES record as the last record on the file?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
| Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: IDCAMS to load a HIGH VALUES record

- **From:** studlow1@hotmail.com (The Mean Farmer)
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T06:51:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0465864.0307300551.14878c6f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<e0465864.0307290656.117872f0@posting.google.com>`

```
Thank you for all of your replies.  It never ceases to surprise me the
wide spread ideas that come out to real questions on this board.

I had thought through this process so just to set some minds at ease:

* the input file I am using will be placed on the mainframe from a
DATABASE on the open system - so there is not a program that
manipulates the data prior to my needing to load it.  It would have
been all too easy to load a HV record after EOF in a prior program. 
Putting a HV dummy record in concatenation is easy enough a solution
as well - but I was sure that there has to be a better way...which is
why I came here.

* This is a new process and no one will need the file before or after
me.  It is only a validation file.  I put toghether the KEY from the
INPUT file, if it is on the VSAM file the record processes.  If it
isn't there on the START command I still need to read the next record
to determine which part(s) are missing so I can report why a record
was rejected.
* Using the File-Status of 46 sounds like a great way to go to me.  If
it comes back then the base element of the KEY is missing and I will
have what I needed.

Should take me 5 minutes to code and test for that.

Thanks again everyone.

> 
> I can sort in a high-values record with the data prior to the IDCAMS
> step,  or I found an ASSEMBLER module that will do this for me, but
> the overhead is not pretty.
```

## Thread: Cobol from MVS to AIX — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Cobol from MVS to AIX

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T15:38:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OxwVa.176603$ic1.4019998@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
What are the major issues for migrating a smallish (10-15 modules average
size of 1500 with each having 500 lines of needless commentary) from an MVS
/ JCL Batch environment to a Unix/AIX environment.   Nearly all will have
some level of DB2 and some will have MQ Series.

This is not something immediate that I have to do, but in a quest to calm
the blood thirsty server-ites  I'd like to believe that the code could be
migrated without rewriting (or running through a conversion) in 'C' or 'C++'
(the C equivalent  to the new  "ADD +1 TO COBOL GIVING COBOL" language).

It would also have to maintain some level of performance - if it were to
drop from say 300 records a second to 10 records a second then it would fail
to impress.

Also, if the conversion methods have had success I would be interested to
know.

If there is a prior thread then I apologize - I haven't really looked
yet..thought I would go straight to the experts.  Just drop it in here and I
will go peruse....thanks.

JCE
```

```
There's no such thing as needless commentary.

That reminds me of the story of the third-year ( oh, you got time for this?
it's kind of long-winded) law student....

MCM
```

```
You've obviously only worked places where the commentary is looked after as
meticulously as the code.

Examples of needless commentary.
1) A prolog listing the input and output files....that's what the file
section is for.....
2) Programmers name...(if you aren't a contractor with ownership rights who
cares)
3) * This adds the subtotals together to get the total
        ADD SUBTOTAL-AMT TO SUBTOTAL-TAX GIVING TOTAL
4) Removed some code with project X
5) Updated by Bob on January 4, 1987 due to customer complaint.
6) Language: COBOL

Thanks for the answer though. I am now well on my way to learning what I
wanted to know.

JCE
```

```
jce wrote:

> You've obviously only worked places where the commentary is looked after as
> meticulously as the code.
> 
> Examples of needless commentary.
> 5) Updated by Bob on January 4, 1987 due to customer complaint.

Bob needs his butt whooped...  What did he update?  Was there a ticket 
number (or a short description of the problem and/or solution)?  It's 
nice to note that you made a change in the code, but how did you change it?

(We have a shop standard that mandates a "modification log" be added to 
the program when you work it, and that this log must include name/rank, 
date (month/year is sufficient), and a citation of a control number, 
which can be used to look up requirements, test plans, design, and the 
like.)
```

```
LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote:

>jce wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>like.)

Modification log was a primative attempt at version control. When version
control (CM) software came IN, modification log should have gone OUT. 

As with so many other things in the mainframe world, people continue doing it
without understanding the historical reasons why it started, nor appreciating
that the practice is now pointless.
```

```
news:vienbf4qch2j42@corp.supernews.com...
| jce wrote:
|
| > You've obviously only worked places where the commentary is looked after
as
| > meticulously as the code.
| >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| nice to note that you made a change in the code, but how did you change
it?
|
| (We have a shop standard that mandates a "modification log" be added to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| like.)

After a period of time, the modification log entry becomes obsolete.
Who cares that someone modified the program in 1987?
It's better to have CURRENT narratives that describe what the program does.
If a modification changes the functionality of the program, the narrative
should be changed also.
I'm NOT saying that a modification log shouldn't be maintained, I am saying
that after a series of modifications the modification log should be trimmed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol from MVS to AIX

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T22:21:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7EgWa.899$kP6.74@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<OxwVa.176603$ic1.4019998@twister.tampabay.rr.com> <bg82mi$ru6$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <vj0Wa.3954$K4.177029@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
I have *NOTHING* against Micro Focus for this, but you should certainly
"check out" IBM COBOL/ Set - if  you want a compiler that is "guaranteed" to
keep current with the syntax/semantics available on IBM mainframes.  It is
my *impression* that Micro Focus is (and has continued to be) pretty
compatible, but IBM uses the "same front end" for their compilers on all
(but OS/400) environments.

On the other hand, if you want compatibility with OLDER IBM compilers (e.g.
OS/VS COBOL), Micro Focus provides such - while IBM does not.

Fujitsu is somewhere between.  It provides OS/VS COBOL compatibility and VS
COBOL II compatibility, but not ALL of the current (post-VS COBOL II)  IBM
mainframe compiler features.

RM, AcuCOBOL, PerCOBOL, (as well as those listed above) etc are probably
best if you think that once you port to AIX, you will be porting to a
"variety" of other Linux or Unix environments (and are less worried about
existing IBM extensions).

NOTE WELL:
  This is intended as "personal opinion only".
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol from MVS to AIX

- **From:** Naum <aznaum@nospam.operamail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T20:21:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80lWa.4385$Ye.3299@fed1read02>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<OxwVa.176603$ic1.4019998@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<OxwVa.176603$ic1.4019998@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
No major issues, but there are subtleties to be on guard for.

- EBCDIC vs. ASCII in data files - depending on compiler, you can 
compile for either but mixing them will end up a giant headache
- Fixed record length vs. line terminated data may stipulate some minor 
changes
- Special PARM card provisions - not too tricky but you need to change 
linkage scheme and do a little addressing dance to allow COBOL programs 
bring in PARM card data that comes in as PARM=... on a JCL EXEC line
- No IDCAMS on AIX - if you have VSAM files to port over, you'll need to 
write C modules to recreate such features as load/extract
- JCL - Korn shell conversion unless you're using some integrated 
facility, use of SORT and Awk/Sed (instead of Easytrieve) and EXPORT 
(environment variable).

In one conversion I was involved with, we tried to use MicroFocus but it 
was unsuitable and did not add any efficiency to the conversion effort 
and hence was scrapped.

--Naum

jce wrote:
> What are the major issues for migrating a smallish (10-15 modules average
> size of 1500 with each having 500 lines of needless commentary) from an MVS
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

---

## Thread: [OT] Fortress Mentality - Doesn't Happen in a Vacuum — 14 this month / 23 total

### [OT] Fortress Mentality - Doesn't Happen in a Vacuum

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-07-29T21:59:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg78pl$r5t$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```


All righty, gang... this one will be a little long and rambling but the 
point will be... in there, someplace, maybe.

There's been a bunch of talk here recently about the rigidity associated 
with a Fortress mentality; one of the things I've mentioned that might 
have gotten lost is that departmental mentalities rarely exist in a 
vacuum and an organisation which values precisely defined, rigid functions 
in all other departments will likely do so in DP/IS/MIS, as well.

Today I went for a hairy-ears interview with a pimp and afterwards got to 
take a web-based tech-test... on various aspects of COBOL '74.

(That has nothing to do with the story that follows but I figured folks 
might get a giggle out of this.)

Anyhow... they looked at my bragsheet and said 'It seems that your last 
few assignments have been consulting'; I responded 'My last few 
assignments seem to go back to 1987, then.'

Then I got... 'Are you open to coming aboard full-time?'... and I must be 
getting old because I did not use my Standard Reply of 'I am *always* 
willing to listen to an offer; if someone wishes to take me out for a meal 
I just might have an appetite.'

Instead I tried something different... I said 'I'm always willing to 
listen to an offer... it's just that the offers I get are so bad.'  The 
pimp frowned, slightly, so I went on.

'Let me explain... a few years back I was on a gig, making a hair under 
US$65/hr, and the project leader started making noises about taking me on 
full-time.  I asked 'What do you have to offer?' and he responded 
'US$67,500/yr to start.'  I said 'If that's all that you have then I can't 
understand what reasons there are for me to consider, seriously, an offer 
to cut my salary in half.'

The pimp nodded and said 'I understand...'... and I smiled, broadly, and 
said 'No, you *don't* understand, just as the manager didn't.  Does the 
company have a good insurance package to offer?  Fine, then explain it to 
me... does the company have a good tuition rebate program?  Good, tell me 
about it... does the company have excellent vacation schedules, maybe a 
travel-agent tie-in for cheap places to go?  Great, let me hear about 
them... but all he had to give was the figure for an annual salary and if 
that, indeed, is all he had... then he was *right* not to go on.  A job's 
a variety of factors and this guy could not, even to his own mind, compete 
with my then-current situation on price... and he had nothing to offer 
beyond that.'

Later on, as I was walking with Killer around ManMade lake, I mused on 
this... and I thought about how little I had seen of bargaing that 
includes playing salary off against other forms of remuneration.

'The salary is a little low... how about this, in addition to the salary I 
get full tuition rebate on *any* graduate-level course I take and get a B 
or better in?

'The salary's a little low... how about raising the company matching-rate 
for my pension contributions from 25% to 40%?'

'The salary's a little low... how about accelerating the schedule and 
amounts by which I accumulate vacation?'

It has been my experience that managers would respond to suggestions such 
as these with a smile and 'That's a good idea but Personnel wouldn't let 
me.'

(side note - if someone with sufficient Corporate Authority makes a call 
to Personnel *anything* can be done... but let's leave that aside for the 
moment)

'That's a good idea but Personnel wouldn't let me'... to some ears that 
might sound a bit Fortressoid, aye.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Fortress Mentality - Doesn't Happen in a Vacuum

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T13:51:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f27cd57.13882305@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<bg78pl$r5t$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>Today I went for a hairy-ears interview with a pimp and afterwards got to 
>take a web-based tech-test... on various aspects of COBOL '74.

I aced (100%) three such COBOL skills tests. Then I scored 65% on BrainBench
COBOL II. Lost a job opportunity because of it. 

Beware of skills tests. Most are ok but some are very poorly written. The one I
flunked was not about COBOL; it was about how to install a COBOL compiler,
written by and from a system programmer's point of view. It had many questions
about obscure compiler options. It asked "What's the difference between V3.6 and
V3.7?" MOST questions were totally inappropriate to assess an applications
programmer. 

Other BrainBench tests are ok. The one titled Micro Focus is pretty good. It's
not about Micro Focus (much); it's about COBOL 85. I wrote to the BrainBench
company explaining all this. As expected, their only response was boiler-plate.

Does anyone else have anecdotes about poorly written tests?

When I was hiring programmers, I did a lot of research on correlating test
scores with measurable aptitude. My technique was to numerically grade
programmers of known ability, give them the test, do a linear regression between
test score and ability. Commonly used tests (in the '80s) with titles like
Programmer's Aptitude Test showed predictive power not much better than random.
After evaluating two dozen tests, some far afield, one I wrote myself, I finally
found one that WORKED. Its correlation with known ability was better than 90%,
over a wide range of abilities. It wasn't about programming; it was about logic.
It had 24 questions and took 6-10 minutes to complete. That's what they SHOULD
be using.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Fortress Mentality - Doesn't Happen in a Vacuum

- **From:** Kevin Hansen <khansen@screenio.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T21:53:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92fgivovi66vevdres0vguc7jl2qfivqmn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bg78pl$r5t$1@panix1.panix.com> <3f27cd57.13882305@news.optonline.com>`

```
On Wed, 30 Jul 2003 13:51:34 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
wrote:

>When I was hiring programmers, I did a lot of research on correlating test
>scores with measurable aptitude. My technique was to numerically grade
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>be using. 

---------
On a more informal level...

We once had a board game around the shop called Twixt, which had very
simple rules but required strong analytical abilities to play well.
Very easy to learn; one game and you got the gist of it.

In a (relatively small) sample, we found that programming ability
correlated strongly with competence at Twixt, even in new players.  We
seriously considered using it as an aptitude-testing tool.

If an individual has good analytical skills, it's no big deal to teach
'em COBOL and put them to work.  They'll catch on in no time.

Kevin

Develop Windows(tm) Applications - in COBOL!
http://www.ScreenIO.com
```

```
A waitress came to me for a programming job because her friends told her she was
SO smart. She knew nothing about programming and, to make it worse, she oozed
emotional injury from every pore .. child abuse, abandonment, hostility.  A most
unattractive applicant. She aced my test -- did it half the time and got 'em all
right. Three months later she was a Senior Programmer. Six months later she was
a Star. A year later she went on to be Programming Manager elsewhere.
```

```
Aye :)

I'm currenly a consultant.  Been on this assignment pusing 8 years.  Back about
4 years ago several other consultants were asked if they wanted to go 'direct'
(jargon around here for going as a employee of the client company).  They
jumped at the chance because the company offered many perks that they weren't
getting as a consultant.  I was kinda hoping that I too would be offered this
chance as while I'm getting pretty good benefits the client's benefits were
superb.  

Then the client company was sold to another company - and it was about 6 months
after that I got my offer - which I turned down.  The purchasing company had
lousy benefits and I made all the negotiating talk DD showed above - with the
same answer he indicated.  Ah well.  Fortress human resourses.
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Fortress Mentality - Doesn't Happen in a Vacuum

- **From:** "jce" <defaultuser@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T03:56:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jr0Wa.3956$K4.177817@twister.tampabay.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<bg78pl$r5t$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bg78pl$r5t$1@panix1.panix.com...
>
> Later on, as I was walking with Killer around ManMade lake, I mused on
> this... and I thought about how little I had seen of bargaing that
> includes playing salary off against other forms of remuneration.


I'm not a contractor and *every* review I get drilled into me that my salary
is only a part of my compensation.
In my instance it's relatively true - I get paid well for the region, get
401k matching, health benefits and a flexible work from home routine.  I
believe the managers are made to say this as the rates of increase in pay
have been reduced across the board for the last few years so they are
emphasizing some other benefits.

The relationship to contractors has cooled recently however, I have seen
standard contract rates cut - stricter adherence to the maximum lifespan of
contract, as well as a more you're in and you're out.  When I first started
contractors were *like* employees only being paid more with less benefits to
kind of average out.

The one major change is that about 4 years ago REGULAR employees used to
QUIT then get hired back as "consultants" for more pay and I don't even hear
whispers of this anymore.  I new one guy who did this and worked for double
pay to work half a day....he said they paid him twice as much to drive him
half as crazy....

JCE
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Fortress Mentality - Doesn't Happen in a Vacuum

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T15:36:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f292c3a.87559373@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<bg78pl$r5t$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>'Let me explain... a few years back I was on a gig, making a hair under 
>US$65/hr, and the project leader started making noises about taking me on 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>me...

How much is a good insurance package worth? Companies say benefits are worth 30%
of base salary. That's an avarage for all employees; that doesn't mean YOUR
benefits will be worth 30%. A minimum wage employee with base $1,000/month
receiving health insurance worth $400/month is getting 40% for that one benefit
alone. A programmer with base $6,500/month and the same health insurance is
getting only 8%. 

You can buy private health insurance for $250/month. The notion that companies
get a 'group discount' is hogwash. Typically, they overpay.

Ballpark figures for the value of benefits per year:

Insurance       $3,000
Tuition             1,000
Holidays           3,000
Vacation          3,000
Total             10,000

So far we have a shortfall of $52,500. (Assuming you don't stay long enough to
be vested in retirement.)

>'The salary is a little low... how about this, in addition to the salary I 
>get full tuition rebate on *any* graduate-level course I take and get a B 
>or better in?

Worth maybe $2,000.

>'The salary's a little low... how about raising the company matching-rate 
>for my pension contributions from 25% to 40%?'

Worth $1,200, assuming $8,000 contribution.

>'The salary's a little low... how about accelerating the schedule and 
>amounts by which I accumulate vacation?'

An extra week is worth $1,500.

Why would anyone give up $52,500 to get $1,500 in benefits?

But wait, there's more. If a contractor plays the per diem game right, (s)he
pays little or no Federal income tax, FICA and State tax. That's worth a
whopping $10,000 to 20,000 per year. 

The downside is contractors are unemployed for 1-6 months between gigs. Using 3
months as an average, and deducting unemployment benefits, that costs $20,000
per year. 

Bottom line: the contractor makes $45,000 more, using your numbers. A more
typical difference is $20,000.
```

---

```
compile correctly.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Netexpress Rebuild error

- **From:** "Steve Rainbird" <steve.rainbird@nospam.mssint.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T18:34:55+00:00

```
Donald,

An entire section of code with no statements is exactly what it says a
"WARNING" in fact what had happened was the whole;  section had been
commented out leaving just the section header and the exit.  So as to avoid
commenting out every where it was called.  Lazy coding I know but not
illegal.

If you actually read the other warnings they were actually for "Original
item is larger than redefinition (80 > 10).  not the other way around.
Again lazy coding I know but nevertheless not illegal.

In fact I have got rid of all the warnings now except the COMP-5 warnings
which are impossible to get rid of and I still get "Rebuild complete with
errors".

Regards

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Netexpress Rebuild error

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T22:53:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h%XVa.2751$lq.539@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bg7v4d$on2$1@sparta.btinternet.com> <tIOVa.4684$Cx4.638151@news20.bellglobal.com> <bg934e$5bp$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <EvXVa.4234$mv6.752793@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald,
  In GENERAL (in an ANSI/ISO conforming program) you are correct.  HOWEVER,
as this was explicitly a NetExpress question, you might be interested in the
"well-documented" extension expressed in syntax rule 2 of the NE V4 LRM
which states (in its entirety),

"When the level-number of data-name-1 is other than 01, it must specify the
same number of character positions that the data item referenced by
data-name-2 contains except that the data-name-1 area can be either smaller
than the data-name-2 area

*>>> the next part is the relevant MF extension

or be larger than the data-name-2 area. In this case, extra storage is
reserved to provide sufficient storage for the number of character positions
in the largest of the redefining or redefined items.

*>>> end of documented extension

It is important to observe that the REDEFINES clause specifies the
redefinition of a storage area, not of the data items occupying the area."

P.S.  Of course, this wasn't the orignal problem anyway, but I thought that
I would provide the information for what it is worth.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Netexpress Rebuild error

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T21:59:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IcXVa.2680$lq.2651@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<bg7v4d$on2$1@sparta.btinternet.com> <tIOVa.4684$Cx4.638151@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Odd by true fact ...

 The ENTIRE procedure division is Optional in the '85 ANSI/ISO Standard.  It
is entirely "valid" to create such a program - and there are even some ODD
(and I have never seen them in the "real world") cases where it accomplishes
something.

For a SUB-program (not main program) there is an "implicit" Exit Program at
the end of "execution".  Therefore, if (for example) you have a main program
CALL a sub-program with an EXTERNAL item in its Working-Storage Section -
but NO procedure division, this will ALLOCATE the external item (if not
already done so in the run-unit).

I see NOTHING wrong with a WARNING message for a program structured this
way - but it certainly is NOT an "error"
```

```
It's useful as a stub.

User might want to name IEFBR14 steps for the convenience of a scheduler.

>For a SUB-program (not main program) there is an "implicit" Exit Program at
>the end of "execution".  Therefore, if (for example) you have a main program
>CALL a sub-program with an EXTERNAL item in its Working-Storage Section -
>but NO procedure division, this will ALLOCATE the external item (if not
>already done so in the run-unit).

On IBM mainframes, it is semi-common to fill an array using an OS LOAD command.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Netexpress Rebuild error

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T19:23:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg9646$3i4$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bg7v4d$on2$1@sparta.btinternet.com> <tIOVa.4684$Cx4.638151@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> I read several errors in the below. An entire section of code with no
> statements in it is more than a "warning", 

I sometimes have empty paragraphs (it was "paragraph or section").  
Sometimes to 'stub-out' code that may be required later.  I used to do it 
before in-line performs were available where the whole perform logic was in 
the VARYING .. UNTIL.

> while redefining an area larger
> than the original definition is definitely illegal, and cannot possibly
> compile correctly.

>> COBCH1202I Original item is larger than redefinition (80 > 7). :

For 01 levels the areas may be different sizes, '85 allows the redefines to 
be smaller _or_ larger.  (ie it is not illegal and should compile 
correctly).

For other levels they must be the same size.
```

<a id="t-322348b718"></a>
## Thread: Line Printer reports and column headings — 27 this month / 44 total

### Line Printer reports and column headings

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T13:35:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RQPVa.80500$0v4.5346018@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Most of us in the mainframe world still have to deal with line printer
reports, and one of the most time consuming tasks is keeping the column
heading lined up with the detail data, especially when someone looks at the
report, and says "It looks good, but could you change the order of the
columns to ....", or "could you put more space between the columns".

I have a solution that makes the task a bit easier by using the detail line
for the column headings.
Moving columns around is no big deal, because the group item (column
heading) contains the detail line data.

I'm posting this because I always see separate working storage layouts for
column headings, and it  needlessly complicates development, and
maintenance.

Here is some sample code:

019700 01 R2-DTL-DETAIL-LINE-1.
019800
019900*    NAMES ENDING IN COH ARE THE COLUMN HEADING
020000
020100     05 R2-DTL-CC                           PIC X      VALUE ' '.
020200     05                                                PIC X
VALUE  SPACES.
020300     05 R2-DTL-BIN-NBR-COH.
020400       10 R2-DTL-BIN-NBR              PIC ZZ9.
020500     05                                                PIC X
VALUE  SPACES.
020600     05 R2-DTL-ITM-CNT-COH.
020700       10 R2-DTL-ITM-CNT              PIC ZZ,ZZ9.
020800     05                                                PIC XX
VALUE  SPACES.
020900     05 R2-DTL-ITEM-ID-COH.
021000       10 R2-DTL-ITEM-ID              PIC X(16).
021100     05                                              PIC XX     VALUE
SPACES.
021200     05 R2-DTL-ITEM-DESC-COH.
021300       10 R2-DTL-ITEM-DESC            PIC X(30).
021400     05                                                  PIC XX
VALUE  SPACES.
021500     05 R2-DTL-ITEM-LOC-COH.
021600       10 R2-DTL-ITEM-LOC             PIC X(16).
021700

036500
036600 3000-01-01-PRT-RPT2-COL-HEAD.
036700
036800     MOVE  '0'                                    TO R2-DTL-CC.
036900     MOVE  'BIN'                               TO R2-DTL-BIN-NBR-COH.
037000     MOVE  '   QTY'                           TO R2-DTL-ITM-CNT-COH.
037100     MOVE  'ITEM ID'                        TO R2-DTL-ITEM-ID-COH.
037200     MOVE  'ITEM DESCRIPTION '  TO R2-DTL-ITEM-DESC-COH.
037300     MOVE  'ITEM LOCATION'        TO R2-DTL-ITEM-LOC-COH.
037400
037500     WRITE  RPT2-SETUP-REPORT-REC-OUT
037600            FROM  R2-DTL-DETAIL-LINE-1
037700
037800     MOVE  SPACES
037900           TO  R2-DTL-BIN-NBR-COH
038000                  R2-DTL-ITM-CNT-COH
038100                  R2-DTL-ITEM-ID-COH
038200                  R2-DTL-ITEM-DESC-COH
038300                  R2-DTL-ITEM-LOC-COH.
```

```
beginning ...

>reports, and one of the most time consuming tasks is keeping the column
```

#### ↳ Re: Line Printer reports and column headings

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T21:54:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s8XVa.2674$lq.458@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<RQPVa.80500$0v4.5346018@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Only SLIGHTLY more difficult (IMHO) than using the built-in "COLUMN"
features of the Report-Writer module - which is available on IBM mainframes
(and I assume others as well).

RW may take SOME work for "proportional font/page printer" environments, but
it sure is a "piece-of-cake" for line printers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Line Printer reports and column headings

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T22:44:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YSXVa.81076$0v4.5383033@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<RQPVa.80500$0v4.5346018@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <s8XVa.2674$lq.458@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:s8XVa.2674$lq.458@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net...
| Only SLIGHTLY more difficult (IMHO) than using the built-in "COLUMN"
| features of the Report-Writer module - which is available on IBM
mainframes
| (and I assume others as well).
|
| RW may take SOME work for "proportional font/page printer" environments,
but
| it sure is a "piece-of-cake" for line printers.

I agree, but it's been years since I've seen Report Writer used, and I was
the only one using it. (Even had a code generator to read the field
descriptions and generate the Report Writer COLUMN statements.)

Even though the COBOL Report Writer is available as a pre-procesor I don't
know any mainframe programmers who would use it, and I doubt many mainframe
shops even have it installed. (It doesn't seem to be bundled with the
compiler. Am I wrong on this? With the newer change managment systems, they
have a menu of compile options, and I've never seen an option to use the
Report Writer pre-compiler. I don't know why Report Writer isn't used in
more IBM shops, but it isn't.)

END OF POST.

|
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Line Printer reports and column headings

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T21:22:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vijjmidkgmd472@corp.supernews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<DnhWa.82371$0v4.5489874@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<RQPVa.80500$0v4.5346018@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <s8XVa.2674$lq.458@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net> <YSXVa.81076$0v4.5383033@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <8KGcnWnoz_Z_GrWiXTWQkQ@giganews.com> <5L7Wa.81672$0v4.5442609@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <vij63djj71i294@corp.supernews.com> <DnhWa.82371$0v4.5489874@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:

> "LX-i" <LXi0007@Netscape.net> wrote in message
> news:vij63djj71i294@corp.supernews.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Keep the spirit, but be prepared for anything.

heh - I am.  I get shot down, but it's usually by management, not by 
other programmers.  They generally think this stuff is really cool.

Well, imagine you've been on COBOL 74 since 1979 - and in 2001, you were 
finally able to start writing in COBOL 85.  Now, there's this guy in the 
office who, in his off time, networks with other COBOL professionals and 
gleans tips on style, practices, and efficient ways of doing things, and 
shares them with his co-workers.  Wouldn't you be inclined to at least 
listen?  :)

Not to toot my own horn, but I brought our entire application into COBOL 
85.  I dug into the system-level workings and got them compliant, and 
after that, it was as easy as a recompile (as not much went away between 
74 and 85).  I did it for several reasons...

1.  Our programs were getting too big to compile without implementing 
segmentation.  The "extended mode" executables created by the Unisys 
COBOL 85 compiler will segment themselves automatically (if you tell 
them to).

2.  We needed to be able to call routines to copy files around. 
Program-Callable FURPUR (PCFP) allowed us to do that, but it only works 
for extended-mode programs.

3.  When all of us first get to our shop, we're given 3 weeks of 
training (beginner, intermediate, and advanced) in COBOL.  But, it's 
COBOL 85 - so many of the things we'd learn, such as Evaluate, or inline 
Performs, we couldn't use.  (I think most folks will agree that an 
Evaluate is more maintainable than a slew of nested If statements...) 
So, this point is really "increased maintainability".

Now, inspired a bit by PECD and his component methodologies he's been 
touting here, I'm working to bring us into the dynamically called 
component world for some of our more common processes.  There are many 
advantages to this, the biggest one being that we don't have to 
recompile every program because a copy proc changed.  :)

Now, I haven't succeeded at everything I've tried.  When I was 
converting the system-level stuff to COBOL 85, there was a basic-mode 
routine that our date/time proc used to get the date and return a 
century too.  (I guess no one figured out YYYYMMDD...)  I changed this 
one line to "Accept W315-Current-Date-S8 From Date YYYYMMDD" - no 
functional change at all - but because it was in a copy proc which was 
copied in every program in the system, I was told I couldn't change it. 
  My solution - copy the whole proc to another proc, increment the 
number by 100 (P315 to P415), leave the paragraphs and variables the 
same, and press on.

A year later, I was given opportunity to change P315 - I made the 
change, and the programs that were using P415 were changed back to the 
"standard" the next time they were worked.

I guess I say all that to say - I think I'll keep my optimism.  When I 
share what I learn, I've done my part - but, we've got a good bunch of 
programmers that are working there too, and they want to expand their 
skills too.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Line Printer reports and column headings

```
For anyone who is interested in an EXCELLENT tutorial (and documentation)
for the IBM "add-on" Report Writer product - which is also available for
many PC/Unix compilers, see:

 http://www.spc-systems.com/

and click on the
  "Introduction and Tutorial"

section.  Many (not all) the "extensions" to the ANSI '85 Standard RW
feature that are available in this product are ALSO included in the 2002 ISO
Standard.
```

#### ↳ Re: Line Printer reports and column headings

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-07-31T20:15:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bg9950$4ru$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<RQPVa.80500$0v4.5346018@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:

> Most of us in the mainframe world still have to deal with line printer
> reports, and one of the most time consuming tasks is keeping the column
> heading lined up with the detail data, especially when someone looks at
> the report, and says "It looks good, but could you change the order of the
> columns to ....", or "could you put more space between the columns".

Have you thought about generating the Cobol code for the line headings and 
data from a text file ?

Have a text file that specifies the layout as, say:

*OUT filename.cpy
*PREFIX R2-DTL-
*HEAD
O BIN    QTY ITEM ID          ITEM DESCRIPTION ....
*LINE
% %%% %%%%%% %%%%%%%%%%%%%%%% %%%%%..
*FIELDS
CC=X
BIN-NBR=ZZ9
ITEM-CNT=ZZ,ZZ9
ITEM-ID=X(16)
ITEM-DESC=X(30)
...
*END

Then the users can fiddle with it themselves and have the code regenerated.

I did this for print layouts and screens (ACCEPT, DISPLAY) some time ago 
and tend to now use templates, but there is a large cost in doing 
templating both in doing the templating code (which should be done once and 
called) and in the run-time.

Templating means, however, that I can have one template to do printed 
output (and this can be different for each user if required) and can change 
the template to output HTML, or XML, or EDIFACT, or to erge file that will 
produce forms.
```

```
repeated process until it was OK.
Then I ran the layout through the Generator.

| Have a text file that specifies the layout as, say:
|

regenerated.

Some of the users I know will fiddle with it, others won't.
They would rather see a report, and then change it.
Even if they design it themselves, they will probably want changes.

A design tool similar to the Report Wizzard in Access would  probably be
acceptable, but not to all users.
Even with the Wizzard, I often have to "program" the Access reports myself.
I have had some users who will do the programming themselves, just give them
an empty database, and the files to load, and they're happier than a pig in
shit.

| I did this for print layouts and screens (ACCEPT, DISPLAY) some time ago
| and tend to now use templates, but there is a large cost in doing
| templating both in doing the templating code (which should be done once
and
| called) and in the run-time.

It isn't easy to write what would in essence be the COBOL REPORT WRITER.
And the concept of using the template would have to be acceptable to the
programmers, and managers in the group.

I doubt that I could get approval to do it.

(Even though the COBOL Report Writer is available as a pre-procesor I don't
know any mainframe programmers who would use it, and I doubt many mainframe
shops even have it installed.)

| Templating means, however, that I can have one template to do printed
| output (and this can be different for each user if required) and can
change
| the template to output HTML, or XML, or EDIFACT, or to erge file that will
| produce forms.

Sounds interesting, could you post the source code?
Only joking!!

P.S. Your solution does have merit (an understatement), but I have to
address the environment that I work in (users, and the other programmers).
What I will probably do is create an edit macro to read the source field
descriptions
(05   some-field  PIC S9(7)v99  comp-3.), and generate the report detail
layout I proposed.
What I wanted to do is get a jump on what I know is coming, and make my job
easier.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Report Writer (was: Line Printer reports and column headings

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-07-30T22:26:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SCXVa.2722$lq.1473@newsread2.news.atl.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<RQPVa.80500$0v4.5346018@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bg9950$4ru$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <fvXVa.81042$0v4.5381637@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:fvXVa.81042$0v4.5381637@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
>
> (Even though the COBOL Report Writer is available as a pre-procesor I
don't
> know any mainframe programmers who would use it, and I doubt many
mainframe
> shops even have it installed.)
>
 <much snippage>

The Report-Writer product available on IBM mainframes (and for many PC
compilers) can be used as a "preoprocessor" - but is best used as an
"integrated co-processor" (just like the current CICS and DB2 support).  It
is in "relatively" common USE (not just availability).

My past (and relatively recent) experience is that the (IBM mainframe) world
is divided into shops where RW is COMMONLY used and those where it is never
used (and never was).  Believe me, Harley, there ARE plenty of shops that
use it frequently and extensively.  My *guess* is that this is less than the
majority, but far from minimal.

This may or may not "improve" now that this is a REQUIRED feature of the
2002 ISO COBOL Standard (with a few enhancements - such as "centering" on a
column - which seems relavant for headers).  As IBM has yet to indicate
when/if they will provide a 2002 conforming compiler, this may seem less
than relevant.  HOWEVER, *IF* (and it is a BIG IF) other vendors do provide
2002 conforming compilers, RW may gain ground as a "commonly accepted and
used" tool. This will be even more true if (as I believe is already
happening) vendors provide "better" extensions for proportional fonts and
page printers.
```

```
I'm using report writer.  Have been for years.  Even won some $$ with it 
back in the 70's.  We had some consultant from Cincom come in and try 
and sell us a product called SOCRATES which was a cludegy (sp?)  
database report generator that interfaced with TOTAL.  I told him I 
could do things easier with COBOL Report writer.  He said BS so we 
wagered a little and both wrote 3 different reports.  My Report writer 
reports were done in half the time and used half the coding statements 
and looked better<G>.  He muttered a lot but paid up and we passed on 
SOCRATES.

PatH
```
